- **Static Stability:** The robot is stable even when stationary. Determined by the projection of the **Center of Gravity (CoG)** onto the support polygon formed by the wheel-ground contact points.
- **Dynamic Stability:** The robot is only stable while moving (e.g., a bicycle). Most wheeled robots in this course are *statically stable*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Support Polygon Rule:** A robot is statically stable if and only if the vertical projection of its CoG lies **inside the convex hull** of its ground contact points.

For an N-wheeled robot, the minimum number of wheels for guaranteed static stability on flat ground is **N = 3** (a triangle cannot be made non-convex). However, **N = 4** is more common in practice to handle uneven terrain where one wheel may momentarily lose contact.

### 2.1.2 Maneuverability (Pillar 2)

Maneuverability is quantified by the **degrees of freedom (DOF)** of the robot in its environment. For ground robots operating on a 2D plane, the maximum DOF is **3**: two translational (forward/backward, left/right) and one rotational (about the vertical axis).

Maneuverability is decomposed into:

$$
M = \delta_m + \delta_s
$$

where:
- $M$ = total maneuverability (maximum = 3 for planar motion)
- $\delta_m$ = degrees of mobility (number of independently achievable motions of the chassis)
- $\delta_s$ = degrees of steerability (number of wheels whose steering angle is independently controllable)

The **maneuverbility** of a robot **must not exceed 3** (it cannot exceed the underlying DOF of the plane).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A robot with $M < 3$ has *kinematic constraints* (it cannot perform some motions, e.g., a car cannot translate sideways). A robot with $M = 3$ is called **omnidirectional**.

### 2.1.3 Controllability (Pillar 3)

Controllability refers to the **number of independent actuators** (motors) the robot has. The relationship to maneuverability is given by:

$$
\delta_m \leq \text{(number of independently driven wheels)} \leq \delta_m + \delta_s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A robot is **fully controllable** (i.e., can achieve all motions allowed by its maneuverability) if and only if the number of independent drive motors satisfies the above inequality.

### 2.1.4 Number of Wheels (Pillar 4)

The wheel count is a discrete design choice. Common configurations include:

- **1 wheel** — impossible to balance statically.
- **2 wheels** — dynamically stable (e.g., Segway-style), requires active balancing.
- **3 wheels** — minimum for static stability. Very common (differential drive + caster).
- **4 wheels** — most common in practice (cars, AGVs). Better weight distribution and redundancy.
- **6+ wheels** — used in rough terrain and space exploration (e.g., Mars rovers).

## 2.2 The Three Standard Wheel Types

| Wheel Type | Description | Traction | Steering | Side Slip | Example Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed (Conventional) Wheel** | Rolls in one direction only. Steering requires a separate steer motor. | High | Requires joint | Cannot slip sideways | Car wheels |
| **Castor Wheel** | A small wheel mounted on a vertical swivel joint (like an office chair wheel). | Moderate | Passive (swivels freely) | Cannot slip sideways | Caster on differential drive |
| **Swedish Wheel** | A small wheel with passive rollers around its circumference. Allows motion in multiple directions. | Moderate | Passive | Can slip sideways (passive) | Mecanum-wheeled robots |
| **Spherical (Ball) Wheel** | A true omnidirectional ball bearing. Extremely complex to actuate. | Complex | None (isotropic) | Isotropic | Research platforms |
| **Tracked Wheel** | Continuous track (like a tank). | Very High | Requires steering | Cannot slip sideways | Tank treads |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal / Ball Wheel:** A special form where the contact point is a sphere, theoretically allowing motion in any direction with low friction. Used in research-grade omnidirectional robots.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Meaning / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | Maneuverability Eqn. | Must satisfy $M \leq 3$ for planar |
| 2 | $M \geq \text{Number of wheels} - 1$ | Gruebler Limit | Lower bound from wheel constraints |
| 3 | $\delta_m \leq N_{d} \leq \delta_m + \delta_s$ | Controllability | $N_d$ = number of driven wheels |
| 4 | $\text{Stability} \iff \text{CoG} \in \text{Support Polygon}$ | Static Stability | Convex hull of contact points |
| 5 | $\omega = v / r$ | Wheel Kinematics | $\omega$ = angular vel, $v$ = linear vel, $r$ = wheel radius |
| 6 | $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$ | Differential Drive Center Velocity | Average of two wheel speeds |
| 7 | $\omega = (v_R - v_L) / L$ | Differential Drive Angular Vel | $L$ = wheelbase |
| 8 | $R = L/2 \cdot (v_R + v_L)/(v_R - v_L)$ | ICR Radius (differential) | $R$ = turn radius |
| 9 | $\text{SSN} = \min(\theta_{\text{tilt}})$ | Static Stability Number | $\theta$ in radians |
| 10 | $\tan(\alpha) = \mu_s$ | Friction Cone | $\mu_s$ = static friction coeff. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas above are **non-pipe** formatted (use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\vert` notation in tables) to remain KTU-printable.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Design Space framework is the **first design decision** a robotics engineer makes. It directly impacts:
- **Warehouse AGVs** → 3-wheel differential drive (low cost, simple control).
- **Autonomous Cars** → 4-wheel Ackermann steering (high speed, road-legal).
- **Mars Rovers** → 6-wheel rocker-bogie (extreme terrain adaptability).
- **Factory AMRs** → Mecanum / Swedish-wheel omnidirectional (tight spaces, holonomic motion).
- **Hospital Service Robots** → Often 2 or 4 caster-supported drive units (passive stability, high payload).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) for a Differential Drive Robot

Consider a two-wheel differential drive robot with:
- Right wheel linear velocity = $v_R$
- Left wheel linear velocity = $v_L$
- Distance between wheels (wheelbase) = $L$
- Both wheels share the same axle and have radius $r$

The angular velocities of the two wheels are:
$$
\omega_R = \frac{v_R}{r}, \qquad \omega_L = \frac{v_L}{r}
$$

**Step 1:** Assume the chassis rotates as a rigid body about an instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) located at some perpendicular distance $R$ from the wheelbase center.

**Step 2:** Each wheel moves on a circle centered at the ICR. The right wheel is at distance $R + L/2$ from ICR; the left wheel is at distance $R - L/2$ (with sign convention).

**Step 3:** The linear velocity of any point on the chassis equals the product of its distance from the ICR and the chassis angular velocity $\Omega$:
$$
v_R = \left(R + \frac{L}{2}\right) \Omega
$$

$$
v_L = \left(R - \frac{L}{2}\right) \Omega
$$

**Step 4:** Subtract the second equation from the first:
$$
v_R - v_L = L \cdot \Omega
$$

$$
\Omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}
$$

**Step 5:** Add the two equations:
$$
v_R + v_L = 2 R \cdot \Omega
$$

$$
2R = \frac{v_R + v_L}{\Omega} = \frac{v_R + v_L}{(v_R - v_L)/L}
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the turn radius $R$:
$$
R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}
$$

**Step 7:** Verify limiting cases:
- If $v_R = v_L$ → denominator = 0 → $R = \infty$ → straight-line motion ✓
- If $v_R = -v_L$ → $R = 0$ → pure rotation in place ✓

$$
\boxed{R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}, \qquad \Omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gruebler's Equation for Planar Mobile Robots

For a planar mechanism (mobile robot on flat ground), Gruebler's formula gives the **degrees of freedom (mobility)**:

$$
M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

where:
- $n$ = number of links (bodies) including ground
- $j_1$ = number of lower-pair joints (1 DOF each: revolute, prismatic)
- $j_2$ = number of higher-pair joints (2 DOF each: e.g., wheel-ground contact)

**Example: Differential Drive Robot (2 drive wheels + 1 caster)**

- Links: chassis, 2 drive wheels, 1 caster wheel, **ground = 5** ($n = 5$)
- Wheel-ground contacts: 3 higher pairs ($j_2 = 3$)
- Each drive wheel is attached to chassis by a revolute joint: 2 lower pairs ($j_1 = 2$)
- Caster has 1 swivel revolute + 1 spin revolute = 2 lower pairs ($j_1$ adds 2)

Total: $j_1 = 4$, $j_2 = 3$, $n = 5$.

$$
M = 3(5-1) - 2(4) - 1(3) = 12 - 8 - 3 = 1
$$

**Result: $M = 1$** → The robot has 1 degree of mobility: it can only move along an arc (its single instantaneous DOF is either straight or turning, but it cannot, for example, translate sideways).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maneuverability Decomposition

For a robot with $N$ wheels of standard type (no special Swedish/spherical wheels), the maneuverability in the plane is bounded by:

$$
M \leq 3
$$

But the design constraint from wheels further restricts:
$$
M \geq N - 1 \quad \text{(for a robot with N standard wheels)}
$$

A robot achieves **maximum maneuverability $M = 3$** (full omnidirectionality) **only if** it has Swedish or spherical wheels, because standard wheels cannot passively translate sideways.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard wheels constrain the chassis** because the no-slip condition forces the velocity at the contact point to be tangent to the wheel. This removes 1 DOF per standard wheel from the chassis motion.

For $N$ standard wheels:
$$
\text{Lost DOF} = N \quad (\text{one per wheel})
$$

$$
\text{Remaining DOF} = 3 - N + (\text{extra from steering}) = 3 - N + \delta_s
$$

But $\delta_s$ adds at most $N$ (if all wheels are steerable), so:
$$
M = 3 - N + \delta_s \quad \text{with} \quad \delta_s \leq N
$$

For an **Ackermann (car) steering** with 2 fixed rear wheels + 2 steered front wheels ($\delta_s = 1$ since the front wheels share one steering motor), with 4 wheels:

$$
M = 3 - 4 + 1 = 0 \quad \text{???}
$$

Wait — this gives 0, which is wrong. The correction: when wheels share a steering mechanism, each loses 1 DOF (no-slip) and the steering motor **adds 1 DOF per pair**:
$$
M = 3 - N + N_s + (\text{steering groups})
$$

For Ackermann: $N = 4$, but rear 2 wheels share constraints, front 2 share 1 steer → $M = 3 - 4 + 1 = 0$ is actually wrong.

**Correct application:** For a 4-wheel car, treat it as $N=3$ effective ground constraints (2 rear + 1 virtual front steered as a unit) with 1 steering DOF:
$$
M = 3 - 3 + 1 = 1
$$

The 1 mobility DOF corresponds to motion along the car's longitudinal axis (with steering adjusting direction). ✓

> [!NOTE]
> The exact Gruebler-based formula varies by linkage topology. KTU expects the **conceptual application**, not the formula for every linkage. Always state your assumed joint structure.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Robot Topologies

| Robot Type | Wheels | $\delta_m$ | $\delta_s$ | $M$ | Omnidirectional? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Differential Drive (2 wheels + 1 caster)** | 2 drive + 1 caster | 2 | 0 | 2 | No | Spin in place, no sideways |
| **Differential Drive (no caster, 2 wheels)** | 2 | 1 | 0 | 1 | No | Dynamic balance required |
| **Tricycle (1 steer + 2 fixed rear)** | 3 | 2 | 1 | 3? | No (kinematic chain) | Car-like |
| **Ackermann Car (2+2)** | 4 | 1 | 1 (front pair) | 2 | No | Real car |
| **Synchro Drive (3 Swedish)** | 3 | 2 | 2 | 4 (limited to 3) | Yes (limited) | All wheels steer and drive together |
| **Mecanum (4 Swedish)** | 4 | 3 | 0 | 3 | **Yes** | Full holonomic |
| **Omnidirectional (3 Swedish, 120°)** | 3 | 3 | 0 | 3 | **Yes** | Full holonomic, $M = 3$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Design Space Decision Framework (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Robot Mission Brief] --> B{Operating Terrain?}
    B -->|Smooth Indoor| C[Smooth Flat Ground]
    B -->|Structured Outdoor| D[Road / Pavement]
    B -->|Rough / Unstructured| E[Rubble / Sand / Stairs]

    C --> F{Load Capacity Required?}
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F -->|Light Payload < 5 kg| G[2 or 3 Wheels]
    F -->|Medium 5-50 kg| H[3 or 4 Wheels]
    F -->|Heavy > 50 kg| I[4 or 6 Wheels]

    G --> J{Wheel Type Selection}
    H --> J
    I --> J

    J -->|High Speed / Roads| K[Fixed Wheels + Steering]
    J -->|Tight Indoor Spaces| L[Swedish / Mecanum Wheels]
    J -->|Rough Terrain| M[Tracked or Rocker-Bogie]

    K --> N[Stability Check: Static OK?]
    L --> N
    M --> N

    N -->|Yes| O[Actuation and Control Design]
    N -->|No| P[Add Wheels or Lower CoG]
    P --> J
```

## 4.2 Wheel Type Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[All Wheel Types] --> B[Standard Wheels]
    A --> C[Special Wheels]

    B --> B1[Fixed Wheel]
    B --> B2[Steerable Wheel]
    B --> B3[Castor Wheel]

    C --> C1[Swedish Wheel]
    C --> C2[Spherical Ball Wheel]
    C --> C3[Tracked Wheel]

    B1 --> R1[No sideways slip allowed]
    B2 --> R2[Steering adds DOF]
    B3 --> R3[Passive swivel, low friction]

    C1 --> R4[Allows passive lateral slip]
    C2 --> R5[Truly omnidirectional contact]
    C3 --> R6[Maximum terrain contact area]
```

## 4.3 Differential Drive Kinematic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Motor Input vR vL] --> B[Wheel Angular Velocity]
    B --> C[Chassis Linear Velocity v]
    B --> D[Chassis Angular Velocity Omega]
    C --> E[Position Update x y]
    D --> F[Heading Update theta]
    E --> G[Robot Pose x y theta]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Feedback to Controller]
    H --> A
```

## 4.4 Maneuverability vs. Wheel Count Visual Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1[1 Wheel - Impossible Static] --> N2[2 Wheels - Active Balance Needed]
    N2 --> N3[3 Wheels - Minimum Static Stability]
    N3 --> N4[4 Wheels - Standard Industrial Choice]
    N4 --> N5[6 Wheels - Rough Terrain Mars Rover]

    N1 --> M1[Maneuverability 0 or 1]
    N2 --> M2[Maneuverability up to 2]
    N3 --> M3[Maneuverability 1 to 3 depending on wheel choice]
    N4 --> M4[Maneuverability 1 to 3 with special wheels]
    N5 --> M5[Maneuverability 1 to 3 with suspension]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These Mermaid block diagrams map the *decision flow* of the Design Space. They abstract the physical design choice into a tractable engineering flowchart, exactly as KTU board questions expect students to discuss when asked "explain the design space."

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Definition Question `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the **Design Space** of a wheeled mobile robot. List the four main issues that define the design space.

**Model Answer:**

The **Design Space** of a wheeled mobile robot is the multi-dimensional parameter space defined by the choice of wheel geometry, wheel count, wheel arrangement, and actuation scheme, constrained by the requirements of stability, maneuverability, and controllability on the target terrain.

The four main issues are:
1. **Stability** — the ability of the robot to remain upright and operational without tipping.
2. **Maneuverability** — the degrees of freedom available to the robot in its operating environment.
3. **Controllability** — the number of independent actuators (motors) that can be commanded.
4. **Number and type of wheels** — the discrete design parameters that fundamentally constrain the other three issues.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for listing the four issues.)*

### Question A2: Concept Question `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Differentiate between **static stability** and **dynamic stability** of a wheeled mobile robot. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

- **Static Stability:** The robot remains upright and balanced even when stationary, with no active control required. Stability is determined purely by geometry: the vertical projection of the center of gravity (CoG) must lie inside the convex hull of the ground contact points.
  - *Example:* A 4-wheeled car parked on a flat surface.

- **Dynamic Stability:** The robot is only stable while it is actively moving. If it stops, it tips over. Requires continuous control input to maintain balance.
  - *Example:* A two-wheeled Segway-style balancing robot, or a bicycle at low speed.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for each definition, 0.5 for each example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

> **[KTU Board Pattern]** Each Part B question has two sub-parts worth 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **three standard wheel types** used in wheeled mobile robots. For each type, state one advantage and one limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **two-wheel differential drive robot** has a wheelbase $L = 0.5$ m, wheel radius $r = 0.1$ m, and the right wheel rotates at $\omega_R = 10$ rad/s, the left wheel at $\omega_L = 6$ rad/s. Calculate: (i) the linear velocity of each wheel, (ii) the chassis angular velocity $\Omega$, and (iii) the radius of curvature $R$ of the path. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Three Standard Wheel Types (7 marks)**

1. **Fixed (Conventional) Wheel** *(2 marks)*
   - **Description:** A wheel that rotates about a fixed axle attached to the chassis. It can roll forward and backward but cannot change direction without external steering.
   - **Advantage:** Simple, robust, high traction on the rolling direction.
   - **Limitation:** Cannot change direction passively; requires a separate steering mechanism.

2. **Castor Wheel** *(2 marks)*
   - **Description:** A wheel mounted on a vertical swivel joint, allowing the wheel to passively rotate to align with the direction of motion. Examples include office-chair wheels and shopping-cart wheels.
   - **Advantage:** Provides passive stability and a third contact point without complex steering.
   - **Limitation:** Introduces uncertainty in the contact angle; can wobble on high-speed turns.

3. **Swedish (Mecanum) Wheel** *(2 marks)*
   - **Description:** A conventional wheel with passive rollers mounted around its circumference at 45° (Mecanum) or 90° (Swedish). The passive rollers allow the wheel to translate sideways as well as roll forward.
   - **Advantage:** Enables omnidirectional (holonomic) motion of the chassis.
   - **Limitation:** Low traction and reduced load capacity; passive rollers wear out quickly. *(1 mark for concluding trade-off summary)*

**(b) Differential Drive Kinematics Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $L = 0.5$ m, $r = 0.1$ m, $\omega_R = 10$ rad/s, $\omega_L = 6$ rad/s.

**(i) Linear Velocities of Wheels (2 marks)**

$$
v_R = \omega_R \cdot r = 10 \cdot 0.1 = 1.0 \ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
v_L = \omega_L \cdot r = 6 \cdot 0.1 = 0.6 \ \text{m/s}
$$

**(ii) Chassis Angular Velocity $\Omega$ (2 marks)**

$$
\Omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{1.0 - 0.6}{0.5} = \frac{0.4}{0.5} = 0.8 \ \text{rad/s}
$$

**(iii) Radius of Curvature $R$ (3 marks)**

$$
R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L} = \frac{0.5}{2} \cdot \frac{1.0 + 0.6}{1.0 - 0.6}
$$

$$
R = 0.25 \cdot \frac{1.6}{0.4} = 0.25 \cdot 4 = 1.0 \ \text{m}
$$

**Final Answer:** $v_R = 1.0$ m/s, $v_L = 0.6$ m/s, $\Omega = 0.8$ rad/s, $R = 1.0$ m. The robot is turning to the left with a 1-meter radius.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the **four fundamental issues of the Design Space** for wheeled mobile robots. Mention how each issue is quantified. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **tricycle-style mobile robot** has two fixed rear wheels (axle distance $L = 0.6$ m) and one front steerable wheel. The front wheel is steered at an angle $\alpha = 30°$ from the longitudinal axis, and the robot moves at forward speed $v = 1.2$ m/s. Compute the **turning radius** $R$ of the robot's ICR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) The Four Issues of the Design Space (7 marks)**

1. **Stability** *(1.5 marks)*
   - Quantified by the **Static Stability Number (SSN)** = minimum tilt angle before tipping.
   - Determined by the **Center of Gravity (CoG)** projection on the support polygon.

2. **Maneuverability ($M$)** *(2 marks)*
   - $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ where $\delta_m$ = degrees of mobility, $\delta_s$ = degrees of steerability.
   - Must satisfy $M \leq 3$ for planar ground motion.
   - $M = 3$ → omnidirectional; $M < 3$ → non-holonomic constraints exist.

3. **Controllability** *(1.5 marks)*
   - Quantified by the number of **independent actuators** (driven wheels + steer motors).
   - Condition: $\delta_m \leq N_d \leq \delta_m + \delta_s$ where $N_d$ = number of driven wheels.

4. **Number and Type of Wheels** *(2 marks)*
   - The discrete design choice. Trade-off between stability (more wheels), simplicity (fewer wheels), and maneuverability (special wheels like Swedish/Mecanum).
   - **3 wheels** = minimum for static stability; **4 wheels** = most common industrial choice.

**(b) Tricycle Turning Radius (7 marks)**

For a tricycle with one steered front wheel, the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) lies on the line extending from the rear axle and on the line perpendicular to the steered front wheel at the contact point.

The geometry gives:

$$
R = \frac{L}{\tan(\alpha)}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute values $L = 0.6$ m, $\alpha = 30°$:

$$
\tan(30°) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.5774
$$

**Step 2:**

$$
R = \frac{0.6}{0.5774} = 1.039 \ \text{m} \approx 1.04 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 (Bonus):** Chassis angular velocity if forward speed is $v = 1.2$ m/s:

$$
\Omega = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{1.2}{1.04} \approx 1.154 \ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Final Answer:** $R \approx 1.04$ m, $\Omega \approx 1.15$ rad/s.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in (b) above:** Many students write $R = 1.04$ without units. **Always include units** to avoid losing 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Confusing the tricycle vs. Ackermann formula:** The tricycle formula is $R = L / \tan(\alpha)$, NOT the Ackermann $R = L / \sin(\alpha)$. Ackermann is for two-wheel steering. **Memorize both.**
> 3. **Sign convention for differential drive:** If $v_R > v_L$, the robot turns **left** (CCW from top view), not right. Always state direction.
> 4. **In Gruebler's equation:** Forgetting to count the **ground** as a link. The ground is always link #1 in mobile robot kinematics. Losing this loses 1 full mark.
> 5. **In maneuverability decomposition:** Writing $M = \delta_m \cdot \delta_s$ (multiplication) instead of $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ (addition). This is a very common slip.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Design Space** = the systematic map of all wheel/actuator choices, bounded by **Stability, Maneuverability, Controllability, and Wheel Count**.
- **Maneuverability Equation:** $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$, with $M \leq 3$ in the plane.
- **Controllability Condition:** $\delta_m \leq N_d \leq \delta_m + \delta_s$.
- **Minimum wheels for static stability = 3**; **omnidirectionality = need Swedish/Spherical wheels**.
- **Differential Drive ICR formulas (HIGH-YIELD):**
  - $v = (v_R + v_L) / 2$
  - $\Omega = (v_R - v_L) / L$
  - $R = (L/2) \cdot (v_R + v_L) / (v_R - v_L)$
- **Tricycle / Ackermann turning radius:** $R = L / \tan(\alpha)$ for tricycle; $R = L / \sin(\alpha)$ for car (Ackermann with small-angle approximation).
- **Gruebler's planar formula:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ — always count the ground as a link.
- **Static stability test:** CoG projection must fall inside the convex hull of wheel contact points.
- **Standard wheels (fixed, steerable, castor) cannot passively translate sideways** — this is why cars and differential drives are non-holonomic.
- **Swedish / Mecanum / Spherical wheels** enable omnidirectional motion by allowing passive lateral slip.
- **Real-world mapping:** Mars rovers (6-wheel rocker-bogie), warehouse AGVs (3-wheel differential), omnidirectional factory AMRs (4 Mecanum).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheeled Locomotion: Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT721 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wheeled Locomotion: Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Wheeled Locomotion in Mobile Robotics?

**Wheeled Locomotion** refers to the methodology by which an autonomous or semi-autonomous mobile robot moves across a surface using wheels as the primary contact and propulsion mechanism. In the context of KTU's **Basics of Mobile Robotics (OERUT721)**, **Case Studies** refer to the systematic analysis of standardized wheel configurations (and the real-world robots that use them) to evaluate their **kinematics**, **maneuverability**, **controllability**, and **stability** under practical operating conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> Wheeled locomotion case studies involve the analytical and graphical evaluation of a robot's drive configuration (number, type, and arrangement of wheels) to determine its Degree of Mobility (DOM), Degree of Steerability (DOS), and Degree of Maneuverability, based on the **Grashof-Type Classification** extended by Campion and Bastin.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy: "The Family Garage"

Imagine walking into a garage containing five different vehicles:

1. **A shopping trolley** (two fixed rear wheels + two freely swiveling front wheels) → this is essentially a **Differential Drive** robot. You push it by spinning the rear wheels at different rates.
2. **A Maruti car** (two rear fixed wheels + two front wheels tied by a steering rod) → this is an **Ackermann Steered** car-like robot.
3. **A forklift** (two fixed drive wheels + one rear steered wheel) → this is a **Tricycle Drive**.
4. **A ball-bearing chair** (wheels that can roll AND spin about the vertical axis simultaneously) → this is a **Swedish/Synchro Drive**.
5. **A mecanum-wheeled AGV in a warehouse** (special wheels with rollers at 45°) → this is an **Omnidirectional Drive**.

Each vehicle is optimized for a *specific* environment. By studying their *cases*, roboticists learn *why* a particular arrangement is chosen for a particular job (indoor mapping, warehouse logistics, road navigation, etc.).

## 1.3 Why Do Case Studies Matter in Module 1?

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):**
> Wheeled Locomotion is one of the most heavily weighted topics because it directly bridges **mechanical design**, **kinematic modeling**, and **control theory**. Case studies give students a structured framework (Campion's Classification) to analyze *any* real robot they encounter in their career.

**Key Metrics Evaluated in Every Case Study:**
- **Degree of Mobility (δ_m):** Number of independently controllable degrees of freedom of the robot's chassis.
- **Degree of Steerability (δ_s):** Number of wheels that can be steered independently about a vertical axis.
- **Degree of Maneuverability (δ_M = δ_m + δ_s):** Total kinematic capability.
- **Wheel Types:** *Standard (fixed), Castored, Swedish, Spherical, Omnidirectional (Mecanum/Universal).*
- **Stability:** *Static* (gravity polygon vs. center of mass) vs. *Dynamic* (inverted pendulum behavior).

## 1.4 Canonical Case Study Robots You MUST Recognize

| Robot | Configuration | Real-World Application |
|-------|---------------|------------------------|
| **Pioneer P3-DX / P3-AT** | 2-Wheel Differential Drive + 1 Caster | Indoor research, SLAM |
| **TurtleBot (Burger/Waffle)** | 2-Wheel Differential Drive + 2 Casters | ROS education, research |
| **Khepera II / IV** | 2-Wheel Differential Drive | Miniature lab research |
| **iRobot Roomba** | 2-Wheel Differential Drive + Caster | Domestic floor cleaning |
| **Waymo / Apollo (Car-Like)** | Ackermann Steered (1-DOF) | Autonomous road vehicles |
| **Fetch Freight** | Differential Drive Base | Warehouse logistics |
| **KUKA OmniMove** | Mecanum 4-Wheel | Flexible factory transport |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wheel Types — The Building Blocks

Every wheeled mobile robot (WMR) is constructed from a combination of these five primitive wheel types. Understanding each is critical before analyzing case studies.

### A. Standard Fixed Wheel
- **Movement Constraints:** 1 — rotation about a horizontal axis perpendicular to the wheel plane.
- **Cannot** change its rolling direction relative to the chassis.
- **Example use:** Rear wheels of a car, drive wheels of Pioneer robot.

### B. Standard Steered Wheel (Centered)
- **Movement Constraints:** 2 — rolling + steering about a vertical axis.
- The steering axis passes through the **center of the wheel**.

### C. Off-Centered Steered Wheel (Caster Wheel)
- **Movement Constraints:** 2 — rolling + steering.
- The steering axis is **offset** from the contact point (like an office chair wheel).
- Provides passive stability; **cannot** be actively steered for control.

### D. Swedish (Omnidirectional) Wheel
- **Movement Constraints:** 2 — rolling + passive rotation of small peripheral rollers.
- **Unique Property:** Rolls forward/backward along the motor axis BUT can also slide laterally due to the passive perpendicular rollers.
- Used in **Synchro Drive** and **Swedish 90° / 45°** configurations.

### E. Spherical Wheel
- **Movement Constraints:** 0 passive / 3 active (if motorized).
- Universal motion; theoretically perfect omnidirectional. **Rare in practice** (mechanical complexity).

## 2.2 The Five Canonical Case Study Configurations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** Be ready to draw and classify all five. The following table is the **single most important** thing in this topic.

| # | Configuration | Wheels Used | DOM (δ_m) | DOS (δ_s) | DOF (δ_M) | Maneuverability Class |
|---|---------------|-------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Differential Drive** | 2 fixed + 1 caster | 2 | 0 | 2 | Type (2,0) |
| 2 | **Tricycle Drive** | 1 fixed + 2 steered | 1 | 1 | 2 | Type (1,1) |
| 3 | **Car-Like (Ackermann)** | 2 fixed + 2 steered (linked) | 1 | 1 | 2 | Type (1,1) |
| 4 | **Synchro Drive** | 3 (or 4) Swedish wheels (all steered together, all driven together) | 2 | 1 | 3 | Type (2,1) |
| 5 | **Omnidirectional (Mecanum)** | 4 mecanum wheels (no steering) | 3 | 0 | 3 | Type (3,0) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal constraints to remember.** The `|...|` notation is written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table safety.

| Formula | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---------|------------|----------------|
| **Degree of Maneuverability** | $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | Total independent controllable motions of the chassis. |
| **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** | $\dot{x}\sin\phi - \dot{y}\cos\phi = 0$ | For a non-holonomic wheel, velocity must be perpendicular to the wheel plane. |
| **Non-Holonomic Constraint (car-like)** | $\dot{x}\sin\phi - \dot{y}\cos\phi = 0$ | The chassis cannot move sideways. |
| **Pure Rolling Constraint (fixed wheel)** | $\dot{x}_c \cos(\alpha+\beta) + \dot{y}_c \sin(\alpha+\beta) + L\dot{\phi}\sin\beta = r\dot{\theta}$ | Relates wheel angular velocity to chassis velocity. |
| **Ackermann Geometry** | $\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = \frac{w}{L}$ | Inner and outer steering angles in a car-like robot. |
| **Differential Drive Forward Kinematics** | $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L), \quad \omega = \frac{r}{2R}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | Linear and angular velocities from wheel speeds. |
| **Differential Drive Inverse Kinematics** | $\omega_R = \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r}, \quad \omega_L = \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r}$ | Wheel speeds to achieve desired (v, ω). |
| **Radius of Curvature (Differential)** | $R = \frac{L}{2}\left(\frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}\right)$ | R → ∞ for straight line; R = L/2 for pure rotation. |
| **Slip Condition (ICR distance)** | $d_{ICR} = \frac{v}{\omega}$ | A wheel at the ICR has zero rolling velocity. |
| **Static Stability (n wheels)** | Polygon of support formed by contact points must contain CoM | WMR is statically stable if CoG lies within support polygon. |
| **Grashof's Inequality (generalized)** | For a 4-bar linkage: S + L ≤ P + Q | Determines continuous rotation capability (rarely needed in 4-WMR but appears in suspensions). |

*Legend: r = wheel radius, L = wheelbase (distance between wheels), 2R = axle length, φ = heading angle, α = wheel angle relative to chassis, β = steering angle.*

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Wheel Choice Matters |
|--------------------|--------------------------|
| **Indoor Service Robots (hotels, hospitals)** | Differential drive chosen for zero turning radius — essential in narrow corridors. |
| **Autonomous Cars (Waymo, Tesla)** | Ackermann chosen because it matches human driving geometry, prevents tire slip at high speed. |
| **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva)** | Differential drive base for omnidirectional maneuvering between shelves. |
| **Heavy Industrial Transport (KUKA, OTTO 1000)** | Omnidirectional mecanum for precise docking in tight factory layouts. |
| **Planetary Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance)** | Rocker-bogie (6-wheel) for **passive suspension** on uneven terrain — a hybrid case study. |
| **Cleaning Robots (Roomba)** | Differential drive for simple, low-cost, reliable floor coverage. |

## 2.5 Campion's Classification Theorem (Theoretical Foundation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Theory Question (10-Mark Favorite):**
> A robot's *instantaneous configuration space* has **3 degrees of freedom** in the plane (x, y, φ). The number of constraints imposed by the wheel arrangement determines the maneuverability class.

- If **δ_m = 3, δ_s = 0** → **Type (3,0)** Omnidirectional.
- If **δ_m = 2, δ_s = 0** → **Type (2,0)** Differential drive.
- If **δ_m = 1, δ_s = 1** → **Type (1,1)** Tricycle / Car-like.
- If **δ_m = 2, δ_s = 1** → **Type (2,1)** Synchro drive.
- If **δ_m = 1, δ_s = 2** → **Type (1,2)** Crab-style.

The general **instantaneous mobility** of a WMR is given by:

$$
M = 3(N_s + N_f + N_o) - 2N_{ic} - N_{cast}
$$

Where $N_s$ = steered wheels, $N_f$ = fixed wheels, $N_o$ = omnidirectional wheels, $N_{ic}$ = independently controlled wheels, $N_{cast}$ = caster wheels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Differential Drive Forward Kinematics (The Pivotal Case Study)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most derivable question** in any KTU Mobile Robotics paper. Memorize it step-by-step.

**Setup:** Two identical wheels of radius **r**, mounted on an axle of length **2R** (so each wheel is at distance **R** from the center). Wheel angular velocities: $\omega_L$ (left) and $\omega_R$ (right). Let **v** be the linear velocity of the chassis center, and **ω** be the angular velocity of the chassis.

**Step 1:** Velocity of the right wheel contact point (relative to ground) is
$$
v_R = r \cdot \omega_R
$$
**Step 2:** Velocity of the left wheel contact point is
$$
v_L = r \cdot \omega_L
$$
**Step 3:** The center of the robot moves with the **average** of the two wheel velocities (because the robot is rigid):
$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)
$$
**Step 4:** The angular velocity **ω** of the robot comes from the **difference** of wheel velocities divided by the axle length 2R:
$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{2R} = \frac{r}{2R}(\omega_R - \omega_L)
$$
**Step 5:** Verify boundary conditions:
- If $\omega_R = \omega_L = \omega_0$ → $\omega = 0$ and $v = r\omega_0$ → **Straight line motion** ✓
- If $\omega_R = -\omega_L$ → $v = 0$ and $\omega = \frac{r\omega_0}{R}$ → **Pure rotation about the center** ✓
- The **turning radius** is therefore:

$$
\rho = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{R(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{\omega_R - \omega_L}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ackermann Steering Geometry

**Setup:** A car-like robot with wheelbase **L** (distance between front and rear axles) and track width **w** (distance between left and right wheels on the same axle). To avoid tire slip during a turn, **all four wheels must rotate about the same Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**.

**Step 1:** Place the ICR at a point on the line extending the rear axle. Let its lateral distance from the robot be **R**.
**Step 2:** For the **inner front wheel**, the steering angle $\delta_i$ satisfies
$$
\tan\delta_i = \frac{L}{R - w/2}
$$
**Step 3:** For the **outer front wheel**, the steering angle $\delta_o$ satisfies
$$
\tan\delta_o = \frac{L}{R + w/2}
$$
**Step 4:** Eliminate **R** by writing it from the first equation:
$$
R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta_i} + \frac{w}{2}
$$
**Step 5:** Substitute into the second equation:
$$
\tan\delta_o = \frac{L}{\frac{L}{\tan\delta_i} + \frac{w}{2} + \frac{w}{2}} = \frac{L \tan\delta_i}{L + w\tan\delta_i}
$$
**Step 6:** Take the reciprocal and simplify. The **classic Ackermann equation** is:

$$
\boxed{\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = \frac{w}{L}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** A perfect Ackermann linkage ensures every wheel's perpendicular intersects at the **single ICR**, eliminating scrub/slip during cornering. Real cars approximate this with the trapezoidal steering linkage.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Non-Holonomic Constraint of a Fixed Wheel

**Setup:** A single fixed wheel with contact point **P** on the robot chassis. The wheel plane makes an angle **α** with the chassis x-axis. The wheel radius is **r**, and the point **P** is at coordinates $(x_p, y_p)$ in the world frame.

**Step 1:** Because the wheel cannot slide **sideways**, the velocity of point P must be **perpendicular** to the wheel plane.
**Step 2:** The unit vector **along** the wheel plane in the world frame is $(\cos(\phi + \alpha), \sin(\phi + \alpha))$, where φ is the chassis heading.
**Step 3:** The velocity of point P in the world frame is:
$$
\vec{v}_P = (\dot{x} - \dot{\phi}y_p,\; \dot{y} + \dot{\phi}x_p)
$$
**Step 4:** The non-holonomic constraint is the dot product of $\vec{v}_P$ with the **direction of the wheel plane** equals zero:
$$
(\dot{x} - \dot{\phi}y_p)\cos(\phi+\alpha) + (\dot{y} + \dot{\phi}x_p)\sin(\phi+\alpha) = 0
$$
**Step 5:** For a centered fixed wheel at the origin (the simplest case), $x_p = y_p = 0$, giving the **pure rolling constraint**:
$$
\dot{x}\cos(\phi+\alpha) + \dot{y}\sin(\phi+\alpha) = r\dot{\theta}
$$
This is the foundation of the **Pfaffian constraint form** used to derive the kinematic model of any WMR.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Instantaneous Maneuverability of an Omnidirectional (Mecanum) Robot

**Setup:** A square robot with four mecanum wheels at corners. Each wheel has rollers oriented at ±45° to the wheel plane.

**Step 1:** For a single mecanum wheel, the velocity vector at the contact point can be decomposed into:
- **Active rolling** along the wheel axis.
- **Passive sliding** perpendicular to the wheel axis (because of the rollers).

**Step 2:** The **forward velocity** in the x-direction of the chassis is contributed equally by all four wheels:
$$
v_x = \frac{r}{4}(\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 + \omega_4)
$$
**Step 3:** The **lateral velocity** in the y-direction is contributed by the differential effect of rollers (this is what gives omnidirectionality):
$$
v_y = \frac{r}{4}(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 - \omega_4)
$$
**Step 4:** The **angular velocity** comes from the differential effect between diagonals:
$$
\omega = \frac{r}{4L + 4W}(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 - \omega_3 + \omega_4)
$$
**Step 5:** Combining, the chassis has **3 independent DOF** $(v_x, v_y, \omega)$ in the plane → **Type (3,0)** omnidirectional. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This derivation is what makes mecanum wheels "omnidirectional" despite the wheels themselves never steering. The "magic" comes from the geometric arrangement of the passive rollers.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Question:** A differential drive mobile robot has wheel radius r = 0.05 m and axle length 2R = 0.4 m. The left wheel rotates at 10 rad/s and the right wheel at 12 rad/s. Calculate the linear velocity, angular velocity, and radius of curvature of the robot.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Linear Velocity** (using $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$):
$$
v = \frac{0.05}{2}(12 + 10) = 0.025 \times 22 = 0.55 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Angular Velocity** (using $\omega = \frac{r}{2R}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$):
$$
\omega = \frac{0.05}{0.4}(12 - 10) = 0.125 \times 2 = 0.25 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Radius of Curvature** (using $\rho = v / \omega$):
$$
\rho = \frac{0.55}{0.25} = 2.2 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Direction:** Since $\omega_R > \omega_L$, the robot turns **left**, so the ICR lies **2.2 m to the left** of the robot center. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Wheeled Locomotion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["Wheeled Mobile Robots"] --> classA["By Wheel Geometry"]
    rootA --> classB["By Case Study Use Case"]
    rootA --> classC["By Maneuverability Class"]

    classA --> wheel1["Fixed Standard Wheel"]
    classA --> wheel2["Steered Standard Wheel"]
    classA --> wheel3["Caster Wheel Passive"]
    classA --> wheel4["Swedish Wheel 45 or 90 degree"]
    classA --> wheel5["Mecanum Omnidirectional Wheel"]
    classA --> wheel6["Spherical Wheel"]

    classB --> usecase1["Differential Drive Pioneer Roomba TurtleBot"]
    classB --> usecase2["Ackermann Steered Autonomous Cars Waymo"]
    classB --> usecase3["Synchro Drive Lab Research IRobot ATRV"]
    classB --> usecase4["Mecanum AGV KUKA OmniMove"]
    classB --> usecase5["Tricycle Drive Forklifts Outdoor Inspection"]

    classC --> classType1["Type 3,0 Omnidirectional"]
    classC --> classType2["Type 2,1 Synchro Drive"]
    classC --> classType3["Type 2,0 Differential Drive"]
    classC --> classType4["Type 1,1 Car-Like and Tricycle"]
    classC --> classType5["Type 1,2 Crab Steering"]

    wheel5 --> classType1
    wheel4 --> classType2
    wheel1 --> classType3
    wheel2 --> classType4
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Wheeled Locomotion System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["High Level Planner Path Goal Pose"] --> inputB["Trajectory Generator Velocity Commands v and omega"]

    inputB --> ctrlA["Wheel Velocity Controller Inverse Kinematics Module"]

    ctrlA --> motorA["Left Motor Driver PWM Signal"]
    ctrlA --> motorB["Right Motor Driver PWM Signal"]

    motorA --> wheel1["Left Wheel Encoder RPM Feedback"]
    motorB --> wheel2["Right Wheel Encoder RPM Feedback"]

    wheel1 --> odometryA["Forward Kinematics Module"]
    wheel2 --> odometryA

    odometryA --> odomOut["Estimated Pose x y phi"]
    odomOut --> inputA

    ctrlA -.-> safetyA["IMU and Wheel Slip Monitor"]
    safetyA -.-> inputB

    inputA --> canBus["CAN Bus or Serial Communication"]
    canBus --> monitoringA["Remote Operator UI Mapping Interface"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Matrix: Differential vs. Ackermann vs. Omnidirectional

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStart["Choose a Case Study Configuration"] --> qOne["Indoor and Low Speed?"]
    qOne -- YES --> diffDrive["Differential Drive Type 2,0"]
    diffDrive --> diffProp["Properties: Zero turning radius, two drive wheels, simple control, cannot strafe laterally"]

    qOne -- NO --> qTwo["Outdoor and High Speed on Roads?"]
    qTwo -- YES --> ackermann["Ackermann Steered Type 1,1"]
    ackermann --> ackProp["Properties: Car-like geometry, no lateral slip, requires steering linkage, larger turning radius"]

    qTwo -- NO --> qThree["Need Holonomic Motion in Tight Spaces?"]
    qThree -- YES --> omni["Omnidirectional Mecanum Type 3,0"]
    omni --> omniProp["Properties: Can translate in any direction and rotate, four mecanum wheels, expensive and roller wear"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map: Maneuverability Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dofA["Degree of Mobility delta_m"] --> comb["Degree of Maneuverability delta_M"]
    dofB["Degree of Steerability delta_s"] --> comb
    comb --> ruleA["Type 3,0: delta_m is 3 delta_s is 0"]
    comb --> ruleB["Type 2,1: delta_m is 2 delta_s is 1"]
    comb --> ruleC["Type 2,0: delta_m is 2 delta_s is 0"]
    comb --> ruleD["Type 1,1: delta_m is 1 delta_s is 1"]
    comb --> ruleE["Type 1,2: delta_m is 1 delta_s is 2"]
    comb --> noteA["Non holonomic if delta_M is less than 3"]
    comb --> noteB["Holonomic if delta_M is equal to 3"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no physical 2D drawings?** Mermaid cannot render true isometric or perspective views of robots. The functional block diagrams and decision-flow trees above serve the same pedagogical purpose for a KTU module-level exam — they show **how** the configuration choice drives the kinematic behavior.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]
**"Differentiate between Degree of Mobility and Degree of Steerability of a wheeled mobile robot. Give an example for each type."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — approx. 80-100 words):**

- **Degree of Mobility (δ_m):** The number of degrees of freedom of the robot's chassis that can be **directly controlled** by changing the wheel rotation rates. Example: A differential drive robot has δ_m = 2 because it can vary its linear velocity **v** and angular velocity **ω** independently via the two wheel speeds.
- **Degree of Steerability (δ_s):** The number of wheels that can be **steered about a vertical axis** independently to alter the robot's ICR location. Example: A car-like robot has δ_s = 1 because the front wheels (linked together) provide only one independent steering input.
- Together they give the **Degree of Maneuverability**: $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$. (Full mark allocation: 1 mark each for definition + 1 mark for example pair.)

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**"State the conditions under which a wheeled mobile robot becomes statically unstable. How is this condition avoided in a 3-wheeled differential drive robot?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A robot is **statically unstable** when its **Center of Gravity (CoG)** projects outside the **support polygon** formed by the contact points of its wheels. In such a case, gravity generates a non-zero moment that topples the robot.

This is **avoided in a 3-wheeled differential drive** (2 drive wheels + 1 caster) by:
1. Placing the **castor wheel at the rear**, ensuring the CoG is always enclosed within the **triangle** formed by the three contact points.
2. Mounting heavy components (battery, motors) low on the chassis, lowering the CoG.
3. Using a **wide wheelbase** to expand the support polygon.

(1 mark for condition, 2 marks for the avoidance techniques.)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Both sub-parts are **compulsory**. Two independent questions are provided below — answer **either** the two sub-parts of **Question A** **or** the two sub-parts of **Question B**.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1]

**"With neat diagrams, explain the Differential Drive and Ackermann Steered wheel configurations as case studies. Compare their maneuverability classes, control complexity, and typical applications."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Differential Drive + Kinematics

**Step 1 — Definition and Diagram:** A differential drive robot uses **two independently driven wheels** mounted on a common axle, plus one or more **passive castor wheels** for balance. The chassis velocity is determined by the difference between the left and right wheel velocities.

**Step 2 — Forward Kinematics Equations** (write both equations explicitly):

$$
v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L), \qquad \omega = \frac{r}{2R}(\omega_R - \omega_L)
$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Kinematics** (used by the controller to set wheel speeds for a desired v, ω):

$$
\omega_R = \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r}, \qquad \omega_L = \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r}
$$
where $L = 2R$ is the axle length.

**Step 4 — Maneuverability Class:** Type **(2, 0)**: $\delta_m = 2$, $\delta_s = 0$, $\delta_M = 2$.

**Step 5 — Special Motions:**
- Pure straight: $\omega_R = \omega_L$.
- Pure rotation in place (zero turning radius): $\omega_R = -\omega_L$.
- Arc of radius $\rho$: $\rho = \frac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2(\omega_R - \omega_L)}$.

**Step 6 — Example Robot:** Pioneer P3-DX, TurtleBot 2, Roomba 600 series.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Ackermann Steered + Comparison

**Step 1 — Definition and Diagram:** A car-like robot uses **two fixed rear wheels** and **two steered front wheels** linked by a trapezoidal **Ackermann linkage** so that all wheels share a common ICR.

**Step 2 — Ackermann Equation:**

$$
\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = \frac{w}{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Non-Holonomic Constraint:** The chassis cannot move sideways:

$$
\dot{x}\sin\phi - \dot{y}\cos\phi = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Maneuverability Class:** Type **(1, 1)**: $\delta_m = 1$, $\delta_s = 1$, $\delta_M = 2$.

**Step 5 — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Differential Drive | Ackermann Steered |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Maneuverability | Type (2,0) | Type (1,1) |
| Turning radius | **0 m** (in place) | $> w/2$ (limited) |
| Lateral motion | Impossible (non-holonomic) | Impossible (non-holonomic) |
| Control complexity | Low (2 motor speeds) | Higher (steering + drive) |
| High-speed stability | Poor (wheel slip) | Excellent (no slip) |
| Typical use | Indoor, research | Roads, autonomous cars |

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Module 1]

**"Explain with derivations the Omnidirectional (Mecanum) and Synchro Drive configurations. Show that the mecanum robot is holonomic while the synchro drive is not."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Synchro Drive

**Step 1 — Configuration:** Three or four **Swedish wheels** arranged symmetrically (typically at 120° or 90°). All wheels are **steered by a common motor** (so δ_s = 1) and all are **driven by another common motor**.

**Step 2 — Kinematic Model:** Let ω_s be the common steering rate, and ω_d the common drive rate. The chassis pose update is:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\phi, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\phi, \quad \dot{\phi} = \omega_s
$$
where $v = r \cdot \omega_d$ is the rolling speed and ω_s is the steering rate.

**Step 3 — Maneuverability Class:** Type **(2, 1)**: $\delta_m = 2$ (translation in x and y), $\delta_s = 1$ (steering).

**Step 4 — Non-Holonomic Proof:** The robot's orientation **φ** is **decoupled** from translation. However, while it **can** translate along any direction (since the Swedish wheels can roll in any direction perpendicular to the rollers), the **steering** motor forces **all** wheels to turn in unison, imposing a **kinematic constraint**:

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{\dot{y}}{\dot{x}}
$$

So although the robot can be positioned at any (x, y), it cannot **simultaneously** translate in x, translate in y, **and** rotate — the **3 DOF are not independent**. Hence, **Synchro Drive is non-holonomic.**

**Step 5 — Example Robot:** Nomad XR4000, iRobot ATRV-Jr.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Omnidirectional (Mecanum)

**Step 1 — Configuration:** Four **mecanum wheels** at the corners of a rectangular chassis. Each wheel has small peripheral rollers oriented at **±45°** to the wheel plane. No steering motor is used → δ_s = 0.

**Step 2 — Velocity Equations** (derived from individual wheel velocity components):

$$
v_x = \frac{r}{4}(\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 + \omega_4)
$$

$$
v_y = \frac{r}{4}(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 - \omega_4)
$$

$$
\omega = \frac{r}{4(L + W)}(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 - \omega_3 + \omega_4)
$$

**Step 3 — Maneuverability Class:** Type **(3, 0)**: $\delta_m = 3$, $\delta_s = 0$, $\delta_M = 3$.

**Step 4 — Holonomic Proof:** From the equations, the chassis has **three independently controllable inputs** $(v_x, v_y, \omega)$ that map **one-to-one** to the four wheel speeds. There is **no kinematic constraint** coupling position and orientation. The robot can:
- Move sideways (pure $v_y$).
- Translate forward (pure $v_x$).
- Rotate in place (pure $\omega$).
- **Simultaneously** combine all three. ✓

Therefore, **Mecanum is holonomic**.

**Step 5 — Example Robot:** KUKA OmniMove, OTTO 100, Fetch Freight1000.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing δ_m with δ_s** — δ_m is about *speed control* of wheels, δ_s is about *steering angle control*. Markers deduct 1 mark for every instance.
> 2. **Writing the Ackermann equation with the wrong sides swapped** — it is $\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = w/L$, NOT $\cot\delta_i - \cot\delta_o$.
> 3. **Forgetting the "+1 caster" in differential drive diagrams** — examiners expect a 3-wheel or 4-wheel (2+2 caster) diagram; a "2-wheel" drawing is unstable and will be marked down 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Not specifying the wheel types** in the configuration (e.g., writing "four mecanum" without saying **±45°** rollers).
> 5. **Mixing up omnidirectional (mecanum) with synchro drive** — both use "special wheels" but their maneuverability classes differ (3,0) vs. (2,1).
> 6. **Skipping units** in the numerical derivation — a value like "$\omega = 0.25$" without "rad/s" loses 0.5 mark in the calculation.
> 7. **Drawing the ICR on the wrong side** of the robot in differential drive — if $\omega_R > \omega_L$, the ICR is to the **left** of the robot.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this section for your last-hour revision.**

- ✅ **Wheeled Locomotion Case Studies** = systematic analysis of *standard drive configurations* to evaluate motion, control, and stability.
- ✅ **Five canonical configurations to know cold:** Differential Drive, Tricycle, Ackermann/Car-Like, Synchro Drive, Omnidirectional (Mecanum).
- ✅ **Maneuverability formula:** $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$. Remember the table mapping each configuration to its class.
- ✅ **Type (3,0) = Holonomic (Mecanum)** — the only fully holonomic wheeled robot in standard classification.
- ✅ **Differential drive kinematics — the "magic two equations":**
  - $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$
  - $\omega = \frac{r}{2R}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$
- ✅ **Ackermann Equation:** $\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = w/L$.
- ✅ **Non-holonomic constraint of a fixed wheel:** velocity must be perpendicular to the wheel plane.
- ✅ **Static Stability** = CoG inside the support polygon (triangle for 3 wheels, rectangle/quadrilateral for 4 wheels).
- ✅ **Caster wheels** are passive stabilizers — never use them for control inputs.
- ✅ **Swedish wheels** enable omnidirectionality through passive perpendicular rollers.
- ✅ **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is at distance $d = v/\omega$ from the chassis center, on the side of the slower wheel.
- ✅ **Differential drive** turning radius ranges from $L/2$ (pure rotation) to $\infty$ (straight line).
- ✅ **Standard example robots:** Pioneer, TurtleBot, Khepera, Roomba (Differential); Waymo, Apollo (Ackermann); KUKA OmniMove (Mecanum); Nomad (Synchro).
- ✅ **Campion's mobility formula:** $M = 3(N_s + N_f + N_o) - 2N_{ic} - N_{cast}$.
- ✅ **Examiner's hot spot:** A 14-mark question almost always asks for a **forward kinematics derivation** of differential drive + a **comparison** with another configuration in tabular form.
- ✅ **Always specify units** in the numerical answer: m/s for v, rad/s for ω, m for r, R, L, w.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Legged Mobile Robots - Leg Configurations and Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT721 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Legged Mobile Robots - Leg Configurations and Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Legged Mobile Robot** is a class of mobile robot that achieves locomotion through the use of discrete, articulated appendages (legs) that alternately establish and break contact with the ground. Unlike wheeled or tracked robots, legged robots possess the inherent ability to traverse discontinuous terrain by isolating the body from ground irregularities through active joint control. The design, coordination, and stability of these robots are governed by the principles of **kinematics**, **multi-body dynamics**, and **zero-moment-point (ZMP) theory**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Under Module 1, this topic establishes the foundational vocabulary for advanced topics in gait planning, dynamic walking, and bio-inspired robotics. Questions frequently appear as 7-mark analytical problems and 14-mark derivations.

### 1.2 Leg Configurations — Classification by Leg Count

The number of legs on a robot profoundly influences its mechanical complexity, control strategy, and stability margin. The principal configurations are:

| Configuration | Leg Count | Canonical Example | Stability Class | Energy Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Uniped** | 1 | SALTO, Raibert's Hopper | Dynamic only | Moderate |
| **Biped** | 2 | ASIMO, Atlas, NAO | Marginally Static / Dynamic | High |
| **Triped** | 3 | Rare (academic only) | Static | Low |
| **Quadruped** | 4 | Spot, ANYmal, BigDog | Static (with 3 legs) | High |
| **Hexapod** | 6 | LAURON, Hexapod platforms | Static (3+ tripod) | Moderate |
| **Octopod** | 8 | Octopus-inspired robots | High Static | Low |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Goat

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine a mountain goat scaling a cliff face. The goat does not roll on wheels — it places each hoof **discretely** on individual rock outcrops. Each hoof placement is a **support point**, and the convex hull of all four hooves touching the rock is the **support polygon**. The goat's center of mass must always project (vertically downward) inside this polygon; otherwise, it tumbles. This is the essence of **static stability** for a quadruped. Now, imagine a human walking — at any instant, only one foot touches the ground, yet the human does not fall because of **dynamic balance** (angular momentum of the swinging leg counteracts gravity-induced tipping). This is **dynamic stability**, the principle that enables bipedal locomotion.

### 1.4 Core Terminology Established in KTU Module 1

- **Degree of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent coordinates required to specify the configuration of a mechanism. A 3D rigid body in space has **6 DOF** (3 translational + 3 rotational).
- **Support Polygon (SP):** The convex hull of all ground-contact points of the robot's supporting legs.
- **Center of Gravity (CG):** The point at which the entire weight of the robot may be considered to act.
- **Statically Stable Walk:** A locomotion regime where the CG projection onto the ground always lies within the support polygon.
- **Gait:** A cyclic, coordinated pattern of leg lift-off and foot-fall events.
- **Stride Length (λ):** The distance traveled by the body in one complete gait cycle.
- **Step Length:** The distance between successive foot placements of the same leg.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants & Metrics Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Standard stability margin threshold for industrial hexapods: **S_min ≥ 50 mm**
> - Default 3D body DOF for any legged platform: **6 DOF**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why "Legs"? — The Motivation

Wheeled robots suffer from a fundamental limitation: their wheels must maintain **continuous contact** with the terrain. A wheel of diameter $d$ cannot surmount an obstacle taller than $d$ without external assistance. Legs, by contrast, perform **discrete contact events**, allowing the robot to:

1. Step over obstacles higher than its wheel diameter.
2. Select optimal ground contact points (active foothold selection).
3. Isolate the body from terrain disturbances through suspension.
4. Operate efficiently on soft, deformable, or vegetated terrain.

The trade-off is **mechanical and control complexity** — each leg introduces 2 to 6 actuated joints.

### 2.2 Number of Legs and the Stability Trade-off

The selection of leg count is dictated by a fundamental equation relating stability, complexity, and energy:

- **Stability** increases with the number of legs (more contact points → larger support polygon).
- **Mechanical complexity** increases linearly with leg count (more actuators, more joints, more mass).
- **Energy efficiency** peaks at 2 to 4 legs (fewer actuators to power and control).

A formally optimal balance for **statically stable** outdoor platforms is achieved at **6 legs** (hexapod), as the classic alternating-tripod gait guarantees $\geq 3$ supporting legs at every instant.

### 2.3 Four-Legged Configurations: Mammal vs. Reptile

For quadrupeds, two biologically inspired configurations dominate:

**Mammalian Configuration (Crawl Gait):**
- Fore and hind legs alternate: (LF, RF) ↑ while (LH, RH) ↓, then vice versa.
- Mimics a horse or dog.
- Body sway is forward-backward, minimal lateral roll.

**Reptilian Configuration (Crawl/Tortoise Gait):**
- Front pair (LF, RF) and rear pair (LH, RH) move simultaneously.
- Mimics a lizard or crocodile.
- Generates pronounced body undulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:** "Differentiate between mammal and reptile configurations of a quadruped robot." The answer hinges on *which pairs of legs are synchronized* during a gait cycle.

### 2.4 Gait Stability Conditions

#### 2.4.1 Static Stability Condition

A legged robot is **statically stable** at time $t$ if and only if the vertical projection of its center of gravity, denoted $G_{xy}(t)$, lies strictly within the convex hull of its support points.

#### 2.4.2 Stability Margin (S)

The **static stability margin** is defined as the minimum perpendicular distance from the CG projection to any edge of the support polygon.

#### 2.4.3 Zero Moment Point (ZMP)

The **ZMP** is the point on the ground about which the net moment of the ground reaction force (GRF) and the inertial forces equals zero. For static stability, the ZMP must coincide with the CG projection and lie inside the support polygon.

#### 2.4.4 Leg Coordination Number

For an $n$-legged robot, the number of distinct ways to choose $k$ legs for support (with $n - k$ in transfer) is the binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{k}$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Body DOF in 3D space | $\text{DOF}_{\text{body}} = 6$ | 3 translations + 3 rotations | dimensionless |
| 2 | DOF per leg (typical) | $\text{DOF}_{\text{leg}} = 3$ | 1 hip abduction + 2 hip/knee flexion | dimensionless |
| 3 | Static stability condition | $G_{xy} \in \text{SupportPolygon}$ | CG projection must lie inside SP | m |
| 4 | Stability margin (signed) | $S = \min_{e \in \partial SP} d(G_{xy}, e)$ | Min. perpendicular distance to SP edge | m |
| 5 | ZMP x-coordinate | $x_{ZMP} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{z,i} \, x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{z,i}}$ | Where $F_{z,i}$ is the vertical GRF at foot $i$ | m |
| 6 | ZMP y-coordinate | $y_{ZMP} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{z,i} \, y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{z,i}}$ | Analogous to x-component | m |
| 7 | Support pattern count | $\displaystyle\binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!\,(n-k)!}$ | Ways to choose $k$ support legs from $n$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Minimum support legs (static) | $k \geq 3$ | Required for SP to be a non-degenerate polygon | dimensionless |
| 9 | Stride length | $\lambda = v \cdot T_c$ | $v$ = body velocity, $T_c$ = gait period | m |
| 10 | Duty factor (leg) | $\beta = \dfrac{T_{\text{stand}}}{T_c}$ | Fraction of cycle a leg is on the ground | dimensionless |
| 11 | Total events in gait table | $N_{\text{events}} = 2^n$ | Each leg is either support or transfer | dimensionless |
| 12 | Longitudinal stability (4-leg) | $S_x = \min\{x_G - x_{\text{front}}, x_{\text{rear}} - x_G\}$ | Min. margin along body x-axis | m |
| 13 | Lateral stability (4-leg) | $S_y = \min\{y_G - y_{\text{left}}, y_{\text{right}} - y_G\}$ | Min. margin along body y-axis | m |
| 14 | Critical CG height (tilt stability) | $h_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{S_{\min}}{\tan(\alpha_{\max})}$ | Max. CG height before tipping at tilt $\alpha_{\max}$ | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of These Equations:**
> - The **stability margin** is the single most-used metric in industrial legged-robot deployment (e.g., Boston Dynamics' Spot uses an online $S$ monitor to refuse walking commands that would breach $S_{\min}$).
> - The **ZMP formula** is the cornerstone of Honda's ASIMO controller and virtually every humanoid gait planner.
> - The **duty factor $\beta$** allows designers to compare gaits across species: human walk $\beta \approx 0.6$; horse gallop $\beta \approx 0.25$; insect tripod $\beta = 0.5$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Search and Rescue:** Quadrupeds like ANYmal navigate post-earthquake rubble where wheels fail.
2. **Planetary Exploration:** NASA's proposed quadruped/hexapod designs target lunar and Martian craters.
3. **Industrial Inspection:** Hexapods walk through petrochemical plants with active stability monitoring.
4. **Assistive Robotics:** Biped humanoids (Pepper, Digit) require dynamic ZMP control to interact with human environments.
5. **Defense:** Legged platforms carry payloads over unstructured battlefield terrain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Static Stability Condition for a Quadruped

**Problem Setup:** Consider a rectangular quadruped with four feet at the corners of a rectangle of length $L$ (front-to-rear) and width $W$ (left-to-right). Determine the longitudinal and lateral stability margins when the robot stands on all four legs with its center of gravity at position $(x_G, y_G)$ measured from the geometric center.

**Step 1 — Define the four support points.**
Let the geometric center of the rectangle coincide with the body CG frame origin. Then the four foot contact points are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{LF}} &= \left( +\frac{L}{2},\; +\frac{W}{2} \right) \quad \text{(Left Front)} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{RF}} &= \left( +\frac{L}{2},\; -\frac{W}{2} \right) \quad \text{(Right Front)} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{LH}} &= \left( -\frac{L}{2},\; +\frac{W}{2} \right) \quad \text{(Left Hind)} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{RH}} &= \left( -\frac{L}{2},\; -\frac{W}{2} \right) \quad \text{(Right Hind)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the support polygon.**
The convex hull of the four points is the rectangle itself. The boundary edges are the four line segments connecting adjacent corners.

**Step 3 — Compute longitudinal distance to edges.**
The front edge of the support polygon lies at $x = +L/2$. The rear edge lies at $x = -L/2$. The longitudinal stability margin is therefore:

$$
S_x = \min \!\left\{ \frac{L}{2} - x_G,\; \frac{L}{2} + x_G \right\}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute lateral distance to edges.**
Similarly, the left and right edges lie at $y = \pm W/2$:

$$
S_y = \min \!\left\{ \frac{W}{2} - y_G,\; \frac{W}{2} + y_G \right\}
$$

**Step 5 — Total stability margin.**
The static stability margin is the minimum of the longitudinal and lateral components (assuming upright, no tilt):

$$
S_{\min} = \min \!\left\{ S_x,\; S_y \right\}
$$

**Step 6 — Critical CG position.**
Stability is lost when $S_{\min} = 0$, i.e., when $x_G = \pm L/2$ (CG reaches the front or rear edge) or $y_G = \pm W/2$ (CG reaches the side edge). Thus, the CG must remain within the open rectangle.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Zero Moment Point (ZMP) for a Multi-Legged Robot

**Problem Setup:** A hexapod robot has six feet in contact with the ground. Each foot $i$ exerts a vertical ground reaction force $F_{z,i}$ and the foot contact point has coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$. Derive the ZMP coordinates.

**Step 1 — Define moments about an arbitrary ground point $P_0 = (x_0, y_0)$.**
The total moment about $P_0$ due to all GRFs is:

$$
M_{P_0} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} F_{z,i} \cdot \left[ (x_i - x_0)(-\hat{j}) + (y_i - y_0)(\hat{i}) \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Define the ZMP.**
The ZMP is the unique point $P_{ZMP} = (x_{ZMP}, y_{ZMP})$ at which the net moment of the active forces (gravity + inertia + GRF) about that point equals zero. Equivalently, it is the centroid of the ground pressure distribution weighted by $F_{z,i}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the definition (vertical force balance).**
Using the principle of moments balance about the ZMP and noting that the inertial and gravitational moments are captured in the equivalent GRF distribution, we obtain:

$$
x_{ZMP} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{z,i} \, x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{z,i}}
$$

**Step 4 — Repeat for the y-component.**

$$
y_{ZMP} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{z,i} \, y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{z,i}}
$$

**Step 5 — Static stability criterion via ZMP.**
For a statically stable configuration:

$$
(x_{ZMP},\; y_{ZMP}) \in \text{SupportPolygon}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example.**
Suppose a hexapod has the following ground reaction forces and foot positions (all in N and m):

| Foot | $F_z$ (N) | $x$ (m) | $y$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 0.30 | 0.20 |
| 2 | 45 | 0.30 | -0.20 |
| 3 | 48 | -0.30 | 0.20 |
| 4 | 47 | -0.30 | -0.20 |
| 5 | 52 | 0.00 | 0.25 |
| 6 | 50 | 0.00 | -0.25 |

The ZMP x-coordinate evaluates to:

$$
x_{ZMP} = \frac{(50)(0.30) + (45)(0.30) + (48)(-0.30) + (47)(-0.30) + (52)(0.00) + (50)(0.00)}{50 + 45 + 48 + 47 + 52 + 50}
$$

$$
x_{ZMP} = \frac{15.0 + 13.5 - 14.4 - 14.1 + 0 + 0}{292} = \frac{0}{292} = 0.000 \text{ m}
$$

The ZMP y-coordinate evaluates to:

$$
y_{ZMP} = \frac{(50)(0.20) + (45)(-0.20) + (48)(0.20) + (47)(-0.20) + (52)(0.25) + (50)(-0.25)}{292}
$$

$$
y_{ZMP} = \frac{10.0 - 9.0 + 9.6 - 9.4 + 13.0 - 12.5}{292} = \frac{1.7}{292} \approx 0.00582 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion.**
Since $(x_{ZMP}, y_{ZMP}) = (0.000,\; 0.00582) \text{ m}$ lies well within the support polygon (rectangle $\pm 0.30 \text{ m} \times \pm 0.25 \text{ m}$), the hexapod is statically stable.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Counting Distinct Support Patterns

**Problem Setup:** For an $n$-legged robot, how many distinct support patterns exist, and how many of these are statically stable?

**Step 1 — Total patterns.**
Each leg has 2 states: Support (S) or Transfer (T). Hence:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 2^n
$$

**Step 2 — Patterns with exactly $k$ support legs.**
The number of patterns with exactly $k$ legs in support is the binomial coefficient:

$$
N(k) = \binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!\,(n-k)!}
$$

**Step 3 — Statically stable patterns.**
A pattern is statically stable if $k \geq 3$ (so that the support polygon is non-degenerate). The total number of stable patterns is:

$$
N_{\text{stable}} = \sum_{k=3}^{n} \binom{n}{k}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical application to a hexapod ($n = 6$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{stable}} &= \binom{6}{3} + \binom{6}{4} + \binom{6}{5} + \binom{6}{6} \\[4pt]
&= 20 + 15 + 6 + 1 = 42
\end{aligned}
$$

Out of $2^6 = 64$ total possible patterns, 42 are statically stable. The most common one chosen is the **alternating tripod** ($k = 3$), of which there are $\binom{6}{3} = 20$ possible tripod patterns, but symmetry reduces these to only 2 distinct stable alternating gaits (Tripod A and Tripod B).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Key Threshold | Governing Equation | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static stability | $k \geq 3$ | $G_{xy} \in SP$ | Hexapods are inherently safer than quadrupeds |
| Stability margin | $S_{\min} > 0$ | $S = \min d(G_{xy}, \partial SP)$ | Quantifies *how* stable, not just *whether* |
| ZMP criterion | $P_{ZMP} \in SP$ | $x_{ZMP} = \sum F_{z,i} x_i / \sum F_{z,i}$ | Cornerstone of humanoid control |
| Gait cycle | $\beta > 0.5$ | $\beta = T_{\text{stand}}/T_c$ | Slow walk vs. fast run distinction |
| CG height limit | $h < h_{\text{crit}}$ | $h_{\text{crit}} = S_{\min}/\tan \alpha_{\max}$ | Tall robots tip more easily |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Legged Robot Stability Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete stability-analysis pipeline for a legged mobile robot, mapping the flow from sensor acquisition through gait decision to actuator command.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Body with n Legs] --> B[Leg State Sensor Array]
    B --> C[Foot Contact Detection Module]
    C --> D[Support Polygon Generator]
    D --> E[Convex Hull Computation]
    E --> F[Center of Gravity Estimator]
    F --> G[Stability Margin Calculator]
    G --> H{S_min greater than threshold}
    H --> I[Stable Configuration]
    H --> J[Unstable Configuration]
    I --> K[ZMP Computation Module]
    K --> L{ZMP inside SP}
    L --> M[Static Walk Permitted]
    L --> N[Dynamic Gait Required]
    J --> O[Gait Replanning Trigger]
    O --> P[Reconfigure Leg States]
    P --> A
    M --> Q[Actuator Command Dispatch]
    N --> Q
    Q --> A
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of Legged Robot Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Legged Mobile Robots] --> B[By Number of Legs]
    A --> C[By Stability Class]
    A --> D[By Gait Pattern]
    B --> B1[1 leg Uniped]
    B --> B2[2 legs Biped]
    B --> B3[4 legs Quadruped]
    B --> B4[6 legs Hexapod]
    B --> B5[8 legs Octopod]
    C --> C1[Static Stability]
    C --> C2[Dynamic Stability]
    C --> C3[Hybrid]
    D --> D1[Wave Gait Slowest]
    D --> D2[Alternating Tripod Hexapod]
    D --> D3[Crawl Gait Quadruped]
    D --> D4[Bound and Gallop Quadruped]
    D --> D5[Mammal Configuration]
    D --> D6[Reptile Configuration]
    B3 --> E1[Mammal Fore Hind Sync]
    B3 --> E2[Reptile Front Rear Sync]
```

### 4.3 Stability Decision State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SenseContacts
    SenseContacts --> ComputeSP: Identify Ground Contact Feet
    ComputeSP --> ProjectCG: Convex Hull of Contact Points
    ProjectCG --> CheckInsideSP: Vertical Projection of CG
    CheckInsideSP --> StableMargin: CG inside SP
    CheckInsideSP --> Unstable: CG outside SP
    StableMargin --> ComputeZMP: S greater than 0
    ComputeZMP --> StaticWalk: ZMP inside SP
    ComputeZMP --> DynamicWalk: ZMP near SP boundary
    StaticWalk --> CommandActuators: Proceed with Static Gait
    DynamicWalk --> CommandActuators: Engage Inertial Control
    Unstable --> ReplanGait: Trigger Stability Recovery
    ReplanGait --> SenseContacts
    CommandActuators --> SenseContacts: Next Gait Cycle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:**
> - **Block 1 (Pipeline):** Demonstrates the real-time control loop a legged robot uses each gait cycle to ensure stability.
> - **Block 2 (Taxonomy):** Provides a complete classification scheme as required by KTU Module 1 descriptive questions.
> - **Block 3 (State Machine):** Models the decision logic distinguishing static from dynamic walking regimes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "support polygon" as applied to a legged mobile robot. Why is its computation critical for static stability?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **support polygon** is defined as the convex hull of all points where the robot's supporting legs make contact with the ground (1 mark). For a legged robot with $k$ supporting legs, the polygon is formed by joining these $k$ ground contact points in their convex envelope (1 mark). Its computation is critical because static stability requires that the vertical projection of the robot's center of gravity must lie strictly inside this polygon; otherwise, the robot will tip over (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between static stability and dynamic stability in legged robots. Give one example of a robot that uses each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Static stability** refers to the ability of a robot to remain balanced under gravity alone, with the CG projection inside the support polygon at every instant — no inertial effects needed (1 mark). **Dynamic stability** allows the robot to be momentarily unstable statically, but uses inertial forces (momentum of swinging limbs, angular momentum) to remain upright (1 mark). Example: A **hexapod in alternating tripod gait** uses static stability; a **bipedal humanoid like ASIMO** uses dynamic stability during walking (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, classify legged mobile robots based on the number of legs. For each configuration, state whether it is capable of static stability, and justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A quadruped robot stands on a horizontal surface with its four feet at the corners of a rectangle of length **1.2 m** and width **0.8 m**. The center of gravity of the robot is located at **(0.15 m, 0.10 m)** from the geometric center of the rectangle. Calculate:
  (i) The longitudinal stability margin.
  (ii) The lateral stability margin.
  (iii) The total static stability margin.
  (iv) Comment on the stability of the robot. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Legged Mobile Robots (7 Marks):**

| # | Configuration | No. of Legs | Static Stability? | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Uniped** | 1 | No | Only one contact point; CG cannot project inside a degenerate polygon. Requires dynamic stability (1.5 marks). |
| 2 | **Biped** | 2 | Marginal | Two contact points form a line; static stability is theoretically possible but impractical. Relies on dynamic stability (1.5 marks). |
| 3 | **Triped** | 3 | Yes | Three contact points form a non-degenerate triangle; CG can project inside (1 mark). |
| 4 | **Quadruped** | 4 | Yes (with ≥3 supporting legs) | Four feet can form a quadrilateral SP; statically stable when 3 or 4 feet are on the ground (1.5 marks). |
| 5 | **Hexapod** | 6 | Yes (high margin) | Six feet with alternating tripod gait ($\geq 3$ in support) yield large SP and high static stability margin (1.5 marks). |

*Diagrams: The student should draw small sketches of each configuration (1 mark for the block of diagrams).*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Support rectangle: $L = 1.2$ m, $W = 0.8$ m
- CG position: $(x_G, y_G) = (0.15, 0.10)$ m

**Step 1 — Longitudinal stability margin:**

$$
S_x = \min \!\left\{ \frac{L}{2} - x_G,\; \frac{L}{2} + x_G \right\} = \min \!\left\{ 0.60 - 0.15,\; 0.60 + 0.15 \right\}
$$

$$
S_x = \min \{ 0.45,\; 0.75 \} = 0.45 \text{ m}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Lateral stability margin:**

$$
S_y = \min \!\left\{ \frac{W}{2} - y_G,\; \frac{W}{2} + y_G \right\} = \min \!\left\{ 0.40 - 0.10,\; 0.40 + 0.10 \right\}
$$

$$
S_y = \min \{ 0.30,\; 0.50 \} = 0.30 \text{ m}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Total static stability margin:**

$$
S_{\min} = \min \{ S_x,\; S_y \} = \min \{ 0.45,\; 0.30 \} = 0.30 \text{ m}
$$

**(1 mark)**
