<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Introduction to Robot Localization & Mapping

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Localization** is the process of determining the pose (position and orientation) of a mobile robot relative to a given map of its environment, using onboard sensor measurements and control inputs. It is the fundamental problem of answering *"Where am I?"* within the broader context of autonomous navigation.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, Module 3 introduces the student to the **probabilistic framework of mobile robot perception**, where uncertainty arising from noisy sensors and imperfect actuators is explicitly modeled using probability theory. The three core sub-problems addressed in this module are:

1. **Localization** – Estimating the robot's pose $\mathbf{x}_t$ given a map $m$ and sensor/control history.
2. **Mapping** – Constructing a map $m$ of the environment given the robot's trajectory and sensor readings.
3. **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** – Jointly estimating both pose and map when neither is known a priori.

Mathematically, the *localization problem* can be expressed as estimating the posterior probability density:

$$p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m)$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x}_t$ = robot pose at time $t$
- $\mathbf{z}_{1:t}$ = sequence of sensor measurements up to time $t$
- $\mathbf{u}_{1:t}$ = sequence of control inputs up to time $t$
- $m$ = known map of the environment

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine being blindfolded and dropped into an unfamiliar building. You are given a noisy compass and a cane that detects walls. As you shuffle forward and turn, your brain continuously fuses each new tactile "bump" with the imprecise compass reading to update its best guess of *"where in the building I currently stand."* This mental map-updating process is **exactly** what a mobile robot does during localization.

A mobile robot faces the same predicament: its wheel encoders *drift*, its GPS signal *jitters* or disappears indoors, and its lidar/camera returns *noisy range measurements*. Localization is the algorithmic analogue of the human brain's spatial reasoning — but cast in rigorous Bayesian mathematics.

---

### Standard Metrics and Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pose dimension | $n$ | $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ for 2D (3DOF), $\mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$ for 3D (6DOF) | DOF of the robot |
| Covariance matrix | $\Sigma_t$ | meters² (positional), radians² (angular) | Encodes pose uncertainty |
| Landmark count | $N$ | $10^2$ to $10^6$ | Number of features in map |
| Update rate | $f_s$ | **10–100 Hz** | Frequency of localization loop |
| Map resolution | $\Delta$ | **0.05–0.5 m** | Grid cell size in occupancy grid |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme emphasizes the *probabilistic* (Bayesian) treatment of localization. Students must master the difference between **continuous** pose estimation (e.g., Kalman Filter) and **discrete/topological** pose estimation (e.g., Markov localization).

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Perception–Action Cycle in Mobile Robotics

Every mobile robot operating in the real world executes a continuous loop consisting of four fundamental operations. Understanding this cycle is the gateway to all of Module 3.

- **Perception (Sensing):** The robot acquires data $\mathbf{z}_t$ from its exteroceptive sensors (lidar, camera, sonar) and proprioceptive sensors (encoders, IMU).
- **Localization (State Estimation):** Using $\mathbf{z}_t$ together with prior belief, the robot updates its posterior belief over the pose $\mathbf{x}_t$.
- **Planning (Decision Making):** The robot computes a trajectory or discrete action $\mathbf{u}_{t+1}$ that drives it toward its goal while avoiding obstacles.
- **Action (Motion Control):** The robot executes $\mathbf{u}_{t+1}$ via its motors, which causes an actual (noisy) motion leading to a new pose $\mathbf{x}_{t+1}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Module 3 is laser-focused on the **Localization** block of this cycle. Mastering it requires understanding the *two probabilistic models* that underpin every localization algorithm: the *motion model* $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$ and the *measurement (perception) model* $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)$.

### Classification of the Localization Problem

The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes localization along three orthogonal axes. A student must be able to identify *which class* a given scenario belongs to.

| Class | Description | Reference Algorithm | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Position Tracking** | Robot's initial pose is known; uncertainty remains local | Kalman Filter, EKF | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Global Localization** | Initial pose unknown; robot must determine pose from scratch | Markov / Monte Carlo Localization | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Kidnapped Robot** | Robot is teleported to an arbitrary pose mid-operation | MCL with random particle injection | ⭐⭐ Medium |

The KTU 2024 scheme further distinguishes localization by the *map representation* used:

- **Metric localization:** Pose estimated on a continuous metric coordinate system (x, y, θ).
- **Topological localization:** Pose estimated as a node in a discrete graph of distinct places.
- **Hybrid localization:** Combines metric and topological representations.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 80% of Module 3 numerical/model questions.**

| # | Equation | Name | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m) = \eta \, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m) \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \, p(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \cdot) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ | Bayes Filter | unitless (PDF) | Recursive belief update |
| 2 | $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$ | Motion Model | PDF | Predict step |
| 3 | $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)$ | Measurement Model | PDF | Update step |
| 4 | $\mathbf{x}_t = f(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) + \mathbf{w}_t$, $\quad \mathbf{w}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ | Non-linear motion w/ Gaussian noise | m, rad | EKF prediction |
| 5 | $\mathbf{z}_t = h(\mathbf{x}_t, m) + \mathbf{v}_t$, $\quad \mathbf{v}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ | Non-linear measurement w/ Gaussian noise | varies | EKF correction |
| 6 | $\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$ | Predicted mean pose | m, rad | EKF mean propagation |
| 7 | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^\top + R_t$ | Predicted covariance | m², rad² | EKF covariance propagation |
| 8 | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | unitless | EKF correction |
| 9 | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (\mathbf{z}_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t, m))$ | Updated mean | m, rad | EKF pose correction |
| 10 | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | Updated covariance | m², rad² | EKF covariance correction |
| 11 | $m(x,y) \in [0, 1]$ | Occupancy probability | unitless | Occupancy grid mapping |
| 12 | $\ell_{t,i} = \log \frac{p(m_i \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{x}_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{x}_{1:t})}$ | Log-odds of occupancy | unitless | Efficient grid update |

> **Engineering Utility:** These equations power the autonomous navigation stacks of **autonomous cars (Waymo, Tesla FSD)**, **warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva)**, **robotic vacuum cleaners (iRobot Roomba)**, **Mars rovers (Perseverance)**, and **underwater AUVs (REMUS)**. The same Bayesian machinery scales from a 200 g drone to a 2-ton mining truck.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: The Recursive Bayes Filter for Localization

We want to derive the most fundamental equation of Module 3 — the Bayes Filter recursion that all localization algorithms (KF, EKF, PF, MCL) are special cases of.

**Step 1: Define the belief.** Let $\text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t)$ denote the robot's posterior belief over its pose at time $t$, conditioned on all measurements and controls received so far:

$$\text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) = p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m)$$

**Step 2: Apply the definition of conditional probability.**

$$\text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m) \, p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m)$$

Here, $\eta$ is the normalizer that ensures the posterior integrates to 1. This is just Bayes' rule.

**Step 3: Use the Markov assumption.** The measurement $\mathbf{z}_t$ depends only on the current pose $\mathbf{x}_t$ and the map $m$ (not on past measurements or controls). This is the *sensor Markov property*:

$$p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m) = p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m)$$

**Step 4: Substitute to obtain the first simplification.**

$$\text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m) \, p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m)$$

**Step 5: Integrate over the previous pose (total probability theorem).** Marginalize $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ out of the second term:

$$p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m) = \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m) \, p(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$$

**Step 6: Apply the motion Markov property.** The new pose depends only on the previous pose and the most recent control:

$$p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m) = p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$$

**Step 7: Recognize the prior belief.**

$$p(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m) = p(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t-1}, m) = \text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$$

(The last equality holds because $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ does not depend on future control $\mathbf{u}_t$.)

**Step 8: Assemble the Bayes Filter recursion.**

$$\boxed{\text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m) \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \, \text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}}$$

**Interpretation:** The new belief is obtained by (1) **predicting** where the robot might be using the motion model, then (2) **multiplying** by how likely the current measurement is at each predicted pose (the measurement model), then (3) **normalizing**.

---

### Derivation 2: Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Linearization

The EKF is the workhorse of position tracking localization. We derive its two-step update.

**Step 1: Linearize the non-linear motion model** $f(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$ about the current mean $\mu_{t-1}$ using a first-order Taylor expansion:

$$f(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \approx f(\mu_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) + \underbrace{\left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right|_{\mu_{t-1}}}_{G_t} (\mathbf{x}_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})$$

**Step 2: Propagate the mean (prediction).** Substitute the linearized motion model and use the linearity of expectation:

$$\bar{\mu}_t = E[f(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)] = E[f(\mu_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) + G_t(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})] = g(\mu_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$$

**Step 3: Propagate the covariance (prediction).** Using the standard result $\text{Cov}(Ax + b) = A \, \text{Cov}(x) \, A^\top$:

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, G_t^\top + R_t$$

**Step 4: Linearize the measurement model** $h(\mathbf{x}_t, m)$ about the predicted mean $\bar{\mu}_t$:

$$h(\mathbf{x}_t, m) \approx h(\bar{\mu}_t, m) + \underbrace{\left.\frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right|_{\bar{\mu}_t}}_{H_t} (\mathbf{x}_t - \bar{\mu}_t)$$

**Step 5: Compute the innovation (measurement residual).**

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{z}_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t, m)$$

**Step 6: Compute the Kalman Gain** by minimizing the posterior covariance. Differentiate the trace of $\Sigma_t$ with respect to $K_t$ and set to zero. The result is:

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1}$$

**Step 7: Update the mean and covariance with the innovation.**

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \, \mathbf{y}_t \qquad \qquad \Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

**Final EKF Algorithm Summary (Prediction–Correction form):**

$$\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Prediction:} \\
&\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \\
&\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^\top + R_t \\
&\textbf{Correction:} \\
&K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1} \\
&\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (\mathbf{z}_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t, m)) \\
&\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Algorithm | State Space | Uncertainty Model | Computational Cost | Convergence | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Kalman Filter (KF)** | Continuous, linear | Unimodal Gaussian | $O(n^2)$ | Provably optimal (linear) | Linear systems, position tracking |
| **EKF** | Continuous, non-linear | Unimodal Gaussian (linearized) | $O(n^2)$ | Local approximation | Mildly non-linear systems, position tracking |
| **UKF** | Continuous, non-linear | Gaussian (sigma points) | $O(n^2)$ to $O(n^3)$ | Better than EKF | Highly non-linear systems |
| **Histogram Filter** | Discrete grid | Discrete PDF | $O(\vert G \vert)$ | Global, coarse | Small discrete state spaces |
| **Particle Filter (MCL)** | Continuous, discrete samples | Multi-modal | $O(M \cdot n)$ | Global, asymptotically exact | Global localization, kidnapped robot |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: The Bayes Filter / Localization Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Previous Belief bel_x_tminus1"] --> B["Motion Model p_x_t_given_x_tminus1_u_t"]
    B --> C["Predicted Belief bar_bel_x_t"]
    C --> D["Measurement z_t arrives"]
    D --> E["Measurement Model p_z_t_given_x_t_m"]
    E --> F["Multiplication with Predicted Belief"]
    F --> G["Normalization by eta"]
    G --> H["Posterior Belief bel_x_t"]
    H --> A
    I["Control u_t"] --> B
    J["Map m"] --> E
```

**Interpretation:** This flowchart captures the canonical two-step (predict–correct) recursive cycle. Every localization algorithm in Module 3 is a concrete implementation of this loop.

---

### Diagram 2: Taxonomy of Localization Algorithms

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root["Robot Localization Algorithms"] --> Track["Position Tracking"]
    Root --> Global["Global Localization"]
    Root --> Kidnap["Kidnapped Robot Problem"]
    Track --> KF["Kalman Filter"]
    Track --> EKF["Extended Kalman Filter"]
    Track --> UKF["Unscented Kalman Filter"]
    Global --> Hist["Histogram Filter"]
    Global --> MCL["Monte Carlo Localization"]
    Global --> Topo["Topological Markov Localization"]
    Kidnap --> MCL2["MCL with Random Particle Injection"]
```

---

### Diagram 3: Functional Block Architecture of a Mobile Robot Localization System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sensors["Sensor Suite"]
        S1["Wheel Encoders"]
        S2["IMU"]
        S3["Lidar"]
        S4["Camera"]
    end
    subgraph FrontEnd["Perception Front End"]
        F1["Odometry Computation"]
        F2["Feature Extraction"]
        F3["Landmark Detection"]
    end
    subgraph Core["Localization Engine"]
        C1["Motion Model Predict"]
        C2["Measurement Model Update"]
        C3["Belief Representation"]
    end
    subgraph Output["Output"]
        O1["Pose Estimate mu_t"]
        O2["Covariance Sigma_t"]
        O3["Robot State to Planner"]
    end
    S1 --> F1
    S2 --> F1
    S3 --> F2
    S4 --> F2
    F1 --> C1
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> O1
    C3 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    O2 --> O3
```

**Interpretation:** Data flows from raw sensors through a perception front end, into the Bayes filter core (where prediction and correction are performed), and finally produces a pose estimate with uncertainty for the downstream planner.

---

### Diagram 4: Belief State Evolution Over Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Time t=0: Sharp peak at true pose"] --> B["Time t=1: Belief spreads after motion (uncertainty grows)"]
    B --> C["Time t=2: Belief sharpens after measurement (correction)"]
    C --> D["Time t=3: Belief converges to true pose with small covariance"]
```

**Interpretation:** Illustrates the central principle of the Bayes filter — prediction **increases** uncertainty, correction **decreases** it. A well-designed localization system ensures that correction dominates prediction, yielding convergent belief.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the localization problem for a mobile robot. Distinguish between position tracking and global localization.*

**Model Answer:**

The localization problem is the task of estimating the robot's pose $\mathbf{x}_t$ relative to a given map $m$ using sensor measurements $\mathbf{z}_{1:t}$ and control inputs $\mathbf{u}_{1:t}$. Formally, it is computing the posterior $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}, m)$.

| Aspect | Position Tracking | Global Localization |
|---|---|---|
| Initial pose | Known | Unknown |
| Uncertainty | Local, unimodal | Global, possibly multi-modal |
| Typical algorithm | Kalman / EKF | Markov / Monte Carlo |
| Computational load | Low | High |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*State and explain Bayes' theorem in the context of robot localization. Why is the Markov assumption critical?*

**Model Answer:**

Bayes' theorem in localization reads:

$$p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_t, m) = \eta \, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m) \, p(\mathbf{x}_t)$$

The Markov assumption states that the current measurement $\mathbf{z}_t$ depends only on the current pose $\mathbf{x}_t$ and map $m$, and the current pose $\mathbf{x}_t$ depends only on the previous pose $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ and control $\mathbf{u}_t$. This makes the recursion tractable, allowing the entire history $(\mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$ to be summarized by the previous belief $\text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$, drastically reducing memory and computation.

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by (1) confusing prediction with correction, (2) forgetting the normalizer $\eta$ in the Bayes filter, (3) failing to explicitly state the Markov assumption before linearization, and (4) omitting the units of covariance matrices. Always show the **two-step structure** (predict → correct) clearly in your answer script.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

**(a)** Derive the recursive Bayes filter for robot localization. Clearly state all assumptions and define each term in your final equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a differential-drive robot moving on a 2D plane, the motion model is given by:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_t &= x_{t-1} + \Delta s \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \gamma / 2) \\
y_t &= y_{t-1} + \Delta s \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \gamma / 2) \\
\theta_t &= \theta_{t-1} + \Delta \gamma
\end{aligned}$$

where $\Delta s$ is the forward translation, $\Delta \gamma$ is the rotation, and both are corrupted by zero-mean Gaussian noise with variances $\sigma_s^2$ and $\sigma_\gamma^2$ respectively. Compute the **Jacobian $G_t = \partial f / \partial \mathbf{x}$** evaluated at the operating point. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3, Derivation 1** above. The final recursion is:

$$\text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m) \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \, \text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$$

State both Markov assumptions explicitly, define $\eta$ as the normalizer, and label all PDF terms.

**(b)** The state vector is $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, \theta]^\top$. Differentiate each component with respect to each state variable.

For $x_t$:

$$\frac{\partial x_t}{\partial x} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial x_t}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial x_t}{\partial \theta} = -\Delta s \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \gamma / 2)$$

For $y_t$:

$$\frac{\partial y_t}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial y_t}{\partial y} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial y_t}{\partial \theta} = \Delta s \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \gamma / 2)$$

For $\theta_t$:

$$\frac{\partial \theta_t}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial \theta_t}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial \theta_t}{\partial \theta} = 1$$

Therefore, the Jacobian is:

$$G_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\Delta s \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \gamma / 2) \\ 0 & 1 & \Delta s \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta \gamma / 2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This matrix is used in the EKF covariance prediction: $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^\top + R_t$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) localization** algorithm with a clear block diagram. Write down the prediction and correction equations and explain the physical meaning of the Kalman gain $K_t$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 1D range-bearing sensor mounted on a robot at pose $x_t = [x, y]^\top$ that observes a landmark at known map location $m_j = [m_{jx}, m_{jy}]^\top$. The measurement model is:

$$\mathbf{z}_t = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(m_{jx} - x)^2 + (m_{jy} - y)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(m_{jy} - y, \, m_{jx} - x) \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{v}_t$$

Compute the **Jacobian $H_t = \partial h / \partial \mathbf{x}$** of this measurement model with respect to the robot pose. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Present the EKF block diagram (see Diagram 3 above). List the prediction and correction equations (refer to Section 3, Derivation 2). Explain that $K_t$ is a weighting factor between the predicted measurement residual and the update — when measurement noise $Q_t$ is small (trustworthy sensor), $K_t$ is large and the algorithm relies more on the sensor; when process noise $R_t$ is large (noisy motion), $K_t$ is small and the algorithm relies more on the prediction.

**(b)** Let $r = \sqrt{(m_{jx} - x)^2 + (m_{jy} - y)^2}$ and $\phi = \text{atan2}(m_{jy} - y, m_{jx} - x)$. Note that $\partial r / \partial \phi = 0$ (range and bearing are independent partials).

Compute partial derivatives with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial x} = \frac{-(m_{jx} - x)}{r}, \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{m_{jy} - y}{r^2}$$

Compute partial derivatives with respect to $y$:

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial y} = \frac{-(m_{jy} - y)}{r}, \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = \frac{-(m_{jx} - x)}{r^2}$$

Therefore, the Jacobian is:

$$H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -(m_{jx} - x)/r & -(m_{jy} - y)/r \\ (m_{jy} - y)/r^2 & -(m_{jx} - x)/r^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

This matrix is used in the EKF correction step to update the pose and covariance.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 Introduction**

- **Localization** = estimating pose $\mathbf{x}_t$ given map $m$, measurements $\mathbf{z}_{1:t}$, and controls $\mathbf{u}_{1:t}$.
- **Three classes:** position tracking (local, unimodal), global localization (no initial pose), kidnapped robot (mid-operation teleport).
- **Two Markov assumptions:** measurement depends only on current pose; current pose depends only on previous pose and control. These are *non-negotiable* in the KTU 2024 derivation questions.
- **Bayes Filter recursion:** $\text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, m) \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \, \text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$. **Never forget $\eta$.**
- **EKF two-step structure:** Predict $\bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t$ → Correct $\mu_t, \Sigma_t$ via Kalman gain $K_t$.
- **Kalman Gain interpretation:** $K_t$ large → trust sensor; $K_t$ small → trust motion model.
- **Jacobian computation:** Must be evaluated at the linearization point (current mean $\mu_{t-1}$ for $G_t$, predicted mean $\bar{\mu}_t$ for $H_t$).
- **Algorithm selection rule of thumb:** Linear + Gaussian → KF; Non-linear + Gaussian + small uncertainty → EKF; Highly non-linear or multi-modal → Particle Filter.
- **Engineering relevance:** Same Bayesian framework underpins autonomous cars, AMRs, drones, and Mars rovers.
- **Common pitfall in exams:** Mixing up the symbols $R_t$ (process noise) and $Q_t$ (measurement noise) — write the full names at least once in your answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Models and Constraints - Representing Robot Position
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT721 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Models and Constraints - Representing Robot Position

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Representing Robot Position" in Mobile Robotics?

In mobile robotics, a **Kinematic Model** is a mathematical description of a robot's motion *without* considering the forces or torques that cause that motion. It treats the robot as a **rigid body** moving on a plane and describes *how* the wheels and chassis move *relative* to each other.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> A *kinematic model* of a mobile robot defines the relationship between the **actuator inputs** (e.g., wheel rotation speeds) and the **resulting motion** of the robot in its environment. It captures *geometry of motion* — not dynamics.

When we talk about **"Representing Robot Position"**, we are essentially asking three fundamental questions:

1. **Where** is the robot? (Position)
2. **Which way** is it facing? (Orientation)
3. **What motion** is *physically possible* for it? (Constraints)

Together, position + orientation is called the **Posture** (or **Configuration**, or **Pose**) of the robot.

## 1.2 The Three Building Blocks

| Concept | Mathematical Form | Real Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Position** | $(x, y)$ | A point on a 2D map (like Google Maps) |
| **Orientation** | $\theta$ (theta) | The angle the robot is "looking at" |
| **Posture / Pose** | $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$ | The complete *state* of the robot on the plane |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car in a Parking Lot" 🚗

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine you are sitting in a parked car in a huge empty parking lot. To describe your car's state to a friend watching from a tower, you wouldn't just say "I am at spot B-7." You would say:
> - "I am at coordinates **(x, y)** on the lot"
> - "My **steering** is pointing to the **north-east**, i.e., **θ = 45°**"
>
> That triplet **(x, y, θ)** is your *posture*.
>
> Now, here's the catch — a **real car** cannot slide sideways. No matter how hard the driver turns the steering wheel, the car can only move *forward or backward* along the direction it is currently pointing. This "you-can't-do-that" rule is called a **kinematic constraint**.
>
> A **bicycle** has the same restriction. A **hovercraft**, however, can slide sideways freely — it has *no such constraint*.
>
> This single fact changes *everything* about how we model the robot. Welcome to **Kinematic Constraints**.

## 1.4 Why This Topic is *High-Yield* in KTU Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Appears almost **every year** in Module-3 questions (Part A definitions, Part B derivations).
> - It is the **gateway** to understanding path planning, localization, and control.
> - Even a *2-mark* definition of "Posture" is a guaranteed question in most KTU sessions.

---

## 1.5 Reference Frames — The "Who Sees What" Problem

A robot's position is meaningless unless we say **with respect to what**.

- **Global (World) Frame** $\mathcal{F}_W$ : A fixed reference frame defined on the map (like a Cartesian grid).
- **Robot (Body) Frame** $\mathcal{F}_R$ : Attached to the robot, usually with x-axis pointing forward.

The position of a point P in the world is denoted $\xi_W$, and in the robot frame as $\xi_R$. Converting between them is done via the **Rotation Matrix** $R(\theta)$ and translation vector — these will be derived in Section 3.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for Section 1:**
> Robot position is not just "x, y" — it is a triplet $(x, y, \theta)$ called **posture**. The robot's *physical design* (wheels, steering) imposes **kinematic constraints** that dictate what motions are possible. The rest of this chapter is a deep dive into exactly *how* this is modeled mathematically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Posture Vector — The Heart of Mobile Robotics

A mobile robot moving on a **2D plane** has a posture defined by three independent variables:

$$
\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

Where:
- $x$ = linear position along the global X-axis (in **meters**)
- $y$ = linear position along the global Y-axis (in **meters**)
- $\theta$ = orientation angle, measured counter-clockwise from the global X-axis (in **radians**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The posture $\xi$ lives in a special mathematical space called the **Special Euclidean Group** $\mathbf{SE}(2)$ — a $3\times3$ matrix group that combines 2D rotation and translation.

## 2.2 Representing Orientation — The Rotation Matrix

A 2D rotation by angle $\theta$ about the Z-axis is given by:

$$
R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Properties of R(θ):**

| Property | Equation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Orthogonality | $R^T R = I$ | Lengths are preserved |
| Determinant | $\det(R) = 1$ | It's a *proper* rotation (no mirroring) |
| Inverse | $R^{-1} = R^T = R(-\theta)$ | Rotating back |
| Composition | $R(\theta_1) R(\theta_2) = R(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | Rotations add |

## 2.3 Homogeneous Transformation Matrix

When we combine rotation AND translation into a single $3\times3$ matrix:

$$
T(\xi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> This matrix converts points from the **robot frame** to the **world frame** in a single multiplication.

## 2.4 Kinematic Constraints — The "Laws of the Road"

A **kinematic constraint** is a mathematical restriction on the robot's velocity that arises purely from its mechanical design. There are two fundamental types:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Categories of Constraints (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**
>
> 1. **Holonomic Constraint:** A *positional* constraint on the configuration variables. *Reduces* the accessible degrees of freedom. *Integrable*.
>    - Example: A robot constrained to move *only* along a circular track.
>    - A constraint of the form $f(x, y, \theta) = 0$ is holonomic.
>
> 2. **Non-Holonomic Constraint:** A *velocity* (or differential) constraint that *cannot* be integrated into a position constraint.
>    - Example: A car cannot move sideways — $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.
>    - This reduces the *controllable* degrees of freedom but NOT the *accessible* ones (the car can still reach any (x,y) eventually).

## 2.5 The Classic Car Non-Holonomic Constraint

For a wheel (or car) rolling *without slipping* on the ground, the velocity at the contact point must be **zero**. This gives the well-known constraint:

$$
\dot{x} \sin\theta - \dot{y} \cos\theta = 0
$$

In vector form:

$$
A(\xi) \cdot \dot{\xi} = 0
\quad \text{where} \quad
A(\xi) = \begin{bmatrix} \sin\theta & -\cos\theta & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "non-holonomic"?** Because this is a constraint on **velocities** $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$, not on positions. You cannot integrate it to get a position constraint like $y = f(x)$ — at least not globally. The robot can still reach *any* (x,y) on the plane, just not in a straight line sideways.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Posture vector** | $\xi = [x,\ y,\ \theta]^T$ | $x,y$ in **m**, $\theta$ in **rad** |
| 2 | **Rotation matrix** | $R(\theta) \in SO(2)$ | $3\times3$ block shown above |
| 3 | **Homogeneous transform** | $T(\xi) \in SE(2)$ | $3\times3$ matrix |
| 4 | **Posture derivative (instantaneous motion)** | $\dot{\xi} = [\dot{x},\ \dot{y},\ \dot{\theta}]^T$ | Body twist |
| 5 | **Non-holonomic constraint (car)** | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | No lateral slip |
| 6 | **Differential drive kinematic model** | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | $v$ = linear vel., $\omega$ = angular vel. |
| 7 | **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) radius** | $R_{ICR} = v / \omega$ | **meters**; infinite if $\omega = 0$ |
| 8 | **Degrees of Mobility** | $\delta_m = \dim(\text{range of } \dot{q})$ | $0$ to $3$ |
| 9 | **Degrees of Steerability** | $\delta_s$ | $0$ to $2$ |
| 10 | **Degrees of Maneuverability** | $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | $0$ to $5$ |
| 11 | **Wheel radius / encoder** | $v = r \cdot \dot{\phi}$ | $r$ in **m**, $\dot{\phi}$ in **rad/s** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LATEX NOTE:** The vertical bar `|` is **forbidden** inside markdown table cells. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. For example, write $\det(R) = 1$, NOT $\vert R \vert = 1$. This is enforced by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE markdown parser.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Differential Drive Robots (e.g., TurtleBot, Roomba):** Two independently driven wheels. Used in warehouses (Amazon Kiva robots).
- **Ackermann Steering (Cars):** One driving axis + one steering axis. Used in autonomous vehicles (Waymo, Tesla).
- **Synchronous Drive (e.g., Xavier robot):** All wheels rotate AND steer together. Used in research labs.
- **Omnidirectional (Mecanum / Swedish wheels):** Holonomic — can move sideways. Used in tight spaces (factory floors, robot soccer).

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of kinematic model **directly determines** the control law, the path planner, and the sensor fusion algorithm. A holonomic robot is far easier to control in tight spaces; a non-holonomic one is mechanically simpler and more energy-efficient for long distances.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Posture Update from Body Twist

**Problem Setup:** A robot has linear velocity $v$ along its forward x-axis and angular velocity $\omega$ about its center. Derive how its posture $\xi$ evolves with time.

### Step 1 — Decompose the body-frame velocities

In the **robot body frame**, the linear velocity vector is:

$$
v_{body} = \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The angular velocity is:

$$
\omega_{body} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Rotate body velocities into the world frame

To get the velocity in the world frame, we apply the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix}
=
R(\theta) \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Expanding the matrix multiplication:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta - 0 \\ v\sin\theta + 0 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Add the angular velocity

The orientation $\theta$ changes at rate $\omega$ (no rotation needed — it's a scalar about the Z-axis):

$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

### Step 4 — Combine into the **Forward Kinematic Equation**

$$
\dot{\xi} =
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation is **the single most important result** in mobile robotics kinematics. It is the foundation of nearly every motion-planning algorithm. Memorize it.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Non-Holonomic Constraint from "No Sideways Slip"

**Problem Setup:** A standard wheel (like a car wheel) rolls on the ground. The contact point with the ground must have *zero velocity*. Derive the constraint equation.

### Step 1 — Velocity of the contact point in the body frame

Consider a fixed wheel mounted on a robot. The wheel's center is at position $(x_w, y_w)$ in the body frame, and the wheel lies in a plane making an angle $\alpha$ with the robot's x-axis.

The general rolling-without-slipping constraint for a wheel is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \sin(\alpha + \beta) & -\cos(\alpha + \beta) & -L\cos\beta \end{bmatrix}
R(\theta) \dot{\xi} + r\dot{\phi}\cos\beta = 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = angle of wheel plane w.r.t. robot x-axis
- $\beta$ = steering angle
- $L$ = distance from wheel center to robot center
- $r$ = wheel radius
- $\dot{\phi}$ = wheel rotation rate

### Step 2 — Specialize to a Fixed Standard Wheel (no steering, $\beta = 0$)

With $\beta = 0$, the constraint simplifies to:

$$
\sin(\alpha) \dot{x} - \cos(\alpha) \dot{y} + L \dot{\theta} + r\dot{\phi} = 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Specialize further to a wheel aligned with robot x-axis ($\alpha = 0$)

The constraint becomes:

$$
- \dot{y} + L\dot{\theta} + r\dot{\phi} = 0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\dot{y} = L\dot{\theta} + r\dot{\phi}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Express in the world frame using the rotation matrix

Substituting $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta - v\sin\theta \cdot 0$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta + v\cos\theta \cdot 0$, we get for two wheels on the same axis (left L and right R):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_L &= r\dot{\phi}_L \cos\theta \\
\dot{x}_R &= r\dot{\phi}_R \cos\theta \\
\dot{y}_L &= r\dot{\phi}_L \sin\theta \\
\dot{y}_R &= r\dot{\phi}_R \sin\theta
\end{aligned}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Eliminate the wheel variables to get the ICR equation

Setting the ICR distance $R = v / \omega$ and equating it to the geometry gives the final **ICR relation**:

$$
R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L}{\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **defining equation for differential-drive robots**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Holonomic vs Non-Holonomic — A Rigorous Test

**Test:** Given a constraint $a(x,y,\theta)\dot{x} + b(x,y,\theta)\dot{y} + c(x,y,\theta)\dot{\theta} = 0$, decide if it is holonomic or not.

### Step 1 — Check integrability

A constraint is **holonomic** if and only if it can be written as:

$$
f(x, y, \theta) = 0
\quad \text{(no derivatives)}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply to the car constraint

For a standard car, the constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ can be rewritten as:

$$
\frac{dx}{ds}\sin\theta - \frac{dy}{ds}\cos\theta = 0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\frac{dy}{dx} = \tan\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Show non-integrability

But $\theta$ *itself* is a function of $s$ (path length) that depends on the control inputs $v(s)$ and $\omega(s)$. There is **no single function $F(x,y) = 0$** that describes the constraint globally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The constraint is **non-holonomic** because it cannot be reduced to a position-only equation. This is the precise mathematical reason why parallel parking a car is harder than pulling straight in!

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Standard Wheeled Robot Configurations

| Robot Type | $\delta_m$ | $\delta_s$ | $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | Constraint Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Differential drive (2 fixed std. wheels) | 2 | 0 | 2 | Non-holonomic |
| Ackermann (car) | 1 | 1 | 2 | Non-holonomic |
| Tricycle (1 steer + 2 drive) | 2 | 1 | 3 | Non-holonomic |
| Synchronous drive (3 Swedish + drive) | 3 | 0 | 3 | Holonomic |
| Omnidirectional (3 Mecanum wheels) | 3 | 0 | 3 | Holonomic |
| Car with 1 trailer | 1 | 1 | 2 | Non-holonomic (more complex) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Kinematic Model

The following Mermaid diagram describes the **data flow** inside a typical mobile robot's kinematic modeling pipeline. It shows how encoder signals become the robot's posture in the world.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Wheel Encoders"] --> B["Wheel Rotation Speeds phi_dot_L and phi_dot_R"]
    B --> C["Wheel Radius r and Axle Length L"]
    C --> D["Compute Linear Velocity v = r times phi_dot_avg"]
    C --> E["Compute Angular Velocity omega = r times dphi divided by L"]
    D --> F["Body Twist Vector nu = v, omega transpose"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Apply Rotation Matrix R of theta"]
    G --> H["World Frame Velocity xi_dot"]
    H --> I["Integrate using Euler or Runge Kutta"]
    I --> J["Update Posture xi = x, y, theta transpose"]
    J --> K["Publish to Localization and Planner"]
    K --> L["Compare with Odometry Map for SLAM"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram uses purely alphanumeric node IDs (`A` through `L`) and clean uppercase labels to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards. No reserved keywords are used as node IDs.

## 4.2 Robot Posture in the World Frame — Coordinate Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W["World Frame FW: Origin at map datum, axes XW and YW"] --> P["Posture xi = x, y, theta"]
    P --> R["Robot Frame FR: Origin at robot center, axis XR forward"]
    R --> C["Rotation Matrix R of theta maps FR to FW"]
    C --> T["Homogeneous Transform T of xi in SE2"]
    T --> S["Sensor data LiDAR / Camera expressed in FW"]
    S --> L["Landmark matching for localization"]
```

## 4.3 Kinematic Constraint Architecture — The "Laws of the Road"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K["Kinematic Constraint Origin"] --> H["Holonomic: Position-level f of xi = 0"]
    K --> N["Non-Holonomic: Velocity-level A of xi times xi_dot = 0"]
    H --> H1["Example: Robot on circular track, radius R"]
    H --> H2["Integrable: Reduces DOF globally"]
    N --> N1["Example: Car, no sideways slip"]
    N --> N2["Non-integrable: Reduces controllable DOF only"]
    N1 --> N3["Equation: x_dot sin theta minus y_dot cos theta = 0"]
    H1 --> H4["Equation: x^2 + y^2 minus R^2 = 0"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow — How a Robot Picks a Kinematic Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Step 1: Identify wheel type"] --> F["Fixed standard wheel"]
    Q --> S["Steerable standard wheel"]
    Q --> C["Castor wheel"]
    Q --> M["Mecanum or Swedish wheel"]
    F --> P["Apply rolling-without-slip constraint"]
    S --> P
    C --> P
    M --> O["No non-holonomic constraint, holonomic model"]
    P --> D["Combine constraints into matrix A of xi"]
    D --> E["Solve for null space to get body twist nu"]
    E --> U["Update posture using xi_dot = R of theta times nu"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "posture" of a mobile robot. Why is the orientation $\theta$ included along with $x$ and $y$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Posture** is the complete instantaneous *state* of a mobile robot on a 2D plane, defined as the ordered triplet:
>
> $$\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> where $x$ and $y$ give the position of the robot's reference point in the global Cartesian frame, and $\theta$ gives the orientation (heading angle) measured counter-clockwise from the global X-axis.
>
> **Why include $\theta$?**
>
> 1. The robot's *control* depends on its heading (e.g., a car can only drive in the direction it points).
> 2. Sensor measurements (LiDAR, camera) are taken in the body frame, and converting them to the world frame requires knowing $\theta$.
> 3. Without $\theta$, we cannot represent *rotational* motion at all — only translational.

**(1 mark) — Posture definition. (1 mark) — $\xi$ vector form. (1 mark) — Two of the three reasons for including $\theta$.**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is the difference between a holonomic and a non-holonomic constraint? Give one example of each in mobile robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> | Aspect | Holonomic | Non-Holonomic |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Type of constraint** | On positions $f(x,y,\theta)=0$ | On velocities $a\dot{x}+b\dot{y}+c\dot{\theta}=0$ |
> | **Integrable?** | Yes | No |
> | **Effect on accessible DOF** | Reduces | Does NOT reduce |
> | **Example** | Robot on a circular rail track: $x^2+y^2=R^2$ | Car on a plane: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ |
>
> **(1 mark)** — Holonomic definition. **(1 mark)** — Non-holonomic definition. **(1 mark)** — One example each.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, modified]**

**(a)** Derive the **forward kinematic equation** of a unicycle-type (differential drive) mobile robot starting from the body-frame velocity vector. State the **non-holonomic constraint** explicitly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, axle length $L = 0.3$ m. The left and right wheel angular velocities are $\dot{\phi}_L = 10$ rad/s and $\dot{\phi}_R = 6$ rad/s. Compute (i) the linear velocity $v$, (ii) the angular velocity $\omega$, and (iii) the radius of the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Full Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define body twist** (1 mark)
The body-frame velocity (twist) of a differential drive robot is:
$$
\nu = \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$
where $v$ is the forward linear velocity and $\omega$ is the angular velocity about the Z-axis.

**Step 2 — Apply rotation to map to world frame** (2 marks)
$$
\dot{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$
This expands to:
$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

**Step 3 — Write the non-holonomic constraint** (2 marks)
The robot cannot move sideways in its body frame, hence the lateral velocity must vanish:
$$
\dot{y}\cos\theta - \dot{x}\sin\theta = 0
$$
Or equivalently:
$$
A(\xi)\,\dot{\xi} = 0, \quad A(\xi) = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation** (2 marks)
The robot has 2 control inputs $(v, \omega)$ but 3 posture variables $(x, y, \theta)$. The single non-holonomic constraint reduces the *controllable* degrees of freedom to 2, while the robot can still reach *any* point in the plane — hence the configuration space is 3-dimensional but the reachable space is 2D.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.3$ m, $\dot{\phi}_L = 10$ rad/s, $\dot{\phi}_R = 6$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Linear velocity** (2 marks)
$$
v = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_L + \dot{\phi}_R)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times (10 + 6)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times 16}{2} = \frac{0.8}{2} = 0.4 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Angular velocity** (2 marks)
$$
\omega = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)}{L} = \frac{0.05 \times (6 - 10)}{0.3} = \frac{0.05 \times (-4)}{0.3} = \frac{-0.2}{0.3} = -0.667 \text{ rad/s}
$$
The negative sign indicates a **clockwise** turn (since $\dot{\phi}_L > \dot{\phi}_R$).

**Step 3 — ICR radius** (2 marks)
$$
R_{ICR} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.4}{-0.667} = -0.6 \text{ m}
$$
The negative sign means the ICR lies on the **left** side of the robot (consistent with turning left because the right wheel is slower).

**Step 4 — Direction verification** (1 mark)
- Left wheel faster → robot turns **left** (counter-clockwise from above) ✓
- Magnitude of ICR: $0.6$ m ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **five basic types of wheels** used in mobile robotics. For each, write the **rolling-without-slipping constraint** equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **degrees of mobility** $\delta_m$, **degrees of steerability** $\delta_s$, and **degrees of maneuverability** $M$ for: (i) a differential drive robot, (ii) an Ackermann-steered car. Justify your answer using the constraint matrices. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Wheel Types and Constraints (7 marks):**

| # | Wheel Type | $\alpha$ | $\beta$ | Constraint Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fixed Standard Wheel** | varies | $0$ | $\sin(\alpha+\beta)\dot{x}_I - \cos(\alpha+\beta)\dot{y}_I - L\cos\beta\,\dot{\theta} + r\dot{\phi} = 0$ |
| 2 | **Steerable Standard Wheel** | fixed | varies | Same form as above, but $\beta$ is a control input |
| 3 | **Castor Wheel** | $0$ | varies | Reduces to $-\dot{y}_I + L\dot{\theta} + r\dot{\phi} = 0$ |
| 4 | **Swedish Wheel** (3 sub-rollers) | varies | $0$ | Two constraints: rolling + lateral passive roller |
| 5 | **Mecanum / Ball Wheel** | — | — | Roller axes at 45°; allow holonomic motion |

**Award distribution (7 marks):**
- (2 marks) — Sketch / diagram of all five wheel types.
- (3 marks) — Constraint equation for fixed standard + steerable standard wheels.
- (2 marks) — Brief note on Swedish / Mecanum allowing holonomic motion.

---

**Part (b) — Mobility / Steerability / Maneuverability (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Recall definitions** (1 mark)
- $\delta_m$ = number of instantaneously controllable DOF (rank of $\dot{q}$ matrix).
- $\delta_s$ = number of independently steerable wheels.
- $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$.

**(i) Differential Drive Robot:** (3 marks)
- Two fixed standard wheels on a single axle, plus one or two castor wheels.
- Each fixed wheel contributes 1 constraint: $A(\xi)\dot{\xi} = 0$.
- Two fixed wheels → 2 constraints, but one is dependent on the other.
- $\delta_m = 3 - 1 = 2$.
- $\delta_s = 0$ (no steering wheels).
- $M = 2 + 0 = 2$.

**(ii) Ackermann Car:** (3 marks)
- One steering axis (front wheels, $\delta_s = 1$) + one driving axis.
- Only 1 independent constraint (the car can only move along its heading).
- $\delta_m = 3 - 1 = 2$? — **Correction**: For a car, $\delta_m = 1$ because the steering angle is *fixed* at any instant for a given control input. Following Campion's classification:
  - $\delta_m = 1$ (can only drive forward/backward in heading direction)
  - $\delta_s = 1$ (one steerable wheel set)
  - $M = 1 + 1 = 2$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign conventions** in $\omega$ and ICR radius — confused students write $|v/\omega|$ instead of preserving the sign that indicates turn direction. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Units** — forgetting to convert $r$ from cm to m, or reporting $\omega$ in rpm instead of rad/s. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Mixing frames** — writing $v\cos\theta$ for the *linear velocity in world frame* vs. $v$ in the *body frame* without clarifying. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 4. **Non-holonomic vs holonomic confusion** — answering "the car has 2 DOF" without explaining the constraint reduces controllable, not accessible, DOF. **(−2 marks)**
> 5. **Skipping the rotation matrix** — directly jumping to $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ without showing $R(\theta)$ multiplication. **(−2 marks)**
> 6. **No sketch** — in 7-mark questions on wheel types, a missing diagram is an automatic **(−2 marks)** deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

- ✅ **Posture** $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$ — the *complete state* of a 2D mobile robot.
- ✅ **Forward kinematics**: $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$.
- ✅ **Rotation matrix** $R(\theta) \in SO(2)$ is orthogonal, $\det = 1$, inverse equals transpose.
- ✅ **Homogeneous transform** $T(\xi) \in SE(2)$ combines rotation + translation.
- ✅ **Non-holonomic constraint** for a car: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ — **cannot** be integrated.
- ✅ **Holonomic constraint**: $f(x, y, \theta) = 0$ — *reduces* accessible DOF.
- ✅ **Differential drive formulas**:
  - $v = r(\dot{\phi}_L + \dot{\phi}_R)/2$
  - $\omega = r(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)/L$
  - $R_{ICR} = v/\omega$
- ✅ **Five wheel types**: fixed standard, steerable standard, castor, Swedish, Mecanum.
- ✅ **Mobility / Steerability / Maneuverability**: $M = \delta_m + \delta_s \in [0, 5]$.
- ✅ **Always state units**: $x, y$ in **m**, $\theta$ in **rad**, $\omega$ in **rad/s**, $v$ in **m/s**.
- ✅ **Always preserve signs** in $\omega$ and ICR — they encode turn direction.
- ✅ **Always show the rotation matrix** in derivations — never skip the $R(\theta)$ step.
- ✅ **Always draw a sketch** in 7-mark wheel-type questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line mantra for the exam hall:**
> *"Posture is three numbers. Velocity is a twist. A non-holonomic constraint is a velocity rule. Holonomic means integrable; non-holonomic means not."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forward Kinematics (FK)** in mobile robotics is the mathematical process of determining the **pose** (position + orientation) of a mobile robot in the **World Frame** $\mathcal{F}_W$, given the **control inputs** applied to its actuators — typically the **angular velocities of its driven wheels** ($\omega_1, \omega_2, \dots, \omega_n$).

The pose of a planar mobile robot is conventionally represented as the 3-element state vector:

$$p = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

where $(x, y)$ is the location of the **robot reference point** (typically the midpoint of the rear axle or the geometric center) in $\mathcal{F}_W$, and $\theta$ is the heading angle measured **counter-clockwise from the global X-axis**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** In Module 3, Forward Kinematics is treated for the three canonical mobile robot platforms:
> 1. **Differential Drive** (two independently driven wheels + casters)
> 2. **Ackermann / Car-like Drive** (single steering + single drive)
> 3. **Unicycle / Bicycle Model** (simplified abstractions)
> The **Inverse Kinematics** (given desired body velocity, find wheel velocities) is also implicitly covered.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Cricket Umpire" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are a **blind cricket umpire** standing on the pitch. You cannot see the batsman, but you can *feel* the ball's spin as it leaves the bowler's hand. From the **spin** (a property you can sense directly), you must predict **where the ball will land and how it will deviate in the air**. The spin is the *control input*; the trajectory is the *pose*.
>
> **Forward Kinematics does exactly this for a robot:** you know the **wheel speeds** (analogous to spin), and you compute the **future position and heading** of the robot (analogous to ball trajectory). No camera, no GPS — just pure geometry + integration of the kinematic equations.

A simpler way to think:

| Concept | Human Analogy | Robot Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Wheel velocities (rad/s) | Throttle + steering wheel | Control input $u = [v, \omega]^T$ |
| Robot frame $\mathcal{F}_R$ | "My body's forward direction" | Body-fixed $X_R$ axis |
| World frame $\mathcal{F}_W$ | "Magnetic North on a map" | Inertial $X_W$ axis |
| Pose $(x, y, \theta)$ | GPS coordinates + compass | State vector $p$ |

---

## 1.3 Key Physical & Geometric Quantities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Constants & Symbols** (use throughout):

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $r$ | Wheel radius | **m (meters)** |
| $b$ or $L$ | Wheelbase (distance between axles / wheels) | **m (meters)** |
| $d$ | Half-axle length (differential drive) | **m (meters)** |
| $\omega_L, \omega_R$ | Left / Right wheel angular velocities | **rad/s** |
| $v_L, v_R$ | Left / Right wheel linear velocities | **m/s** |
| $v$ | Robot's linear (forward) velocity | **m/s** |
| $\omega$ | Robot's angular velocity (yaw rate) | **rad/s** |
| $\delta$ | Steering angle (Ackermann) | **rad** |
| $R$ | Instantaneous radius of curvature | **m (meters)** |
| $ICC$ | Instantaneous Center of Curvature | point in plane |
| $\theta$ | Heading angle w.r.t. global X-axis | **rad** |
| $\Delta t$ | Sampling / control period | **s (seconds)** |

---

## 1.4 Why Forward Kinematics is the Foundation of Everything Else

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> - **Localization:** Dead reckoning relies entirely on FK to estimate pose from wheel encoders.
> - **Path Tracking:** Pure-pursuit, Stanley controller all need FK pose.
> - **Obstacle Avoidance:** Predict future position to check for collisions.
> - **SLAM:** Predicted motion (via FK) is compared with sensor measurements to build the map.
> - **Formation Control:** Each robot in a swarm needs FK to share its state with neighbours.

Without a correct forward kinematic model, **every higher-level mobile robot algorithm is built on sand**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Coordinate Frames You Must Master

> [!NOTE]
> **Frame Hierarchy in Mobile Robotics:**

1. **World Frame $\mathcal{F}_W = \{X_W, Y_W\}$** — Fixed to the environment (the "map").
2. **Robot Frame $\mathcal{F}_R = \{X_R, Y_R\}$** — Body-fixed, $X_R$ points "forward" along the robot's longitudinal axis, $Y_R$ points "left" of the robot.
3. **Wheel Frame $\mathcal{F}_i$** — Attached to each driven wheel $i$, with axis along its rolling direction.

The forward kinematic equation is fundamentally a **velocity transformation** from the wheel space to the world space:

$$\dot{p}_{\mathcal{W}} = R(\theta) \cdot \dot{p}_{\mathcal{R}}$$

where $R(\theta)$ is the **2D rotation matrix** that maps robot-frame velocities to world-frame velocities.

---

## 2.2 The Robot Jacobian: The Heart of Forward Kinematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **Jacobian Matrix** $J(\theta)$ linearly maps the **body velocity** $u = [v, \omega]^T$ to the **world velocity** $\dot{p} = [\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^T$.

For a planar (2D) mobile robot, the **kinematic equation** is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = J(\theta) \cdot u$$

**Why this matrix matters:**
- The **first column** (for $v$) tells you: "if the robot moves forward, the world-frame $\dot{x}$ is $\cos\theta$ and $\dot{y}$ is $\sin\theta$" — the classic $v\cos\theta$, $v\sin\theta$ projection.
- The **second column** (for $\omega$) tells you: "rotation only changes heading $\theta$, not position in a single instant" — hence the zeros in $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$.
- This matrix is a **non-integrable (non-holonomic)** system because $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ identically, encoding the **no-sideways-slip** constraint.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **⭐ HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — memorize these for KTU 2024 exams ⭐**

### (A) Differential Drive Robot

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_L = r\,\omega_L$,  $v_R = r\,\omega_R$ | Wheel linear speeds from motor speeds |
| 2 | $v = \dfrac{r}{2}\,(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ | Robot's forward velocity (mean of both) |
| 3 | $\omega = \dfrac{r}{2d}\,(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | Robot's angular velocity (yaw rate) |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{v}{\omega} = \dfrac{d\,(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$ | Instantaneous radius of curvature (ICR distance) |
| 5 | $ICC = (x - R\sin\theta,\; y + R\cos\theta)$ | Coordinates of the Instantaneous Center of Curvature |

### (B) Ackermann / Car-Like Drive

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{L}{\tan\delta}$ | Radius of the turning arc (Ackermann geometry) |
| 7 | $\omega = \dfrac{v}{R} = \dfrac{v\tan\delta}{L}$ | Yaw rate from steering angle |
| 8 | $v = r\,\omega_{drive}$ | Forward speed (single driven wheel) |
| 9 | $\delta_{\min} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L}{R_{\max}}\right)$ | Minimum steering angle to negotiate a turn of radius $R_{\max}$ |

### (C) Unicycle Model (single abstract wheel)

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ | World X velocity |
| 11 | $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ | World Y velocity |
| 12 | $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Yaw rate |

### (D) Bicycle Model (car abstraction)

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 13 | $\dot{x} = v\cos(\theta + \beta)$ | Front-axle velocity in world |
| 14 | $\dot{y} = v\sin(\theta + \beta)$ | Front-axle velocity in world |
| 15 | $\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{v}{L}\sin\beta$ | Yaw rate; $\beta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L_r\tan\delta}{L}\right)$ (slip angle) |

### (E) Pose Update (Discrete Integration via Euler Method)

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 16 | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t$ | Discrete X position |
| 17 | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k\sin\theta_k\,\Delta t$ | Discrete Y position |
| 18 | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k\,\Delta t$ | Discrete heading |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU NOTE:** For a **circular arc trajectory** (exact analytical solution, used by KTU examiners for "show that" type questions):
>
> $$x_{k+1} = x_k + R\bigl[\sin(\theta_k + \omega\Delta t) - \sin\theta_k\bigr]$$
>
> $$y_{k+1} = y_k - R\bigl[\cos(\theta_k + \omega\Delta t) - \cos\theta_k\bigr]$$
>
> $$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega\Delta t$$
>
> where $R = v / \omega$. This is **exact** (no Euler error) and KTU loves to ask students to derive this!

### (F) Homogeneous Transformation (2D)

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 19 | $T(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Pure rotation about Z-axis (2D) |
| 20 | $T(x,y) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & x \\ 0 & 1 & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Pure translation in 2D |
| 21 | $T = T_{\text{trans}} \cdot T_{\text{rot}}$ | Pose of robot w.r.t. world |

---

## 2.4 The Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC) — Deep Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Why does a differential drive robot always rotate about a *single* point at every instant?**
> Because of the **rolling-without-slipping** constraint: every wheel's velocity vector must be **perpendicular to the line connecting it to the ICC**. With only 2 driven wheels on a rigid axle, the two perpendiculars intersect at exactly **one** point — that point is the ICC. The robot's body sweeps a circular arc about this point.

Special cases:
- If $\omega_R = \omega_L$: $R \to \infty$ ⇒ **straight-line motion** (ICC at infinity).
- If $\omega_R = -\omega_L$: $R = 0$ ⇒ **rotation in place** (ICC at the robot center).
- If $\omega_L = 0$: **pivot turn** about the left wheel — radius equals $2d$ (the full wheel separation).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of FK Model |
|---|---|
| **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicles)** in warehouses | Estimating pose from wheel encoders for path following |
| **Autonomous Cars (Waymo, Tesla)** | Bicycle model is the industry standard for trajectory planning |
| **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance)** | Differential drive + rocker-bogie FK; pose used for science pointing |
| **Service Robots (Roomba)** | Differential drive FK for room coverage algorithms |
| **Drone Swarms (with ground robots)** | FK is shared via ROS topics `/odom` for cooperative behavior |
| **Disaster Response Robots (DARPA SubT)** | FK pose combined with LiDAR for SLAM in GPS-denied environments |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive derivations follow. NO step is skipped. Every transition is shown explicitly.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Differential Drive Forward Kinematics (from first principles)

### Setup

Consider a **differential drive** mobile robot with:
- Two identical driven wheels of radius $r$, separated by a distance $2d$ (so each wheel is at $\pm d$ along the robot's lateral $Y_R$ axis).
- One or more passive caster wheels (assumed massless, no kinematic effect).
- A **body-fixed frame** $\mathcal{F}_R$ with origin at the **center of the axle** between the two driven wheels.
- A **world frame** $\mathcal{F}_W$ with origin at $(0,0)$.

Let the robot's pose in $\mathcal{F}_W$ be $p = (x, y, \theta)$, where $\theta$ is the angle from $X_W$ to $X_R$, measured counter-clockwise.

### Step 1 — Write the rolling-without-slipping constraint

Each wheel's contact point on the ground has **zero velocity**. The wheel center moves with linear speed $v_i$ along $X_R$, and the wheel rotates with angular speed $\omega_i$:

$$v_i = r\,\omega_i, \quad i \in \{L, R\}$$

### Step 2 — Express the body-frame linear and angular velocity in terms of the wheels

The robot is rigid. Therefore the **forward velocity** $v$ of the robot center is the **average** of the two wheel speeds (since the center is the midpoint of the axle):

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$

The **angular velocity** $\omega$ about the body Z-axis is found from the relative motion of the two wheels. The right wheel is at lateral position $+d$, so its forward velocity is $v + d\omega$; equating with $v_R$:

$$v_R = v + d\omega$$

Similarly, the left wheel is at $-d$:

$$v_L = v - d\omega$$

### Step 3 — Solve the system for $v$ and $\omega$

Add the two equations from Step 2:

$$v_R + v_L = 2v \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$

Subtract the left from the right:

$$v_R - v_L = 2d\omega \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{2d} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{2d}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These are the TWO master equations of differential drive FK.** Memorize them.

### Step 4 — Project body velocity into the world frame

The robot's **body** has velocity components $(v, 0, \omega)$ in $\mathcal{F}_R$ — i.e., it moves forward at $v$ along $X_R$ and rotates at $\omega$ about $Z_R$. To express this in the world frame, we apply the 2D rotation matrix $R(\theta)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

The angular velocity is invariant under frame change (it is a *pseudoscalar* in 2D):

$$\dot{\theta} = \omega$$

### Step 5 — Final vector form

$$\boxed{\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\ \dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \dfrac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\ \dfrac{r}{2d} & -\dfrac{r}{2d} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_R \\ \omega_L \end{bmatrix}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This $3 \times 2$ matrix is called the **Configuration Jacobian** $J_c$. The fact that it is **tall** ($3 > 2$) reflects that the differential drive is **underactuated** — 2 control inputs cannot independently control all 3 pose variables instantaneously. Motion is constrained to the **no-sideways-slip manifold**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Ackermann Steering Forward Kinematics

### Setup

A car-like robot has:
- **Wheelbase** $L$ (distance between front and rear axles).
- **Steering angle** $\delta$ at the front wheels.
- **Forward velocity** $v$ at the rear (driven) wheels.
- Origin of $\mathcal{F}_R$ at the **rear-axle midpoint**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)

When the car turns, the **rear wheels** roll on a circle of radius $R$ centered at the ICC. By Ackermann geometry, the **front wheels** roll on the *same* circle (with the inner wheel turned sharper than the outer wheel, for true Ackermann steering).

From the right triangle formed by the rear axle, the ICC, and the front axle:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{L}{R}$$

Therefore:

$$R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the yaw rate

The angular velocity of the car is:

$$\omega = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{v \tan\delta}{L}$$

### Step 3 — Write the body velocity in world frame

The rear axle midpoint (origin of $\mathcal{F}_R$) moves at speed $v$ in direction $\theta$ (the heading):

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \\ \dfrac{v\tan\delta}{L} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Equivalent control matrix

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & \dfrac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ v\tan\delta \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Ackermann's two control inputs are $v$ and $\delta$ (NOT $v$ and $\omega$). The mapping from $u = [v, \delta]^T$ to $\dot p$ is **nonlinear** (because of $\tan\delta$ and the $\cos\theta$, $\sin\theta$ terms). To use linear control theory, one often uses the equivalent unicycle inputs $u' = [v, \omega]^T$ with $\omega = v\tan\delta / L$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Pose Update (Euler Integration)

> [!NOTE]
> **This derivation shows how a digital controller uses the FK model to advance the pose from time $t_k$ to $t_{k+1} = t_k + \Delta t$.**

### Step 1 — Discretize the continuous-time FK

Given the differential equations $\dot p = f(p, u)$, the simplest discrete approximation is the **Forward Euler Method**:

$$p_{k+1} \approx p_k + f(p_k, u_k)\,\Delta t$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the unicycle FK equations

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$$
$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$$
$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t$$

### Step 3 — Write compactly in vector form

$$p_{k+1} = p_k + \begin{bmatrix} v_k \cos\theta_k \\ v_k \sin\theta_k \\ \omega_k \end{bmatrix} \Delta t$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning:** Euler integration is **first-order accurate**. For accurate path tracking, KTU often asks for the **exact circular-arc update** (next derivation). Don't claim Euler is "exact" — it is not.

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4 — Exact Circular-Arc Pose Update (HIGH-YIELD ⭐)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the derivation KTU examiners love. Practice it thoroughly.**

### Step 1 — Realize the path is a circular arc

If $v$ and $\omega$ are held constant over $[t_k, t_{k+1}]$ with $\omega \neq 0$, the robot traces a **circular arc** of radius $R = v/\omega$ and angle $\Delta\theta = \omega\Delta t$ about the ICC at $p_{ICC} = (x_c, y_c)$.

### Step 2 — Find the ICC coordinates

From the **kinematic relation**, the ICC is located at a perpendicular distance $R$ from the robot's heading direction, **on the left** of the robot (for $\omega > 0$):

$$x_c = x_k - R\sin\theta_k$$
$$y_c = y_k + R\cos\theta_k$$

### Step 3 — Express the new pose via rigid rotation

The new pose $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1})$ is the result of rotating the vector $\vec{ICC \to p_k}$ by angle $\Delta\theta$ counter-clockwise, then re-attaching it to the ICC.

Rotation matrix in 2D:

$$R_{\Delta\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\Delta\theta & -\sin\Delta\theta \\ \sin\Delta\theta & \cos\Delta\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Write the rotated vector

$$\begin{bmatrix} x_{k+1} - x_c \\ y_{k+1} - y_c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\Delta\theta & -\sin\Delta\theta \\ \sin\Delta\theta & \cos\Delta\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_k - x_c \\ y_k - y_c \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $x_c, y_c$

$$x_{k+1} = x_c + (x_k - x_c)\cos\Delta\theta - (y_k - y_c)\sin\Delta\theta$$
$$y_{k+1} = y_c + (x_k - x_c)\sin\Delta\theta + (y_k - y_c)\cos\Delta\theta$$

### Step 6 — Simplify

Using $x_k - x_c = R\sin\theta_k$ and $y_k - y_c = -R\cos\theta_k$:

$$x_{k+1} = x_c + R\sin\theta_k\cos\Delta\theta + R\cos\theta_k\sin\Delta\theta$$
$$y_{k+1} = y_c + R\sin\theta_k\sin\Delta\theta - R\cos\theta_k\cos\Delta\theta$$

### Step 7 — Apply the angle addition formula

$\sin(A + B) = \sin A\cos B + \cos A\sin B$ and $\cos(A + B) = \cos A\cos B - \sin A\sin B$:

$$x_{k+1} = x_c + R\sin(\theta_k + \Delta\theta)$$
$$y_{k+1} = y_c - R\cos(\theta_k + \Delta\theta)$$

### Step 8 — Substitute $x_c, y_c, R = v/\omega$ back

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned} x_{k+1} &= x_k + \frac{v}{\omega}\bigl[\sin(\theta_k + \omega\Delta t) - \sin\theta_k\bigr] \\ y_{k+1} &= y_k - \frac{v}{\omega}\bigl[\cos(\theta_k + \omega\Delta t) - \cos\theta_k\bigr] \\ \theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega\Delta t \end{aligned}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** Set $\omega = 0$ and apply the L'Hôpital limit (or Taylor-expand). You should recover the **straight-line** Euler update. The exact arc update has **zero discretization error in pose** for constant $v, \omega$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A differential drive robot has $r = 0.05\,\text{m}$, $d = 0.15\,\text{m}$. Starting from pose $(0, 0, 0)$, it is given wheel speeds $\omega_R = 10\,\text{rad/s}$ and $\omega_L = 6\,\text{rad/s}$ for $\Delta t = 0.1\,\text{s}$. Compute the new pose using (a) Euler and (b) exact arc.

### (a) Euler Method

Compute $v$ and $\omega$:

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) = \frac{0.05}{2}(10 + 6) = 0.4\,\text{m/s}$$

$$\omega = \frac{r}{2d}(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \frac{0.05}{2 \cdot 0.15}(10 - 6) = 0.6\overline{6}\,\text{rad/s}$$

Apply Euler:

$$x_1 = 0 + 0.4\cos(0)\cdot 0.1 = 0.04\,\text{m}$$
$$y_1 = 0 + 0.4\sin(0)\cdot 0.1 = 0.0\,\text{m}$$
$$\theta_1 = 0 + 0.6\overline{6}\cdot 0.1 = 0.06\overline{6}\,\text{rad}$$

### (b) Exact Arc Method

Compute $R = v/\omega = 0.4 / 0.6\overline{6} = 0.6\,\text{m}$ and $\Delta\theta = 0.6\overline{6}\cdot 0.1 = 0.06\overline{6}\,\text{rad}$.

Apply exact formula:

$$x_1 = 0 + 0.6\,[\sin(0.06\overline{6}) - \sin(0)] = 0.6 \cdot 0.0666 = 0.04\,\text{m}$$

$$y_1 = 0 - 0.6\,[\cos(0.06\overline{6}) - \cos(0)] = -0.6 \cdot (-0.00222) = 0.00133\,\text{m}$$

$$\theta_1 = 0 + 0.06\overline{6} = 0.06\overline{6}\,\text{rad}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The $y$-displacement from Euler is **0** (since $\sin 0 = 0$), but the **exact** update gives a small but non-zero $y$. This is the error introduced by linearization. KTU questions sometimes ask you to compare both and explain the discrepancy.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on diagrams:** Mobile robot physical geometry is best represented here as **Mermaid block-architecture flow diagrams** showing the chain of mathematical transformations from wheel input to world-frame pose, plus a schematic of the two main drivetrains.

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Forward Kinematics Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Wheel Inputs\n(omega_L, omega_R or delta, v_drive)"] --> B["Wheel to Body\nTransformation\n(v = r/2 (omegaR+omegaL))"]
    B --> C["Body Velocity Vector\nu = v , omega"]
    C --> D["Jacobian Matrix\nJ(theta) = cos theta, 0; sin theta, 0; 0, 1"]
    D --> E["World Frame Velocity\np_dot = x_dot, y_dot, theta_dot"]
    E --> F["Integrator\n(Forward Euler or Exact Arc)"]
    F --> G["Updated Pose\n(x_k+1, y_k+1, theta_k+1)"]
    G --> H["Feedback to Controller"]
    H --> A
```
