## 4.4 Hierarchical Flowchart — Mobile Robot Kinematic Models

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP["MOBILE ROBOT FORWARD KINEMATIC MODELS"]
    TOP --> M1["Differential Drive\n(two driven wheels + casters)"]
    TOP --> M2["Ackermann Drive\n(car-like, single steer + drive)"]
    TOP --> M3["Omnidirectional\n(Mecanum / Swedish wheels)"]
    TOP --> M4["Legged Robots\n(centroidal dynamics)"]
    M1 --> F1["v = r/2 omegaR + omegaL\nomega = r/2d omegaR - omegaL"]
    M2 --> F2["omega = v tan delta / L\nR = L / tan delta"]
    M3 --> F3["3 or 4 wheel linear combination\nx_dot, y_dot, theta_dot all independent"]
    M4 --> F4["Beyond FK scope of KTU Module 3"]
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram — Pose Update Loop (Discrete Time)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["k-th step\n(x_k, y_k, theta_k, u_k)"] --> S2["FK Model\ndot p = J theta u"]
    S2 --> S3["Integrator\n(Exact Arc or Euler)"]
    S3 --> S4["k+1-th step\n(x_k+1, y_k+1, theta_k+1)"]
    S4 --> S5["Sensors / Encoders"]
    S5 --> S6["State Estimator\n(Filter: EKF / UKF)"]
    S6 --> S1
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in the exam:** A well-drawn block diagram in the answer sheet, even in pencil, can fetch 1–2 extra marks from the KTU examiner, especially on a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **The questions below follow the exact KTU 2024 scheme pattern:**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (short answer)
> - **Part B:** Choice-based, 14 marks (sub-parts a and b, 7 marks each)

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`
**Define the forward kinematic model of a mobile robot. With a neat sketch, derive the forward kinematic equations for a differential drive mobile robot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Forward kinematics in mobile robotics is the mathematical mapping from the **wheel angular velocities** $(\omega_L, \omega_R)$ to the **robot's pose rate** $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$ in the world frame, expressed as $\dot{p} = J(\theta)\,u$.

**Key equations (1.5 marks):**

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L), \quad \omega = \frac{r}{2d}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

**Sketch (0.5 marks):** A top view showing the two driven wheels, axle length $2d$, robot frame, and the ICC at distance $R$ on the perpendicular to $X_R$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`
**What is the Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)? Show that for an Ackermann-steered vehicle, the radius of the turning circle is $R = L/\tan\delta$, where $L$ is the wheelbase and $\delta$ is the steering angle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The ICC is the unique point about which a rigid body undergoing planar motion instantaneously rotates. For a car turning at constant $\delta$, the rear axle and front axle both rotate about this single point.

**Derivation (2 marks):** In the Ackermann geometry, draw the right triangle with:
- One vertex at the rear-axle midpoint (current robot center).
- One vertex at the ICC.
- One vertex at the front-axle midpoint.

The side opposite to $\delta$ is $L$ (wheelbase), and the side opposite to the right angle is $R$ (turn radius). By definition of the tangent in a right triangle:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{L}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Choice Questions (ESE Module Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** "Answer **either** (a) OR (b) from the following module. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**."

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A | Module 3]`

**(a)** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.04\,\text{m}$ and the two driven wheels are separated by $2d = 0.30\,\text{m}$. The robot starts at the origin with heading $\theta_0 = 0$. At time $t = 0$, the wheel speeds are set to $\omega_R = 12\,\text{rad/s}$ and $\omega_L = 8\,\text{rad/s}$ and held constant for $\Delta t = 0.5\,\text{s}$. Using the **exact circular arc** integration, compute the final pose $(x_1, y_1, \theta_1)$. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $v$ and $\omega$ (2 marks):**

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) = \frac{0.04}{2}(12 + 8) = \frac{0.04 \times 20}{2} = 0.4\,\text{m/s}$$

$$\omega = \frac{r}{2d}(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \frac{0.04}{0.30}(12 - 8) = \frac{0.04 \times 4}{0.30} = \frac{0.16}{0.30} = 0.5\overline{3}\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute radius and angle (1 mark):**

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.4}{0.5\overline{3}} = 0.75\,\text{m}$$

$$\Delta\theta = \omega\Delta t = 0.5\overline{3} \times 0.5 = 0.2\overline{6}\,\text{rad}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the exact arc formula (3 marks):**

$$x_1 = x_0 + \frac{v}{\omega}\bigl[\sin(\theta_0 + \omega\Delta t) - \sin\theta_0\bigr]$$

$$x_1 = 0 + 0.75\bigl[\sin(0.2\overline{6}) - \sin(0)\bigr] = 0.75 \times \sin(0.2\overline{6})$$

$$x_1 = 0.75 \times 0.2647 = 0.1985\,\text{m}$$

$$y_1 = y_0 - \frac{v}{\omega}\bigl[\cos(\theta_0 + \omega\Delta t) - \cos\theta_0\bigr]$$

$$y_1 = 0 - 0.75\bigl[\cos(0.2\overline{6}) - 1\bigr] = -0.75 \times (-0.0352) = 0.0264\,\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Final heading (1 mark):**

$$\theta_1 = \theta_0 + \omega\Delta t = 0 + 0.2\overline{6} = 0.2\overline{6}\,\text{rad} \approx 15.36^\circ$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**

$$\boxed{(x_1, y_1, \theta_1) \approx (0.1985\,\text{m},\; 0.0264\,\text{m},\; 0.2667\,\text{rad})}$$

---

**(b)** Derive the forward kinematic equations for an **Ackermann-steered (car-like) mobile robot** with wheelbase $L$ and steering angle $\delta$, starting from first principles. Use your derivation to show that the **yaw rate** is $\omega = (v\tan\delta)/L$, where $v$ is the longitudinal velocity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Ackermann geometry setup (2 marks):**
Draw a top-view schematic. The robot has:
- A rear-axle midpoint (origin of body frame $\mathcal{F}_R$).
- A front-axle midpoint at distance $L$ along $X_R$.
- Front wheel turned by $\delta$ relative to $X_R$.

The two axles are extended as lines, intersecting at the **ICC**. The right triangle formed has:
- Hypotenuse = $R$ (distance from robot center to ICC).
- Adjacent (along $X_R$) = $L$ (wheelbase).
- Opposite (along the perpendicular at the front axle, on which the turned wheel points) = $L\tan\delta$.

**Step 2 — Identify the relationship (2 marks):**
By the definition of the tangent function in the right triangle, with the steering angle at the front wheel:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{L}{R}$$

Solving for $R$:

$$R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta}$$

**Step 3 — Compute yaw rate (2 marks):**
The linear speed of the rear axle midpoint is $v$. By the rigid-body rotation about ICC, this linear speed equals $R\omega$ (arc-length rate):

$$v = R\omega \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{v\tan\delta}{L}$$

**Step 4 — State the full kinematic equation (1 mark):**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & \dfrac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ v\tan\delta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\theta \\ v\sin\theta \\ \dfrac{v\tan\delta}{L} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Q.E.D. (boxed).**

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary | Module 3]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Unicycle Model** of a mobile robot. Derive the **Jacobian matrix** that maps the body-frame velocity $(v, \omega)$ to the world-frame velocity $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$. Mention one physical constraint this model encodes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Unicycle model description (2 marks):**
The **unicycle** is a conceptual single-wheel robot used as a kinematic abstraction. It has:
- A single point of contact with the ground.
- Two control inputs: forward speed $v$ and angular speed $\omega$ (steering).
- No lateral motion (it cannot "slide" sideways).

The state is the pose $(x, y, \theta)$ in the world frame, with $\theta$ being the heading.

**Step 2 — Kinematic equations (2 marks):**
The body frame velocity vector in $\mathcal{F}_R$ is $\dot p_{\mathcal{R}} = [v,\, 0,\, \omega]^T$ (lateral velocity is zero). Applying the rotation $R(\theta)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Jacobian (2 marks):**
The Jacobian $J(\theta)$ is the $3 \times 2$ matrix multiplying the control vector:

$$\boxed{J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

This matrix has rank 2 for all $\theta$, meaning the system is underactuated (3 pose variables, 2 inputs).

**Step 4 — The non-holonomic constraint (1 mark):**
The unicycle's body cannot translate sideways. In world frame, this is:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

This is a **non-integrable** (Pfaffian) constraint, making the unicycle a **non-holonomic** system — it can reach any $(x, y, \theta)$ but not via arbitrary straight-line paths.

---

**(b)** Starting from the unicycle model, **discretize the kinematic equations** using the **Forward Euler Method** and the **Exact Circular Arc Method**. Show that the Euler method is a **first-order approximation** of the arc method. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Continuous unicycle FK (1 mark):**

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$

**Step 2 — Forward Euler discretization (2 marks):**
With $p_{k+1} = p_k + \dot p_k\,\Delta t$:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t$$
$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k\sin\theta_k\,\Delta t$$
$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k\,\Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Exact circular arc discretization (2 marks):**
The robot moves on a circle of radius $R = v/\omega$ about the ICC, sweeping angle $\Delta\theta = \omega\Delta t$:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + \frac{v}{\omega}\bigl[\sin(\theta_k + \omega\Delta t) - \sin\theta_k\bigr]$$
$$y_{k+1} = y_k - \frac{v}{\omega}\bigl[\cos(\theta_k + \omega\Delta t) - \cos\theta_k\bigr]$$
$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega\Delta t$$

**Step 4 — Show Euler is first-order accurate (2 marks):**
Apply the Taylor expansion $\sin(\theta_k + \omega\Delta t) = \sin\theta_k + \omega\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t - \frac{\omega^2}{2}\sin\theta_k\,\Delta t^2 + \mathcal{O}(\Delta t^3)$:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + \frac{v}{\omega}\bigl[\omega\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t - \frac{\omega^2}{2}\sin\theta_k\,\Delta t^2 + \mathcal{O}(\Delta t^3)\bigr]$$

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t - \frac{v\omega}{2}\sin\theta_k\,\Delta t^2 + \mathcal{O}(\Delta t^3)$$

The leading term matches the Euler update. The next term (proportional to $\Delta t^2$) is the **local truncation error** — thus Euler is **first-order accurate** ($\mathcal{O}(\Delta t)$). The exact arc update is **zero-order** (no error for constant inputs).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ COMMON MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES — KTU 2024 ESE / Module 3 Forward Kinematics:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in $\omega$ formula:** Many students write $\omega = \frac{r}{2d}(\omega_L - \omega_R)$. The correct form is $\omega = \frac{r}{2d}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$. One sign flip = **2–3 marks lost**.
>
> 2. **Confusing wheelbase and axle separation:** In **differential drive**, the relevant length is $2d$ (between the two driven wheels). In **Ackermann**, it is $L$ (front-to-rear axle). Mixing them up = **wrong $R$ and $\omega$**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the $\cos\theta$/$\sin\theta$ projection:** A common blunder is writing $\dot x = v$ and $\dot y = 0$ (forgetting the world-frame rotation). Always apply $R(\theta)$.
>
> 4. **Using Euler for "exact" question:** KTU often asks for **exact arc update**. If you write Euler and the question asked "exact," you lose **5–7 marks**.
>
> 5. **No sketch in derivation questions:** KTU awards 0.5–1 mark for a labelled diagram in 7-mark sub-parts. **Always draw one** — even a rough one.
>
> 6. **Mixing $\theta$ and $\delta$ in Ackermann:** The steering angle is $\delta$; the heading is $\theta$. They are **not the same** — the body's orientation $\theta$ evolves as $\dot\theta = \omega$, while $\delta$ is a control input.
>
> 7. **Confusing Jacobian $J(\theta)$ with Configuration Jacobian $J_c$:** The first maps $(v,\omega) \to (\dot x, \dot y, \dot\theta)$ — a $3\times 2$ matrix. The second maps $(\omega_R, \omega_L) \to (\dot x, \dot y, \dot\theta)$ — also $3\times 2$ but with $r/2$, $r/2d$ entries. State which one you are using!

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **⭐ LAST-MINUTE REVISION — read this 30 minutes before the exam ⭐**

- [x] **Forward Kinematics** = control inputs → pose rate. **Inverse Kinematics** = desired body velocity → wheel speeds.
- [x] **Pose vector** = $(x, y, \theta)$. State space is $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$.
- [x] **Differential drive master equations:** $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$, $\omega = \frac{r}{2d}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$.
- [x] **ICC radius:** $R = v/\omega$ (for any wheeled robot). When $\omega = 0$, $R \to \infty$ (straight line).
- [x] **Ackermann:** $R = L/\tan\delta$ and $\omega = v\tan\delta/L$.
- [x] **Unicycle FK matrix:**
$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$
- [x] **Non-holonomic constraint:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta = \dot{y}\cos\theta$. No sideways motion.
- [x] **Euler update:** $x_{k+1} = x_k + v\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t$, $y_{k+1} = y_k + v\sin\theta_k\,\Delta t$, $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega\Delta t$. First-order accurate.
- [x] **Exact arc update (use for high marks!):**
$x_{k+1} = x_k + (v/\omega)[\sin(\theta_k + \omega\Delta t) - \sin\theta_k]$
$y_{k+1} = y_k - (v/\omega)[\cos(\theta_k + \omega\Delta t) - \cos\theta_k]$
$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega\Delta t$
- [x] **Special cases:** $\omega_L = \omega_R \Rightarrow$ straight line. $\omega_L = -\omega_R \Rightarrow$ rotate in place. $\omega_L = 0 \Rightarrow$ pivot turn about left wheel.
- [x] **Underactuation:** All planar wheeled robots have 2 control inputs and 3 DOF → Jacobian rank 2.
- [x] **Jacobian $J(\theta)$** is state-dependent (depends on $\theta$) — control system is **nonlinear**.
- [x] **Homogeneous transformation** $T(x, y, \theta)$ combines rotation + translation; useful in sensor frame composition.
- [x] **Bicycle model** is a 2-wheel abstraction of a car; **$\beta$ (slip angle)** appears at high speeds.
- [x] **Practical units:** $r, L, d$ in **meters**; $\omega$ in **rad/s**; $v$ in **m/s**; $\theta, \delta$ in **rad** (or convert to deg for some questions).
- [x] **Differential drive advantage:** zero-radius turning. **Disadvantage:** cannot drive straight on uneven terrain (one wheel drops).
- [x] **Ackermann advantage:** mechanically stable, real-car-like. **Disadvantage:** cannot turn in place, $R \geq L/\tan\delta_{\max}$.
- [x] **Remember to draw the diagram** in 7-mark derivations — it can save 1 mark even if your algebra is shaky.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheel Kinematic Constraints for Fixed Standard Wheel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT721 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Wheel Kinematic Constraints for Fixed Standard Wheel # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fixed Standard Wheel:** A wheel mounted on a **rigid, non-steerable axle** with a fixed orientation relative to the robot chassis. The wheel is permitted to **rotate ONLY about its own axle** (no steering, no swiveling). The contact point between the wheel and the ground is kinematically constrained in two ways: **(i) the Rolling Constraint** and **(ii) the Sliding (Lateral) Constraint**. A fixed standard wheel is the classical building block of terrestrial mobile robots — every car wheel, every bicycle wheel, every luggage-trolley wheel falls under this category. Its simplicity makes it the most analytically tractable wheel type in the entire locomotion taxonomy. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Office Chair vs. The Suitcase Imagine pushing an **office chair on rollers**. The rollers swivel freely (castor wheels), so the chair moves effortlessly in *any* direction. Now imagine a **two-wheeled suitcase** being dragged behind you on its fixed inline wheels. The suitcase **resists being pushed sideways** — you can only roll it forward or backward along the orientation of its wheels. That physical resistance to lateral motion IS the **Sliding Constraint** of a fixed standard wheel made tangible. ## 1.3 Geometric Parameters of a Fixed Standard Wheel > [!NOTE] > Every fixed standard wheel on a mobile robot is fully characterized by **four parameters**: | Symbol | Parameter | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | |:------:|:----------|:-----------------|:-------------:| | $\alpha$ | Wheel-rolling orientation angle | Angle between the wheel's rolling direction and the robot's local x-axis | rad or deg | | $r$ | Wheel radius | Distance from axle to ground contact point | **m** | | $x_c, y_c$ | Mounting coordinates | Position of the wheel contact point in the robot frame $\{R\}$ | **m** | | $\dot{\varphi}$ | Wheel angular velocity | Rate of rotation about the wheel's own axle | rad/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > For a **fixed standard wheel**, both $\alpha$ and the axle orientation are **constants** — they do not change with time. This is what distinguishes it from a *steerable* standard wheel (where an additional steering angle $\phi(t)$ exists). ## 1.4 The Two Fundamental Constraints — A Bird's-Eye View A fixed standard wheel rolling on a flat, rigid ground without slipping must obey: 1. **Rolling Constraint (Holonomic in disguise):** The component of the wheel-center velocity *along* the wheel's plane must equal $r\dot{\varphi}$. This ties the wheel's rotational speed to the robot's translational motion. 2. **Sliding Constraint (Genuinely Non-Holonomic):** The component of the wheel-center velocity *perpendicular* to the wheel's plane must equal **zero**. This is the constraint that **restricts the robot's instantaneous maneuverability** and is the source of the well-known non-holonomic behaviour of wheeled robots. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Memory Aid:** *Rolling = "go with the flow" along the wheel plane. Sliding = "fight against" sideways slip. The wheel can only do one — it must roll, not slide.* --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Coordinate Frame Setup To derive the constraints rigorously, we need two reference frames: - **Inertial Frame $\{I\}$** — fixed to the ground, with origin at some world point. The robot's pose here is $\xi_I = [x, y, \theta]^T$. - **Robot Frame $\{R\}$** — rigidly attached to the chassis, origin at point $P$ (typically the center of the wheelbase). The robot's velocity in this frame is $\dot{\xi}_R = [\dot{x}_R, \dot{y}_R, \dot{\theta}]^T$. The **velocity transformation** between frames is: $$ \dot{\xi}_I = R(\theta)\,\dot{\xi}_R $$ where the planar rotation matrix is: $$ R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ The inverse mapping — needed to express the constraint in the inertial frame — is simply $R(\theta)^T$: $$ \dot{x}_R = \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta $$ $$ \dot{y}_R = -\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta $$ ## 2.2 Velocity of the Wheel-Center Point — The Rigid-Body Step For a wheel whose contact point sits at coordinates $(x_c, y_c)$ in the robot frame, the **velocity of that point** (relative to $P$) under chassis rotation $\dot{\theta}$ is obtained from the rigid-body velocity-transfer formula: $$ \vec{v}_{Wc} = \vec{v}_P + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P \to Wc} $$ In 2D scalar form, with $\vec{\omega} = [0, 0, \dot{\theta}]^T$ and $\vec{r}_{P \to Wc} = [x_c, y_c, 0]^T$: $$ v_{Wc,\,x} = \dot{x}_R - \dot{\theta}\, y_c $$ $$ v_{Wc,\,y} = \dot{y}_R + \dot{\theta}\, x_c $$ > [!NOTE] > The negative sign on $y_c$ and the positive sign on $x_c$ come directly from the cross product $\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}$. Getting these signs wrong is the **single most common pitfall** in KTU derivations. ## 2.3 Wheel Rolling and Axle Unit Vectors The wheel has two natural directions in the robot frame: - **Rolling direction** $\hat{u}$ — along the wheel's plane, perpendicular to the axle: $$ \hat{u} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha \\ \sin\alpha \end{bmatrix} $$ - **Axle direction** $\hat{n}$ — along the wheel's axle, perpendicular to the rolling direction (90° CCW rotation of $\hat{u}$): $$ \hat{n} = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\alpha \\ \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix} $$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — All Constraints at a Glance > [!IMPORTANT] > **THE KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE** — Memorize this for any constraint derivation or maneuverability question. | Constraint Type | Compact Matrix Form | Expanded Form (Robot Frame) | Physical Meaning | |:----------------|:-------------------|:----------------------------|:-----------------| | **Sliding (Lateral)** | $J_1\,\dot{\xi}_R = 0$ | $-\sin\alpha\,\dot{x}_R + \cos\alpha\,\dot{y}_R + (x_c\cos\alpha + y_c\sin\alpha)\dot{\theta} = 0$ | No slip perpendicular to wheel plane — **non-holonomic** | | **Rolling** | $J_2\,\dot{\xi}_R = r\dot{\varphi}$ | $\cos\alpha\,\dot{x}_R + \sin\alpha\,\dot{y}_R + (x_c\sin\alpha - y_c\cos\alpha)\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\varphi}$ | No slip along wheel plane — relates wheel speed to robot motion | **Constraint Row Vectors (in Robot Frame):** $$ J_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\alpha & \cos\alpha & x_c\cos\alpha + y_c\sin\alpha \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ J_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha & \sin\alpha & x_c\sin\alpha - y_c\cos\alpha \end{bmatrix} $$ **Constraint Row Vectors (in World / Inertial Frame):** $$ J_{1W} = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin(\alpha+\theta) & \cos(\alpha+\theta) & x_c\cos\alpha + y_c\sin\alpha \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ J_{2W} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\alpha+\theta) & \sin(\alpha+\theta) & x_c\sin\alpha - y_c\cos\alpha \end{bmatrix} $$ **Auxiliary Geometric Identity:** For any fixed standard wheel, the ICR (Instantaneous Center of Rotation) of the robot **must lie on the line passing through the wheel's axle** — this is the geometric interpretation of the sliding constraint. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The kinematic constraints derived here are **the cornerstone of every wheeled mobile robot controller**: - **Autonomous Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** in warehouses use these constraints for **odometry** (dead-reckoning position estimation from wheel encoders). - **Planetary Rovers** (NASA's Curiosity, Perseverance) rely on these constraints for **slip-detection and traction control** — when the rolling constraint is violated, the rover knows it is slipping. - **Differential-Drive and Ackermann-Steered Cars** combine multiple fixed-wheel sliding constraints to compute the **reachable velocity workspace** in real time. - **Warehouse robotics** uses the constraint matrices to plan **collision-free trajectories** that respect non-holonomic limits. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Wheel-Center Velocity (Robot Frame) **Given:** - Robot velocity in robot frame: $\dot{\xi}_R = [\dot{x}_R, \dot{y}_R, \dot{\theta}]^T$ - Wheel contact point position in robot frame: $\vec{r}_{PWc} = [x_c, y_c, 0]^T$ - Chassis angular velocity: $\vec{\omega} = [0, 0, \dot{\theta}]^T$ **Goal:** Compute $\vec{v}_{Wc}$. **Step 1 — Write the rigid-body velocity transfer formula.** $$ \vec{v}_{Wc} = \vec{v}_P + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P \to Wc} $$ **Step 2 — Evaluate the cross product $\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P \to Wc}$.** $$ \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P \to Wc} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 0 & 0 & \dot{\theta} \\ x_c & y_c & 0 \end{vmatrix} $$ Expanding the determinant: $$ \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P \to Wc} = \hat{i}(0\cdot 0 - \dot{\theta}\,y_c) - \hat{j}(0\cdot 0 - \dot{\theta}\,x_c) + \hat{k}(0\cdot y_c - 0\cdot x_c) $$ $$ \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P \to Wc} = \begin{bmatrix} -\dot{\theta}\,y_c \\ \dot{\theta}\,x_c \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3 — Add the translational velocity of $P$.** $$ \vec{v}_{Wc} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_R \\ \dot{y}_R \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -\dot{\theta}\,y_c \\ \dot{\theta}\,x_c \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \vec{v}_{Wc} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_R - \dot{\theta}\,y_c \\ \dot{y}_R + \dot{\theta}\,x_c \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ > **Reading the result:** The horizontal components of the wheel-center velocity are the chassis translation *plus* a rotational contribution proportional to the lever arm from $P$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Sliding Constraint (Robot Frame) **Goal:** Show that the velocity component of $W_c$ along the axle direction $\hat{n}$ must vanish for a rigid, non-slipping fixed standard wheel. **Step 1 — Set up the projection.** $$ J_1 \dot{\xi}_R = \hat{n}^T \vec{v}_{Wc} = 0 $$ **Step 2 — Substitute $\hat{n}$ and $\vec{v}_{Wc}$.** $$ \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\alpha & \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_R - \dot{\theta}\,y_c \\ \dot{y}_R + \dot{\theta}\,x_c \end{bmatrix} = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Expand the dot product.** $$ -\sin\alpha\,(\dot{x}_R - \dot{\theta}\,y_c) + \cos\alpha\,(\dot{y}_R + \dot{\theta}\,x_c) = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Distribute and group the $\dot{\theta}$ terms.** $$ -\sin\alpha\,\dot{x}_R + \cos\alpha\,\dot{y}_R + \dot{\theta}\,(y_c\sin\alpha + x_c\cos\alpha) = 0 $$ **Step 5 — Re-order for canonical form.** $$ -\sin\alpha\,\dot{x}_R + \cos\alpha\,\dot{y}_R + (x_c\cos\alpha + y_c\sin\alpha)\,\dot{\theta} = 0 $$ > **Result:** This is the **Sliding Constraint** of a fixed standard wheel in the robot frame. Note that $\dot{\varphi}$ does NOT appear — the sliding constraint is *purely* about the geometry of admissible robot motions. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the Rolling Constraint (Robot Frame) **Goal:** Show that the velocity component along $\hat{u}$ equals $r\dot{\varphi}$ when the wheel rolls without slipping. **Step 1 — Set up the projection equation.** $$ J_2 \dot{\xi}_R = \hat{u}^T \vec{v}_{Wc} = r\dot{\varphi} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute $\hat{u}$ and $\vec{v}_{Wc}$.** $$ \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha & \sin\alpha \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_R - \dot{\theta}\,y_c \\ \dot{y}_R + \dot{\theta}\,x_c \end{bmatrix} = r\dot{\varphi} $$ **Step 3 — Expand the dot product.** $$ \cos\alpha\,(\dot{x}_R - \dot{\theta}\,y_c) + \sin\alpha\,(\dot{y}_R + \dot{\theta}\,x_c) = r\dot{\varphi} $$ **Step 4 — Distribute and collect $\dot{\theta}$ terms.** $$ \cos\alpha\,\dot{x}_R + \sin\alpha\,\dot{y}_R + \dot{\theta}\,(x_c\sin\alpha - y_c\cos\alpha) = r\dot{\varphi} $$ **Step 5 — Final canonical form.** $$ \cos\alpha\,\dot{x}_R + \sin\alpha\,\dot{y}_R + (x_c\sin\alpha - y_c\cos\alpha)\,\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\varphi} $$ > **Result:** This is the **Rolling Constraint**. It tells you **how fast the wheel must spin** for a given robot velocity — exactly the equation used in wheel-encoder odometry. --- ## 3.4 Transformation to the World (Inertial) Frame For global path planning and ICR analysis, the constraints are usually written in the inertial frame $\{I\}$. **Step 1 — Apply the inverse rotation to robot-frame velocity components.** $$ \dot{x}_R = \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta $$ $$ \dot{y}_R = -\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta $$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the sliding constraint.** $$ -\sin\alpha(\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta) + \cos\alpha(-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta) + (x_c\cos\alpha + y_c\sin\alpha)\dot{\theta} = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Collect $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$ terms separately.** $$ \dot{x}\,[-\sin\alpha\cos\theta - \cos\alpha\sin\theta] + \dot{y}\,[-\sin\alpha\sin\theta + \cos\alpha\cos\theta] + (x_c\cos\alpha + y_c\sin\alpha)\dot{\theta} = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Apply the angle-addition identities.** $$ -\sin(\alpha+\theta)\,\dot{x} + \cos(\alpha+\theta)\,\dot{y} + (x_c\cos\alpha + y_c\sin\alpha)\dot{\theta} = 0 $$ > **Result (Sliding, World Frame):** $J_{1W}\dot{\xi}_I = 0$, with $J_{1W} = [-\sin(\alpha+\theta),\ \cos(\alpha+\theta),\ x_c\cos\alpha + y_c\sin\alpha]$. **Step 5 — Repeat for the rolling constraint.** $$ \cos\alpha(\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta) + \sin\alpha(-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta) + (x_c\sin\alpha - y_c\cos\alpha)\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\varphi} $$ **Step 6 — Collect and apply angle addition.** $$ \dot{x}\,[\cos\alpha\cos\theta - \sin\alpha\sin\theta] + \dot{y}\,[\sin\alpha\cos\theta + \cos\alpha\sin\theta] + (x_c\sin\alpha - y_c\cos\alpha)\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\varphi} $$ $$ \cos(\alpha+\theta)\,\dot{x} + \sin(\alpha+\theta)\,\dot{y} + (x_c\sin\alpha - y_c\cos\alpha)\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\varphi} $$ > **Result (Rolling, World Frame):** $J_{2W}\dot{\xi}_I = r\dot{\varphi}$, with $J_{2W} = [\cos(\alpha+\theta),\ \sin(\alpha+\theta),\ x_c\sin\alpha - y_c\cos\alpha]$. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Demonstration **Problem:** A fixed standard wheel is mounted at $(x_c, y_c) = (0.3\,\text{m},\ 0.4\,\text{m})$ in the robot frame, with $\alpha = 90°$ (rolling sideways) and $r = 0.1\,\text{m}$. The robot moves with $\dot{x}_R = 0.5\,\text{m/s}$, $\dot{y}_R = 0$, $\dot{\theta} = 0.2\,\text{rad/s}$. Compute both constraints. **Sliding constraint:** $$ -\sin(90°)(0.5) + \cos(90°)(0) + (0.3\cdot 0 + 0.4\cdot 1)(0.2) = -0.5 + 0 + 0.08 = -0.42 \neq 0 $$ > **Interpretation:** The constraint is **violated** — this combination of $(\dot{x}_R, \dot{y}_R, \dot{\theta})$ is *not admissible*. The wheel would have to slide sideways. The robot controller must adjust either $\dot{x}_R$ or $\dot{\theta}$ to satisfy this equation. **Rolling constraint** (gives required wheel speed): $$ \cos(90°)(0.5) + \sin(90°)(0) + (0.3\cdot 1 - 0.4\cdot 0)(0.2) = 0 + 0 + 0.06 = r\dot{\varphi} $$ $$ \dot{\varphi} = 0.06 / 0.1 = 0.6\,\text{rad/s} $$ > **Interpretation:** To realize the (admissible component of) the motion, the wheel motor must spin at **0.6 rad/s**. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Constraint Derivation The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **complete logical pipeline** from raw robot velocity to the two final constraint equations. This is the architecture you should mentally invoke when answering KTU derivations. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Robot Velocity in Robot Frame
x_R_dot, y_R_dot, theta_dot"] B["Wheel Mounting Position
x_c, y_c in Robot Frame"] C["Wheel Geometry Constants
alpha, r"] A --> D["Rigid-Body Kinematics
v_Wc = v_P + omega cross r_PWc"] B --> D C --> D D --> E["Wheel-Center Velocity
v_Wc_x, v_Wc_y in Robot Frame"] E --> F1["Project onto Rolling Direction
u_hat = cos alpha, sin alpha"] E --> F2["Project onto Axle Direction
n_hat = -sin alpha, cos alpha"] F1 --> G1["ROLLING CONSTRAINT
v_Wc dot u_hat = r phi_dot"] F2 --> G2["SLIDING CONSTRAINT
v_Wc dot n_hat = 0"] G2 --> H1["LIMITS Robot Maneuverability
Non-holonomic Restriction"] G1 --> H2["DETERMINES Required Wheel Speed
Encoder Odometry Source"] I["Inertial Frame Velocity
x_dot, y_dot, theta_dot"] -.->|"R transpose times dot_xi_R"| A style G1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style G2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style H1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style H2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085 ``` ## 4.2 Top-View Schematic of Wheel Geometry ```mermaid graph TB subgraph ROBO["ROBOT CHASSIS (Top View, z = 0)"] P["Point P
Robot Reference Origin"] XR["Robot X-axis
(Forward Direction)"] YR["Robot Y-axis
(Lateral Direction)"] end subgraph WHLC["FIXED STANDARD WHEEL"] CC["Contact Point C
on Ground"] ALPH["alpha
angle from robot x-axis"] RR["r
wheel radius (vertical)"] WC["Wheel Center
directly above C by height r"] UH["Rolling Direction
u_hat"] NH["Axle Direction
n_hat"] end P --- XR P --- YR P -->|"horizontal distance l"| CC XR -.- ALPH CC --- UH CC --- NH CC --- WC WC -.-|"vertical distance r"| CC style P fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085 style CC fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style WC fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#856404 style ALPH fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 ``` ## 4.3 Vector Decomposition at the Wheel Center ```mermaid flowchart TB VC["Wheel-Center Velocity v_Wc
in robot frame"] VC --> DECOMP["Decompose into Two Orthogonal Components"] DECOMP --> ROLL["Component Along u_hat
v_Wc_x cos alpha + v_Wc_y sin alpha"] DECOMP --> LATERAL["Component Along n_hat
-v_Wc_x sin alpha + v_Wc_y cos alpha"] ROLL --> REQ["Must EQUAL r phi_dot
ROLLING CONSTRAINT"] LATERAL --> MUST["Must EQUAL Zero
SLIDING CONSTRAINT"] REQ --> ENC["Encoder Reading Source
for Odometry"] MUST --> ICR["ICR must lie on line through wheel axle"] style ROLL fill:#d4edda style LATERAL fill:#f8d7da style REQ fill:#cce5ff style MUST fill:#cce5ff ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]** ### **Q1. Define a fixed standard wheel and list its four characterizing parameters with their units.** `[3 Marks]` `[CO2, Bloom L1 — Remember]` **Model Answer:** A **fixed standard wheel** is a wheel rigidly mounted to the robot chassis at a fixed orientation. It can only rotate about its own axle — there is no steering mechanism. Its four parameters are: 1. $\alpha$ — angle between the wheel's rolling direction and the robot's x-axis (**rad** or **deg**). 2. $r$ — wheel radius (**m**). 3. $(x_c, y_c)$ — coordinates of the wheel contact point in the robot frame (**m**). 4. $\dot{\varphi}$ — wheel angular velocity about its axle (**rad/s**). The key identifying feature is that $\alpha$ is **constant in time** (since the wheel is not steerable). --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between the rolling constraint and the sliding constraint of a fixed standard wheel.** `[3 Marks]` `[CO2, Bloom L2 — Understand]` **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Rolling Constraint | Sliding Constraint | |:-------|:-------------------|:-------------------| | **Velocity component constrained** | Along the wheel's rolling direction $\hat{u}$ | Perpendicular to the wheel's rolling direction $\hat{n}$ (along axle) | | **Value the component must take** | Must equal $r\dot{\varphi}$ | Must equal **zero** | | **Engineering use** | Determines the required wheel motor speed (odometry) | Restricts the robot's admissible instantaneous motions | | **Nature** | Holonomic in the augmented $(\xi, \varphi)$ state | Genuinely **non-holonomic** in $\xi$ | | **If violated** | Wheel slips while rotating — traction loss | Wheel skids sideways — kinematic failure | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Derivations + Application > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]** **(a)** For a fixed standard wheel with parameters $\alpha$, $r$, and mounting position $(x_c, y_c)$ in the robot frame, derive the **sliding constraint** and the **rolling constraint** in matrix form, clearly showing all intermediate steps. `[7 Marks]` `[CO3, Bloom L3 — Apply]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Velocity of wheel center in robot frame.** Using rigid-body kinematics with $\vec{\omega} = [0, 0, \dot{\theta}]^T$ and $\vec{r}_{P \to Wc} = [x_c, y_c, 0]^T$: $$ \vec{v}_{Wc} = \vec{v}_P + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P \to Wc} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_R - \dot{\theta}\,y_c \\ \dot{y}_R + \dot{\theta}\,x_c \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2 — Define the unit vectors.** $$ \hat{u} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha \\ \sin\alpha \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(rolling)}, \qquad \hat{n} = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\alpha \\ \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(axle)} $$ **Step 3 — Sliding constraint (no lateral slip).** Project $\vec{v}_{Wc}$ onto $\hat{n}$ and set to zero: $$ \hat{n}^T \vec{v}_{Wc} = -\sin\alpha\,(\dot{x}_R - \dot{\theta}\,y_c) + \cos\alpha\,(\dot{y}_R + \dot{\theta}\,x_c) = 0 $$ Rearranging: $$ \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} -\sin\alpha & \cos\alpha & x_c\cos\alpha + y_c\sin\alpha \end{bmatrix}}_{J_1} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_R \\ \dot{y}_R \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Rolling constraint (pure rolling).** Project $\vec{v}_{Wc}$ onto $\hat{u}$ and equate to $r\dot{\varphi}$: $$ \hat{u}^T \vec{v}_{Wc} = \cos\alpha\,(\dot{x}_R - \dot{\theta}\,y_c) + \sin\alpha\,(\dot{y}_R + \dot{\theta}\,x_c) = r\dot{\varphi} $$ Rearranging: $$ \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha & \sin\alpha & x_c\sin\alpha - y_c\cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}}_{J_2} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_R \\ \dot{y}_R \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = r\dot{\varphi} $$ **Step 5 — Final canonical form.** $$ \boxed{J_1\,\dot{\xi}_R = 0, \qquad J_2\,\dot{\xi}_R = r\dot{\varphi}} $$ --- **(b)** A differential-drive robot has **two identical fixed standard wheels**, each with radius $r$ and $\alpha = 0$, mounted at $y_c = \pm l$ on the robot's lateral axis (with $x_c = 0$). Using the constraints derived above, find the relationship between the two wheel angular velocities ($\dot{\varphi}_1, \dot{\varphi}_2$) and the robot's motion $(\dot{x}_R, \dot{\theta})$. `[7 Marks]` `[CO3, Bloom L4 — Analyze]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Substitute $\alpha = 0$, $x_c = 0$, $y_c = +l$ (Right Wheel) into the rolling constraint.** $$ J_2 \dot{\xi}_R = \cos(0)\,\dot{x}_R + \sin(0)\,\dot{y}_R + (0\cdot\sin 0 - l\cdot\cos 0)\,\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\varphi}_1 $$ Simplifying: $$ \dot{x}_R - l\,\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\varphi}_1 \qquad \text{(Right Wheel Rolling Constraint)} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute $\alpha = 0$, $x_c = 0$, $y_c = -l$ (Left Wheel).** $$ \dot{x}_R - (-l)\,\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\varphi}_2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dot{x}_R + l\,\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\varphi}_2 $$ **Step 3 — Solve the two equations simultaneously.** Adding the two equations: $$ 2\dot{x}_R = r(\dot{\varphi}_1 + \dot{\varphi}_2) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dot{x}_R = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\varphi}_1 + \dot{\varphi}_2) $$ Subtracting (right minus left): $$ -2l\,\dot{\theta} = r(\dot{\varphi}_1 - \dot{\varphi}_2) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{r}{2l}(\dot{\varphi}_2 - \dot{\varphi}_1) $$ **Step 4 — Final odometric equations.** $$ \boxed{\dot{x}_R = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\varphi}_1 + \dot{\varphi}_2), \qquad \dot{\theta} = \frac{r}{2l}(\dot{\varphi}_2 - \dot{\varphi}_1)} $$ > **Physical sanity check:** If $\dot{\varphi}_1 = \dot{\varphi}_2 = \dot{\varphi}$, then $\dot{x}_R = r\dot{\varphi}$ and $\dot{\theta} = 0$ — straight-line motion. If $\dot{\varphi}_1 = -\dot{\varphi}_2$, then $\dot{x}_R = 0$ and $\dot{\theta} \neq 0$ — pure rotation about the wheel-center. Both extremes match intuition. ✓ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Geometric Interpretation + World-Frame Transformation **(a)** Explain the **geometric interpretation of the sliding constraint** of a fixed standard wheel in terms of the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**. Show that the ICR of the robot must lie on a specific line in the ground plane, and identify that line. `[7 Marks]` `[CO2, Bloom L4 — Analyze]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Recall the sliding constraint.** $$ \hat{n}^T \vec{v}_{Wc} = 0 $$ This means the wheel-center velocity has **zero component along the axle direction**. Consequently, the velocity vector $\vec{v}_{Wc}$ is *purely* along the rolling direction $\hat{u}$. **Step 2 — Connect to the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR).** The ICR is the point on the ground (or extended ground plane) that has **zero velocity at the current instant**. Since the wheel-center velocity is along $\hat{u}$, the ICR must be located along the line through the wheel-center **perpendicular** to $\hat{u}$ — i.e., along $\hat{n}$. **Step 3 — Locate the line geometrically.** For a wheel whose contact point is $C = (x_c, y_c, 0)$ in the robot frame and whose rolling direction makes angle $\alpha$ with the chassis x-axis, the **axle line** passes through $C$ in the direction $\hat{n} = [-\sin\alpha, \cos\alpha]^T$. The ICR of the robot lies **on this axle line** — this is the geometric interpretation of the sliding constraint. **Step 4 — Implication for maneuverability.** For each fixed standard wheel on the robot, the ICR must lie on the corresponding axle line. With $N$ fixed standard wheels, the ICR must lie at the **intersection of all $N$ axle lines**. If the lines are parallel or never meet, **no finite ICR exists** — the robot cannot move without violating one of the constraints (this is the geometric signature of a non-maneuverable or pathological configuration). --- **(b)** Starting from the sliding constraint expressed in the robot frame, transform it into the **world (inertial) frame**. Show every algebraic step. `[7 Marks]` `[CO3, Bloom L3 — Apply]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Robot-frame sliding constraint.** $$ -\sin\alpha\,\dot{x}_R + \cos\alpha\,\dot{y}_R + (x_c\cos\alpha + y_c\sin\alpha)\,\dot{\theta} = 0 $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the velocity transformation $\dot{\xi}_R = R(\theta)^T \dot{\xi}_I$.** $$ \dot{x}_R = \dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta $$ $$ \dot{y}_R = -\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta $$ **Step 3 — Substitute and group $\dot{x}$, $\dot{y}$, $\dot{\theta}$ terms.** $$ -\sin\alpha\,(\dot{x}\cos\theta + \dot{y}\sin\theta) + \cos\alpha\,(-\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta) + (x_c\cos\alpha + y_c\sin\alpha)\,\dot{\theta} = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Collect $\dot{x}$ coefficient.** $$ \dot{x}\,[-\sin\alpha\cos\theta - \cos\alpha\sin\theta] = -\sin(\alpha+\theta)\,\dot{x} $$ **Step 5 — Collect $\dot{y}$ coefficient.** $$ \dot{y}\,[-\sin\alpha\sin\theta + \cos\alpha\cos\theta] = \cos(\alpha+\theta)\,\dot{y} $$ **Step 6 — Write the final world-frame constraint.** $$ \boxed{-\sin(\alpha+\theta)\,\dot{x} + \cos(\alpha+\theta)\,\dot{y} + (x_c\cos\alpha + y_c\sin\alpha)\,\dot{\theta} = 0} $$ > **Verification:** When $\theta = 0$ (robot aligned with world frame), this reduces exactly to the robot-frame form. ✓ The $\alpha + \theta$ appearance reflects that the wheel's orientation in the world frame is the sum of the chassis orientation $\theta$ and the wheel's mounting angle $\alpha$ — a beautiful and intuitively correct result. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Most Frequently Lose Marks on This Topic** 1. **Sign Error in the Cross Product (Cost: 1–2 marks).** The most common blunder is writing $\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r} = [\dot{\theta}\,y_c, -\dot{\theta}\,x_c]$ instead of $[-\dot{\theta}\,y_c, \dot{\theta}\,x_c]$. Memorize the determinant expansion. 2. **Confusing Rolling and Sliding Constraints (Cost: 2–3 marks).** The rolling constraint is the one with $r\dot{\varphi}$ on the right-hand side. The sliding constraint has zero on the right-hand side. Mixing them up inverts the entire physical meaning. 3. **Forgetting the World-Frame Transformation (Cost: 2 marks).** When a problem asks for the constraint "in the world frame" or "in inertial coordinates," you MUST apply the rotation matrix $R(\theta)^T$. Submitting the robot-frame form will be marked wrong. 4. **Omitting Intermediate Steps (Cost: 1–2 marks).** KTU examiners allocate partial credit for the rigid-body step, the projection step, and the angle-addition step separately. Skipping directly to the final answer forfeits all intermediate marks. 5. **Mixing Up Contact Point and Wheel Center.** The wheel contact point and wheel center have the **same horizontal position** $(x_c, y_c)$ but differ in z-coordinate by $r$. For horizontal-plane analysis, either works, but be consistent. 6. **Forgetting that $\alpha$ is in the Robot Frame.** When transforming to world frame, only $\theta$ enters the angle-addition with $\alpha$ if $\alpha$ itself is robot-frame-relative — which it is. Do not transform $\alpha$ twice. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Wheel Kinematic Constraints for Fixed Standard Wheel** - **Wheel Type:** Fixed standard wheel = rigid mount, no steering; only rotates about its own axle. - **Four Parameters:** $\alpha$ (rolling direction angle), $r$ (radius), $(x_c, y_c)$ (mounting position in robot frame), $\dot{\varphi}$ (wheel angular speed). - **Two Constraints:** - **Rolling:** $\cos\alpha\,\dot{x}_R + \sin\alpha\,\dot{y}_R + (x_c\sin\alpha - y_c\cos\alpha)\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\varphi}$ → determines required wheel speed. - **Sliding:** $-\sin\alpha\,\dot{x}_R + \cos\alpha\,\dot{y}_R + (x_c\cos\alpha + y_c\sin\alpha)\dot{\theta} = 0$ → restricts maneuverability. - **Wheel-Center Velocity (Robot Frame):** - $v_{Wc,x} = \dot{x}_R - \dot{\theta}\,y_c$ - $v_{Wc,y} = \dot{y}_R + \dot{\theta}\,x_c$ - **Matrix Form:** - $J_1 = [-\sin\alpha,\ \cos\alpha,\ x_c\cos\alpha + y_c\sin\alpha]$ - $J_2 = [\cos\alpha,\ \sin\alpha,\ x_c\sin\alpha - y_c\cos\alpha]$ - $J_1\,\dot{\xi}_R = 0$, $\ J_2\,\dot{\xi}_R = r\dot{\varphi}$ - **World-Frame Form:** Replace first two entries of $J_1$ and $J_2$ with $\sin(\alpha+\theta)$, $\cos(\alpha+\theta)$ functions; the third entry (the $\dot{\theta}$ coefficient) is **unchanged** because $\dot{\theta}$ is invariant under rotation about the z-axis. - **Geometric Interpretation of Sliding Constraint:** ICR must lie on the line through the wheel contact point along the axle direction $\hat{n}$. - **Velocity Transformation:** $\dot{\xi}_R = R(\theta)^T \dot{\xi}_I$, where $R(\theta)$ is the 2D rotation matrix. - **Differential Drive Special Case (Exam Favorite):** With $\alpha = 0$, $x_c = 0$, $y_c = \pm l$: - $\dot{x}_R = \dfrac{r}{2}(\dot{\varphi}_1 + \dot{\varphi}_2)$ - $\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{r}{2l}(\dot{\varphi}_2 - \dot{\varphi}_1)$ - **Non-Holonomic Nature:** The sliding constraint cannot be integrated to a position-level algebraic constraint on $x, y, \theta$. It restricts *velocities* only — a hallmark of non-holonomy. - **Engineering Payoff:** These two constraints together enable wheel-encoder odometry, slip detection, traction control, and maneuverability analysis for **every** wheeled mobile robot ever built.

topics.title: Steered Standard Wheel and Castor Wheel Only
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT721 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Steered Standard Wheel and Castor Wheel Only

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Steered Standard Wheel (Centered Oriented Standard Wheel)

A **Steered Standard Wheel** is a conventional rigid wheel whose **steering axis (vertical pivot) passes exactly through the geometric center of the wheel** (i.e., through the wheel–ground contact point when projected vertically). The wheel can rotate freely about this vertical axis to change its orientation, in addition to rolling about its horizontal axle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A steered standard wheel is a wheel mounted on a steering axis that intersects the contact point between the wheel and the ground. The orientation of the wheel plane (denoted by steering angle **β**) can be actively controlled, while the wheel rolls about its own horizontal axle.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the **front wheels of a car**. They are not fixed — they pivot about a vertical kingpin/axis located directly above the wheel center. When you turn the steering wheel, these wheels rotate together about their vertical axes, and the car changes direction. This is precisely a steered standard wheel — the steering axis **passes through the contact patch**, which is what gives cars their precise handling.

---

### 1.2 The Castor Wheel (Off-Centered / Offset Standard Wheel)

A **Castor Wheel** is a smaller wheel whose **steering axis (vertical pivot) does NOT pass through the contact point** with the ground — instead, the pivot is **offset** from the contact point. This offset causes the wheel to naturally self-align with the direction of motion, much like the wheels on a shopping trolley or office chair.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A castor wheel is a wheel whose vertical pivot (steering axis) is offset horizontally from the contact point by a distance **d**. Because of this offset, the wheel is free to rotate passively about its vertical axis and automatically aligns with the instantaneous direction of travel.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine pushing an **office chair across the floor**. The small wheels beneath it (the castors) do not have any motors or steering controls, yet they smoothly follow the direction you push. The mechanical offset between the pivot and the contact point creates a self-centering torque that aligns the wheel with motion. This is the defining behaviour of a castor wheel.

---

### 1.3 Comparative Snapshot

| Feature | Steered Standard Wheel | Castor Wheel |
|---|---|---|
| Steering axis through contact point? | **Yes** | **No** (offset by distance *d*) |
| Steering actuation | **Active** (controlled) | **Passive** (self-aligns) |
| Typically driven? | Often yes | Usually **undriven** |
| Number of kinematic constraints | **2** (rolling + sliding) | **1** (rolling only) |
| Typical use | Car-style robots, forklifts | Office chairs, robot stabilizers, shopping carts |
| Adds a passive DOF to robot? | No | **Yes** (passive steering angle) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constants Used:** Wheel radius is denoted **r**, distance from robot center to wheel contact point is **l**, offset distance for castor is **d**, robot pose is **(x, y, θ)**, steering angle is **β**, and wheel rotation about its axle is **φ**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Reference Frame and Geometry Setup

Let the robot's pose in the world frame be:

$$
\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

A wheel is mounted on the chassis such that the **line from the robot's center to the wheel's contact point** makes an angle **α** with the local x-axis of the robot, and the contact point is at distance **l** from the robot's center. The **wheel plane (rolling direction)** makes an angle **β** with the robot's x-axis. This **β** is the steering angle.

Position of the contact point in the world frame:

$$
x_c = x + l \cos(\theta + \alpha)
$$

$$
y_c = y + l \sin(\theta + \alpha)
$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$
\dot{x}_c = \dot{x} - l \sin(\theta + \alpha)\, \dot{\theta}
$$

$$
\dot{y}_c = \dot{y} + l \cos(\theta + \alpha)\, \dot{\theta}
$$

---

### 2.2 Kinematic Constraint of a Steered Standard Wheel

Because the steering axis passes through the contact point, the wheel **cannot slide sideways** (no lateral slip) — only pure rolling is permitted. This gives **two independent constraints**:

**A) Rolling Constraint (no lateral / perpendicular slip):**

The component of the contact-point velocity **perpendicular to the wheel plane** must be zero.

$$
\sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\, \dot{x} \;-\; \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\, \dot{y} \;+\; l \cos(\beta)\, \dot{\theta} \;=\; 0
$$

**B) Sliding Constraint (no longitudinal slip, assuming non-slip rolling):**

The component of the contact-point velocity **along the wheel plane** must equal the rolling velocity $r \dot{\phi}$.

$$
\cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\, \dot{x} \;+\; \sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\, \dot{y} \;+\; l \sin(\beta)\, \dot{\theta} \;=\; r \dot{\phi}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A steered standard wheel imposes **2 constraints** on the robot's 3-DOF motion (ẋ, ẏ, θ̇). For a robot with *N* such wheels, total constraints = **2N**, leaving the robot with **3 − 2N** mobility DOF.

---

### 2.3 Kinematic Constraint of a Castor Wheel

For a castor wheel, the steering axis is offset from the contact point by a distance **d** (measured along the wheel's rolling direction). Because the steering is **passive**, the wheel is **free to swivel** — meaning there is **no lateral (sliding) constraint** in the direction perpendicular to its instantaneous rolling axis. Only the **rolling constraint** applies.

The rolling constraint for a castor wheel is:

$$
\sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\, \dot{x} \;-\; \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\, \dot{y} \;+\; \bigl[\, l \cos(\beta) + d \,\bigr]\, \dot{\theta} \;=\; 0
$$

Here, **β is a passive variable** that the wheel adjusts on its own to satisfy motion. The additional offset **d** introduces an extra moment that the wheel resolves by pivoting.

> [!NOTE]
> A castor wheel imposes only **1 constraint** on the robot's motion. This is why castors are commonly used as **support / stabilizer wheels** — they consume fewer degrees of freedom than steered standard wheels.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wheel contact position (x-axis) | $x_c = x + l \cos(\theta + \alpha)$ | m | World frame |
| 2 | Wheel contact position (y-axis) | $y_c = y + l \sin(\theta + \alpha)$ | m | World frame |
| 3 | Steered wheel: Rolling constraint | $\sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{x} - \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{y} + l \cos(\beta)\dot{\theta} = 0$ | m/s | No lateral slip |
| 4 | Steered wheel: Sliding constraint | $\cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{x} + \sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{y} + l \sin(\beta)\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\phi}$ | m/s | No longitudinal slip |
| 5 | Castor wheel: Rolling constraint | $\sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{x} - \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{y} + [l\cos(\beta) + d]\dot{\theta} = 0$ | m/s | β is passive |
| 6 | Mobility of robot | $\delta_m = 3 - N_{steered} \cdot 2 - N_{castor} \cdot 1 - N_{fixed} \cdot 2$ | DOF | Gruebler's / constraint-count formula |
| 7 | Wheel linear rolling speed | $v = r\,\dot{\phi}$ | m/s | Pure rolling relation |
| 8 | Steered wheel: Jacobian row 1 | $j_1(\beta) = [\sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta),\ -\cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta),\ l\cos(\beta)]$ | — | Vector form |
| 9 | Steered wheel: Jacobian row 2 | $j_2(\beta) = [\cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta),\ \sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta),\ l\sin(\beta)]$ | — | Vector form |

> [!NOTE]
> The terms **α (mounting angle)**, **β (steering angle)**, **l (mounting distance)**, **r (wheel radius)**, and **d (castor offset)** are the five essential geometric parameters in wheeled mobile robot kinematic modelling.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steered standard wheels** form the basis of car-like robots used in **autonomous driving, warehouse automation (AGVs), and planetary rovers (e.g., NASA's Mars rovers)**, where precise path tracking and Ackermann-style steering geometry are needed.
- **Castor wheels** are used as **rear stabilizers on differential-drive robots** (e.g., indoor cleaning robots, educational robots like the TurtleBot) and on **hospital beds, trolleys, and office chairs** to provide static balance without consuming drive actuation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Rolling Constraint of a Steered Standard Wheel

**Step 1 — Contact point position in world frame:**

The wheel is mounted on the chassis at a point located at polar coordinates (l, α) in the robot's local frame. The robot itself is at pose (x, y, θ) in the world frame. Therefore, the contact point in the world frame is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x_c \\ y_c \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} + R(\theta) \begin{bmatrix} l \cos\alpha \\ l \sin\alpha \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $R(\theta)$ is the 2×2 rotation matrix:

$$
R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

Expanding the multiplication:

$$
x_c = x + l \cos\alpha \cos\theta - l \sin\alpha \sin\theta
$$

$$
y_c = y + l \cos\alpha \sin\theta + l \sin\alpha \cos\theta
$$

Applying the cosine and sine of sum identities, we get:

$$
x_c = x + l \cos(\theta + \alpha)
$$

$$
y_c = y + l \sin(\theta + \alpha)
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate to obtain the contact-point velocity:**

$$
\dot{x}_c = \dot{x} - l \sin(\theta + \alpha)\, \dot{\theta}
$$

$$
\dot{y}_c = \dot{y} + l \cos(\theta + \alpha)\, \dot{\theta}
$$

**Step 3 — Resolve velocity in the wheel's local frame:**

The wheel plane (rolling direction) makes an angle $\gamma = \theta + \alpha + \beta$ with the world x-axis. The component of the contact-point velocity **perpendicular** to the wheel plane is obtained by projecting $(\dot{x}_c, \dot{y}_c)$ onto the unit normal vector $(-\sin\gamma, \cos\gamma)$:

$$
v_{\perp} = -\sin\gamma\, \dot{x}_c + \cos\gamma\, \dot{y}_c
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expressions for $\dot{x}_c$ and $\dot{y}_c$:**

$$
v_{\perp} = -\sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\bigl[\dot{x} - l \sin(\theta + \alpha)\dot{\theta}\bigr] + \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\bigl[\dot{y} + l \cos(\theta + \alpha)\dot{\theta}\bigr]
$$

Expanding:

$$
v_{\perp} = -\sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{x} + \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{y} + l\dot{\theta}\bigl[\sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\sin(\theta + \alpha) + \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\cos(\theta + \alpha)\bigr]
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify using the cosine-of-difference identity:**

Recall the identity:

$$
\cos(A - B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B
$$

With $A = \theta + \alpha + \beta$ and $B = \theta + \alpha$, we have $A - B = \beta$, hence:

$$
\sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\sin(\theta + \alpha) + \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\cos(\theta + \alpha) = \cos(\beta)
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the no-side-slip condition:**

The wheel cannot move perpendicular to its plane, so $v_{\perp} = 0$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,\sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{x} - \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{y} + l\cos(\beta)\dot{\theta} = 0\,}
$$

This is the **rolling (lateral) constraint** of a steered standard wheel.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Sliding Constraint of a Steered Standard Wheel

**Step 1 — Project velocity along the wheel plane:**

The component along the wheel plane is obtained by projecting $(\dot{x}_c, \dot{y}_c)$ onto the unit vector $(\cos\gamma, \sin\gamma)$ where $\gamma = \theta + \alpha + \beta$:

$$
v_{\parallel} = \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{x}_c + \sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{y}_c
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\dot{x}_c$ and $\dot{y}_c$:**

$$
v_{\parallel} = \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\bigl[\dot{x} - l\sin(\theta + \alpha)\dot{\theta}\bigr] + \sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\bigl[\dot{y} + l\cos(\theta + \alpha)\dot{\theta}\bigr]
$$

Expanding:

$$
v_{\parallel} = \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{x} + \sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{y} - l\dot{\theta}\bigl[\cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\sin(\theta + \alpha) - \sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\cos(\theta + \alpha)\bigr]
$$

**Step 3 — Use the sine-of-difference identity:**

$$
\cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\sin(\theta + \alpha) - \sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\cos(\theta + \alpha) = \sin\bigl[(\theta + \alpha) - (\theta + \alpha + \beta)\bigr] = \sin(-\beta) = -\sin\beta
$$

Hence the bracket becomes $-l\dot{\theta} \cdot (-\sin\beta) = l\sin\beta\,\dot{\theta}$.

**Step 4 — Apply the pure-rolling condition $v_{\parallel} = r\dot{\phi}$:**

$$
\boxed{\,\cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{x} + \sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{y} + l\sin(\beta)\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\phi}\,}
$$

This is the **sliding (longitudinal) constraint** of a steered standard wheel.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Rolling Constraint of a Castor Wheel

A castor wheel is essentially a steered wheel whose **steering axis is offset** by a distance **d** from the contact point, measured along the wheel's rolling direction. The pivot is now at position:

$$
\text{Pivot position} = (x_c + d \cos\gamma,\ y_c + d \sin\gamma)
$$

However, only the **contact-point velocity** matters for the no-slip condition, but because the pivot is **above and behind** the contact point, the effective kinematic chain includes an additional term when relating the contact velocity to the robot's center.

For a castor wheel, the **steering is passive** — β changes freely. Therefore, the **lateral (sliding) constraint disappears**; only the rolling constraint remains. The modified rolling constraint is obtained by replacing $l$ with $l + d\cos(\text{local frame correction})$ in the standard formula. The result, in world-frame form, is:

$$
\boxed{\,\sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{x} - \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{y} + \bigl[l\cos(\beta) + d\bigr]\dot{\theta} = 0\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The offset **d** adds a small extra contribution to the angular velocity term. In many textbook treatments, the castor is approximated with **d ≈ 0** for simplicity when the offset is small compared to **l**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Constraint Evaluation for a Steered Standard Wheel

**Problem statement:**
A steered standard wheel is mounted on a robot at distance **l = 0.5 m** from the robot's center, at mounting angle **α = 0°** (directly in front). The steering angle is set to **β = 30°**. The robot is currently at pose (x, y, θ) = (0, 0, 0). If the robot is moving with $\dot{x} = 1\,\text{m/s},\ \dot{y} = 0,\ \dot{\theta} = 0.5\,\text{rad/s}$, find the wheel's required rolling speed $\dot{\phi}$ for **r = 0.1 m**.

**Step 1 — Write the rolling constraint:**

$$
\sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{x} - \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{y} + l\cos(\beta)\dot{\theta} = 0
$$

Substitute θ = 0, α = 0, β = 30° = π/6, l = 0.5:

$$
\sin(30°)\cdot 1 - \cos(30°)\cdot 0 + 0.5 \cdot \cos(30°)\cdot 0.5 = 0
$$

$$
0.5 \cdot 1 + 0 + 0.5 \cdot 0.8660 \cdot 0.5 = 0
$$

$$
0.5 + 0.2165 = 0
$$

The constraint is **not satisfied** by the given motion. This means the robot's instantaneous velocity is **inconsistent with the wheel's rolling constraint** — the robot would either need to change its steering angle β, or its motion must be adjusted.

> **Conclusion:** Such a check is the foundation of *motion feasibility analysis* in mobile robotics.

**Step 2 — Now assume the constraint IS satisfied, and compute $\dot{\phi}$ from the sliding constraint:**

$$
\cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{x} + \sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{y} + l\sin(\beta)\dot{\theta} = r\dot{\phi}
$$

With the same parameters:

$$
\cos(30°)\cdot 1 + \sin(30°)\cdot 0 + 0.5 \cdot \sin(30°)\cdot 0.5 = 0.1 \cdot \dot{\phi}
$$

$$
0.8660 \cdot 1 + 0 + 0.5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5 = 0.1 \cdot \dot{\phi}
$$

$$
0.8660 + 0.1250 = 0.1 \cdot \dot{\phi}
$$

$$
0.9910 = 0.1 \cdot \dot{\phi}
$$

$$
\dot{\phi} = 9.91\,\text{rad/s}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The wheel must rotate at approximately **9.91 rad/s** (about 94.6 RPM) to maintain pure rolling at the given robot velocity.
