## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between a steered standard wheel and a castor wheel based on the position of the steering axis and the number of kinematic constraints each imposes on the robot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **steered standard wheel** has its steering (vertical pivot) axis **passing through the wheel's contact point** with the ground. As a result, it is **actively steered** and imposes **two kinematic constraints** on the robot's motion — a *rolling constraint* (no lateral slip) and a *sliding constraint* (pure rolling, no longitudinal slip). The steering angle **β is controlled** by an actuator.

A **castor wheel**, on the other hand, has its steering axis **offset by a distance d from the contact point**. The wheel **passively self-aligns** with the direction of motion. Because of the free swivel, the lateral constraint vanishes, and it imposes **only one kinematic constraint** — the *rolling constraint*. The steering angle **β is passive** and adjusts based on forces.

> **Mark split:** 1.5 marks for steered standard wheel distinction, 1.5 marks for castor wheel distinction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the rolling and sliding constraint equations for a steered standard wheel mounted at distance l from the robot's center, with mounting angle α and steering angle β. Explain the physical meaning of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Rolling constraint** (no lateral slip at the contact point):

$$
\sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\,\dot{x} \;-\; \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\,\dot{y} \;+\; l\cos(\beta)\,\dot{\theta} \;=\; 0
$$

**Physical meaning:** The component of the contact-point velocity perpendicular to the wheel plane is zero — the wheel cannot slide sideways.

**Sliding constraint** (pure rolling, no longitudinal slip):

$$
\cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\,\dot{x} \;+\; \sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\,\dot{y} \;+\; l\sin(\beta)\,\dot{\theta} \;=\; r\dot{\phi}
$$

**Physical meaning:** The component of the contact-point velocity along the wheel plane equals the rolling velocity $r\dot{\phi}$ — there is no slipping in the rolling direction.

> **Mark split:** 1 mark per equation + 0.5 marks per explanation × 2 = 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. (a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**Derive the rolling constraint equation for a steered standard wheel whose attachment point on the chassis is at polar coordinates (l, α) in the robot frame, with steering angle β. State clearly all assumptions used.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Assumptions (1 mark):**
   - Robot moves on a flat, horizontal plane.
   - The wheel is rigid and maintains contact with the ground at all times.
   - The steering axis passes through the contact point (defining feature of a steered standard wheel).
   - No slipping at the contact point.

2. **Position of contact point in world frame (1.5 marks):**

$$
x_c = x + l \cos(\theta + \alpha), \qquad y_c = y + l \sin(\theta + \alpha)
$$

3. **Velocity of contact point (1.5 marks):**

$$
\dot{x}_c = \dot{x} - l \sin(\theta + \alpha)\,\dot{\theta}, \qquad \dot{y}_c = \dot{y} + l \cos(\theta + \alpha)\,\dot{\theta}
$$

4. **Projection perpendicular to wheel plane (1.5 marks):**

The wheel plane makes angle $\gamma = \theta + \alpha + \beta$ with the world x-axis. Projecting $(\dot{x}_c, \dot{y}_c)$ onto $(-\sin\gamma, \cos\gamma)$ and setting to zero (no lateral slip):

$$
-\sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{x}_c + \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{y}_c = 0
$$

5. **Substitution and simplification using $\cos\beta$ identity (1.5 marks):**

$$
\sin(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{x} - \cos(\theta + \alpha + \beta)\dot{y} + l\cos(\beta)\dot{\theta} = 0
$$

This is the required rolling constraint.

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**A mobile robot has two steered standard wheels (front) and one castor wheel (rear). The front wheels are mounted at l = 0.4 m, α = 0° (left) and α = 180° (right), both with β = 0°. The castor wheel is at the rear with l = 0.3 m, α = 270°, and offset d = 0.05 m. Compute the degree of mobility of the robot. Discuss whether this configuration is holonomic.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Constraint counting (2 marks):**
   - 2 steered standard wheels × 2 constraints each = **4 constraints**
   - 1 castor wheel × 1 constraint = **1 constraint**
   - Total constraints = **5**

2. **Mobility formula (2 marks):**

$$
\delta_m = 3 - (\text{total constraints}) = 3 - 5 = -2
$$

3. **Interpretation (1.5 marks):**

A negative mobility indicates the configuration is **over-constrained** (kinematically impossible as described). To achieve feasible motion, the steering angles β of the two front wheels must be coordinated (e.g., Ackermann steering), reducing the effective constraints to 3. In that case:

$$
\delta_m = 3 - 3 = 0 \text{ (instantaneously), but steerable} \Rightarrow \text{car-like non-holonomic}
$$

4. **Holonomicity discussion (1.5 marks):**

The robot is **non-holonomic** because the constraints involve velocities, not just positions. The robot cannot move sideways instantaneously — it must manoeuvre to achieve lateral displacement. This is the classic car-like behaviour.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. (a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024]**
**Explain, with diagrams, the geometric construction of a castor wheel. How is its kinematic constraint different from that of a steered standard wheel? Why is the offset distance d important?**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Geometric construction (2 marks):** A castor wheel consists of a small wheel mounted on a vertical pivot (swivel axis) that is **offset by a distance d from the contact point** with the ground. The pivot is connected to the robot chassis via a fork/bracket. The wheel can rotate freely about the pivot and also about its own horizontal axle.

2. **Mechanical role of offset d (2 marks):** The offset d means that any lateral force on the wheel creates a **moment about the pivot axis**, causing the wheel to swivel until the contact-point velocity aligns with the wheel's rolling direction. This is what enables **passive self-alignment**.

3. **Difference in kinematic constraints (2 marks):**
   - Steered standard wheel: **2 constraints** (rolling + sliding). Lateral slip is forbidden.
   - Castor wheel: **1 constraint** (rolling only). The wheel can swivel freely, so no sliding/lateral constraint exists.
   - The rolling constraint for castor contains an extra term **d** in the angular velocity coefficient.

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Castors are excellent low-cost stabilizers that **do not consume actuation** and impose fewer mobility constraints on the robot, making them ideal for differential-drive platforms.

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024]**
**For a single steered standard wheel with l = 0.3 m, α = 90°, β = 45°, r = 0.08 m, and the robot moving with $\dot{x} = 0.5\,\text{m/s},\ \dot{y} = 0.2\,\text{m/s},\ \dot{\theta} = 0.3\,\text{rad/s}$ at θ = 0, verify whether the rolling constraint is satisfied. If yes, compute the required wheel angular speed $\dot{\phi}$.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Compute $\theta + \alpha + \beta$ (0.5 marks):**

$$
\gamma = 0 + 90° + 45° = 135°
$$

2. **Evaluate rolling constraint LHS (2.5 marks):**

$$
LHS = \sin(135°)\cdot 0.5 - \cos(135°)\cdot 0.2 + 0.3\cdot\cos(45°)\cdot 0.3
$$

$$
LHS = 0.7071 \cdot 0.5 - (-0.7071) \cdot 0.2 + 0.3 \cdot 0.7071 \cdot 0.3
$$

$$
LHS = 0.3536 + 0.1414 + 0.0636 = 0.5586
$$

3. **Conclusion on rolling constraint (1 mark):**

The LHS is **not zero** (it equals ≈ 0.559), so the **rolling constraint is NOT satisfied** with the given parameters. The robot's motion is inconsistent with the wheel's current steering angle.

4. **Reconciliation: Recompute β such that constraint holds (1.5 marks):**

To make the constraint hold, the steering angle must be adjusted. Setting the equation to zero and solving numerically for β (given all other parameters) gives the feasible β — this is a typical inverse-kinematics problem in WMR control.

5. **If the constraint were satisfied, compute $\dot{\phi}$ from sliding constraint (1.5 marks):**

$$
r\dot{\phi} = \cos(135°)\cdot 0.5 + \sin(135°)\cdot 0.2 + 0.3 \cdot \sin(45°) \cdot 0.3
$$

$$
r\dot{\phi} = (-0.7071)(0.5) + (0.7071)(0.2) + 0.3(0.7071)(0.3)
$$

$$
r\dot{\phi} = -0.3536 + 0.1414 + 0.0636 = -0.1486
$$

$$
\dot{\phi} = \frac{-0.1486}{0.08} = -1.857\,\text{rad/s}
$$

The negative sign indicates the wheel must rotate in the reverse direction (i.e., it is moving backward relative to the current steering orientation).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "rolling" with "sliding" constraint directions:** Rolling is perpendicular to the wheel plane; sliding is along the wheel plane. Swapping these in an exam costs **2–3 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Forgetting the angle sum $(\theta + \alpha + \beta)$:** Many students write only $\beta$ inside the trig functions. The full world-frame angle **must include θ and α**. Marks lost: ~1.5.
> 3. **Mixing up $l$ (mounting distance) and $r$ (wheel radius):** $l$ appears in the angular-velocity term, $r$ only in the rolling-velocity term of the sliding constraint. Confusing them is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Assuming castor wheels are equivalent to steered wheels:** Castor wheels do **NOT** have a sliding constraint. Adding one incorrectly is a major error.
> 5. **Sign convention errors:** The cosine difference identity $\cos(A-B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B$ must be applied carefully. Sign errors in the coefficient of $\dot{\theta}$ are very common.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **steered standard wheel** has its steering axis **through the contact point** → imposes **2 constraints** (rolling + sliding).
- A **castor wheel** has its steering axis **offset by distance d** → imposes **only 1 constraint** (rolling only).
- The **rolling constraint** says the wheel's contact-point velocity component **perpendicular to the wheel plane is zero** (no side slip).
- The **sliding constraint** says the contact-point velocity component **along the wheel plane equals $r\dot{\phi}$** (pure rolling).
- Key geometric parameters: **l** (mounting distance), **α** (mounting angle), **β** (steering angle), **r** (wheel radius), **d** (castor offset).
- The full world-frame angle appearing in trig functions is **$\theta + \alpha + \beta$**, not just $\beta$.
- Mobility formula: $\delta_m = 3 - N_{steered} \cdot 2 - N_{castor} \cdot 1 - N_{fixed} \cdot 2$.
- **β is active** for steered standard wheels, **passive** for castor wheels.
- Castor wheels **do not need actuators** — they are passive stabilizer wheels.
- Two steered standard wheels on the same axle need **Ackermann coordination** to avoid over-constraint.
- Standard robotic platforms (TurtleBot, Fetch, differential drives) commonly use **two fixed/differential drive wheels + one or two castor wheels** for stability.
- Always distinguish **world frame (x, y, θ)** from **robot frame (local l, α)** when setting up the geometry.
- The Jacobian rows $j_1(\beta)$ and $j_2(\beta)$ are the **constraint vectors** that the robot's twist $\xi = [\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}]^T$ must satisfy.
- For an exam, **memorize the structure of the constraint equations** and understand the derivation — derivation questions carry 7 marks each.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Kinematic Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT721 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robot Kinematic Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Robot Kinematic Constraints** are the mathematical restrictions imposed on the motion of a mobile robot that arise from the physical and mechanical coupling between the robot's wheels (or other locomotion primitives) and the ground. These constraints limit the velocities (linear and angular) that the robot's body can instantaneously achieve at any configuration, without violating the no-slip rolling condition at the wheel–ground contact points.

In the broader sense, kinematic constraints define the relationship between the **control inputs** (wheel velocities, steering angles) and the **resulting motion of the robot's reference frame** in the world. They form the foundation of both **forward kinematics** (determining robot pose from wheel inputs) and **inverse kinematics** (determining wheel inputs to achieve a desired pose change).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> A kinematic constraint is a restriction on the motion of a mechanical system that depends only on the geometric and instantaneous kinematic parameters (positions, velocities), and is independent of the forces causing the motion.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shopping Cart on a Wet Floor"

Imagine you are pushing a **standard 4-wheeled shopping cart** in a supermarket. If you push it straight forward, it moves forward smoothly. If you push it sideways, the front wheels (which are *steerable* but *non-driven*) cannot roll sideways — they skid. This is a **non-holonomic constraint**: the cart cannot translate directly along its lateral axis, even though *in principle* the cart occupies a 2D plane (it has full *positional* freedom).

Now consider a person **ice-skating**. A skater can move forward, backward, and turn, but cannot slide perfectly sideways — the blade of the skate is aligned with motion. Again, a non-holonomic restriction.

**In contrast**, a **hovercraft** or **holonomic robot** (e.g., mecanum-wheeled, omni-directional) is like a person walking freely in any direction — it has no such sideways restriction. This is a **holonomic system**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Holonomic** = "Whole-law" (Greek *holos* = whole). The constraints are integrable, so the system can reach any configuration reachable by the equations of motion alone.
> **Non-holonomic** = Constraints cannot be integrated into a position-only relationship.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Constraints (High-Yield Taxonomy)

| Type | Description | Example Robot |
|------|-------------|---------------|
| **Holonomic** | Constraints integrable to position-level equations | Mecanum-wheel robot, omnidirectional |
| **Non-holonomic** | Velocity-level constraints only; not integrable | Differential drive, Ackermann car |
| **Scleronomic** | Time-independent (most mobile robots) | Standard wheeled robots |
| **Rheonomic** | Explicitly time-dependent | Robots with actively variable suspension |
| **Bilateral** | Two-sided (equality) constraints | Standard rolling wheel |
| **Unilateral** | One-sided (inequality) constraints | Wheel that can lift off ground |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU board exams, **holonomic vs. non-holonomic** and **wheel classification** are the two highest-weight classifications. Memorize the differences.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Number of wheels** of a robot: $n$
- **Number of steered wheels**: $n_s$
- **Number of driven wheels**: $n_d$
- **Degree of Mobility (DOF-M)**: $\delta_m$ — number of independently controllable degrees of freedom of the robot's motion
- **Degree of Steerability (DOF-S)**: $\delta_s$ — number of independently controllable steering axes
- **Degree of Maneuverability**: $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$
- **Radius of curvature**: $R$ (in meters)
- **Wheel radius**: $r$ (in meters)
- **Distance between wheels (track)**: $L$ or $2l$ (in meters)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The metric $\delta_M$ (maneuverability) was formalized by **Borenstein and Everett** for classifying mobile robots in the textbook *Navigating Mobile Robots*. This is a high-yield KTU reference.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wheel Types and Their Constraints

Every wheeled mobile robot is fundamentally composed of wheels, and each wheel type imposes its own constraint equations. According to the **Borenstein classification** used in KTU syllabus:

### 2.1.1 Fixed Wheel (Standard Wheel)

A wheel whose **steering axis is fixed** relative to the robot chassis and whose **rolling axis is fixed**. It can only roll forward/backward along its rolling direction.

**Constraint equations** (for a fixed standard wheel located at $(x, y)$ relative to the robot center):

$$\dot{x}_r \sin(\alpha + \beta) - \dot{y}_r \cos(\alpha + \beta) - l\dot{\phi} \cos(\beta) = 0$$

$$\dot{x}_r \cos(\alpha + \beta) + \dot{y}_r \sin(\alpha + \beta) = r\dot{\theta}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = steering axis angle relative to chassis (for fixed wheel, $\alpha$ is constant)
- $\beta$ = rolling axis offset from steering axis (for standard wheel, $\beta = 0$)
- $l$ = perpendicular distance from wheel center to robot center
- $\phi$ = robot's heading angle (orientation in world frame)
- $\dot{\theta}$ = wheel's spin rate
- $\dot{x}_r, \dot{y}_r$ = robot's body-frame velocities

### 2.1.2 Centered Orientable (Steerable) Wheel

Same as a fixed wheel, but the steering angle $\alpha$ is **controllable**. Adds one additional DOF (steering). Has **two constraints**.

### 2.1.3 Off-Centered Orientable (Swedish/Castor) Wheel

A wheel whose **rolling axis does not intersect** with the steering axis. Adds two constraints.

### 2.1.4 Castor Wheel

A wheel with offset rolling axis (passive). Constrained in two directions.

### 2.1.5 Swedish Wheel

An omni-wheel with passive rollers on its circumference. Has constraints different from standard wheels.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exams, the **fixed standard wheel** constraint derivation is most commonly asked.

---

## 2.2 Borenstein's Mobility Formula (KTU High-Yield)

For a robot with $n$ wheels:

$$\delta_m = n - \sum_{i} c_i$$

Where $c_i$ is the number of constraints imposed by the $i^{th}$ wheel.

The **generalized Borenstein formula** for maneuverability:

$$\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$$

With $\delta_s$ being the number of independently steerable wheels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a standard differential drive (2 fixed standard wheels + 1 castor): $\delta_m = 2$, $\delta_s = 0$, hence $\delta_M = 2$ (planar position).
> For a car (Ackermann): $\delta_m = 1$, $\delta_s = 1$, hence $\delta_M = 2$.

---

## 2.3 Holonomic vs. Non-Holonomic — Analytical Test

A constraint of the form:

$$A(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}} = 0$$

is **non-holonomic** if it is **non-integrable**, i.e., there is **no function** $f(\mathbf{q})$ such that the constraint can be rewritten purely in terms of positions $\mathbf{q}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Pfaffian test**: If a constraint $a(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}} = 0$ cannot be expressed as $\frac{d}{dt}f(\mathbf{q}) = 0$ for any smooth $f$, it is non-holonomic.

### 2.3.1 Example — Differential Drive

A differential drive robot at position $(x, y, \theta)$ has the constraint:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

This is non-holonomic because no function $f(x, y, \theta)$ exists whose time derivative yields this equation.

---

## 2.4 Forward & Inverse Kinematics of Differential Drive

The **forward kinematic model** of a differential drive robot gives the world-frame velocity from wheel velocities:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\ \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\ \frac{r}{2L} & -\frac{r}{2L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}_R \\ \dot{\phi}_L \end{bmatrix}$$

Where:
- $r$ = wheel radius
- $2L$ = distance between the two drive wheels (track length)
- $\dot{\phi}_R, \dot{\phi}_L$ = right and left wheel angular velocities

The **inverse kinematic model**:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}_R \\ \dot{\phi}_L \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{r} & \frac{L}{r} \\ \frac{1}{r} & -\frac{L}{r} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

Where $v$ is the robot's linear velocity and $\omega$ is its angular velocity.

---

## 2.5 Ackermann Steering Geometry

For a car-like robot, the **Ackermann condition** ensures that all wheels roll without slipping by sharing a common **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**.

The relationship between inner and outer steering angles:

$$\cot(\delta_o) - \cot(\delta_i) = \frac{w}{L}$$

Where:
- $\delta_i$ = inner wheel steering angle
- $\delta_o$ = outer wheel steering angle
- $w$ = track width
- $L$ = wheelbase

The **turning radius** of the vehicle (measured to the center of the rear axle):

$$R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta}$$

Where $\delta$ is the average steering angle.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Fixed standard wheel constraint | $\dot{x}_r \sin\alpha - \dot{y}_r \cos\alpha - l\dot{\phi} = 0$ | (assuming $\beta=0$) |
| 2 | Fixed standard wheel rolling | $\dot{x}_r \cos\alpha + \dot{y}_r \sin\alpha = r\dot{\theta}$ | rolling without slip |
| 3 | Degree of Mobility | $\delta_m = n - \sum c_i$ | Borenstein |
| 4 | Degree of Steerability | $\delta_s =$ # steerable wheels | Borenstein |
| 5 | Degree of Maneuverability | $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | Borenstein |
| 6 | Differential drive forward K | $\dot{x} = (r/2)(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)\cos\theta$ | linear $v_x$ |
| 7 | Differential drive forward K | $\dot{y} = (r/2)(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)\sin\theta$ | linear $v_y$ |
| 8 | Differential drive forward K | $\dot{\theta} = (r/2L)(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)$ | angular $\omega$ |
| 9 | Differential drive inverse K | $\dot{\phi}_R = (v + L\omega)/r$ | right wheel |
| 10 | Differential drive inverse K | $\dot{\phi}_L = (v - L\omega)/r$ | left wheel |
| 11 | Ackermann condition | $\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = w/L$ | steering geometry |
| 12 | Turning radius | $R = L/\tan\delta$ | center of rear axle |
| 13 | Non-holonomic constraint | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | lateral velocity = 0 |
| 14 | ICR relation | $v_i = \omega R_i$ | all wheels share $\omega$ |
| 15 | Holonomic test | $\partial a_i/\partial q_j = \partial a_j/\partial q_i$ | Frobenius condition |

---

## 2.7 Engineering Applications

- **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** use Ackermann kinematics for efficient navigation in aisle-based environments.
- **Autonomous cars** depend critically on Ackermann geometry for tire wear minimization and slip avoidance.
- **Industrial AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots)** typically use differential drive for in-place rotation and omnidirectional mecanum wheels for tight spaces.
- **Planetary rovers** (e.g., NASA's Mars rovers) use rocker-bogie suspensions with steered wheels, requiring explicit kinematic constraint analysis.
- **Service robots** in hospitals and hotels leverage holonomic platforms for dense human environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Kinematic constraints are **not just theoretical** — they directly determine a robot's path-planning strategy (Dubins curves for non-holonomic, Reeds-Shepp for car-like, omnidirectional for holonomic).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fixed Standard Wheel Constraint (Full Derivation)

**Setup:** A fixed standard wheel is attached to the robot chassis at position $(x_b, y_b)$ in the robot's body frame. The steering axis makes angle $\alpha$ with the robot's forward $x$-axis (fixed for a fixed wheel). The wheel rolls without slip on the ground.

**Goal:** Express the no-slip constraints in terms of robot body velocities.

### Step 1 — Position of the wheel contact point in world frame

Let the robot be at world position $(x, y)$ with heading $\phi$. The wheel contact point in the world frame is:

$$x_w = x + x_b \cos\phi - y_b \sin\phi$$
$$y_w = y + x_b \sin\phi + y_b \cos\phi$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to time

Using the product rule on the trigonometric terms (and noting that $x_b, y_b$ are constants in body frame):

$$\dot{x}_w = \dot{x} - \dot{\phi}(x_b \sin\phi + y_b \cos\phi)$$
$$\dot{y}_w = \dot{y} + \dot{\phi}(x_b \cos\phi - y_b \sin\phi)$$

### Step 3 — Transform to body frame velocities

The body-frame velocities $(\dot{x}_r, \dot{y}_r)$ relate to world-frame velocities by rotation through $-\phi$:

$$\dot{x}_r = \dot{x}\cos\phi + \dot{y}\sin\phi$$
$$\dot{y}_r = -\dot{x}\sin\phi + \dot{y}\cos\phi$$

Solving inversely:

$$\dot{x} = \dot{x}_r \cos\phi - \dot{y}_r \sin\phi$$
$$\dot{y} = \dot{x}_r \sin\phi + \dot{y}_r \cos\phi$$

### Step 4 — Perpendicular no-slip constraint (lateral)

The wheel cannot slide **sideways** (perpendicular to its rolling direction). The unit vector perpendicular to the rolling direction is $(\sin\alpha, -\cos\alpha)$ in body frame. Setting the lateral velocity component to zero:

$$\dot{x}_r \sin\alpha - \dot{y}_r \cos\alpha - l\dot{\phi} = 0$$

> **Note:** The term $l\dot{\phi}$ accounts for the angular velocity of the chassis multiplied by the perpendicular distance $l$ from robot center to wheel contact (lever-arm effect).

### Step 5 — Parallel no-slip constraint (rolling)

The wheel **rolls without slipping** along its rolling direction. The unit vector along the rolling direction is $(\cos\alpha, \sin\alpha)$. The velocity along this direction equals $r\dot{\theta}$ (wheel radius times wheel angular velocity):

$$\dot{x}_r \cos\alpha + \dot{y}_r \sin\alpha = r\dot{\theta}$$

These are the **two constraints** imposed by a single fixed standard wheel.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Forward Kinematics of Differential Drive (Step-by-Step)

**Setup:** Two identical drive wheels of radius $r$ are mounted on a common axle, separated by distance $2L$. The wheel rotational velocities are $\dot{\phi}_R$ (right) and $\dot{\phi}_L$ (left).

### Step 1 — Linear velocity of each wheel contact point

Right wheel contact point linear speed:
$$v_R = r \dot{\phi}_R$$

Left wheel contact point linear speed:
$$v_L = r \dot{\phi}_L$$

### Step 2 — Robot's overall linear velocity

The robot's center-of-mass moves at the **average** of the two wheel velocities (since both are tangential to the same forward direction):

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Robot's angular velocity

The angular velocity arises from the **difference** in wheel velocities, since the robot pivots about an instantaneous center. The track width is $2L$, so:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{2L} = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)}{2L}$$

### Step 4 — World-frame velocities

The robot's heading is $\theta$. The world-frame linear velocities are projections of $v$ onto $x$- and $y$-axes:

$$\dot{x} = v \cos\theta = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L) \cos\theta$$

$$\dot{y} = v \sin\theta = \frac{r}{2}(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L) \sin\theta$$

### Step 5 — Combine into matrix form

Stacking into a matrix-vector form for compactness:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta & \frac{r}{2}\cos\theta \\ \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta & \frac{r}{2}\sin\theta \\ \frac{r}{2L} & -\frac{r}{2L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}_R \\ \dot{\phi}_L \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ackermann Steering Geometry

**Setup:** A four-wheeled vehicle with wheelbase $L$ (front-to-rear axle distance) and track width $w$. The front wheels are steered at angles $\delta_i$ (inner) and $\delta_o$ (outer) when turning.

### Step 1 — Ackermann's Geometric Requirement

For **no slip** at any wheel, all four wheels must rotate about a common **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**. This means the perpendicular to each wheel's rolling direction must pass through the ICR.

### Step 2 — Geometry of Inner Wheel

Let $R_i$ be the radius from ICR to the inner wheel. The inner wheel is at the inner-front corner of the vehicle:

$$R_i = \frac{L}{\tan\delta_i}$$

### Step 3 — Geometry of Outer Wheel

Similarly, the outer wheel:

$$R_o = \frac{L}{\tan\delta_o}$$

### Step 4 — Geometric Constraint

The track width relates the two radii:

$$R_o = R_i + w$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{L}{\tan\delta_o} = \frac{L}{\tan\delta_i} + w$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{L}{\tan\delta_o} - \frac{L}{\tan\delta_i} = w$$

Multiplying both sides by $\frac{1}{L}$:

$$\frac{1}{\tan\delta_o} - \frac{1}{\tan\delta_i} = \frac{w}{L}$$

Or in cotangent form:

$$\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = \frac{w}{L}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU-style)

Let $L = 2.5$ m, $w = 1.5$ m, and inner wheel steering angle $\delta_i = 30°$.

**Find:** Outer wheel steering angle.

$$\cot\delta_o = \cot(30°) + \frac{1.5}{2.5} = 1.7321 + 0.6 = 2.3321$$

$$\delta_o = \arccot(2.3321) = \arctan\left(\frac{1}{2.3321}\right) = \arctan(0.4288) = 23.21°$$

**Find:** Turning radius to the midpoint of rear axle:

$$R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta} = \frac{2.5}{\tan(26.6°)} \approx 5.0 \text{ m}$$

(where $\delta$ is the average: $\delta = (30 + 23.21)/2 = 26.6°$)

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Borenstein's Maneuverability Theorem

**Statement:** A mobile robot with $n$ wheels has:

$$\delta_m = n - \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i$$

where $c_i$ is the number of constraints from wheel $i$.

### Justification

Each wheel imposes some constraints. A standard wheel (fixed) has $c_i = 2$ (one perpendicular, one parallel). A castor has $c_i = 2$ as well but with passive steering. A Swedish wheel with rollers perpendicular to rolling axis has $c_i = 3$ (rolling + 2 orthogonal roller axes are free).

The robot's total degrees of freedom in 2D plane motion is **3** (two translations + one rotation). The mobility is bounded above by this value:

$$\delta_m \leq 3 - \delta_s$$

Wait — let me re-derive correctly. The **maneuverability formula** states:

$$\delta_M = 3 - \epsilon$$

where $\epsilon$ is the number of constraints that couple the wheels to reduce independent motions.

### Practical Examples for KTU

**Example 1:** Differential drive with 2 fixed standard wheels + 1 castor
- Fixed wheel: $c = 2$ each → $2 \times 2 = 4$ constraints
- Castor: $c = 2$ → 2 constraints
- Total constraints: 6
- But the system is over-constrained; real constraints reduce to $\delta_m = 2$.

**Example 2:** Synchronous drive (3 Swedish wheels)
- Swedish wheel: $c = 3$ each → $3 \times 3 = 9$ constraints
- $\delta_m = 9 - 9 = 0$? Actually Swedish wheels impose fewer effective constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, you only need to **state** Borenstein's formula and **apply** it to the wheel configurations listed in the syllabus (differential, Ackermann, synchro).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Inverse Kinematics of Differential Drive

**Given:** Desired body-frame velocities $(v, \omega)$.
**Find:** Wheel rotational velocities $(\dot{\phi}_R, \dot{\phi}_L)$.

### Step 1 — Express velocities in terms of wheel inputs

From forward kinematics:
$$v = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)}{2}$$
$$\omega = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)}{2L}$$

### Step 2 — Solve simultaneously

Adding the two equations (after multiplying the second by $L$):
$$v + L\omega = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)}{2} + \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)}{2} = r\dot{\phi}_R$$

Therefore:
$$\dot{\phi}_R = \frac{v + L\omega}{r}$$

Subtracting:
$$v - L\omega = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)}{2} - \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)}{2} = r\dot{\phi}_L$$

Therefore:
$$\dot{\phi}_L = \frac{v - L\omega}{r}$$

### Step 3 — Matrix form

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}_R \\ \dot{\phi}_L \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{r} & \frac{L}{r} \\ \frac{1}{r} & -\frac{L}{r} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 3.6 Numerical Example — KTU Exam Pattern

**Problem:** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheel separation $2L = 0.3$ m. The right wheel rotates at $\dot{\phi}_R = 10$ rad/s and the left at $\dot{\phi}_L = 6$ rad/s. Find $(v, \omega)$ and the turning radius $R$.

### Solution:

**Linear velocity:**
$$v = \frac{0.05(10 + 6)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times 16}{2} = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Angular velocity:**
$$\omega = \frac{0.05(10 - 6)}{0.3} = \frac{0.05 \times 4}{0.3} = 0.667 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Turning radius:**
$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.4}{0.667} = 0.6 \text{ m}$$

**Verification:** Instantaneous center is 0.6 m from robot center, on the side of the slower (left) wheel — ✓ physically correct.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Wheel Configurations vs. Mobility

| Robot Type | Wheels | Constraints | $\delta_m$ | $\delta_s$ | $\delta_M$ | Holonomic? |
|------------|--------|-------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Differential Drive | 2 fixed std + 1 castor | 4 + 2 = 6 | 2 | 0 | 2 | No |
| Ackermann Car | 2 fixed rear + 2 steerable front | 4 + 4 | 1 | 1 | 2 | No |
| Synchro Drive | 3 Swedish (oriented) | 9 - 3 = 6 | 2 | 1 | 3 | No |
| Omnidirectional (Mecanum) | 4 mecanum | 4 × 3 = 12, but free rolling | 3 | 0 | 3 | **Yes** |
| Tricycle | 2 fixed rear + 1 steerable front | 4 + 2 | 1 | 1 | 2 | No |
| Unicycle (theoretical) | 1 sphere | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | **Yes** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Robot Kinematic Constraint Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Robot Kinematic Constraints"] --> B["Holonomic"]
    A --> C["Non-Holonomic"]
    B --> B1["Position Level Restriction"]
    B --> B2["Integrable Velocity Constraints"]
    B --> B3["Example: Mecanum Wheel Robot"]
    C --> C1["Velocity Level Only"]
    C --> C2["Non Integrable Form"]
    C --> C3["Example: Differential Drive"]
    C --> C4["Example: Ackermann Car"]
    A --> D["Wheel Level Constraints"]
    D --> D1["Fixed Standard Wheel 2 Constraints"]
    D --> D2["Steerable Wheel 2 Constraints + 1 DOF"]
    D --> D3["Castor Wheel 2 Constraints Passive"]
    D --> D4["Swedish Wheel 3 Constraints"]
    D --> D5["Mecanum Wheel 3 Constraints"]
    A --> E["Mobility Metrics"]
    E --> E1["Degree of Mobility delta_m"]
    E --> E2["Degree of Steerability delta_s"]
    E --> E3["Maneuverability delta_M = delta_m + delta_s"]
    A --> F["Kinematic Models"]
    F --> F1["Forward Kinematics"]
    F --> F2["Inverse Kinematics"]
    F1 --> F1a["Wheel Inputs to Robot Pose"]
    F2 --> F2a["Robot Velocity to Wheel Commands"]
```


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define non-holonomic constraint. Give one example of a mobile robot that exhibits it. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **non-holonomic constraint** is a kinematic restriction on a mechanical system that involves velocities but cannot be integrated into a position-only equation. Such constraints restrict the **instantaneous direction of motion** but do not restrict the **set of reachable configurations** (at least in theory).

**Example:** A **differential drive robot** has the constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$, meaning it cannot move sideways relative to its body. However, by a sequence of forward and turning motions, it can reach any $(x, y, \theta)$ configuration (this property is called *controllability*).

> [!NOTE]
> Mentioning "integrability" earns full marks. Avoid the common error of confusing holonomic with omnidirectional — they are different concepts.

---

### Question 2. Differentiate between holonomic and non-holonomic mobile robots. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Holonomic | Non-Holonomic |
|--------|-----------|---------------|
| Constraint integrability | Integrable | Non-integrable |
| Instantaneous DOF | Equal to total DOF | Less than total DOF |
| Lateral motion | Possible | Impossible |
| Path planning | Direct line-segment paths | Requires Dubins/Reeds-Shepp |
| Examples | Mecanum, omnidirectional | Differential drive, Ackermann |
| Controllability | Trivial | Non-trivial (path-dependent) |

A **holonomic** robot has full instantaneous freedom of motion in all directions permitted by its configuration space, while a **non-holonomic** robot has fewer controllable degrees of freedom than its total configuration space dimensionality.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a)** Derive the two kinematic constraints for a fixed standard wheel mounted on a mobile robot. Clearly state the assumptions. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The wheel rolls on a flat, horizontal surface.
2. There is no slip at the wheel-ground contact (pure rolling).
3. The wheel is rigid and does not deform.
4. The steering axis is fixed relative to the chassis.

**Setup:** Place a fixed standard wheel at body-frame coordinates $(x_b, y_b)$, with steering axis making angle $\alpha$ with the robot's longitudinal $x$-axis. Rolling axis coincides with steering axis (so $\beta = 0$ for standard wheel).

**Step 1 — World-frame position of wheel contact:**

$$x_w = x + x_b\cos\phi - y_b\sin\phi$$
$$y_w = y + x_b\sin\phi + y_b\cos\phi$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate:**

$$\dot{x}_w = \dot{x} - \dot{\phi}(x_b\sin\phi + y_b\cos\phi)$$
$$\dot{y}_w = \dot{y} + \dot{\phi}(x_b\cos\phi - y_b\sin\phi)$$

**Step 3 — Transform to body frame** via rotation by $-\phi$:

$$\dot{x}_r = \dot{x}\cos\phi + \dot{y}\sin\phi$$
$$\dot{y}_r = -\dot{x}\sin\phi + \dot{y}\cos\phi$$

**Step 4 — Perpendicular no-slip constraint** (velocity along direction perpendicular to rolling axis = 0):

The perpendicular direction to the rolling axis is $(-\sin\alpha, \cos\alpha)$. For pure rolling, the component of velocity along this direction must vanish:

$$-\dot{x}_r\sin\alpha + \dot{y}_r\cos\alpha - l\dot{\phi} = 0$$

or equivalently:

$$\boxed{\dot{x}_r \sin\alpha - \dot{y}_r \cos\alpha - l\dot{\phi} = 0}$$

where $l$ is the perpendicular distance from the robot center to the wheel contact line.

**Step 5 — Rolling constraint** (velocity along rolling axis = $r\dot{\theta}$):

The velocity component along the rolling direction $(\cos\alpha, \sin\alpha)$ equals the wheel's tangential speed:

$$\boxed{\dot{x}_r \cos\alpha + \dot{y}_r \sin\alpha = r\dot{\theta}}$$

**Conclusion:** A fixed standard wheel contributes **two independent constraints** — one lateral (no side slip) and one rolling (pure rolling). These are the foundational constraints for kinematic analysis of wheeled mobile robots.

---

**Part (b)** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.08$ m and wheel separation $2L = 0.4$ m. The robot is to follow a circular path with linear velocity $v = 0.5$ m/s and angular velocity $\omega = 1$ rad/s. Compute the required wheel angular velocities and the radius of curvature. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the inverse kinematics equations:**

$$\dot{\phi}_R = \frac{v + L\omega}{r}$$
$$\dot{\phi}_L = \frac{v - L\omega}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute given values:**

- $v = 0.5$ m/s
- $\omega = 1$ rad/s
- $r = 0.08$ m
- $L = 0.4/2 = 0.2$ m

**Step 3 — Right wheel angular velocity:**

$$\dot{\phi}_R = \frac{0.5 + (0.2)(1)}{0.08} = \frac{0.5 + 0.2}{0.08} = \frac{0.7}{0.08} = 8.75 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Left wheel angular velocity:**

$$\dot{\phi}_L = \frac{0.5 - (0.2)(1)}{0.08} = \frac{0.5 - 0.2}{0.08} = \frac{0.3}{0.08} = 3.75 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Radius of curvature:**

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.5}{1} = 0.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Verification via instantaneous center:**

The ICR lies on the line connecting the two wheel contact points, on the side of the slower wheel (left).

Distance from robot center to ICR along the wheel-base line:

$$d = \frac{v}{\omega} = 0.5 \text{ m}$$

So ICR is 0.5 m to the left of the robot center (since left wheel is slower). ✓

**Final Answers:**
- $\dot{\phi}_R = 8.75$ rad/s
- $\dot{\phi}_L = 3.75$ rad/s
- $R = 0.5$ m

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Part (a)** State and explain Borenstein's formula for the degree of mobility, degree of steerability, and degree of maneuverability of a mobile robot. Apply the formula to compute these metrics for (i) a differential drive robot with 2 fixed standard wheels and 1 castor wheel, and (ii) an Ackermann-steered car with 2 fixed rear wheels and 2 steerable front wheels. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Borenstein's Formulas (1995):**

For a robot with $n$ wheels, where wheel $i$ contributes $c_i$ constraints:

$$\delta_m = n - \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i$$

where $\delta_m$ is the **Degree of Mobility** (number of independently controllable DOF of the robot's motion).

$$\delta_s = \text{Number of independently steerable wheels}$$

where $\delta_s$ is the **Degree of Steerability**.

$$\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$$

where $\delta_M$ is the **Degree of Maneuverability** (total controllable DOF).

**Application (i) — Differential Drive:**

- Fixed standard wheel: $c_i = 2$ → 2 wheels contribute $4$ constraints
- Castor wheel: $c_i = 2$ → contributes $2$ constraints
- Total wheels: $n = 3$

$$\delta_m = 3 - (4 + 2) = 3 - 6 = -3$$

This negative value indicates the system is **over-constrained**; the effective mobility is determined by the **non-redundant subset** of constraints. In practice, for a differential drive:

$$\delta_m^{eff} = 2 \text{ (two independent translations, no sideways motion)}$$

For steerability, only the fixed wheels have non-controllable steering ($\alpha$ fixed), so:

$$\delta_s = 0$$

$$\delta_M = 2 + 0 = 2$$

**Application (ii) — Ackermann Car:**

- Fixed rear wheels: $c_i = 2$ each → $4$ constraints
- Steerable front wheels: $c_i = 2$ each (steering doesn't add constraints, adds DOF)
- Total wheels: $n = 4$

$$\delta_m = 4 - (2 + 2 + 2 + 2) = 4 - 8 = -4$$

Effective mobility:

$$\delta_m^{eff} = 1 \text{ (forward direction only)}$$

Steerability (front wheels can be steered, but they are coupled by Ackermann linkage — considered as one DOF):

$$\delta_s = 1$$

$$\delta_M = 1 + 1 = 2$$

**Conclusion:** Both robots have $\delta_M = 2$, which is sufficient to reach any $(x, y)$ position in the plane, though via different motion primitives.

---

**Part (b)** With a neat diagram, derive the Ackermann steering condition for a four-wheeled vehicle. An Ackermann vehicle has wheelbase $L = 2.8$ m and track width $w = 1.6$ m. If the inner front wheel is steered at $\delta_i = 35°$, find the outer wheel steering angle $\delta_o$ and the radius of curvature $R$ measured from the center of the rear axle. **[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation of Ackermann Condition:**

For a four-wheeled vehicle to roll without slipping, all four wheels must rotate about a **common instantaneous center of rotation (ICR)**. The inner and outer front wheels trace circles of radii $R_i$ and $R_o$ around this ICR.

From the geometry:
$$R_i = \frac{L}{\tan\delta_i}, \quad R_o = \frac{L}{\tan\delta_o}$$

Since $R_o = R_i + w$:

$$\frac{L}{\tan\delta_o} = \frac{L}{\tan\delta_i} + w$$

Dividing by $L$ and rearranging:

$$\boxed{\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = \frac{w}{L}}$$

This is the **Ackermann condition**.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $L = 2.8$ m, $w = 1.6$ m, $\delta_i = 35°$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\cot\delta_i$:**

$$\cot(35°) = \frac{1}{\tan(35°)} = \frac{1}{0.7002} = 1.4281$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\cot\delta_o$:**

$$\cot\delta_o = \cot\delta_i + \frac{w}{L} = 1.4281 + \frac{1.6}{2.8}$$

$$\cot\delta_o = 1.4281 + 0.5714 = 1.9995$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\delta_o$:**

$$\tan\delta_o = \frac{1}{1.9995} = 0.5001$$

$$\delta_o = \arctan(0.5001) = 26.57°$$

**Step 4 — Compute average steering angle:**

$$\delta_{avg} = \frac{\delta_i + \delta_o}{2} = \frac{35° + 26.57°}{2} = 30.78°$$

**Step 5 — Compute radius of curvature (from rear axle midpoint):**

$$R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta_{avg}} = \frac{2.8}{\tan(30.78°)} = \frac{2.8}{0.5954} = 4.703 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Outer wheel steering angle: $\delta_o \approx 26.57°$
- Radius of curvature: $R \approx 4.70$ m

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Kinematic Constraints Questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Confusion in Inverse Kinematics** (Loses 2 marks): Many students write $\dot{\phi}_R = (v - L\omega)/r$ instead of $(v + L\omega)/r$. Remember: positive $\omega$ (counter-clockwise) means right wheel must rotate **faster** than left.
>
> 2. **Confusing $L$ and $2L$** (Loses 1 mark): Some problems give the "wheel separation" as $2L$ (track width). If a problem states $L = 0.2$ m as half-track, do NOT plug it into the $2L$ formula. Read carefully.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Ackermann Geometry Justification** (Loses 2 marks): When deriving Ackermann, students write the formula but skip **why** — i.e., the ICR shared by all wheels. Always explain the geometric origin.
>
> 4. **Missing the "Non-Holonomic" Justification** (Loses 1-2 marks): Defining a constraint is half the answer. KTU expects you to **state why it is non-holonomic** (non-integrability).
>
> 5. **Mixing up Holonomic and Omnidirectional** (Loses 1 mark): Holonomic refers to *controllability*, not omnidirectionality of wheels. A mecanum robot is both, but a perfectly driven unicycle (theoretical) is holonomic without being omnidirectional in wheel arrangement.
>
> 6. **Unit Inconsistency** (Loses 0.5-1 mark): If radius is in cm, convert to m before substituting. Final answer should always have **SI units**.
>
> 7. **Skipping Diagrams** (Loses 1-2 marks): For long-answer questions, always draw a labeled diagram showing wheel arrangement, ICR, angles $\alpha$, $\beta$, distances $l$, $L$, $w$. Even a hand-drawn sketch on the answer sheet earns partial credit.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Kinematic constraints** are velocity-level restrictions from wheel-ground contact (no-slip).
- ✅ **Holonomic** constraints are **integrable** (position-level); **Non-holonomic** are not.
- ✅ Differential drive constraint: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ (lateral velocity = 0 in body frame).
- ✅ Five major wheel types: **Fixed standard, Steerable standard, Castor, Swedish, Mecanum**.
- ✅ **Borenstein's maneuverability**: $\delta_M = \delta_m + \delta_s$.
- ✅ **Differential drive forward K**: $v = (r/2)(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)$, $\omega = (r/2L)(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)$.
- ✅ **Differential drive inverse K**: $\dot{\phi}_R = (v + L\omega)/r$, $\dot{\phi}_L = (v - L\omega)/r$.
- ✅ **Ackermann condition**: $\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = w/L$.
- ✅ **Turning radius** of car: $R = L/\tan\delta$ (from rear axle center).
- ✅ **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is the key concept behind all no-slip rolling analyses.
- ✅ **Configuration space** of planar mobile robot = SE(2) = 3D (x, y, θ).
- ✅ Mecanum-wheeled and Swedish-wheeled robots are **omnidirectional**; differential and Ackermann are **non-holonomic**.
- ✅ Path planners must respect non-holonomic constraints — use **Dubins curves** for forward-only cars, **Reeds-Shepp** for forward/reverse cars.
- ✅ Memorize the **Borenstein classification** — it is a recurring KTU exam topic.
- ✅ Practice deriving both forward and inverse kinematics for differential drive — it's the most asked numerical problem.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "DASH"**
> **D**ifferential drive: 2 wheels
> **A**ckermann: 4 wheels, 1 steerable pair
> **S**ynchro drive: 3 Swedish wheels, all rotate together
> **H**olonomic (mecanum): 4 special wheels with rollers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Past Question Themes:**
> - "Derive the no-slip constraint for a fixed standard wheel" — almost guaranteed annually.
> - "Differential drive forward and inverse kinematics with numerical values."
> - "Compare holonomic vs non-holonomic with examples."
> - "Borenstein's maneuverability classification of [some robot]."
> - "Ackermann steering geometry numerical problem."

topics.title: Examples of Robot Kinematic Models and Constraints: Differential-Drive Robot Only
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT721 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Examples of Robot Kinematic Models and Constraints: Differential-Drive Robot Only

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Differential-Drive Robot (DDR)** is a non-holonomic, two-wheeled mobile robot whose locomotion is achieved by independently controlling the angular velocities of two coaxial drive wheels mounted on a common axle. The difference between the wheel velocities produces rotation, while the average produces translation. The robot's posture (pose) in the 2D world frame is completely described by the configuration vector:

$$\mathbf{q} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

where $(x, y)$ is the position of the robot's reference point (typically the center of the axle) and $\theta$ is the orientation angle measured from the global X-axis to the robot's local x-axis (forward direction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The differential-drive robot is the **canonical textbook example** used in KTU to teach *kinematic constraints* because it elegantly demonstrates the difference between **Holonomic** and **Non-Holonomic** systems. A DDR has 3 configuration variables but only 2 controllable degrees of freedom, producing one **non-integrable (non-holonomic) velocity constraint**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in an **office chair with wheels** (the kind on a smooth floor). You have two hand-rims (large wheels) — one under each hand. This is precisely a differential-drive system.

- **Push both hands equally forward** → the chair moves straight ahead (pure translation).
- **Push only the right hand forward** → the chair pivots around the left wheel (pure rotation, clockwise from top view).
- **Push the right hand forward and pull the left hand back at equal rates** → the chair spins in place about its own center.

You **cannot** make the chair slide sideways directly — no matter how hard you push your hands in opposite vertical directions, the chair simply spins. This "cannot-move-sideways" property is the **non-holonomic constraint**, and it is the defining mathematical feature of the differential-drive robot.

### 1.3 Physical Setup & Standard Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Notation Convention (Used Across KTU Exams):**
> - $r$ = Radius of each drive wheel (meters)
> - $L$ (or $2b$, or $d$) = Distance between the two wheel contact points (wheelbase / track width)
> - $\dot{\phi}_L$ = Angular velocity of the left wheel (rad/s)
> - $\dot{\phi}_R$ = Angular velocity of the right wheel (rad/s)
> - $v_L = r\dot{\phi}_L$ = Linear velocity of the left wheel contact point
> - $v_R = r\dot{\phi}_R$ = Linear velocity of the right wheel contact point
> - $v$ = Forward linear velocity of the robot's center
> - $\omega = \dot{\theta}$ = Angular velocity of the robot (yaw rate)

### 1.4 Why Differential-Drive? Engineering Relevance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this topic in your KTU syllabus?**
> Differential drives are mechanically **simple**, **highly maneuverable** (zero turning radius), and dominate real-world applications: **AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) in warehouses (Amazon Kiva robots), robotic vacuum cleaners (Roomba), planetary exploration rovers (NASA Sojourner, Mars rovers), and warehouse logistics fleets (Fetch, MiR)**. Mastering its kinematics is the gateway to all higher mobile-robotics topics: trajectory tracking, SLAM, and motion planning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Kinematic Decomposition — The "Why" Behind the Equations

The kinematic modeling of any wheeled mobile robot proceeds through **three logical layers**. For the differential drive, these layers are:

#### Layer A: Actuator → Wheel Contact Point (Rolling Without Slipping)
Each drive wheel touches the ground at a single point. Assuming **pure rolling** (no skidding, no slipping), the linear velocity of the wheel contact point equals the wheel's tangential velocity:

$$v_{\text{contact}} = r \cdot \dot{\phi}$$

Because the wheels are coaxial and constrained to roll without slipping **perpendicular to the wheel axle**, the robot body cannot translate along the axle direction instantaneously. This single physical fact produces the famous non-holonomic constraint.

#### Layer B: Wheel Contact Velocities → Robot Body Velocities
By rigid-body kinematics, the linear velocity of the robot's center-of-axle point is the **average** of the two wheel velocities (since the center is the midpoint of the wheelbase). The angular velocity is produced by the **velocity difference** divided by the wheelbase:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$$

#### Layer C: Robot Body Velocities → Pose Rate in World Frame
This is the **forward kinematics model**. The body-frame linear velocity $v$ must be projected onto the world frame using the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

This is a classic example of a **driftless, non-linear control-affine system** of the form $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = B(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{u}$.

### 2.2 The Non-Holonomic Constraint — Rigorous Statement

The differential drive robot's velocity in the world frame is constrained to lie **only along the instantaneous axis of the wheelbase** (i.e., along the robot's local x-axis). The lateral (sideways) velocity must be **identically zero** at every instant:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Non-Holonomic"?**
> This constraint involves **velocities**, not just positions. It **cannot** be integrated to a positional constraint of the form $f(x, y, \theta) = 0$. If it could, the robot would be holonomic. The fact that you cannot integrate this single velocity equation into a positional one is the precise mathematical definition of *non-holonomic*. KTU examiners love asking this distinction.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE FOR THE ESE — It is the most-tested block of formulas for Module 3.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Output Quantity | Inputs Required |
|---|--------------|----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Left wheel linear velocity | $v_L = r\dot{\phi}_L$ | m/s | $r$ (m), $\dot{\phi}_L$ (rad/s) |
| 2 | Right wheel linear velocity | $v_R = r\dot{\phi}_R$ | m/s | $r$ (m), $\dot{\phi}_R$ (rad/s) |
| 3 | Robot linear velocity | $v = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | m/s | $v_R$, $v_L$ |
| 4 | Robot angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{L}$ | rad/s | $v_R$, $v_L$, $L$ |
| 5 | Inverse kinematics (wheel ← robot) | $\dot{\phi}_L = \dfrac{v - \omega L/2}{r}$ | rad/s | $v$, $\omega$, $L$, $r$ |
| 6 | Inverse kinematics (wheel ← robot) | $\dot{\phi}_R = \dfrac{v + \omega L/2}{r}$ | rad/s | $v$, $\omega$, $L$, $r$ |
| 7 | Forward kinematics (pose rate) | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ | m/s | $v$, $\theta$ |
| 8 | Forward kinematics (pose rate) | $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ | m/s | $v$, $\theta$ |
| 9 | Forward kinematics (pose rate) | $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ | rad/s | $\omega$ |
| 10 | Non-holonomic constraint | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | scalar | $\dot{x}$, $\dot{y}$, $\theta$ |
| 11 | Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) radius | $R = \dfrac{v}{\omega} = \dfrac{L(v_R+v_L)}{2(v_R-v_L)}$ | m | all velocities |
| 12 | Pose update (Euler integration, small $\Delta t$) | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega \Delta t$ | rad | $\theta_k$, $\omega$ |

### 2.4 Special Motion Cases (Highly Testable)

These are derived directly by substituting extreme values into the formulas above:

- **Pure Straight Translation:** $v_R = v_L \Rightarrow \omega = 0$, robot moves along a straight line in direction $\theta$.
- **Pure Rotation In-Place:** $v_R = -v_L \Rightarrow v = 0$, $\omega = \dfrac{2v_R}{L}$, robot pivots about its center.
- **Right Turn (Arc):** $v_R < v_L$ (and both positive) → robot follows a circular arc curving left.
- **Stopped:** $v_R = v_L = 0$ → no motion.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are not merely academic. They are embedded in the **low-level motion controllers** of every commercial mobile robot:

- **Vacuum robots** (e.g., iRobot Roomba) use the inverse kinematics to convert a desired $(v, \omega)$ command into individual wheel PWM signals.
- **Warehouse AMRs** (Autonomous Mobile Robots) use the forward kinematics model inside their **odometry** algorithm to dead-reckon their pose as they execute trajectories.
- **Autonomous cars** with differential braking use an analogous formulation for slip-based yaw estimation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A: Forward Kinematics from Wheel Velocities to Robot Body Velocity

We begin with two rigid wheels of radius $r$ rolling on the ground without slipping, separated by wheelbase $L$.

**Step 1 — Identify the contact velocities.**
The no-slip condition means the velocity of the wheel-ground contact point equals the tangential velocity of the wheel. For the left and right wheels:

$$v_L = r\dot{\phi}_L \qquad \text{and} \qquad v_R = r\dot{\phi}_R$$

**Step 2 — Apply rigid-body kinematics to the axle.**
Consider the axle as a rigid bar pivoting instantaneously about an Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) located somewhere on the line of the axle. Let $R$ be the signed perpendicular distance from the ICR to the robot center (positive to the left of forward direction).

**Step 3 — Express each wheel velocity in terms of $v$ and $\omega$.**
The left wheel is at distance $L/2$ from the center, the right wheel at distance $L/2$ on the other side:

$$v_R = v + \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2} \qquad \text{and} \qquad v_L = v - \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

> *Logic:* A point at distance $d$ from the center of a rotating rigid body has additional velocity $\omega d$ in the body's tangent direction.

**Step 4 — Solve the linear system for $v$ and $\omega$.**
Adding the two equations eliminates $\omega$:

$$v_R + v_L = 2v \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$

Subtracting the two equations eliminates $v$:

$$v_R - v_L = \omega L \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$$

This completes the **forward kinematics at the body level**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation B: Inverse Kinematics from Robot Body Velocity to Wheel Velocities

This is the reverse mapping: given a desired $(v, \omega)$, find the required wheel angular velocities $(\dot{\phi}_L, \dot{\phi}_R)$.

**Step 1 — Start from the forward relations:**

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} \qquad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $v_R$ and $v_L$.**
Multiply the first equation by 2 and add the second (multiplied by $L/2$):

$$2v = v_R + v_L$$
$$\frac{\omega L}{2} = \frac{v_R - v_L}{2}$$

Adding gives $v_R$:

$$v_R = v + \frac{\omega L}{2}$$

Subtracting gives $v_L$:

$$v_L = v - \frac{\omega L}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Convert linear wheel velocities back to angular wheel velocities using $v = r\dot{\phi}$:**

$$\dot{\phi}_R = \frac{v_R}{r} = \frac{1}{r}\left(v + \frac{\omega L}{2}\right)$$

$$\dot{\phi}_L = \frac{v_L}{r} = \frac{1}{r}\left(v - \frac{\omega L}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compact matrix form (highly testable):**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}_R \\ \dot{\phi}_L \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & L/2 \\ 1 & -L/2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

This matrix is the **inverse kinematic mapping** $\mathbf{u} = J^{-1}(q)\dot{\mathbf{q}}$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation C: World-Frame Forward Kinematics (Pose Propagation)

The robot's body-frame velocity vector is $\mathbf{u} = [v, \omega]^T$. To express this in the world frame, we apply the planar rotation matrix:

$$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Decompose pose rate into x, y, theta components.**
The x-component of world-frame velocity is $v$ projected onto the world X-axis, which equals $v\cos\theta$ (since $\theta$ is measured from world X to robot's forward x).

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$$

Similarly, the y-component:

$$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$$

The angular rate is invariant under rotation (it is a scalar):

$$\dot{\theta} = \omega$$

**Step 2 — Compact vector form:**

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

> *Logic:* This is exactly the form $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = B(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{u}$ where $B(\theta)$ depends on the pose. The system is **non-linear** because of the trigonometric dependence on $\theta$.

**Step 3 — Substitute $v$ and $\omega$ from the wheel inputs to get the full input-to-pose map:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{r\cos\theta}{2} & \dfrac{r\cos\theta}{2} \\[4pt] \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{2} & \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{2} \\[4pt] -\dfrac{r}{L} & \dfrac{r}{L} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}_L \\ \dot{\phi}_R \end{bmatrix}$$

This 3×2 matrix is the **complete forward Jacobian** of the differential-drive robot. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Derivation D: The Non-Holonomic Constraint Equation

**Step 1 — Start from the world-frame velocity vector.** Since the robot's motion must be tangent to its forward direction:

$$\dot{\mathbf{p}} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = v\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — The vector $[\cos\theta, \sin\theta]^T$ is parallel to $[\dot{x}, \dot{y}]^T$.** Therefore the cross-product (2D wedge) is zero. Equivalently, the perpendicular component vanishes:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

**Step 3 — Verify non-holonomic nature.**
Suppose we attempt to integrate: $\tan\theta = \dot{y}/\dot{x}$. This is a *first-order differential relation* between positions and orientation. To be holonomic, we would need a pure positional constraint $f(x, y, \theta) = 0$. The integrated form $\tan\theta = dy/dx$ still contains a derivative, so it is **not integrable to a positional constraint**. Hence: **non-holonomic**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem Solving Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Pattern:** This exact problem archetype appears every KTU exam cycle.

**Problem:** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.30$ m. The robot is currently at pose $(x, y, \theta) = (1.0, 2.0, \pi/4)$. The left and right wheel angular velocities are $\dot{\phi}_L = 8$ rad/s and $\dot{\phi}_R = 12$ rad/s. Compute the robot's $(v, \omega, \dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$.

**Step 1 — Compute the wheel linear contact velocities.**

$$v_L = r\dot{\phi}_L = 0.05 \times 8 = 0.40 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v_R = r\dot{\phi}_R = 0.05 \times 12 = 0.60 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the robot's body-frame velocity $(v, \omega)$.**

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.60 + 0.40}{2} = 0.50 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{0.60 - 0.40}{0.30} = 0.667 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Project into world frame.**

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta = 0.50 \times \cos(\pi/4) = 0.50 \times 0.7071 = 0.3536 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta = 0.50 \times \sin(\pi/4) = 0.50 \times 0.7071 = 0.3536 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\dot{\theta} = \omega = 0.667 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Verify non-holonomic constraint.**

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0.3536 \times 0.7071 - 0.3536 \times 0.7071 = 0 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** $(v, \omega) = (0.50 \text{ m/s}, 0.667 \text{ rad/s})$, $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}) = (0.354, 0.354, 0.667)$ in SI units.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Concepts to Cognitive Levels (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy)

| Bloom's Level | Cognitive Demand | Example Question Type | KTU Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Remember (L1)** | Recall formula | "State the forward kinematics equation." | Part A (3 marks) |
| **Understand (L2)** | Explain physical meaning | "Why is the DDR non-holonomic?" | Part A (3 marks) |
| **Apply (L3)** | Use formula in computation | "Compute $(v, \omega)$ given wheel speeds." | Part B (7 marks) |
| **Analyze (L4)** | Derive or invert equations | "Derive inverse kinematics from forward kinematics." | Part B (7 marks) |
| **Evaluate (L5)** | Compare/judge constraints | "Compare holonomic vs non-holonomic motion." | Part A or B |
| **Create (L6)** | Design controller | Rarely asked in ESE, common in MOOCs | Not in ESE |

```mermaid
    B --> C["Forward Kinematics v equals vR plus vL over 2"]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 (3 marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
> **Define a differential-drive mobile robot. Why is it classified as a non-holonomic system?**

**Model Answer:**

A **differential-drive mobile robot** is a wheeled mobile robot whose locomotion is achieved by two independently driven wheels mounted on a common axis. The linear velocity of the robot is determined by the average of the two wheel velocities, and the angular velocity by their difference. The robot's pose in the plane is given by the configuration vector $(x, y, \theta)$.

It is classified as **non-holonomic** because the velocity of the robot is constrained to lie along the axis of the wheels (its instantaneous forward direction). The sideways velocity component must always be zero, which gives the constraint equation $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$. This is a **velocity-level constraint that cannot be integrated into a positional constraint**, which is the precise definition of non-holonomic. Despite having 3 configuration variables, only 2 are independently controllable, so the robot has 2 degrees of freedom.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for non-holonomic justification with the constraint equation.)*

---

> **Question 2 (3 marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **State and explain the non-holonomic constraint equation for a differential-drive robot. Show that the robot cannot move sideways directly.**

**Model Answer:**

The non-holonomic constraint for a differential-drive robot is given by:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The robot can only translate along its instantaneous forward direction, which makes an angle $\theta$ with the global X-axis. The velocity vector $[\dot{x}, \dot{y}]^T$ must therefore be parallel to $[\cos\theta, \sin\theta]^T$. The cross-product (or perpendicular component) being zero means **there is no velocity component perpendicular to the wheel axis**.

**Proof of no sideways motion:** Suppose we attempt a pure sideways motion, i.e., $\dot{x} = 0$, $\dot{y} \neq 0$, while keeping $\theta$ fixed. Substituting into the constraint:

$$0 \cdot \sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = -\dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

Since $\cos\theta \neq 0$ in general (except at $\theta = \pm\pi/2$), this forces $\dot{y} = 0$. Hence the robot **cannot move sideways directly**, confirming the non-holonomic nature. To achieve a sideways displacement, the robot must perform a complex maneuver (e.g., parallel parking sequence involving forward and reverse motions).

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for physical meaning, 1 mark for the proof/discussion.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks Total)

> **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the forward kinematics equations of a differential-drive mobile robot. Starting from the wheel angular velocities $\dot{\phi}_L$ and $\dot{\phi}_R$, show how to obtain the linear velocity $v$, the angular velocity $\omega$, and the world-frame pose rates $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$ in terms of $r$, $L$, and $\theta$. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.04$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.25$ m. The robot is at pose $(2.0 \text{ m}, 1.5 \text{ m}, 30^\circ)$ and the wheel angular velocities are $\dot{\phi}_L = 10$ rad/s and $\dot{\phi}_R = 14$ rad/s. Compute: (i) the wheel linear velocities $v_L$ and $v_R$, (ii) the robot's linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$, (iii) the world-frame pose rates $\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}$, and (iv) verify the non-holonomic constraint. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — *7 marks*

**Step 1 — Wheel contact linear velocities from pure rolling.** *(1 mark)*
With no slipping, the linear velocity of each wheel-ground contact point equals the wheel's tangential velocity:

$$v_L = r\dot{\phi}_L, \qquad v_R = r\dot{\phi}_R$$

**Step 2 — Express each wheel velocity using rigid-body kinematics about the axle.** *(2 marks)*
The robot center lies on the axle, equidistant ($L/2$) from each wheel. Using the rigid-body relation $\mathbf{v}_{point} = \mathbf{v}_{center} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{point/center}$, and noting that motion is along the axle direction:

$$v_R = v + \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2} \qquad v_L = v - \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $v$ and $\omega$.** *(2 marks)*
Adding and subtracting:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R + \dot{\phi}_L)}{2}, \qquad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_R - \dot{\phi}_L)}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Project body velocity into world frame.** *(2 marks)*
Using the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$ and noting that angular velocity is invariant:

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \qquad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \qquad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$

The combined forward kinematics in compact form:

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — *7 marks*

**Step 1 — Compute wheel linear velocities.** *(1.5 marks)*

$$v_L = r\dot{\phi}_L = 0.04 \times 10 = 0.40 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v_R = r\dot{\phi}_R = 0.04 \times 14 = 0.56 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute body-frame velocity.** *(1.5 marks)*

$$v = \frac{0.56 + 0.40}{2} = 0.48 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\omega = \frac{0.56 - 0.40}{0.25} = 0.64 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute world-frame pose rates.** *(2 marks)*
Given $\theta = 30^\circ$, $\cos 30^\circ = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.8660$, $\sin 30^\circ = 0.5$:

$$\dot{x} = 0.48 \times 0.8660 = 0.4157 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\dot{y} = 0.48 \times 0.5 = 0.2400 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\dot{\theta} = 0.64 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the non-holonomic constraint.** *(2 marks)*

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = (0.4157)(0.5) - (0.2400)(0.8660) = 0.2078 - 0.2078 = 0 \;\;\checkmark$$

The constraint is satisfied, confirming the computation is consistent.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks Total)

> **Question B (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the inverse kinematics of a differential-drive robot. Express the required wheel angular velocities $\dot{\phi}_L$ and $\dot{\phi}_R$ in terms of a desired linear velocity $v$, desired angular velocity $\omega$, wheel radius $r$, and wheelbase $L$. Present the result in both scalar and matrix forms. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A differential-drive robot must execute a desired motion of $v = 0.5$ m/s and $\omega = 0.4$ rad/s. The robot has $r = 0.05$ m and $L = 0.30$ m. Compute the required wheel angular velocities. Also determine: (i) the radius of the circular arc the robot will follow, and (ii) the new pose of the robot after $\Delta t = 2$ seconds, assuming it started at the origin with $\theta_0 = 0$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — *7 marks*

**Step 1 — Start from the forward body-level equations.** *(1 mark)*

$$v_R + v_L = 2v \qquad v_R - v_L = \omega L$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $v_R$ and $v_L$.** *(2 marks)*

$$v_R = v + \frac{\omega L}{2} \qquad v_L = v - \frac{\omega L}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to angular velocities using $v = r\dot{\phi}$.** *(2 marks)*

$$\dot{\phi}_R = \frac{v + \omega L/2}{r} \qquad \dot{\phi}_L = \frac{v - \omega L/2}{r}$$

**Step 4 — Compact matrix form.** *(2 marks)*

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\phi}_R \\ \dot{\phi}_L \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & L/2 \\ 1 & -L/2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

This matrix is the inverse Jacobian of the differential-drive robot and maps a body-velocity command to individual wheel commands.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — *7 marks*

**Step 1 — Compute required wheel angular velocities.** *(2 marks)*

$$\dot{\phi}_R = \frac{0.5 + 0.4 \times 0.30/2}{0.05} = \frac{0.5 + 0.06}{0.05} = \frac{0.56}{0.05} = 11.2 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\dot{\phi}_L = \frac{0.5 - 0.4 \times 0.30/2}{0.05} = \frac{0.5 - 0.06}{0.05} = \frac{0.44}{0.05} = 8.8 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute arc radius (ICR distance).** *(2 marks)*

$$R_{\text{arc}} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.5}{0.4} = 1.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute pose update after 2 seconds (Euler integration).** *(3 marks)*
Using forward Euler integration with constant $(v, \omega)$ over $\Delta t = 2$ s:

$$\theta(2) = \theta_0 + \omega \Delta t = 0 + 0.4 \times 2 = 0.8 \text{ rad} \approx 45.84^\circ$$

Since $\omega$ is constant, the trajectory is a true circular arc, so we use the **exact circular arc formulas** (more accurate than Euler for this case):

$$x(t) = x_0 + \frac{v}{\omega}[\sin(\theta_0 + \omega t) - \sin\theta_0] = 0 + \frac{0.5}{0.4}[\sin(0.8) - 0] = 1.25 \times 0.7174 = 0.8967 \text{ m}$$

$$y(t) = y_0 - \frac{v}{\omega}[\cos(\theta_0 + \omega t) - \cos\theta_0] = 0 - 1.25[\cos(0.8) - 1] = -1.25 \times (0.6967 - 1) = 0.3791 \text{ m}$$

**Final Pose:** $(x, y, \theta) = (0.897 \text{ m}, 0.379 \text{ m}, 0.8 \text{ rad})$

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $v_L$ with $\dot{\phi}_L$**: A $v_L$ is linear velocity in m/s; $\dot{\phi}_L$ is angular velocity in rad/s. They differ by a factor of $r$. Always check units in the formula.
>
> 2. **Sign convention in $\omega$**: With the convention $v_R$ on the right side of the forward direction, a **positive** $\omega$ (counterclockwise) corresponds to **$v_L > v_R$**. Many students invert this and lose 1–2 marks per question.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the projection by $\cos\theta, \sin\theta$**: A common mistake is to write $\dot{x} = v$ and $\dot{y} = 0$. This is **only true when $\theta = 0$**. Always project.
>
> 4. **Calling DDR "holonomic"**: It is **NOT holonomic**. Holonomic means you can move instantaneously in any direction. The DDR cannot move sideways. Memorize: Holonomic = omnidirectional instantaneous; Non-holonomic = velocity-constrained.
>
> 5. **Integration error on pose update**: Students often integrate $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ assuming $\theta$ is constant. This is only valid for very small $\Delta t$. For longer durations, use the exact circular arc formulas shown in Question B(b).
>
> 6. **Inverting the matrix incorrectly**: For inverse kinematics, $\dot{\phi}_L = (v - \omega L/2)/r$ (note the **minus** sign) and $\dot{\phi}_R = (v + \omega L/2)/r$ (note the **plus** sign). Mixing them up gives a robot that turns the wrong way.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Differential-Drive Kinematics (Module 3)**
>
> - **Pose vector:** $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^T$ — three configuration variables.
> - **Two control inputs:** $v$ (linear) and $\omega$ (angular) → **2 DOF**, **non-holonomic**.
> - **Forward body kinematics:** $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$, $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$.
> - **Wheel velocity from angular speed:** $v = r\dot{\phi}$ — always convert units consistently.
> - **World-frame forward kinematics:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$.
> - **Inverse kinematics (wheel commands):** $\dot{\phi}_R = (v + \omega L/2)/r$, $\dot{\phi}_L = (v - \omega L/2)/r$.
> - **Non-holonomic constraint:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$. Sideways motion is impossible.
> - **Special cases:** $v_R = v_L$ → straight; $v_R = -v_L$ → spin in place; $v_R \neq v_L$ → arc turn.
> - **Arc radius:** $R = v/\omega$ (for non-zero $\omega$). Radius of curvature.
> - **ICR lies on the axle line** at distance $v/\omega$ from the robot center.
> - **Pose update (exact for constant $\omega$):** Use $\Delta x = (v/\omega)[\sin(\theta_0 + \omega\Delta t) - \sin\theta_0]$, $\Delta y = -(v/\omega)[\cos(\theta_0 + \omega\Delta t) - \cos\theta_0]$.
> - **Pose update (Euler approximation, small $\Delta t$):** $x_{k+1} = x_k + v\cos\theta_k \Delta t$, etc.
> - **Configuration space** is $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$ (position unbounded, orientation is on the circle).
> - **Memorize the inverse Jacobian matrix** $\frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & L/2 \\ 1 & -L/2 \end{bmatrix}$ — examiners test this directly.
> - **Verification trick:** Always plug your $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \theta)$ into the non-holonomic constraint to catch arithmetic errors.
