<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the "Kidnapped Robot Problem" in the context of mobile robot localization. Why is it considered more challenging than global localization?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The Kidnapped Robot Problem refers to a scenario where a mobile robot, already localized, is **suddenly teleported to an arbitrary new location** without any notification to the robot's localization system.
- **(1 mark)** The robot's internal belief becomes **incorrect and inconsistent** with the true pose — the existing posterior distribution is now centered at the wrong location.
- **(1 mark)** It is more challenging than global localization because the robot must (a) **detect** that its belief has failed, (b) **abandon** the current converged estimate, and (c) **re-localize globally** from scratch. Standard EKF fails because it assumes a unimodal Gaussian and cannot recover. Particle filters handle it via random particle injection.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is perceptual aliasing? Give a real-world example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Perceptual aliasing is the phenomenon where **two or more distinct locations in the environment produce identical or highly similar sensor signatures**, making them indistinguishable to the robot.
- **(1 mark)** **Mathematical form:** $p(z_t \mid x_t^{(1)}) \approx p(z_t \mid x_t^{(2)})$ for $x_t^{(1)} \neq x_t^{(2)}$, causing the posterior belief to remain **multimodal**.
- **(1 mark)** **Example:** Two identical office corridors in a building, observed by a 1D LiDAR that only sees parallel walls. Or two visually identical parking spots in a featureless parking lot observed by a downward camera.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]

**(a) [7 marks]** Enumerate and explain **five major challenges in robot localization**, classifying each as sensor-related, environment-related, or algorithmic-related.

**(b) [7 marks]** A differential-drive robot travels **500 m** with **1.5% systematic odometry error**. After 500 m, it observes two landmarks whose true ranges are $r_1 = 5.0$ m and $r_2 = 3.0$ m. The LiDAR has measurement noise $\sigma_r = 0.1$ m for each range. Compute (i) the raw odometric error, and (ii) the fused position error after landmark correction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **"kidnapped robot problem"** in detail. Explain how (i) EKF localization and (ii) Particle Filter localization respond to this challenge. Which is more robust and why?

**(b) [7 marks]** With a block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a robust localization system** that handles perceptual aliasing, sensor noise, and the kidnapped robot problem simultaneously. State the role of the effective sample size $N_{\text{eff}}$ in this architecture.

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Five Major Challenges [7 marks]

1. **Odometry Drift [1 mark]** — *Sensor-related.* Wheel encoders integrate motion; even 1% drift gives 10 m error per km. Drift grows **unboundedly with distance**.

2. **Sensor Noise [1 mark]** — *Sensor-related.* LiDAR, sonar, and camera measurements are corrupted by Gaussian and non-Gaussian noise. The measurement model is $z_t = h(x_t) + \epsilon_t$.

3. **Perceptual Aliasing [2 marks]** — *Environment-related.* Distinct locations yield identical sensor signatures. Causes multimodal posteriors. The robot's belief $bel(x_t)$ contains multiple peaks of comparable height.

4. **Kidnapped Robot Problem [1.5 marks]** — *Operational-related.* Sudden teleportation invalidates the current belief. The system must detect the failure and globally re-localize.

5. **Computational Complexity / Real-time Constraints [1.5 marks]** — *Algorithmic-related.* Particle filters scale as $O(N \cdot M)$; for $N = 5000$ and $M = 10000$, real-time operation demands optimized implementations and possibly GPU acceleration.

#### Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]

**(i) Raw odometric error [2 marks]:**

$$E_{\text{odom}} = D \cdot \alpha = 500 \times 0.015 = 7.5 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Fused error after landmark correction [5 marks]:**

Two independent LiDAR measurements each with $\sigma_r = 0.1$ m. Combined variance (assuming independent measurements to the same unknown position):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{LiDAR,combined}}^2 &= \frac{\sigma_r^2}{2} = \frac{0.01}{2} = 0.005 \text{ m}^2 \\
\sigma_{\text{LiDAR,combined}} &= \sqrt{0.005} \approx 0.0707 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply weighted fusion with odometry:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{odom}}^2 &= (7.5)^2 = 56.25 \text{ m}^2 \\
\sigma_{\text{fused}}^2 &= \frac{56.25 \times 0.005}{56.25 + 0.005} = \frac{0.28125}{56.255} \approx 0.005 \text{ m}^2 \\
\sigma_{\text{fused}} &\approx 0.0707 \text{ m} \approx 7 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The raw error of **7.5 m** is reduced to approximately **7 cm** — over 100× improvement, demonstrating the power of external landmark observations.

---

### Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Kidnapped Robot Problem [7 marks]

- **Definition [2 marks]:** The robot is suddenly moved to an arbitrary new location. The localization system's current belief is now wrong, but the system does not know it.

- **EKF response [2 marks]:** The Extended Kalman Filter assumes a **unimodal Gaussian belief**. After kidnapping, the true pose is far from the predicted mean. The EKF assigns **very low likelihood** to the true location and **converges to the wrong peak**. **It cannot recover** without external intervention.

- **Particle Filter response [2 marks]:** The Particle Filter maintains a set of $N$ weighted hypothesis samples. After kidnapping, the weights of all current particles collapse to near-zero (since they no longer match observations). The **effective sample size $N_{\text{eff}}$ drops sharply**. The algorithm detects this and **resamples**, and a fraction (1–5%) of particles are **randomly re-injected** uniformly across the map. This enables **global recovery**.

- **Conclusion [1 mark]:** Particle Filter is more robust for the kidnapped robot problem.

#### Part (b) — Architecture [7 marks]

Refer to the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.1**.

- **Sensor pre-processing [1 mark]**: Filter raw LiDAR/Camera data to remove outliers.
- **Feature extraction [1 mark]**: Extract landmarks (corners, edges, SIFT/ORB features).
- **Belief update [2 marks]**: Apply Bayes filter — prediction using odometry $u_t$, update using $p(z_t \mid x_t)$.
- **$N_{\text{eff}}$ check [2 marks]**: Compute $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum_i (w_t^{(i)})^2$. If $N_{\text{eff}} < N/2$ (threshold), trigger resampling. This implicitly handles the kidnapped robot problem because a sudden loss of weight diversity signals belief failure.
- **Recovery mode [1 mark]**: If localization fails repeatedly, the system enters a global re-localization mode with uniform particle initialization.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. Students often confuse **global localization** with the **kidnapped robot problem**. They are NOT the same. Global localization assumes the robot is unsure from the start; kidnapping assumes it was sure and got disrupted.
> 2. When asked for the **fused error**, students frequently add variances incorrectly. The correct formula for independent measurements is $\sigma_{\text{fused}}^{-2} = \sigma_1^{-2} + \sigma_2^{-2}$.
> 3. Students forget that **odometric error grows unboundedly with distance** — never saturates. This is the most-missed point in derivation questions.
> 4. Many students do not mention **particle depletion** when discussing the kidnapped robot problem. Random particle injection is the key recovery mechanism.
> 5. In numerical problems, **units must be consistent** (meters vs. centimeters, radians vs. degrees). Marks are deducted silently for unit errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Challenges in Localization**

- **Localization** = estimating pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^T$ from sensors and motion.
- **Four core challenges:** Sensor noise & odometry drift, Perceptual aliasing, Kidnapped robot problem, Dynamic environments.
- **Odometry drift** grows **linearly with distance**: $E = D \cdot \alpha$. A 1% drift = 10 m error per km.
- **Perceptual aliasing** causes **multimodal posteriors**; distinguishable mathematically by $p(z_t \mid x^{(1)}) \approx p(z_t \mid x^{(2)})$.
- **Kidnapped robot problem** = sudden pose discontinuity; requires **belief recovery** — Particle Filters handle it via random particle injection; **EKF cannot recover**.
- **Position tracking** (known initial pose) is easier than **global localization** (unknown initial pose).
- **SLAM** is the hardest case — localization and mapping must be solved **simultaneously** (chicken-and-egg).
- **Bayes Filter** is the unifying mathematical framework: prediction + measurement update.
- **Effective sample size $N_{\text{eff}}$** is the key health indicator of a particle filter. If $N_{\text{eff}} < N/2$, resample.
- **LiDAR landmark fusion** reduces error dramatically — from meters (raw odometry) to centimeters.
- **Real-world tolerances:** Warehouse AMRs need $\pm 2$ cm; autonomous cars need $\pm 10$ cm; sub-millimeter for surgical robots.
- **Algorithm robustness ranking (most to least robust to challenges):** Particle Filter > Grid-based Markov > EKF > Dead Reckoning.
- **Computational cost ranking (lowest to highest):** Dead Reckoning < EKF < Particle Filter < Grid-based.
- **Always** mention **uncertainty propagation** in long answers — KTU examiners award bonus marks for probabilistic thinking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: An Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT721 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** An Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Odometry** is the most widely used method for determining the instantaneous position of a mobile robot by measuring the rotation of its wheels (typically using optical encoders) and integrating the resulting incremental motions over time. The process of integrating wheel rotations to estimate the change in position and orientation of a robot is called **dead reckoning**.

However, the incremental motion estimates produced by wheel encoders are inherently noisy due to imperfections in the physical world (wheel slip, floor unevenness, calibration errors). An **Error Model for Odometric Position Estimation** is therefore a formal mathematical framework that characterizes, quantifies, and propagates the uncertainties associated with the encoder-derived pose estimate, ultimately producing a confidence region (often an ellipse) around the estimated robot pose.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> The error model discussed in the canonical literature (Borenstein & Feng, 1996, *Measurement and Correction of Systematic Odometry Errors in Mobile Robots*) is the de-facto reference. The model separates errors into two fundamentally different categories: **Systematic (Bias) Errors** and **Non-Systematic (Noise) Errors**, and treats them with completely different mathematical tools (deterministic functions vs. stochastic covariance propagation).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are blindfolded in a large, unfamiliar hall and asked to walk to the opposite wall. You can only count your steps (each step ≈ one wheel encoder tick).

- If your left leg is slightly longer than your right, every step you take will curve you gently to the right. After 100 steps, you may be facing a completely different direction. This is a **Systematic Error** — it has a clear, repeatable cause and accumulates *predictably*.
- If the floor is slippery, sometimes your foot slips a little — a fraction of a step. This is a **Non-Systematic Error** — it is random, unpredictable, and must be modeled using *statistics*.

The **Error Model** is the math notebook in which you keep track of *how badly* your blindfold-walk is going. After 100 steps, the model does not just say, "You are at point P"; it says, "You are at point P, and we are 95% confident that you are within this elliptical region around P." That ellipse is your robot's **uncertainty bound** — and it grows the longer you walk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** Examiners love to test whether the student understands that *systematic* and *non-systematic* errors require *different* mathematical treatments. Conflating them is the #1 reason students lose marks on this topic.

| Term | Meaning | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **b** | Wheelbase (distance between drive wheels) | **mm** |
| **r** | Nominal wheel radius | **mm** |
| **N** | Encoder resolution (ticks per wheel revolution) | **ticks/rev** |
| **c<sub>m</sub>** | Distance traveled per encoder tick | **mm/tick** |
| **D** | Total distance traveled by the robot | **mm** or **m** |
| **σ** | Standard deviation of a random variable | **same as variable** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Classes of Odometric Error

### 2.1.1 Systematic Errors (Deterministic Biases)

Systematic errors are *repeatable* and *deterministic* — the same cause produces the same magnitude of error every time. Because they are deterministic, they can (in principle) be measured and compensated for. There are four primary systematic error sources:

1. **Unequal Wheel Diameters (E<sub>d</sub>):** Even tiny manufacturing tolerances mean the two drive wheels rarely have *exactly* the same diameter. If one is slightly larger, the robot will curve toward the smaller-diameter wheel.
2. **Uncertainty About the Wheelbase (E<sub>b</sub>):** If the wheelbase used in software differs from the true mechanical wheelbase, the heading update $\Delta\theta$ is wrong.
3. **Wheel Radius Miscalibration (E<sub>r</sub>):** The encoder resolution is converted to distance using a nominal wheel radius. A miscalibrated radius biases *every* distance reading.
4. **Misalignment of Wheels (E<sub>t</sub>):** The effective rolling axis of the wheels may not be perpendicular to the wheelbase, introducing a small lateral velocity component.

### 2.1.2 Non-Systematic Errors (Random Noise)

Non-systematic errors are *unpredictable* and *non-repeatable*. They can only be characterized statistically. The dominant sources are:

- **Travel Over Uneven Floor (E<sub>s</sub>):** Bumps, cracks, and carpet edges cause instantaneous wheel slip or bounce.
- **Travel Over Unexpected Objects:** Running over a small object lifts a wheel slightly, changing the effective wheel contact point.
- **Wheel Slippage (E<sub>s</sub>):** Slick surfaces, acceleration-induced slip, and skidding during turning.
- **External Forces:** Contact with obstacles, people, or cables.

Because these are stochastic, we model them with **probability distributions** — typically assuming a Gaussian (Normal) distribution, so the math reduces to propagating **covariance matrices**.

## 2.2 Why We Need a Separate Model for Each Class

- *Systematic* errors accumulate in a **bounded but unbounded** way — they grow with the *square root* of the distance traveled for some components and linearly for others. They cause the robot's *mean* pose to drift away from the truth.
- *Non-systematic* errors accumulate as a **random walk** — the uncertainty region grows as the *square root* of distance traveled. They cause the *variance* of the pose to grow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Insight:** A robot can be *calibrated* (to minimize systematic errors) but it can never be made perfectly immune to non-systematic errors — that is why every industrial mobile robot (AGV, AMR) fuses odometry with absolute sensors (LiDAR, cameras, beacons) in a Kalman Filter or Particle Filter.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\Delta S = \frac{\Delta S_r + \Delta S_l}{2}$ | Mean distance increment | Odometry update |
| 2 | $\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta S_r - \Delta S_l}{b}$ | Heading increment | Odometry update |
| 3 | $x' = x + \Delta S \cos\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$ | Position update in x | Odometry update |
| 4 | $y' = y + \Delta S \sin\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)$ | Position update in y | Odometry update |
| 5 | $\theta' = \theta + \Delta\theta$ | Heading update | Odometry update |
| 6 | $x_\varepsilon(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\cos\theta + \tfrac{L\,\sin\theta}{b}\right) E_d \cdot D$ | Systematic position error, x | Error model |
| 7 | $y_\varepsilon(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\sin\theta - \tfrac{L\,\cos\theta}{b}\right) E_d \cdot D$ | Systematic position error, y | Error model |
| 8 | $\theta_\varepsilon(t) = \tfrac{E_d}{b} \cdot D$ | Systematic heading error | Error model |
| 9 | $\Sigma = J \, \Sigma_{\text{inc}} \, J^{T}$ | Linearized covariance propagation | Non-systematic model |
| 10 | $\sigma_{xx} = (\Delta x)^2 \sigma_{\Delta x}^2 + (\Delta y)^2 \sigma_{\Delta y}^2$ | Variance in x over one step | Non-systematic model |
| 11 | $\sigma_{yy} = (\Delta y)^2 \sigma_{\Delta x}^2 + (\Delta x)^2 \sigma_{\Delta y}^2$ | Variance in y over one step | Non-systematic model |
| 12 | $\sigma_{xy} = \Delta x \Delta y\!\left(\sigma_{\Delta x}^2 - \sigma_{\Delta y}^2\right)$ | Covariance xy over one step | Non-systematic model |
| 13 | $a = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\sigma_{xx}+\sigma_{yy}+\sqrt{\Delta}\right)}$ | Semi-major axis of error ellipse | Uncertainty bound |
| 14 | $b = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\sigma_{xx}+\sigma_{yy}-\sqrt{\Delta}\right)}$ | Semi-minor axis of error ellipse | Uncertainty bound |
| 15 | $\phi = \tfrac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left(\tfrac{2\sigma_{xy}}{\sigma_{xx}-\sigma_{yy}}\right)$ | Orientation of error ellipse | Uncertainty bound |
| 16 | $\Delta = (\sigma_{xx}-\sigma_{yy})^2 + 4\sigma_{xy}^2$ | Discriminant of ellipse equation | Uncertainty bound |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention note:** In the KTU board exam, $E_d$ is usually defined as the *relative* difference in wheel diameters (a dimensionless ratio), and $D$ is the *total* distance traveled. The factor $L$ in Eqs. 6–7 represents the displacement along the robot's path. Always re-read the question's symbol glossary before writing your answer.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

This error model is the mathematical backbone of:

- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics):** Fusing wheel odometry with LiDAR scan matching.
- **Autonomous Cars (Waymo, Cruise):** Dead reckoning between GPS fixes; the wheel-encoder error model bounds the search radius for scan-matching.
- **Mars Rovers (NASA Curiosity, Perseverance):** Visual odometry + wheel odometry fusion with rigorous uncertainty ellipses plotted on the operator's terrain map.
- **Surgical Robots (da Vinci):** Tool-tip position estimation from joint encoders with covariance-propagated uncertainty bounds to assist the surgeon.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Odometry Update Equations

Consider a **differential-drive robot** with two independently driven wheels separated by a wheelbase $b$. The left wheel rotates by an angle $\Delta\phi_l$ and the right wheel by $\Delta\phi_r$ over one sampling interval. The corresponding linear displacements of the contact points are:

$$\Delta S_l = r \cdot \Delta\phi_l, \qquad \Delta S_r = r \cdot \Delta\phi_r$$

Because the two wheels trace arcs of the **same instantaneous center of rotation (ICR)**, the robot's motion can be described as a pure translation $\Delta S$ along the bisector of the wheelbase followed by a pure rotation $\Delta\theta$ in place. The instantaneous curvature radius is $R$.

**Step 1 — Express the arc lengths in terms of the ICR radius:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S_r &= (R + b/2)\Delta\theta \\
\Delta S_l &= (R - b/2)\Delta\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract to find $\Delta\theta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S_r - \Delta S_l &= b \cdot \Delta\theta \\
\Rightarrow \Delta\theta &= \frac{\Delta S_r - \Delta S_l}{b}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add to find $\Delta S$ (average arc length):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S_r + \Delta S_l &= 2R\Delta\theta \\
\Rightarrow \Delta S &= \frac{\Delta S_r + \Delta S_l}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the midpoint orientation as $\theta + \Delta\theta/2$ (midpoint rule for arc integration):**

The displacement of the robot's reference point in the world frame is then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x &= \Delta S \cos\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
\Delta y &= \Delta S \sin\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Update the pose:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + \Delta x \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k + \Delta y \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \Delta\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The midpoint rule $\theta + \Delta\theta/2$ is preferred over the simple Euler rule ($\theta$ only) because it makes the odometry *time-reversible* — i.e., the inverse transformation recovers the original pose to numerical precision. This property is exploited in graph-SLAM back-ends.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Systematic Error Model (Borenstein & Feng, 1996)

Assume the robot travels a total distance $D$ along a straight path at heading $\theta$ relative to the global X-axis. The dominant systematic error is the **unequal wheel diameter error** $E_d$, defined as the *difference* in wheel diameters normalized by the *average* wheel diameter (dimensionless).

We treat the robot as if the **left** wheel has diameter $d_l$ and the **right** wheel has diameter $d_r$, with $d_l \ne d_r$. The average is $\bar{d} = (d_l + d_r)/2$, and the relative error is:

$$
E_d = \frac{d_r - d_l}{\bar{d}}
$$

**Step 1 — True distances traveled by each wheel:**

The robot commands both wheels to rotate by the same encoder count, so the *commanded* linear distance at each wheel is identical: $\Delta S_{cmd}$. However, the *true* distance covered differs because the effective radii differ:

$$
\Delta S_r^{true} = \Delta S_{cmd}\!\left(1 + \frac{E_d}{2}\right), \qquad \Delta S_l^{true} = \Delta S_{cmd}\!\left(1 - \frac{E_d}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Heading drift per unit command:**

Using the odometry update equation for heading:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\theta &= \frac{\Delta S_r^{true} - \Delta S_l^{true}}{b} = \frac{\Delta S_{cmd} \cdot E_d}{b}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cumulative heading error over total distance $D$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_\varepsilon(D) &= \sum_{i} \Delta\theta_i = \frac{E_d}{b} \sum_{i}\Delta S_{cmd,i} = \frac{E_d}{b} \cdot D
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Position error in the world frame:**

If the robot moves along heading $\theta$ for a distance $L$ (the actual path length it *thought* it was on), but the heading is drifting by $\theta_\varepsilon$ linearly with distance, the position error in the world frame can be obtained by integrating the drift. For the case of a *constant* commanded heading $\theta_c$ and a path of length $L$:

The instantaneous heading at arc length $s$ is $\theta_c + \frac{E_d}{b} s$. Integrating the world-frame displacements:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_\varepsilon &= \int_{0}^{L} \cos\!\left(\theta_c + \frac{E_d}{b}s\right) ds - L\cos\theta_c \\
y_\varepsilon &= \int_{0}^{L} \sin\!\left(\theta_c + \frac{E_d}{b}s\right) ds - L\sin\theta_c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Closed-form evaluation of the integrals:**

Using the standard integrals $\int \cos(\alpha s + \beta) ds = \frac{1}{\alpha}\sin(\alpha s + \beta)$ and similarly for sine, with $\alpha = E_d/b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_\varepsilon &= \frac{b}{E_d}\!\left[\sin\!\left(\theta_c + \frac{E_d}{b}L\right) - \sin\theta_c\right] - L\cos\theta_c \\
y_\varepsilon &= -\frac{b}{E_d}\!\left[\cos\!\left(\theta_c + \frac{E_d}{b}L\right) - \cos\theta_c\right] - L\sin\theta_c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Small-angle linearization (for small $E_d$ or small $L$):**

If $\frac{E_d L}{b} \ll 1$, the small-angle approximations $\sin\epsilon \approx \epsilon$ and $\cos\epsilon \approx 1 - \epsilon^2/2$ yield the *linear* (and far more commonly used) form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_\varepsilon(t) &\approx \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\cos\theta_c + \frac{L\sin\theta_c}{b}\right) E_d \cdot D \\
y_\varepsilon(t) &\approx \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\sin\theta_c - \frac{L\cos\theta_c}{b}\right) E_d \cdot D
\end{aligned}
$$

These are the canonical **systematic odometric error equations** appearing in the KTU syllabus.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Non-Systematic Error Covariance Propagation

For a *single* incremental motion $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ with known variances $\sigma^2_{\Delta x}$ and $\sigma^2_{\Delta y}$ and covariance $\sigma_{\Delta x \Delta y}$, the variance of the cumulative position after $N$ steps is computed under the assumption of **independence between steps** (random walk):

**Step 1 — Sum of independent random vectors:**

If $X = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\Delta x_i$ and $Y = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\Delta y_i$, and the increments are independent and identically distributed, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2_{xx} &= N\,\sigma^2_{\Delta x}, \quad \sigma^2_{yy} = N\,\sigma^2_{\Delta y}, \quad \sigma_{xy} = N\,\sigma_{\Delta x \Delta y}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of total distance $D$ and number of steps $N$:**

For a constant stride $\Delta s$ and constant direction $\alpha$ (i.e., $\Delta x = \Delta s \cos\alpha$, $\Delta y = \Delta s \sin\alpha$), the variances of the *increments* $\sigma^2_{\Delta x}$ and $\sigma^2_{\Delta y}$ are proportional to the incremental *translational* variance $k_{\text{tr}}$:

$$
\sigma^2_{\Delta x} = k_{\text{tr}}\cos^2\alpha, \qquad \sigma^2_{\Delta y} = k_{\text{tr}}\sin^2\alpha
$$

Substituting and summing over $N$ steps with $N \cdot \Delta s = D$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2_{xx} &= N\,k_{\text{tr}}\cos^2\alpha = \frac{D}{\Delta s}\,k_{\text{tr}}\cos^2\alpha \\
&= D\,k_{\text{tr}}\cos\alpha \cdot \frac{\cos\alpha}{\Delta s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Recognising $\Delta x = \Delta s \cos\alpha$ and $\Delta y = \Delta s \sin\alpha$, this simplifies to the **chained-variance form**:

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma^2_{xx} = \sum_{i} \left[(\Delta x_i)^2\,\sigma^2_{\Delta x} + (\Delta y_i)^2\,\sigma^2_{\Delta y}\right]\;}
$$

This is the form that the examiner will most often ask you to reproduce.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Error Ellipse (Confidence Region)

The robot's pose uncertainty is a 2D Gaussian with covariance matrix:

$$
\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma^2_{xx} & \sigma_{xy} \\ \sigma_{xy} & \sigma^2_{yy} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The 1-$\sigma$ confidence contour is the set of points $\mathbf{p}$ for which $\mathbf{p}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{p} = 1$. The eigenvalues of $\Sigma$ are:

$$
\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma^2_{xx} + \sigma^2_{yy}}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma^2_{xx}-\sigma^2_{yy}}{2}\right)^2 + \sigma^2_{xy}}
$$

The square roots of the eigenvalues are the semi-axes of the ellipse:

$$
\boxed{\;a = \sqrt{\lambda_1}, \qquad b = \sqrt{\lambda_2}\;}
$$

The orientation of the major axis is:

$$
\boxed{\;\phi = \frac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left(\frac{2\sigma_{xy}}{\sigma^2_{xx} - \sigma^2_{yy}}\right)\;}
$$

These three quantities $(a, b, \phi)$ define the **position error ellipse** and are what appear on the KTU examiner's marking scheme.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Sources → Effect on Pose

| Error Source | Symbol | Category | Effect on $x$ | Effect on $y$ | Effect on $\theta$ | Compensation Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Unequal wheel diameter | $E_d$ | Systematic | Linear in $D$ | Linear in $D$ | Linear in $D$ | Autocalibration (U-turn test) |
| Wheelbase error | $E_b$ | Systematic | Quadratic in $D$ | Quadratic in $D$ | Linear in $D$ | Mechanical measurement |
| Wheel radius | $E_r$ | Systematic | Linear in $D$ | Linear in $D$ | None | Encoder calibration |
| Wheel misalignment | $E_t$ | Systematic | Linear in $D$ | Linear in $D$ | Linear in $D$ | Mechanical alignment |
| Floor unevenness | $E_s$ | Non-systematic | $\propto\sqrt{D}$ | $\propto\sqrt{D}$ | $\propto\sqrt{D}$ | Suspension / filtering |
| Wheel slip | $E_s$ | Non-systematic | $\propto\sqrt{D}$ | $\propto\sqrt{D}$ | $\propto\sqrt{D}$ | Slip-detector / IMU fusion |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Architecture of the Odometric Error Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Wheel Encoder Ticks] --> B[Encoder Resolution N]
    B --> C[Distance Increment per Wheel]
    C --> D[Odometry Update Equations]
    D --> E[Estimated Pose x y theta]
    F[Systematic Error Sources] --> G[Deterministic Bias Functions]
    G --> H[Position Error x_eps y_eps theta_eps]
    H --> I[Corrected Pose]
    E --> I
    I --> J[Kalman Filter or Scan Matcher]
    K[Non-Systematic Error Sources] --> L[Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    L --> M[Random Walk Propagation]
    M --> N[Error Ellipse a b phi]
    I --> N
    N --> O[Final Pose with Uncertainty Bound]
```

## 4.2 Decomposition of the Two Error Classes

```mermaid
graph LR
    err[Odometric Error]
    err --> sys[Systematic Errors]
    err --> nonsys[Non-Systematic Errors]
    sys --> ed[Unequal Wheel Diameter E_d]
    sys --> eb[Wheelbase Error E_b]
    sys --> er[Wheel Radius E_r]
    sys --> et[Wheel Misalignment E_t]
    nonsys --> es1[Floor Unevenness]
    nonsys --> es2[Wheel Slippage]
    nonsys --> es3[External Disturbances]
    ed --> fn1[Deterministic Function f E_d D]
    eb --> fn2[Deterministic Function f E_b D]
    es1 --> cov1[Covariance Propagation]
    es2 --> cov1
    es3 --> cov1
    fn1 --> out[Pose Error]
    fn2 --> out
    cov1 --> out
```

## 4.3 Propagation Pipeline from Encoder to Error Ellipse

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Step 1: Read Encoder Counts] --> s2[Step 2: Convert to Wheel Distances]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3: Apply Odometry Update]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4: Compute Incremental Covariances]
    s4 --> s5[Step 5: Sum Covariances Random Walk]
    s5 --> s6[Step 6: Eigen Decomposition]
    s6 --> s7[Step 7: Extract Semi Axes a b and Orientation phi]
    s7 --> s8[Step 8: Plot Error Ellipse Around Pose]
```

## 4.4 Differential-Drive Kinematic Schematic (Text-Based Fallback)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph RobotChassis[Robot Chassis]
        WL[Left Wheel r l] -- b distance --> WR[Right Wheel r r]
        C[Reference Point x y theta] --- WL
        C --- WR
    end
    WL --> encL[Encoder Left ticks]
    WR --> encR[Encoder Right ticks]
    encL --> proc[Microcontroller Odometry]
    encR --> proc
    proc --> pose[Pose Estimate x y theta]
    proc --> cov[Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    pose --> out[Final Estimate with Ellipse]
    cov --> out
```
