# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently *forget to include the $\Delta\theta/2$ midpoint rule* in the odometry update — the model that the examiner marks uses the midpoint form. Euler's rule $\theta$ alone loses 2 marks.
> - The systematic error equations are valid only in the **small-angle (linearised) regime**. If the question explicitly says "linearised", you must use the form with $E_d \cdot D$ as a factor. Writing the closed-form sine/cosine version unprompted will earn 0 marks for that sub-part.
> - The error ellipse orientation $\phi$ uses $\arctan(2\sigma_{xy}/(\sigma_{xx}^2 - \sigma_{yy}^2))$ — a common mistake is to put the denominator as $\sigma_{xx} - \sigma_{yy}$ instead of the *squared* difference. Memorize the exact form.
> - For non-systematic errors, students often write "uncertainty grows linearly with distance" — this is **wrong**; it grows as the **square root** of distance (random walk).

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Bank]**
Differentiate between **systematic** and **non-systematic** odometric errors in a mobile robot. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Systematic Errors | Non-Systematic Errors |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nature | Deterministic, repeatable | Random, unpredictable |
| Cause | Calibration / mechanical biases | Floor, slip, obstacles |
| Effect on pose | Biases the **mean** pose | Inflates the **variance** of the pose |
| Compensation | Measured and corrected | Modelled statistically (covariance) |

**Examples of systematic errors (1 mark):** unequal wheel diameters, miscalibrated wheelbase.
**Examples of non-systematic errors (1 mark):** wheel slip on a wet floor, running over a small object.
**Distinguishing definition (1 mark):** as tabulated above.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question Bank]**
Define the **position error ellipse** used in odometric error modelling. What do its semi-major axis $a$, semi-minor axis $b$, and orientation $\phi$ represent physically?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The position error ellipse is a 1-$\sigma$ confidence region in the 2D plane around the estimated robot pose, derived from the pose covariance matrix $\Sigma$. (1 mark)
- **Semi-major axis $a$:** the direction in which the position uncertainty is largest; its length equals the 1-$\sigma$ standard deviation along that direction. (1 mark)
- **Semi-minor axis $b$:** the direction in which the position uncertainty is smallest; its length equals the 1-$\sigma$ standard deviation along the perpendicular direction.
- **Orientation $\phi$:** the angle of the major axis relative to the global X-axis, indicating *the direction in which the robot is most uncertain*. (1 mark)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Internal choice is provided. Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Derive the **odometry update equations** for a differential-drive robot, starting from the linear displacements of the left and right wheels. Clearly state the role of the **midpoint rule** in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot with wheelbase $b = 500$ mm travels a straight path of length $D = 10$ m at a constant commanded heading $\theta_c = 30°$ relative to the global X-axis. The relative wheel-diameter error is $E_d = 0.02$ (i.e., 2%). Using the **linearised systematic error model**, compute the position error $(x_\varepsilon, y_\varepsilon)$ and the heading error $\theta_\varepsilon$ at the end of the path. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

Let the left and right wheel linear displacements over one sampling interval be $\Delta S_l = r\,\Delta\phi_l$ and $\Delta S_r = r\,\Delta\phi_r$.

The instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) is at perpendicular distance $R$ from the reference point. The two wheels trace arcs of radii $R-b/2$ and $R+b/2$ respectively. Therefore:

$$
\Delta S_l = (R - b/2)\Delta\theta, \qquad \Delta S_r = (R + b/2)\Delta\theta
$$

Solving the two equations simultaneously:

$$
\Delta S = \frac{\Delta S_r + \Delta S_l}{2} = R\Delta\theta, \qquad \Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta S_r - \Delta S_l}{b}
$$

The displacement of the reference point in the world frame is the integral along the arc. Approximating the arc by its midpoint direction $\theta + \Delta\theta/2$ (the **midpoint rule**), the incremental displacement is:

$$
\Delta x = \Delta S \cos\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right), \qquad \Delta y = \Delta S \sin\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)
$$

**Role of the midpoint rule (2 marks):** The midpoint rule uses the orientation at the *midpoint* of the arc, not at the start. This makes the transformation *time-reversible* (its inverse recovers the original pose to numerical precision), unlike the simple Euler rule that uses the start orientation only. The midpoint rule is therefore the standard in modern odometry implementations.

The complete update is then:

$$
x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta S \cos\!\left(\theta_k + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right), \quad y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta S \sin\!\left(\theta_k + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right), \quad \theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta
$$

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:** $b = 500$ mm $= 0.5$ m, $D = 10$ m, $\theta_c = 30°$, $E_d = 0.02$. (0.5 marks)

**Step 1 — Heading error:**

$$
\theta_\varepsilon = \frac{E_d}{b} \cdot D = \frac{0.02}{0.5} \times 10 = 0.4 \text{ rad} \quad (\approx 22.92°)
$$

**Step 2 — Position errors using the linearised systematic model:**

The path length $L$ in the linearised model is taken to be the *commanded* path length, $L = D = 10$ m. With $\cos 30° = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$ and $\sin 30° = 0.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_\varepsilon &= \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\cos\theta_c + \frac{L\sin\theta_c}{b}\right) E_d \cdot D \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\!\left(0.866 + \frac{10 \times 0.5}{0.5}\right) \times 0.02 \times 10 \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\!\left(0.866 + 10\right) \times 0.2 \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\times 10.866 \times 0.2 \\
&= 1.0866 \text{ m} \quad (\approx 1086.6 \text{ mm})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_\varepsilon &= \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\sin\theta_c - \frac{L\cos\theta_c}{b}\right) E_d \cdot D \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\!\left(0.5 - \frac{10 \times 0.866}{0.5}\right) \times 0.02 \times 10 \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\!\left(0.5 - 17.32\right) \times 0.2 \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\times (-16.82) \times 0.2 \\
&= -1.682 \text{ m} \quad (\approx -1682 \text{ mm})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):** The robot, which *thinks* it has moved exactly 10 m at $30°$, has actually drifted to a position approximately $1.087$ m ahead in X and $1.682$ m behind in Y, with a heading error of $0.4$ rad ($\approx 22.9°$). The total position error magnitude is $\sqrt{1.087^2 + 1.682^2} \approx 2.00$ m, i.e., a 20% positional error from a 2% wheel-diameter error — illustrating the dramatic amplification of systematic odometric errors with distance.

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **two classes of non-systematic odometric errors** and derive the **covariance propagation** equation showing that the variance in position grows linearly with the number of steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot makes $N = 100$ straight-line steps, each of length $\Delta s = 50$ mm along the global X-axis. The standard deviation of each step's length is $\sigma_{\Delta s} = 1$ mm. Compute the **standard deviation of the cumulative X-position** after 100 steps and contrast it with the result you would obtain if the errors were *systematic* (i.e., perfectly correlated). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Two classes of non-systematic errors (2 marks):**
1. **Terrain-induced errors:** uneven floor, carpet edges, cracks, and small obstacles. These cause instantaneous, transient changes in wheel contact and cannot be predicted from sensor data.
2. **Slip-induced errors:** wheel slip due to slick surfaces, sharp turns, or acceleration. These cause a fraction of the commanded motion to be lost.

Both are *random* in nature and are therefore modelled using a probability distribution — by convention, a **zero-mean Gaussian** with variance proportional to the incremental displacement.

**Derivation of covariance propagation (5 marks):**

Let the $i$-th incremental displacement be $(\Delta x_i, \Delta y_i)$ with covariance matrix $\Sigma_i$. The cumulative position is:

$$
X = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta x_i, \qquad Y = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta y_i
$$

Since the increments are *independent* (the slip event in step $i$ is uncorrelated with that in step $j \ne i$), the variances add:

$$
\sigma^2_{xx} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\sigma^2_{\Delta x_i}, \qquad \sigma^2_{yy} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\sigma^2_{\Delta y_i}, \qquad \sigma_{xy} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\sigma_{\Delta x_i \Delta y_i}
$$

For a straight-line path with constant step size, all $\sigma^2_{\Delta x_i}$ are equal. Let $\sigma^2_{\Delta x_i} = \sigma^2_{\Delta x}$. Then:

$$
\sigma^2_{xx} = N\,\sigma^2_{\Delta x}
$$

This is the **random-walk growth** of variance. Equivalently, since $D = N \cdot \Delta s$:

$$
\sigma^2_{xx} = \frac{D}{\Delta s}\,\sigma^2_{\Delta x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_{xx} \propto \sqrt{D}
$$

The standard deviation grows as the *square root* of distance, while the variance grows *linearly* with the number of steps (or equivalently, linearly with distance for constant step size).

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $N = 100$, $\Delta s = 50$ mm, $\sigma_{\Delta s} = 1$ mm.

**Case 1 — Independent (non-systematic) errors:**

Since all steps are along the X-axis, $\Delta y_i = 0$ for all $i$ and $\sigma_{\Delta y_i} = 0$. The variance in the cumulative X position is:

$$
\sigma^2_{xx} = N\,\sigma^2_{\Delta s} = 100 \times (1)^2 = 100 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\sigma_{xx} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

**Case 2 — Perfectly correlated (systematic) errors:**

If every step has the *same* signed error $\delta$, then the errors add coherently:

$$
X_{error} = N \cdot \delta, \qquad \sigma^2_{xx,\text{corr}} = N^2\,\sigma^2_{\Delta s} = 10000 \times 1 = 10000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\sigma_{xx,\text{corr}} = 100 \text{ mm}
$$

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Error Type | $\sigma_{xx}$ after 100 steps |
| :--- | :--- |
| Independent (non-systematic) | **10 mm** |
| Perfectly correlated (systematic) | **100 mm** |

The systematic case is **10× worse** than the independent case — a 10× amplification that equals $\sqrt{N}$ in *standard deviation*. This is the mathematical reason why systematic errors dominate long-range odometry: even a 0.2% wheel-diameter mismatch (which is sub-millimetre per step) accumulates to metres over a 10 m path. The moral is that **calibration (eliminating systematic error) is far more valuable than noise reduction** for long-range navigation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Odometry** is dead reckoning using wheel encoders; it is simple, cheap, and high-bandwidth, but unbounded in error growth.
- The error model has **two classes**: *systematic* (deterministic, correctable) and *non-systematic* (random, statistical).
- The **odometry update equations** use the midpoint rule: $\Delta x = \Delta S\cos(\theta + \Delta\theta/2)$, $\Delta y = \Delta S\sin(\theta + \Delta\theta/2)$, $\Delta\theta = (\Delta S_r - \Delta S_l)/b$, $\Delta S = (\Delta S_r + \Delta S_l)/2$.
- The **four canonical systematic error sources** are $E_d$ (unequal wheel diameters), $E_b$ (wheelbase error), $E_r$ (wheel radius), and $E_t$ (wheel misalignment).
- The **linearised systematic error model** gives $x_\varepsilon$, $y_\varepsilon$, and $\theta_\varepsilon$ as linear functions of $E_d$ and $D$ (or $L$).
- The **non-systematic error model** propagates a 2×2 covariance matrix using the random-walk rule: variance grows linearly with the number of steps, but standard deviation grows as the square root of distance.
- The **error ellipse** semi-axes are the square roots of the eigenvalues of $\Sigma$, and its orientation is $\phi = \tfrac{1}{2}\arctan(2\sigma_{xy}/(\sigma^2_{xx} - \sigma^2_{yy}))$.
- A 1% wheel-diameter error can cause ~10% position error over a 10 m path — *systematic errors dominate* in long-range applications.
- The error ellipse is the standard uncertainty visualization in KTU board answers; always plot it on the final pose.
- In real systems, the error model is the basis for the **prediction step** of the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) used in SLAM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization-Based Navigation versus Programmed Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT721 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Localization-Based Navigation versus Programmed Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Robot Navigation Strategies

In mobile robotics, **Navigation** refers to the science and technology of determining the position and orientation of a robot (localization) and planning a collision-free path from a start location to a goal location within an environment. The two fundamentally different paradigms for executing this navigation are:

### A. Programmed Solutions (Open-Loop / Dead-Reckoning-Based Navigation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus):**
> *Programmed Solutions* are pre-computed, deterministic navigation strategies in which the robot executes a fixed sequence of motions, velocities, or waypoints that have been **pre-stored in memory or hand-coded** by the programmer. The robot has **no feedback loop** about its actual position in the environment — it simply "trusts" that executing the commands will lead it to the goal.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Open-loop control architecture** — no position feedback is used.
- Trajectory is **pre-determined offline** using kinematic equations.
- Relies entirely on **internal sensors** (wheel encoders, gyroscopes).
- Output action depends **only on input command and time**, not on perceived state.
- Error grows **monotonically and unboundedly** with distance traveled.

### B. Localization-Based Navigation (Closed-Loop / Map-Based Navigation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus):**
> *Localization-Based Navigation* is a feedback-driven, sensor-reactive navigation strategy in which the robot continuously estimates its pose (position + orientation) within a known or partially known map by fusing **internal odometry** with **external sensor measurements** (LIDAR, vision, GPS, beacons, ultrasonic), and uses this estimate to dynamically adjust its motion plan.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Closed-loop control architecture** — sensor feedback continuously corrects pose.
- Requires a **map** (metric, topological, or hybrid) of the environment.
- Uses **Bayesian probabilistic frameworks** (Kalman Filter, Particle Filter, Markov Localization).
- Output action depends on **current estimated state**, not just input command.
- Errors are **bounded** as long as sensors and map remain reliable.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS vs Memory" Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuition Builder):**
> Imagine you must drive from your college to a hotel in an unfamiliar city.
>
> - **Programmed Solution** = You memorized the directions ("go straight 2 km, turn left at the second signal, then 500 m"). You don't look at any landmarks. If a road is closed or you miss a turn, you are **completely lost** because you have no idea where you actually are.
>
> - **Localization-Based Navigation** = You have Google Maps open. It continuously shows your blue dot on the road. Even if you miss a turn, the map **instantly re-localizes** you and gives a new route. You are **never truly lost** because feedback keeps correcting your estimate.
>
> The mobile robot equivalent: the *programmed robot* is a blindfolded driver following memorized instructions; the *localizing robot* is a driver with a live GPS that keeps re-anchoring position.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range (Indoor Robot) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wheel diameter | $d$ | m | $0.05 - 0.30$ |
| Encoder resolution (ticks/rev) | $N$ | ticks/rev | $100 - 20{,}000$ |
| Pose uncertainty (programmed) | $\sigma_{xy}$ | m | Grows linearly: $0.01$ → $>1.0$ |
| Pose uncertainty (localized) | $\sigma_{xy}$ | m | Bounded: typically $0.02 - 0.10$ |
| Map resolution | $r_{map}$ | m/cell | $0.01 - 0.50$ |
| Sensor update rate | $f_s$ | Hz | $5 - 40$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The defining contrast between the two paradigms is the **presence or absence of a position-feedback loop**. KTU questions frequently test whether students can identify which approach is appropriate for a given environment (structured vs unstructured, static vs dynamic).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Programmed Solution (Dead Reckoning Pipeline)

The "Programmed Solution" pipeline has four discrete stages. **No stage involves sensing the external world.**

**Step 1 — Path Planning (Offline)**
The user or a planning algorithm generates a sequence of $(x, y, \theta)$ waypoints or a sequence of $(v, \omega)$ velocity commands.

**Step 2 — Command Storage**
The waypoints / commands are stored as a finite array in robot memory.

**Step 3 — Execution (Open Loop)**
The low-level controller applies wheel voltages to track the commanded velocities using only **internal feedback** (e.g., motor current/tachometer), not external position.

**Step 4 — Termination**
When the final waypoint is reached, the robot stops. **No verification** is performed that the actual pose matches the desired pose.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does it fail?** Every real wheel has finite radius error, wheel slip, surface irregularities, and encoder quantization. Because none of these are measured externally, they accumulate without bound. This is the **fundamental flaw** of programmed solutions.

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Localization-Based Navigation Pipeline

This is a **Sense → Think → Act** closed loop, executed continuously (typically at $5 - 40$ Hz).

**Step 1 — Prediction (Motion Update)**
Internal sensors (encoders + IMU) propagate the previous pose estimate forward in time using the **kinematic motion model**.

**Step 2 — Observation (Measurement Update)**
External sensors (LIDAR, camera, GPS, beacons) observe landmarks or features in the environment.

**Step 3 — Correction (Bayesian Fusion)**
The robot's belief about its pose is corrected by comparing predicted observations with actual observations. The fusion uses a probabilistic filter (Kalman, Particle, or Markov).

**Step 4 — Re-Planning (Optional)**
If the corrected pose deviates significantly from the planned path, a **local replanner** generates new waypoints. This is **impossible in programmed solutions**.

**Step 5 — Actuation**
Updated velocity commands are sent to the wheels, closing the loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept — The Bayes Filter Backbone:**
> Almost every modern localization algorithm is a concrete implementation of the recursive Bayes filter:
> $$ \text{Bel}(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\,\text{Bel}(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1} $$
> where $x_t$ is pose, $u_t$ is control, $z_t$ is measurement, and $\eta$ is a normalizer. Kalman Filter assumes Gaussian (unimodal), Particle Filter (Monte Carlo) handles multi-modal beliefs.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward Kinematics (Differential Drive) | $\begin{aligned} x_{t+1} &= x_t + v\cos\theta_t\,\Delta t \\ y_{t+1} &= y_t + v\sin\theta_t\,\Delta t \\ \theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \omega\,\Delta t \end{aligned}$ | Used in **prediction step** of localization & in programmed solutions |
| 2 | Odometric Position Update | $\begin{aligned} \Delta s &= (\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L)/2 \\ \Delta\theta &= (\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L)/b \\ x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s\cos(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2) \\ y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s\sin(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2) \end{aligned}$ | Core equation when wheel encoders are the only sensor |
| 3 | Dead-Reckoning Error Growth | $\sigma_{xy}^2(t) \approx k_x\,D^2(t)$ | Variance grows as square of **distance traveled** $D$ |
| 4 | Odometric Error Propagation | $\begin{aligned} \sigma_{\Delta s}^2 &\approx \tfrac{1}{4}(\sigma_{\Delta s_R}^2 + \sigma_{\Delta s_L}^2) \\ \sigma_{\Delta\theta}^2 &\approx \tfrac{1}{b^2}(\sigma_{\Delta s_R}^2 + \sigma_{\Delta s_L}^2) \end{aligned}$ | How encoder noise maps to pose noise |
| 5 | Bayes Filter (General) | $\text{Bel}(x_t) = \eta\,P(z_t\mid x_t)\!\int\! P(x_t\mid u_t,x_{t-1})\text{Bel}(x_{t-1})dx_{t-1}$ | Foundational equation of localization |
| 6 | Kalman Filter Update | $\begin{aligned} K_t &= \bar{\Sigma}_t H^T(H\bar{\Sigma}_t H^T + Q)^{-1} \\ \mu_t &= \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - H\bar{\mu}_t) \\ \Sigma_t &= (I - K_t H)\bar{\Sigma}_t \end{aligned}$ | Optimal under linear-Gaussian assumptions |
| 7 | Particle Filter Update | $w_t^{[i]} = \eta\,P(z_t\mid x_t^{[i]})\,w_{t-1}^{[i]}$ | Resampling-based, handles non-linear/multi-modal beliefs |
| 8 | Beacon Trilateration | $d_i = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2}$ | Range-based localization from $n \geq 3$ beacons |
| 9 | GPS Dilution of Precision | $\sigma_{\text{pos}} = \text{DOP}\cdot\sigma_{\text{pseudorange}}$ | Effective position error |
| 10 | Cramér-Rao Lower Bound | $\text{Var}(\hat{x}) \geq J^{-1}$ | Theoretical minimum variance for any unbiased estimator |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Equation 2 (Odometric Position Update) is the **single most-asked formula** in Module 4 derivations. Memorize it with both differential-drive and Ackermann variants.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Paradigm Used |
|---|---|---|
| Industrial AGVs (warehouses) | Moving pallets on fixed magnetic strips | Programmed |
| Hospital delivery robots | Navigating dynamic corridors with people | Localization-based |
| Autonomous cars (Waymo, Tesla) | Highway + urban driving | Localization-based (GPS + LIDAR + vision) |
| Lawn-mower robots | Mowing bounded lawns with buried wire | Programmed |
| Planetary rovers (Mars rovers) | Long-distance drives with sparse landmarks | Hybrid (programmed w/ visual odometry correction) |
| Defense unmanned ground vehicles | Reconnaissance in GPS-denied zones | Localization-based (SLAM) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Odometric Position Update (Equation 2)

This is the **backbone equation** of any programmed (dead-reckoning) navigation system. It also serves as the **prediction step** in localization-based systems.

### Setup

Consider a **differential-drive robot** with two independently driven wheels separated by axle length $b$ (also called the wheelbase). Let $\Delta s_R$ and $\Delta s_L$ be the arc lengths traversed by the right and left wheels over one sampling interval $\Delta t$, as measured by wheel encoders.

**Step 1 — Compute the linear displacement of the robot's center.**

The robot's center moves forward by the *average* of the two wheel displacements because the center is geometrically midway between the wheels.

$$
\Delta s \;=\; \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the rotational displacement (heading change).**

If the right wheel moves more than the left wheel, the robot rotates clockwise. The instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) lies on the line connecting the wheels, and the rotation angle is:

$$
\Delta\theta \;=\; \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}
$$

This comes from the relationship $\Delta s_R = (b/2)\Delta\theta$ and $\Delta s_L = -(b/2)\Delta\theta$ for a pure rotation.

**Step 3 — Update the global pose.**

The robot's pose $(x_t, y_t, \theta_t)$ is in the world frame. The local displacement $\Delta s$ is along the robot's current heading $\theta_t$. The exact trigonometric form uses the *mid-heading* $\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2$ (not $\theta_t$) to avoid first-order integration error — this is called the **mid-point integration** method:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s \cdot \cos\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\[4pt]
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s \cdot \sin\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\[4pt]
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \Delta\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 1 and Step 2 expressions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \left(\frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{2b}\right) \\[6pt]
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \left(\frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{2b}\right) \\[6pt]
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **exact closed-form** update used by virtually every mobile robot base.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Dead-Reckoning Error Growth (Equation 3)

This derivation shows **why programmed solutions fail over long distances**.

### Assumptions

- Each wheel encoder has an independent zero-mean Gaussian error with standard deviation $\sigma_e$ (in meters per encoder tick).
- Wheel slippage adds additional zero-mean error $\sigma_s$.
- Errors are uncorrelated between wheels and across time steps.

### Step 1 — Single-Step Variance

For one time step, the position variance (assuming $\theta$ is small for linearization) is:

$$
\sigma_{\Delta x}^2 \;=\; \sigma_{\Delta y}^2 \;=\; \sigma_e^2 + \sigma_s^2 \;\equiv\; \sigma_0^2
$$

### Step 2 — Variance after $N$ time steps (independent errors)

Because errors at different time steps are independent, variances add:

$$
\sigma_{xy}^2(t) \;=\; N \cdot \sigma_0^2 \;=\; \frac{t}{\Delta t}\cdot\sigma_0^2
$$

Since $N = D / \Delta s$ where $D$ is total distance and $\Delta s$ is per-step displacement:

$$
\sigma_{xy}^2(D) \;=\; \frac{D}{\Delta s}\cdot\sigma_0^2 \;\equiv\; k_x D^2
$$

where $k_x = \sigma_0^2 / (D \cdot \Delta s)$ — i.e., the variance grows **quadratically with distance**, while the standard deviation (the typical "position error") grows **linearly**:

$$
\sigma_{xy}(D) \;=\; \sqrt{k_x}\cdot D \quad \text{(error grows linearly with distance)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** This linear-in-distance error growth is the **single most important property** distinguishing programmed solutions from localization-based ones. Localization bounds this error via measurement updates.

### Step 3 — Numerical Illustration

For a robot with $\sigma_0 = 0.005$ m per step and $\Delta s = 0.01$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D = 1\;\text{m}: \quad \sigma_{xy} &= \sqrt{0.005^2 \cdot 100} = 0.050\;\text{m} \\
D = 10\;\text{m}: \quad \sigma_{xy} &= \sqrt{0.005^2 \cdot 1000} = 0.158\;\text{m} \\
D = 100\;\text{m}: \quad \sigma_{xy} &= \sqrt{0.005^2 \cdot 10000} = 0.500\;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

A 100× increase in distance gives a 10× increase in error — exactly the linear-growth signature of dead reckoning.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Attribute | Programmed Solution | Localization-Based Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Control type | **Open-loop** | **Closed-loop** |
| Sensors required | Internal only (encoders) | Internal + External (LIDAR, GPS, vision) |
| Map requirement | None | Yes (metric / topological / hybrid) |
| Computational cost | **Very low** | Moderate to high |
| Memory requirement | KB | MB – GB (for map) |
| Position error vs distance | **Unbounded, grows linearly** | **Bounded** (sensor-dependent) |
| Adaptability to environment changes | **None** | High |
| Robustness to wheel slip | **Very poor** | Good (sensors compensate) |
| Suitability for dynamic environments | Poor | Excellent |
| Suitability for static structured environment | Excellent (and cheaper) | Overkill |
| Example algorithms | A\* (offline), spline paths, waypoint sequencing | EKF, Particle Filter, SLAM, Markov Localization |
| Failure mode | Silent drift, no recovery | Sensor occlusion, map corruption |
| KTU-typical example | Industrial conveyor robot | Autonomous vacuum cleaner (Roomba) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Programmed (Open-Loop) Navigation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User / Planner] -->|Offline path| B[Command Sequence Storage]
    B -->|v, omega at time t| C[Low-Level Motor Controller]
    C -->|Voltage PWM| D[Wheel Motors]
    D -->|Wheel rotation| E[Robot moves]
    E -->|Encoder ticks| F[Tachometer Feedback]
    F -->|Only speed feedback, not position| C
    E -.->|No external sensing| G[Environment]
    G -.->|Not measured| A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The dashed feedback loop from `Robot moves` to `Environment` and back to `User` is **NOT present** in a true open-loop system — this is the visual signature of programmed solutions. Only the inner speed-control loop exists; there is no position-correction loop.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Localization-Based (Closed-Loop) Navigation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Path Planner Goal Pose] --> B[Trajectory Generator]
    B -->|Desired velocity| C[Motion Controller]
    C -->|Wheel voltages| D[Motor Driver]
    D --> E[Robot Base Differential Drive]
    E -->|Wheel rotation| F[Wheel Encoders]
    E -->|Landmarks, walls| G[External Sensors LIDAR Camera GPS]
    F --> H[Sensor Fusion Module]
    G --> H
    H -->|Corrected pose estimate x y theta| I[Pose Estimator Kalman or Particle]
    I -->|Current pose| C
    I -->|Current pose| B
    K[Stored Map] --> H
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **two feedback loops** — (1) inner loop from encoders to motion controller (velocity), and (2) outer loop from external sensors through fusion back to the controller (position). This outer loop is what makes localization-based navigation correct errors that open-loop cannot.

---

## 4.3 State-Transition Diagram — Bayes Filter Localization Loop

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitialBelief
    InitialBelief --> Predict : Set Bel x0 prior
    Predict --> Measure : Apply motion model u_t
    Measure --> Correct : Acquire sensor z_t
    Correct --> Predict : Update Bel x_t with Bayes rule
    Correct --> Actuate : Send corrected pose to controller
    Actuate --> Predict : Next time step
    Correct --> [*] : Goal reached
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Paradigm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([New Mobile Robot Project]) --> Q1{Is environment fixed and structured?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Are paths highly repetitive?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| P1[Use Programmed Solution - Cheapest]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need dynamic obstacle avoidance?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q4{Need operation in unknown environment?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| L1[Use Localization-Based Mandatory]
    Q3 -->|No| P2[Use Programmed acceptable but limited]
    Q4 -->|Yes| L2[Use SLAM Localization-Based]
    Q4 -->|No| L3[Use Map-Based Localization]
    P1 --> End([Deploy])
    P2 --> End
    L1 --> End
    L2 --> End
    L3 --> End
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Which navigation strategy is appropriate for X scenario?", trace through this decision tree. The rule of thumb is: **if anything in the world can move or change after deployment, you need localization**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**Differentiate between programmed navigation and localization-based navigation in mobile robots. Mention one scenario where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (Structured):**

| Basis | Programmed Navigation | Localization-Based Navigation |
|---|---|---|
| Control loop | Open-loop | Closed-loop |
| Sensor reliance | Internal encoders only | Internal + External (LIDAR, GPS, vision) |
| Map usage | Not required | Required |
| Error behavior | Unbounded growth | Bounded by sensor accuracy |
| Computational cost | Low | High |

**Scenario for programmed:** Industrial Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV) moving along magnetic tape in a warehouse — fixed path, structured environment.
**Scenario for localization:** Autonomous vacuum cleaner (e.g., Roomba) operating in a dynamic home environment with furniture rearrangement. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**Define dead reckoning. Why does the position error in dead reckoning grow without bound?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Dead Reckoning** is the process of estimating a robot's current pose by integrating (summing) incremental motions measured by internal sensors such as wheel encoders and gyroscopes, starting from a known initial pose.

**Reason for unbounded error growth:**
Dead reckoning uses only **internal sensors** that measure wheel rotations. These measurements contain:
1. Encoder quantization error (finite ticks/rev),
2. Wheel diameter tolerance,
3. Wheel slip on the surface,
4. Floor irregularities.

Because there is no external reference to detect or correct these errors, they **accumulate additively at every time step**. The position standard deviation grows **linearly with distance traveled**:

$$
\sigma_{xy}(D) = \sqrt{k_x}\cdot D
$$

Hence the error is unbounded as $D \to \infty$. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]
**(a)** Derive the odometric position update equations for a differential-drive mobile robot, explaining the role of the midpoint integration method. State clearly the meaning of each variable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot has wheelbase $b = 0.30$ m and wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m. Its left and right wheel encoders report displacements $\Delta s_L = 0.020$ m and $\Delta s_R = 0.022$ m during one sampling interval. If the robot is currently at pose $(x, y, \theta) = (1.0, 2.0, 30°)$, compute the new pose. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(a) — Full Derivation

**Given:** A differential-drive robot with two wheels of radius $r$, separated by axle length $b$.

**Variables:**
- $\Delta s_L$ = distance traveled by left wheel (m)
- $\Delta s_R$ = distance traveled by right wheel (m)
- $\Delta s$ = displacement of robot center (m)
- $\Delta\theta$ = change in heading (rad)
- $(x_t, y_t, \theta_t)$ = current pose in world frame
- $(x_{t+1}, y_{t+1}, \theta_{t+1})$ = updated pose

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The robot's center is midway between the wheels, so its forward displacement is the average:

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Pure rotation about the ICR gives:

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}
$$

**Step 3:** Project local displacement into world frame using the *mid-heading* $\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2$ (midpoint integration reduces first-order Euler error from $O(\Delta t^2)$ to $O(\Delta t^3)$):

$$
x_{t+1} = x_t + \Delta s \cdot \cos\!\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)
$$

$$
y_{t+1} = y_t + \Delta s \cdot \sin\!\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta\theta
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting Steps 1 and 2:

$$
\boxed{\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \left(\frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{2b}\right) \\[6pt]
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \left(\frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{2b}\right) \\[6pt]
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}
\end{aligned}}
$$

**Why midpoint integration?** Standard Euler integration uses the *initial* heading $\theta_t$ for the entire interval, which assumes the robot's orientation is constant over $\Delta t$. The midpoint method averages the start and end orientations, giving a much more accurate trajectory especially for large $\Delta\theta$. **(7 marks — full marks)**


#### Solution to Question A(b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $b = 0.30$ m, $\Delta s_L = 0.020$ m, $\Delta s_R = 0.022$ m, current pose $(x, y, \theta) = (1.0, 2.0, 30°)$.

**Step 1:** Compute $\Delta s$:

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2} = \frac{0.022 + 0.020}{2} = \frac{0.042}{2} = 0.021 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\Delta\theta$:

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b} = \frac{0.022 - 0.020}{0.30} = \frac{0.002}{0.30} = 0.00667 \text{ rad}
$$

Convert to degrees: $0.00667 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \approx 0.382°$.

**Step 3:** Compute the mid-heading:

$$
\theta_{\text{mid}} = \theta_t + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2} = 30° + \frac{0.382°}{2} = 30° + 0.191° = 30.191°
$$

**Step 4:** Compute new $x$:

$$
x_{t+1} = 1.0 + 0.021 \cdot \cos(30.191°) = 1.0 + 0.021 \cdot 0.8641 = 1.0 + 0.01815 = 1.01815 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute new $y$:

$$
y_{t+1} = 2.0 + 0.021 \cdot \sin(30.191°) = 2.0 + 0.021 \cdot 0.5034 = 2.0 + 0.01057 = 2.01057 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute new $\theta$:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = 30° + 0.382° = 30.382°
$$

**Final Pose:**

$$
\boxed{(x, y, \theta) \approx (1.018\;\text{m},\; 2.011\;\text{m},\; 30.38°)}
$$

**(7 marks — full marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]
**(a)** Explain the Bayes filter framework for robot localization. State its recursive form and explain how the Kalman Filter and Particle Filter implement it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot uses dead reckoning with encoder standard deviation $\sigma_e = 0.003$ m per step and step length $\Delta s = 0.015$ m. Estimate the standard deviation of position error after the robot has traveled (i) $D = 5$ m and (ii) $D = 50$ m. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(a) — Bayes Filter

**Concept:** The Bayes filter is the mathematical foundation of all probabilistic localization algorithms. It maintains a *belief* — a probability distribution over the robot's pose $x_t$ given all measurements $z_{1:t}$ and controls $u_{1:t}$.

**Recursive Form:**

$$
\text{Bel}(x_t) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \int P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\,\text{Bel}(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}
$$

where:
- $P(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ = **motion model** (prediction)
- $P(z_t \mid x_t)$ = **measurement model** (correction)
- $\eta$ = normalization constant
- $\text{Bel}(x_{t-1})$ = prior belief

**Two-Step Cycle:**
1. **Predict** — propagate belief forward using motion model and control $u_t$.
2. **Correct** — multiply predicted belief by measurement likelihood and normalize.

**Kalman Filter Implementation:**
- Assumes all distributions are **Gaussian** (unimodal, linearizable).
- Represented by mean $\mu_t$ and covariance $\Sigma_t$.
- Optimal for **linear systems with Gaussian noise**.
- Cannot represent multi-modal beliefs (e.g., global localization ambiguity).

**Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization) Implementation:**
- Represents belief as a **set of weighted samples** (particles) $\{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}$.
- Handles **non-linear, non-Gaussian, multi-modal** distributions.
- Three steps: (i) sample from motion model, (ii) weight by $P(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$, (iii) resample.
- Used in practice for global localization (kidnapped robot problem).

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Kalman Filter | Particle Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Belief shape | Gaussian | Arbitrary |
| Multi-modal | No | Yes |
| Computational cost | Low (matrix ops) | Higher (many samples) |
| KTU example | Indoor AGV with smooth motion | Roomba global localization |

**(7 marks — full marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(b) — Error Growth Numerical

**Given:** $\sigma_e = 0.003$ m, $\Delta s = 0.015$ m.

**Step 1:** Per-step position variance:

$$
\sigma_0^2 = \sigma_e^2 = (0.003)^2 = 9 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Number of steps for distance $D$:

$$
N = \frac{D}{\Delta s}
$$

**Step 3:** Variance after $N$ steps (independent errors):

$$
\sigma_{xy}^2 = N \cdot \sigma_0^2 = \frac{D}{\Delta s}\cdot\sigma_0^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_{xy} = \sigma_e\sqrt{\frac{D}{\Delta s}}
$$

**(i) For $D = 5$ m:**

$$
N = \frac{5}{0.015} = 333.33 \text{ steps}
$$

$$
\sigma_{xy} = 0.003 \cdot \sqrt{333.33} = 0.003 \cdot 18.257 = 0.0548 \text{ m} \approx 5.48 \text{ cm}
$$

**(ii) For $D = 50$ m:**

$$
N = \frac{50}{0.015} = 3333.33 \text{ steps}
$$

$$
\sigma_{xy} = 0.003 \cdot \sqrt{3333.33} = 0.003 \cdot 57.735 = 0.1732 \text{ m} \approx 17.32 \text{ cm}
$$

**Comment:**
A **10× increase in distance** (5 m → 50 m) caused only a **~3.16× increase in position error** (5.48 cm → 17.32 cm), confirming the **square-root relationship**:

$$
\frac{\sigma_{xy}(50)}{\sigma_{xy}(5)} = \sqrt{\frac{50}{5}} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162
$$

Equivalently, error grows **linearly with distance** $\sigma_{xy} \propto D$. Hence dead-reckoning error is unbounded and grows linearly with distance, justifying why localization-based solutions are mandatory for long-distance navigation. **(7 marks — full marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Using $\theta_t$ instead of $\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2$** — this loses **2 marks** in derivations. Midpoint integration is a KTU-favored concept.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert degrees ↔ radians** — angles in $\sin/\cos$ MUST be in **radians** for numerical computation; convert at the end if the answer needs degrees. **Costs 1 mark** if missed.
> 3. **Writing "error is bounded" without mentioning sensors** — always link bounded error to **external measurement updates**, not just state it abstractly.
> 4. **Confusing Kalman Filter with Particle Filter use-cases** — KF cannot handle the kidnapped robot problem; PF can. This is a favorite **2-mark difference question**.
> 5. **In derivation questions, skipping intermediate steps** — KTU awards marks for **each step**, not just the final boxed equation. Show every algebraic transition.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Programmed solutions** are **open-loop** — no feedback about true position.
- [x] **Localization-based** solutions are **closed-loop** — sensor feedback continuously corrects pose.
- [x] **Dead reckoning** integrates wheel encoder displacements starting from a known pose.
- [x] **Odometric update** uses midpoint integration: $\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2$ inside $\sin/\cos$.
- [x] **Dead-reckoning error** grows **linearly** with distance: $\sigma_{xy} \propto D$.
- [x] **Wheelbase** $b$ (axle length) appears in **denominator** of $\Delta\theta$ — smaller $b$ → larger heading change.
- [x] **Bayes filter** = predict (motion model) + correct (measurement model), recursively.
- [x] **Kalman Filter** → Gaussian, unimodal, linear-Gaussian systems only.
- [x] **Particle Filter** → arbitrary distributions, multi-modal, handles kidnapped robot problem.
- [x] **Beacon/GPS trilateration** uses $n \geq 3$ range measurements to solve for $(x, y)$.
- [x] **DOP** (Dilution of Precision) scales pseudorange noise to effective position error.
- [x] **Use programmed solution** when: environment is fixed, structured, repetitive, cost-sensitive.
- [x] **Use localization-based** when: environment is dynamic, unstructured, or long-distance.
- [x] **SLAM** = simultaneous localization + mapping (used when no prior map exists).
- [x] **Hybrid approach** (programmed with visual odometry correction) used in planetary rovers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Belief Representation - Single-Hypothesis Belief
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT721 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Belief Representation - Single-Hypothesis Belief

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In mobile robotics, **Belief** refers to the robot's internal estimate of its own state (and, by extension, the state of the environment) given all sensor measurements and control inputs up to the current time step. Mathematically, the belief is a probability distribution over the state space.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Belief is the robot's internal knowledge about its state, encoded as a probability distribution over the state space. A **Single-Hypothesis Belief** is the simplest form where the robot maintains exactly one hypothesis (one point estimate) about its state at any given time.*

A **Single-Hypothesis Belief** restricts the robot's internal knowledge to **one and only one estimate** of its state. This single hypothesis is typically represented as:

- A **point estimate** (deterministic): $\text{bel}(x_t) = \hat{x}_t$
- A **unimodal Gaussian distribution** (probabilistic): $\text{bel}(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ with a single mean $\mu_t$ and a single covariance matrix $\Sigma_t$

where $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$ is the robot's pose at time $t$, consisting of 2D position $(x, y)$ and heading angle $\theta$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Single-Map Tourist"**
>
> Imagine a tourist in an unfamiliar city who carries **only one paper map** and **one highlighter**. He believes, at every moment, that *he is exactly at the spot marked on the map*. When he walks (control input $u_t$) and sees a landmark (measurement $z_t$), he slightly adjusts the marker's position. He never considers the possibility that the map itself could be wrong, nor does he keep alternative "backup" copies of the map.
>
> - The **single mark on the map** = the *single hypothesis* of his location.
> - The **confidence circle** he draws around the mark = the *Gaussian covariance* (uncertainty).
> - The fact that he carries **only one map** = the *single-hypothesis* constraint.
>
> If he ever gets lost in a region that looks identical to many other places (the "kidnapped robot problem" or symmetric environments), a single-hypothesis tourist is doomed — he cannot represent the ambiguity of "I could be at Place A *or* Place B *or* Place C." This limitation is exactly why mobile robotics graduates from single-hypothesis to multi-hypothesis representations (particle filters, grid-based methods).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard metrics used in Single-Hypothesis Belief representation:**
> - **State dimension ($n$):** For 2D ground robots, $n = 3$ (i.e., $x, y, \theta$).
> - **Covariance matrix dimension:** $n \times n = 3 \times 3$ (or $2 \times 2$ for 2D position-only).
> - **Uncertainty bound ($\sigma$):** Standard deviation in metres (m) for position, radians (rad) for orientation.
> - **Mahalanobis distance threshold ($d^2$):** Typically $\chi^2_{n, 0.95}$ for data association gating in EKF localization.

---

## 1.4 Where Does Single-Hypothesis Belief Sit in the Robotics Stack?

| Layer | Role | Single-Hypothesis Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Perception | Sensor measurement $z_t$ | Range finders, cameras, IMU |
| State Estimation | Belief update | **Kalman Filter / EKF** |
| Control | Action selection $u_t$ | PID, MPC |
| Planning | Path generation | A*, RRT |

The **state estimation layer** is precisely where single-hypothesis belief lives — represented most commonly by the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or the **unscented Kalman filter (UKF)** when non-linearities exist.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The probabilistic formulation of belief begins with Bayes' theorem applied recursively through time. The single-hypothesis restriction means the posterior is always a **single mode** (a single peak in probability).

### 2.1.1 Belief Decomposition (Bayes Filter)

The recursive Bayes filter computes the posterior belief:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$$

where the predicted belief (before incorporating $z_t$) is:

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

and $\eta$ is the normalization constant.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **single-hypothesis** Gaussian representation, $\text{bel}(x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$ is a single Gaussian. The integral of two Gaussians yields another Gaussian — hence the belief **remains unimodal** (single-hypothesis) throughout the recursion.

### 2.1.2 Markov Assumption

Single-hypothesis belief relies on the **first-order Markov assumption**:

- The current state $x_t$ is sufficient to predict the next state $x_{t+1}$.
- The current measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current state $x_t$.

This makes the belief update tractable as a 2-step process: **Predict** $\rightarrow$ **Update**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists every formula you must memorize for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations on Single-Hypothesis Belief.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State Vector (2D) | $x_t = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$ | $x, y$ in m; $\theta$ in rad | Robot pose in planar world |
| 2 | Point Belief (deterministic) | $\text{bel}(x_t) = \hat{x}_t$ | $\hat{x}_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Dead reckoning |
| 3 | Gaussian Belief (probabilistic) | $\text{bel}(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ | $\mu_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$, $\Sigma_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | EKF Localization |
| 4 | Multivariate Gaussian PDF | $p(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^n \vert\Sigma\vert}} \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x-\mu)\right)$ | $n$ = state dim | Probability density at a state |
| 5 | Motion Model (linear) | $x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + w_t$ | $A_t, B_t$: matrices; $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ | Linear KF prediction |
| 6 | Motion Model (non-linear) | $x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$ | $g(\cdot)$: non-linear fn | EKF prediction |
| 7 | Measurement Model (linear) | $z_t = C_t x_t + v_t$ | $C_t$: matrix; $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ | Linear KF update |
| 8 | Measurement Model (non-linear) | $z_t = h(x_t) + v_t$ | $h(\cdot)$: non-linear fn | EKF update |
| 9 | KF Prediction — Mean | $\bar{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$ | $\bar{\mu}_t$: predicted mean | State prediction step |
| 10 | KF Prediction — Covariance | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$ | $\bar{\Sigma}_t$: predicted covariance | Uncertainty propagation |
| 11 | EKF Jacobian (motion) | $G_t = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{\mu_{t-1}, u_t}$ | $G_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | Linearization of $g$ |
| 12 | EKF Jacobian (measurement) | $H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ | $H_t \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | Linearization of $h$ |
| 13 | Kalman Gain | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | $K_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ | Weighting of innovation |
| 14 | KF Update — Mean | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - H_t \bar{\mu}_t)$ | $\mu_t$: posterior mean | State correction |
| 15 | KF Update — Covariance | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | $\Sigma_t$: posterior covariance | Uncertainty update |
| 16 | Innovation (residual) | $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ | $\nu_t \in \mathbb{R}^m$ | Mismatch signal |
| 17 | Mahalanobis Distance | $d^2 = \nu_t^T S_t^{-1} \nu_t$, with $S_t = H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t$ | $d^2$: scalar | Data association gate |
| 18 | Whiteness Test | $E[\nu_t \nu_t^T] = S_t$ | Scalar check | Filter consistency check |

## 2.3 The Two-Phase Recursion (Predict → Update)

The single-hypothesis belief update happens in **two phases**:

### Phase 1 — PREDICTION (Time Update)
Uses the motion model and control input to propagate the belief forward in time:
- **Mean:** $\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$
- **Covariance:** $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$

> Uncertainties **grow** here because of process noise $R_t$.

### Phase 2 — MEASUREMENT UPDATE (Correction)
Uses the sensor measurement to refine the belief:
- **Innovation:** $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$
- **Kalman Gain:** $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$
- **Corrected Mean:** $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t$
- **Corrected Covariance:** $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$

> Uncertainties **shrink** here because observations reduce ambiguity.

## 2.4 Why Single-Hypothesis? — Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where Single-Hypothesis Belief is the Industry Standard:**
> - **GPS/INS Fusion in Autonomous Vehicles:** The onboard navigation stack uses an EKF with a single pose hypothesis per vehicle.
> - **Aircraft Inertial Navigation:** Single-hypothesis Kalman filters are the backbone of aerospace GNC (Guidance, Navigation, Control).
> - **Robotic Vacuum Cleaners (early SLAM):** Many use a single pose hypothesis with scan matching corrections.
> - **Quadrotor Drone Stabilization:** PX4 and ArduPilot firmware use EKF cores with single-hypothesis state.
> - **Mars Rovers (NASA):** Visual odometry + IMU is fused into a single-hypothesis EKF.
>
> In all these applications, the environment is **structured and unambiguous enough** that a single hypothesis remains consistent over time.

## 2.5 Limitations of Single-Hypothesis Belief

> [!WARNING]
> **Single-hypothesis belief fails when:**
> 1. **Symmetric / repetitive environments** (corridors, parking lots) — the robot cannot resolve which corridor it is in.
> 2. **Kidnapped robot problem** — abrupt displacement cannot be detected.
> 3. **Multi-modal posteriors** — the true belief should have several peaks, but the single Gaussian collapses them into one mean (a totally wrong estimate).
> 4. **Data association ambiguity** — wrong landmark matches cause the filter to diverge.
>
> For these cases, multi-hypothesis methods (Particle Filters, Grid-based Markov Localization) are mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Gaussian Prediction Step

We want to derive the propagation of a Gaussian belief through a **non-linear motion model** $x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$, where $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$.

### Given
- Prior belief: $x_{t-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$
- Motion model: $x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$
- Process noise: $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$, independent of $x_{t-1}$

### Step-by-step Derivation

**Step 1 — Linearize the non-linear motion model $g(\cdot)$ using a first-order Taylor expansion about the operating point $\mu_{t-1}$:**

$$g(x_{t-1}, u_t) \approx g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + G_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})$$

where the Jacobian is:

$$G_t = \frac{\partial g(x_{t-1}, u_t)}{\partial x_{t-1}}\bigg\vert_{x_{t-1} = \mu_{t-1}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the linearized model into the state equation:**

$$x_t \approx g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + G_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1}) + w_t$$

**Step 3 — Compute the expected value (predicted mean):**

$$\bar{\mu}_t = E[x_t] = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + G_t \underbrace{E[x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1}]}_{= 0} + \underbrace{E[w_t]}_{= 0}$$

$$\therefore \; \bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the predicted covariance. By definition $\bar{\Sigma}_t = E[(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t)^T]$:**

$$x_t - \bar{\mu}_t = G_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1}) + w_t$$

Since $w_t$ is independent of $x_{t-1}$, cross-terms vanish:

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = E\!\left[\left(G_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1}) + w_t\right)\left(G_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1}) + w_t\right)^T\right]$$

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \, E[(x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})(x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})^T] \, G_t^T + E[w_t w_t^T]$$

$$\boxed{\; \bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, G_t^T + R_t \;}$$

> This is the **EKF prediction covariance formula** — equation #12 in the formula sheet.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Kalman Gain (Minimizing Posterior Covariance)

We want to derive the optimal Kalman gain $K_t$ that minimizes the posterior covariance trace.

### Given
- Predicted state: $\bar{\mu}_t$ with covariance $\bar{\Sigma}_t$
- Measurement model: $z_t = H_t x_t + v_t$, with $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$
- Posterior mean: $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - H_t \bar{\mu}_t)$

### Step-by-step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the posterior mean and define innovation $\nu_t$:**

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t, \quad \text{where} \quad \nu_t = z_t - H_t \bar{\mu}_t$$

**Step 2 — Express the posterior error $e_t = \mu_t - x_t$ (the deviation from the true state):**

$$e_t = \mu_t - x_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t - x_t$$

Substitute $\nu_t = H_t(\bar{\mu}_t - x_t) + v_t$:

$$e_t = (\bar{\mu}_t - x_t) + K_t \left[H_t(\bar{\mu}_t - x_t) + v_t\right] = (I + K_t H_t)(\bar{\mu}_t - x_t) + K_t v_t$$

**Step 3 — Write the posterior covariance $\Sigma_t = E[e_t e_t^T]$:**

$$\Sigma_t = (I + K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t (I + K_t H_t)^T + K_t Q_t K_t^T$$

**Step 4 — Expand the first term:**

$$\Sigma_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t + K_t H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t + \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T K_t^T + K_t H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T K_t^T + K_t Q_t K_t^T$$

**Step 5 — Take the trace and differentiate w.r.t. $K_t$ (set to zero for optimum). Using $\frac{\partial \, \text{tr}(A K^T)}{\partial K} = A$ and $\frac{\partial \, \text{tr}(A K B K^T)}{\partial K} = 2 A K B$ (for symmetric $B$):**

$$\frac{\partial \, \text{tr}(\Sigma_t)}{\partial K_t} = 2 \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + 2 K_t (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $K_t$:**

$$K_t (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t) = -\bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T$$

$$\boxed{\; K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T \, (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1} \;}$$

> This is the **Kalman Gain** — equation #13 in the formula sheet.

**Step 7 — Substitute $K_t$ back into the covariance update to get the **Joseph form** (numerically stable):**

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t (I - K_t H_t)^T + K_t Q_t K_t^T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The simplified form $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ is algebraically equivalent but can suffer from numerical instability. The **Joseph form** above is the KTU-recommended implementation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!TIP]
> **KTU-style numerical problem:** A 1D mobile robot has state $x_t$ (position in m). At $t=0$: $\mu_0 = 0$ m, $\Sigma_0 = 1$ m². It moves with control $u_1 = 2$ m (process noise variance $R = 0.5$ m²) and observes $z_1 = 2.5$ m (measurement noise variance $Q = 0.3$ m²). Motion model: $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t$. Measurement model: $z_t = x_t$. Find $\mu_1$ and $\Sigma_1$.

**Step 1 — Prediction (Mean):**
Since $g(x, u) = x + u$:
$$\bar{\mu}_1 = \mu_0 + u_1 = 0 + 2 = 2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Prediction (Covariance):** Jacobian $G_1 = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x} = 1$:
$$\bar{\Sigma}_1 = G_1^2 \Sigma_0 + R = (1)(1) + 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Innovation:**
$$\nu_1 = z_1 - H_1 \bar{\mu}_1 = 2.5 - (1)(2) = 0.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Innovation covariance:** $H_1 = 1$:
$$S_1 = H_1^2 \bar{\Sigma}_1 + Q = (1)(1.5) + 0.3 = 1.8 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Kalman Gain:**
$$K_1 = \frac{\bar{\Sigma}_1 H_1}{S_1} = \frac{1.5 \times 1}{1.8} = 0.833$$

**Step 6 — Update (Mean):**
$$\mu_1 = \bar{\mu}_1 + K_1 \nu_1 = 2 + (0.833)(0.5) = 2.417 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Update (Covariance):**
$$\Sigma_1 = (1 - K_1 H_1) \bar{\Sigma}_1 = (1 - 0.833)(1.5) = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$$

> **Result:** Posterior belief at $t=1$ is $\mathcal{N}(2.417, \, 0.25)$. Note: the posterior uncertainty (0.25) is **lower** than either prior (1.5) or measurement (0.3) — this is the hallmark of Bayesian fusion.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Point Belief (Dead Reckoning) | Gaussian Belief (EKF) | Multi-Hypothesis (Particle) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Representation | $\hat{x}_t$ | $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ | Set of $N$ samples $\{x_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)}\}$ |
| Storage | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(Nn)$ |
| Handles multi-modal? | No | No | Yes |
| Handles non-linearity? | Trivially | First-order (EKF) / Unscented (UKF) | Yes (exact) |
| Computation per step | $O(n)$ | $O(n^3)$ (matrix inversion) | $O(Nn)$ |
| Robust to kidnapping? | No | No | Yes |
| KTU exam weight | High | **Highest** | High (Module 5) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Single-Hypothesis Belief Update Cycle

The following block diagram captures the canonical EKF / single-hypothesis belief update cycle. Each block represents a functional stage, and arrows show data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA["Prior Belief bel x at t-1\nMean mu_prev\nCovariance Sigma_prev"]
    inputB["Control Input u_t\nfrom motion controller"]
    inputC["Sensor Measurement z_t\nfrom range camera lidar"]
    blockA["Motion Model\ng x_prev u_t\nLinearize to G_t"]
    blockB["Prediction Step\nMean bar mu_t = g\nCovariance bar Sigma_t = G Sigma G^T + R"]
    blockC["Measurement Model\nh bar mu_t\nLinearize to H_t"]
    blockD["Innovation\nnu_t = z_t minus h bar mu_t"]
    blockE["Kalman Gain\nK_t = bar Sigma H^T\ntimes H bar Sigma H^T + Q inverse"]
    blockF["Update Step\nmu_t = bar mu_t + K nu_t\nSigma_t = I minus K H bar Sigma"]
    outputA["Posterior Belief bel x at t\nMean mu_t\nCovariance Sigma_t\nSINGLE HYPOTHESIS"]
    blockG["Process Noise R_t"]
    blockH["Measurement Noise Q_t"]

    inputA --> blockA
    inputB --> blockA
    blockA --> blockB
    blockG --> blockB
    blockB --> blockC
    blockC --> blockD
    inputC --> blockD
    blockD --> blockE
    blockB --> blockE
    blockH --> blockE
    blockE --> blockF
    blockB --> blockF
    blockD --> blockF
    blockF --> outputA
    outputA -. feedback to next step .-> inputA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** At each time step, the *Prior Belief* is combined with the *Control Input* via the *Motion Model* to produce a *Prediction*. This prediction is then corrected using the *Sensor Measurement* and the *Kalman Gain*, yielding the *Posterior Belief* — which becomes the prior for the next time step. The cycle is closed (dashed feedback arrow).

## 4.2 Concept Map — Belief Representation Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Belief Representation in Mobile Robotics"]
    nodeA["Single Hypothesis\nBelief"]
    nodeB["Multi Hypothesis\nBelief"]
    subA1["Point Estimate\nx hat t"]
    subA2["Unimodal Gaussian\nN mu t Sigma t"]
    subB1["Grid Based\nHistogram Filter"]
    subB2["Sample Based\nParticle Filter"]
    subB3["MHT\nMulti Hypothesis Tracker"]
    subA2a["Linear KF\nMotion A B"]
    subA2b["Extended KF\nJacobian G H"]
    subA2c["Unscented KF\nSigma Points"]

    rootA --> nodeA
    rootA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> subA1
    nodeA --> subA2
    subA2 --> subA2a
    subA2 --> subA2b
    subA2 --> subA2c
    nodeB --> subB1
    nodeB --> subB2
    nodeB --> subB3
```

## 4.3 State Space Geometric View (Text Schematic)

Imagine the 2D plane on which a mobile robot navigates. The single-hypothesis belief can be visualized as:

```
     y
     ↑
     |        ┌───────────┐
     |        │   Mean μ  │  ← Single point estimate
     |        │   (x̂,ŷ)  │
     |        │  ┌─────┐  │
     |        │  │  •  │  │  ← The hypothesis location
     |        │  └─────┘  │
     |        │  ╱     ╲  │
     |        │ │ 3σ    │ │  ← Uncertainty ellipse
     |        │  ╲      ╱  │     (covariance Σ visualized)
     |        │   ╲    ╱   │
     |        │    ╲  ╱    │
     |        │     ╲╱     │
     |        └───────────┘
     |____________________→ x
```

> The **single dot** is the hypothesis. The **ellipse** around it (formed by the covariance matrix's eigenvalues) represents the 3σ confidence region — the area in which the robot believes it lies with **99.7% probability** (for a Gaussian). Outside this ellipse, alternative hypotheses are **not represented** — that is the single-hypothesis limitation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Each Part A question must be answered in **30–40 words** as per KTU 2024 valuation norms. Marks are awarded for: definition (1), key term (1), example or equation (1).*

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set A]`

**Define Single-Hypothesis Belief in mobile robotics. How is it mathematically represented?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Single-Hypothesis Belief** is the robot's internal estimate of its state assuming exactly one possible pose/configuration at any time. It is mathematically represented either as a point estimate $\text{bel}(x_t) = \hat{x}_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ or as a unimodal Gaussian $\text{bel}(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ with one mean vector $\mu_t$ and one covariance matrix $\Sigma_t$.

*(Marking: 1 mark definition, 1 mark for point estimate form, 1 mark for Gaussian form with variables.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP Set B]`

**List any two limitations of Single-Hypothesis Belief representation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Cannot represent multi-modal posteriors:** In symmetric or repetitive environments (e.g., identical corridors), the true belief should have multiple peaks, but a single Gaussian collapses them into one incorrect mean.
2. **Cannot recover from the kidnapped robot problem:** If the robot is abruptly teleported, a single-hypothesis tracker cannot detect the discontinuity or relocalize.

*(Marking: 1.5 marks each limitation with a brief justification.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *Each Part B question in KTU 2024 has **internal choice**. You must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B**. Each chosen question carries 14 marks split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.*

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the EKF prediction step for single-hypothesis belief. State clearly the role of the Jacobian $G_t$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the problem (1 mark):**
Given prior belief $x_{t-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{t-1}, \Sigma_{t-1})$ and non-linear motion model $x_t = g(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$ with $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$, derive $\bar{\mu}_t$ and $\bar{\Sigma}_t$.

**Step 2 — Taylor expansion of $g$ (2 marks):**
Linearize about $\mu_{t-1}$:

$$g(x_{t-1}, u_t) \approx g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + G_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})$$

**Step 3 — Compute predicted mean (1 mark):**

$$\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$

**Step 4 — Compute predicted covariance (2 marks):**
With Jacobian $G_t$ propagating prior uncertainty and $R_t$ adding process noise:

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$$

**Step 5 — Role of $G_t$ (1 mark):**
$G_t = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\big\vert_{\mu_{t-1}, u_t}$ linearizes the non-linear motion model, mapping the prior uncertainty from the $t-1$ state space into the $t$ state space.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A 1D robot has $\mu_0 = 0$ m, $\Sigma_0 = 2$ m². Motion model: $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + w_t$ with $u_1 = 1$ m, $R_1 = 0.4$ m². Measurement: $z_1 = 1.2$ m, $Q_1 = 0.2$ m², $H = 1$. Compute $\mu_1$ and $\Sigma_1$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Predicted mean (1 mark):**
$$\bar{\mu}_1 = \mu_0 + u_1 = 0 + 1 = 1 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Predicted covariance (1 mark):**
Jacobian $G_1 = 1$:
$$\bar{\Sigma}_1 = (1)^2 (2) + 0.4 = 2.4 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Innovation (1 mark):**
$$\nu_1 = z_1 - H \bar{\mu}_1 = 1.2 - (1)(1) = 0.2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Innovation covariance (1 mark):**
$$S_1 = H^2 \bar{\Sigma}_1 + Q_1 = (1)(2.4) + 0.2 = 2.6 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Kalman gain (1 mark):**
$$K_1 = \frac{\bar{\Sigma}_1 H}{S_1} = \frac{2.4}{2.6} = 0.923$$

**Step 6 — Posterior mean (1 mark):**
$$\mu_1 = \bar{\mu}_1 + K_1 \nu_1 = 1 + (0.923)(0.2) = 1.185 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Posterior covariance (1 mark):**
$$\Sigma_1 = (1 - K_1 H) \bar{\Sigma}_1 = (1 - 0.923)(2.4) = 0.185 \text{ m}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $\mu_1 = 1.185$ m, $\Sigma_1 = 0.185$ m². ✓ *Note posterior uncertainty (0.185) is far less than predicted (2.4) — consistent Bayesian fusion.*

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the role of the Kalman Gain $K_t$ in single-hypothesis belief update. Discuss the limiting cases $K_t \to 0$ and $K_t \to I$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of Kalman gain (2 marks):**
The Kalman gain $K_t$ weights the relative trust between the *prediction* and the *measurement*:

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$

It determines how much the innovation $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ corrects the predicted state.

**Step 2 — Case $K_t \to 0$ (1.5 marks):**
This happens when $Q_t \to 0$ (very accurate sensor) and/or $\bar{\Sigma}_t$ is small. The filter **fully trusts the measurement**, so $\mu_t \approx \bar{\mu}_t$ is rejected in favour of $z_t$. The state is *forced* to align with the sensor.

**Step 3 — Case $K_t \to I$ (for $H = I$) (1.5 marks):**
This happens when $Q_t \to \infty$ (sensor is useless) and/or $\bar{\Sigma}_t$ is large. The filter **fully trusts the prediction**, ignoring the measurement. $\mu_t \approx \bar{\mu}_t$ is retained.

**Step 4 — General behaviour and Mahalanobis gating (2 marks):**
$K_t$ acts as a **continuous weighting knob**: large when measurement is informative, small when it is noisy. It is selected to **minimise the trace of posterior covariance** $\Sigma_t$ — the optimality criterion. The Mahalanobis distance $d^2 = \nu_t^T S_t^{-1} \nu_t$ uses $S_t = H \bar{\Sigma}_t H^T + Q_t$ to gate (accept/reject) measurements, a key check for filter consistency.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A 2D robot uses single-hypothesis EKF localization with state $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$. The prior is $\mu_0 = [0, 0, 0]^T$ and $\Sigma_0 = \text{diag}(1, 1, 0.1)$. The motion model is $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t$ where $u_1 = [0.5, 0, 0]^T$ with $R = \text{diag}(0.1, 0, 0)$. A landmark at $(2, 0)$ is observed with range $z_1 = 1.4$ m and bearing $\theta_z = 0$ rad. Measurement noise $Q = \text{diag}(0.05, 0.02)$. The Jacobian $H$ at the predicted pose is approximately $H = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$. Compute $K_1$ and the updated state $\mu_1$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Predicted mean (1 mark):**
$$\bar{\mu}_1 = \mu_0 + u_1 = [0.5, 0, 0]^T$$

**Step 2 — Predicted covariance (1 mark):**
With $G = I$ (linear motion) and $R = \text{diag}(0.1, 0, 0)$:
$$\bar{\Sigma}_1 = I \cdot \Sigma_0 \cdot I^T + R = \text{diag}(1.1, 1, 0.1)$$

**Step 3 — Predicted measurement (1 mark):**
$$h(\bar{\mu}_1) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(2-0.5)^2 + 0^2} \\ \text{atan2}(0, 2-0.5) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Innovation (0.5 marks):**
$$\nu_1 = z_1 - h(\bar{\mu}_1) = \begin{bmatrix} 1.4 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Innovation covariance (1.5 marks):**
$$S_1 = H \bar{\Sigma}_1 H^T + Q$$
$$H \bar{\Sigma}_1 H^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.96 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$S_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1.15 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.98 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Kalman gain (1 mark):**
$$K_1 = \bar{\Sigma}_1 H^T S_1^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.4 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.870 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.505 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.957 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.707 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Updated state (1 mark):**
$$\mu_1 = \bar{\mu}_1 + K_1 \nu_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.957 \\ 0.707 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -0.1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.404 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

> The corrected $x$-position moved from 0.5 m toward the measured range 1.4 m, as expected.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Single-Hypothesis Belief Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Jacobian:** Students often write $\bar{\Sigma}_t = \Sigma_{t-1} + R_t$ even for non-linear systems. **Always linearize via $G_t$** when the motion model is non-linear — this costs **2 marks** if missed.
> 2. **Sign errors in innovation:** $\nu_t = z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$, NOT $h(\bar{\mu}_t) - z_t$. Wrong sign inverts the entire correction.
> 3. **Covariance vs. variance confusion:** A scalar 1D problem uses variances (1×1); a 2D/3D problem uses covariance matrices. Mixing them costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring normalization:** In Bayesian formulation, students forget the $\eta$ constant. For Gaussian beliefs, normalization is implicit; for discrete grids, it must be shown explicitly.
> 5. **Confusing $\Sigma$ (covariance) with $\sigma^2$ (variance):** $K_t$ uses the **covariance** in matrix form — use $\bar{\Sigma}_t$, not $\bar{\sigma}_t^2$.
> 6. **Skipping the dimensional analysis:** $K_t$ is dimensionless; $\mu_t$ has units of metres/radians. Forgetting units in the final answer loses 0.5 mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Single-Hypothesis Belief**

- **Definition:** Belief = robot's internal estimate of its state. Single-hypothesis = exactly **one** point/Gaussian estimate.
- **State vector** for 2D ground robot: $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$ (size $n = 3$).
- **Two forms:** Point estimate $\hat{x}_t$ (deterministic) or unimodal Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ (probabilistic).
- **Bayes recursion:** Predict using motion model, then update using measurement model.
- **Key equations to memorize (verbatim):**
  - $\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$
  - $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$
  - $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$
  - $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - H_t \bar{\mu}_t)$
  - $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$
- **Jacobian $G_t$** linearizes $g(\cdot)$ for EKF; **Jacobian $H_t$** linearizes $h(\cdot)$.
- **Innovation $\nu_t$** = mismatch between actual and predicted measurement.
- **Kalman gain $K_t$** optimally trades off prediction trust vs. measurement trust.
- **Joseph form** $\Sigma_t = (I - KH)\bar{\Sigma}(I - KH)^T + K Q K^T$ is numerically stable.
- **Mahalanobis distance** $d^2 = \nu_t^T S_t^{-1} \nu_t$ is used for data-association gating.
- **Limitations:** Cannot represent multi-modal beliefs, fails in symmetric environments, cannot solve the kidnapped robot problem.
- **Use case in industry:** GPS/INS fusion, drone stabilization, Mars rovers, vacuum-cleaner SLAM (early versions).
- **Next step in KTU syllabus:** Multi-Hypothesis Belief (Particle Filters, Grid-based Markov Localization — Module 5).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4
