<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4: Multiple-Hypothesis Belief

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Belief in Mobile Robotics):**
> In probabilistic mobile robotics, the **belief** of a robot is defined as the posterior probability distribution over the robot's state $x_t$ at time $t$, conditioned on all past sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control actions $u_{1:t}$, along with the map $m$. Formally, $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple-Hypothesis Belief (MHB) — KTU Board Definition:**
> A **Multiple-Hypothesis Belief** is a representation of the robot's state uncertainty in which the belief distribution is approximated (or expressed exactly) as a *finite mixture* of $M$ weighted hypotheses, each hypothesis being a candidate state estimate. Mathematically,
> $$bel(x_t) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} \, \delta(x_t - x_t^{[i]})$$
> where $w_t^{[i]}$ is the non-negative weight of hypothesis $i$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} = 1$. Each hypothesis $x_t^{[i]}$ is a *plausible* robot state and its weight reflects the relative plausibility of that state given the data seen so far.

### 1.1 Why Multiple Hypotheses Are Needed

A **single-hypothesis** belief (e.g., a single Gaussian $x_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$) cannot represent *multi-modal* uncertainty. Many real-world robotic problems are inherently multi-modal:

| Problem | Why Single-Hypothesis Fails | Why Multi-Hypothesis Succeeds |
|---|---|---|
| Global localization (kidnapped robot) | Single Gaussian cannot span a large unknown space | Hypotheses cover the entire map; the correct one is selected via measurements |
| Symmetric environments (corridors, identical rooms) | All symmetric positions have the same likelihood | One hypothesis per symmetry branch is maintained |
| Data association ambiguity | A single landmark match is forced | Multiple landmark-to-landmark matching hypotheses are tracked |
| Perceptual aliasing | Distinct locations look identical in sensors | Each alias is a separate hypothesis with its own weight |
| Robust recovery from failure | Tracking error is unbounded once lost | Multiple "rescue" hypotheses are kept active |

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Detective's Suspect Board:**
> Imagine a detective trying to locate a missing person in a city of 1 million people. The detective pins **several photographs** (hypotheses) on a board, each representing a *possible* location. As new information comes in (a witness report, a phone ping, a sighting), the detective updates a *weight* (probability) on each photograph: photos that fit the new information get heavier pins, photos that contradict it get their pins removed. The board is the **multiple-hypothesis belief**, the photographs are the **hypotheses**, and the pins are the **weights**.
> 
> The detective never throws away *all* photographs just because one looks unlikely — they keep a *portfolio* of plausible candidates. The total number of photographs is bounded (the detective has finite wall space), so low-weight photos are periodically pruned. This is *exactly* how a mobile robot's localization filter works.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Minimum hypotheses** for global localization: $M \geq 1$ (typically $M \gg 1$).
- **Particle count** (a common MHB implementation): typically **100 to 5000** particles for indoor mobile robots.
- **Weight threshold** for pruning: typically $w_{min} \approx 0.001$ (i.e., a hypothesis with weight below 0.1% is discarded).
- **Effective sample size** threshold for resampling: typically $N_{eff} < 0.5 \, N$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Belief Representations

The choice of representation determines whether a single or multiple-hypothesis approach is used:

| Representation | Form | Single/Multiple | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Gaussian** | $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$ | Single-hypothesis | Tracking with low uncertainty (EKF) |
| **Histogram / Grid** | Discrete cells over state space | Multi-hypothesis (implicit) | Global localization in small maps |
| **Particle Filter (SIR)** | Set of weighted samples $\{(x^{[i]}, w^{[i]})\}$ | Multi-hypothesis (sample-based) | Non-linear, non-Gaussian problems |
| **Mixture of Gaussians** | $\sum_i w^{[i]} \mathcal{N}(\mu^{[i]}, \Sigma^{[i]})$ | Multi-hypothesis (parametric) | Multi-modal tracking with smooth modes |
| **Exact Multi-Hypothesis (MHT)** | Track-oriented hypotheses with trees | Multi-hypothesis (combinatorial) | Multi-target tracking, data association |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Focal Point:** The most common MHB used in KTU-board questions is the **particle-filter representation**, which is a *sample-based* multiple-hypothesis belief. The Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) algorithm is the canonical example.

### 2.2 Particle Filter as Multiple-Hypothesis Belief

The particle filter maintains $M$ samples (hypotheses) of the state, each with a weight. This is the **most KTU-relevant** form of MHB.

$$bel(x_t) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} \, \delta(x_t - x_t^{[i]})$$

where the normalization is $\sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} = M$ if using the *importance-sampling* form, or $\sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} = 1$ if using the *normalized* form. The two are equivalent up to a global factor.

### 2.3 The Bayes Filter Update in MHB Form

The general recursive Bayes filter update for a multiple-hypothesis belief is:

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

In the particle-filter implementation, this becomes three steps: **predict, weight, resample**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three steps?** The predict step propagates hypotheses forward through the motion model; the weight step updates each hypothesis's plausibility using the sensor model; the resample step prunes low-weight hypotheses and duplicates high-weight ones to maintain a healthy hypothesis population.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $bel(x_t) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} \delta(x_t - x_t^{[i]})$ | Multiple-hypothesis belief representation | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} = 1$ | Normalization of weights | dimensionless |
| 3 | $w_t^{[i]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$ | Weight update from sensor likelihood | dimensionless |
| 4 | $x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$ | Hypothesis propagation via motion model | varies (m, rad) |
| 5 | $\eta = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{M} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)\right]^{-1}$ | Normalizing constant | dimensionless |
| 6 | $N_{eff} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} (w_t^{[i]})^2}$ | Effective number of hypotheses (Kish's formula) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $w_{t}^{[i]} = \dfrac{1}{M}$ after resampling | Reset weights to uniform after resampling | dimensionless |
| 8 | $r_i = r_{i-1} + w_t^{[i]}$ (cumulative) | Systematic resampling rule | dimensionless |
| 9 | $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t) \sim \mathcal{N}(g(x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t), R_t)$ | Motion model with Gaussian noise | state units |
| 10 | $p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m) \propto \exp\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}(z_t - h(x_t^{[i]}, m))^T Q^{-1} (z_t - h(x_t^{[i]}, m))\right)$ | Sensor likelihood (Gaussian beam model) | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the weight-update equation, students often write $w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$ and *forget* the proportionality sign or normalization. The weights **must** be normalized so they sum to 1 (or to $M$ in the unnormalized form). Forgetting $\eta$ is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

Multiple-hypothesis belief representations are the workhorse of:

- **Autonomous vehicles (self-driving cars)**: Tracking multiple possible locations of pedestrians, vehicles, and the ego-vehicle in heavy occlusion.
- **Warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva)**: Global localization after being moved, where the robot must re-localize without any prior pose estimate.
- **Mars rovers (NASA Perseverance)**: Visual place recognition with extreme perceptual aliasing in rocky terrain — multiple matched locations are tracked until disambiguated.
- **Indoor cleaning robots (Roomba)**: The "kidnapped robot" recovery uses MHB to recover from being manually relocated.
- **Surveillance drones**: Multi-hypothesis tracking (MHT) of multiple ground targets.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes Filter to Particle Filter (Multiple-Hypothesis Belief)

The derivation connects the abstract recursive Bayes filter to the concrete particle-filter update rule. This is a **high-yield derivation** for KTU 14-mark questions.

**Goal:** Show that the particle-filter weight update $w_t^{[i]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$ follows rigorously from the Bayes filter recursion, using importance sampling.

**Step 1 — Start from the recursive Bayes filter posterior:**

By the Markov property and Bayes' rule, the posterior over the trajectory is:

$$p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, p(x_{1:t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}, m)$$

The constant $\eta$ normalizes the result to be a valid probability distribution.

**Step 2 — Marginalize to the current state only:**

To obtain $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$, we integrate (sum, in discrete form) over all past states:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

This is the **prediction + correction** form of the Bayes filter, valid for *any* belief representation.

**Step 3 — Represent the prior belief as a sum of Dirac deltas:**

Assume the previous belief is approximated by a set of $M$ weighted point hypotheses:

$$bel(x_{t-1}) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, \delta(x_{t-1} - x_{t-1}^{[i]})$$

This is the **multiple-hypothesis belief** at time $t-1$.

**Step 4 — Substitute the MHB form into the prediction integral:**

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, \delta(x_{t-1} - x_{t-1}^{[i]}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

Pull the sum outside the integral (linearity of integration):

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, \delta(x_{t-1} - x_{t-1}^{[i]}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 5 — Use the sifting property of the Dirac delta:**

The integral $\int f(x_{t-1}) \, \delta(x_{t-1} - x_{t-1}^{[i]}) \, dx_{t-1} = f(x_{t-1}^{[i]})$ by definition. Applying this:

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$$

The motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$ tells us how to *propagate* hypothesis $i$ to a new predicted hypothesis $\tilde{x}_t^{[i]}$.

**Step 6 — Apply the sensor correction (multiplication by likelihood):**

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$$

For the discrete sample case, we evaluate this *only* at the propagated sample points $\tilde{x}_t^{[i]}$:

$$w_t^{[i]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid \tilde{x}_t^{[i]}, m)$$

This is the famous **importance-weight update** for the particle filter.

**Step 7 — Normalize the weights:**

$$\eta = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{M} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, p(z_t \mid \tilde{x}_t^{[i]}, m)\right]^{-1}, \quad w_t^{[i]} = \eta \, w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, p(z_t \mid \tilde{x}_t^{[i]}, m)$$

The belief at time $t$ is now:

$$bel(x_t) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} \, \delta(x_t - \tilde{x}_t^{[i]})$$

**This is the new multiple-hypothesis belief.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each hypothesis is propagated through the motion model, then re-weighted by how well its predicted sensor reading matches the actual reading. Hypotheses that "see" the right things become heavy; hypotheses that "see" wrong things become light.

### 3.2 Derivation: Effective Number of Hypotheses (Kish's Formula)

Given normalized weights $w_t^{[i]}$ with $\sum_i w_t^{[i]} = 1$, the **effective sample size** $N_{eff}$ measures how many "truly independent" hypotheses the particle set represents. A set with one dominant weight has $N_{eff} \approx 1$; a uniform set has $N_{eff} = M$.

**Step 1 — Define the estimator of an expectation under the discrete belief:**

For any function $f$, $\mathbb{E}[f(x_t)] = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} f(x_t^{[i]})$.

**Step 2 — Compute the variance of the importance weights:**

The weights are random variables whose variance quantifies "spread." Consider the second moment:

$$\mathbb{E}[(w_t^{[i]})^2] = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} \cdot (w_t^{[i]})^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{M} (w_t^{[i]})^2$$

(Here we used the law of total expectation with the belief itself as the sampling distribution.)

**Step 3 — Define effective sample size as the inverse of this second moment:**

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} (w_t^{[i]})^2}$$

**Step 4 — Verify boundary cases:**

- *Uniform weights*: $w_t^{[i]} = 1/M$ for all $i$. Then $\sum (w_t^{[i]})^2 = M \cdot (1/M)^2 = 1/M$, so $N_{eff} = M$. ✓ (All hypotheses are equally informative.)
- *Single dominant hypothesis*: $w_t^{[1]} = 1$, all others zero. Then $\sum (w_t^{[i]})^2 = 1$, so $N_{eff} = 1$. ✓ (Only one effective hypothesis.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Pattern:** Examiners often ask to *derive* Kish's formula or to *compute* $N_{eff}$ for a small example. Memorize the formula and the two boundary cases.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A mobile robot in a corridor has a belief represented by 5 hypotheses with the following (unnormalized) likelihood scores after observing a landmark:

| Hypothesis $i$ | $x_t^{[i]}$ (m) | $p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.1 | 0.05 |
| 2 | 4.3 | 0.60 |
| 3 | 5.0 | 0.25 |
| 4 | 7.8 | 0.08 |
| 5 | 9.2 | 0.02 |

**Step 1 — Compute the normalizing constant:**

$$\eta = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{5} p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)\right]^{-1} = \left[0.05 + 0.60 + 0.25 + 0.08 + 0.02\right]^{-1} = \frac{1}{1.00} = 1.00$$

**Step 2 — Compute normalized weights:**

$$w_t^{[1]} = 0.05, \quad w_t^{[2]} = 0.60, \quad w_t^{[3]} = 0.25, \quad w_t^{[4]} = 0.08, \quad w_t^{[5]} = 0.02$$

Sum check: $0.05 + 0.60 + 0.25 + 0.08 + 0.02 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 3 — Identify the most likely hypothesis (MAP estimate):**

$x_{MAP} = x_t^{[2]} = 4.3$ m, with weight 0.60.

**Step 4 — Compute effective number of hypotheses:**

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{(0.05)^2 + (0.60)^2 + (0.25)^2 + (0.08)^2 + (0.02)^2}$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.0025 + 0.36 + 0.0625 + 0.0064 + 0.0004} = \frac{1}{0.4318} \approx 2.32$$

This is far less than $M=5$, so the belief is *concentrated* on hypothesis 2 — most of the particles are not contributing much independent information.

**Step 5 — Decide on resampling:**

Since $N_{eff} \approx 2.32 < 0.5 \times 5 = 2.5$, resampling is triggered. After resampling, hypothesis 2 will dominate and many particles will collapse onto it.

### 3.4 Hypothesis Management Strategies

Multiple-hypothesis belief is only useful if the hypothesis set is **managed** over time. The three standard management operations are:

1. **Generation (Branching):** When new ambiguity is detected (e.g., a new landmark is seen that could be one of two identical landmarks), each existing hypothesis is *split* into multiple child hypotheses, one per ambiguity. Weights are split according to the prior.
2. **Update (Re-weighting):** Each hypothesis's weight is updated using the sensor likelihood, as derived above.
3. **Pruning / Merging:** Hypotheses with weights below a threshold $w_{min}$ are discarded. Hypotheses whose states are within a small distance $\epsilon$ of each other are merged into one with summed weight.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Multiple-Hypothesis Belief in a Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start(["Time t-1: Belief bel(x_{t-1}) = {x_prev[i], w_prev[i]}"]) --> Predict["Motion Model: p(x_t | x_{t-1}[i], u_t)"]
    Predict --> PropH["Propagate each hypothesis to new state x_pred[i]"]
    PropH --> MeasArr["Sensor measurement z_t arrives"]
    MeasArr --> SensMod["Sensor Model: p(z_t | x_pred[i], m)"]
    SensMod --> Weight["Re-weight: w_t[i] = eta * w_prev[i] * p(z_t | x_pred[i], m)"]
    Weight --> Norm["Normalize: sum_i w_t[i] = 1"]
    Norm --> NeffCheck{"N_eff < N/2 ?"}
    NeffCheck -- "Yes" --> Resample["Resample M hypotheses from weighted set"]
    NeffCheck -- "No" --> Skip["Keep current set"]
    Resample --> NewBel["Reset weights to 1/M"]
    Skip --> NewBel
    NewBel --> Output["New Belief bel(x_t) = {x_t[i], w_t[i]}"]
    Output --> Action["Robot acts: execute u_{t+1}"]
    Action --> Start
```

### 4.2 Hypothesis Lifecycle: Generate → Update → Prune → Merge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SG1 ["Time t-1: 3 hypotheses"]
        H1p["H1: x=2.1m, w=0.30"]
        H2p["H2: x=4.3m, w=0.55"]
        H3p["H3: x=9.2m, w=0.15"]
    end
    SG1 --> Predict["Predict step"]
    Predict --> SG2["Time t: After prediction, before update"]
    subgraph SG2
        H1q["H1: x=2.2m, w=0.30"]
        H2q["H2: x=4.5m, w=0.55"]
        H3q["H3: x=9.0m, w=0.15"]
    end
    SG2 --> Update["Update with sensor z_t"]
    Update --> SG3["Time t: After re-weighting"]
    subgraph SG3
        H1r["H1: w=0.05 (low)"]
        H2r["H2: w=0.85 (high)"]
        H3r["H3: w=0.10 (low)"]
    end
    SG3 --> Prune["Prune below w_min = 0.10"]
    Prune --> SG4["Time t: After pruning"]
    subgraph SG4
        H2s["H2: w=1.00 (renormalized)"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Comparison: Single-Hypothesis vs. Multiple-Hypothesis Belief

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SS ["Single-Hypothesis Belief"]
        SH1["x = 4.5 m, sigma = 0.3 m"]
        SH2["Probability density: single Gaussian peak"]
    end
    subgraph MS ["Multiple-Hypothesis Belief"]
        MH1["H1: x = 2.1 m, w = 0.05"]
        MH2["H2: x = 4.3 m, w = 0.60"]
        MH3["H3: x = 5.0 m, w = 0.25"]
        MH4["H4: x = 7.8 m, w = 0.08"]
        MH5["H5: x = 9.2 m, w = 0.02"]
    end
    Note1["Single Gaussian cannot capture multi-modal posteriors"]
    Note2["MHB can represent distinct peaks (e.g., symmetric corridor)"]
    SS -.-> Note1
    MS -.-> Note2
```

### 4.4 Hypothesis Tree: Data Association Ambiguity

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root["Hypothesis 0: Start, w=1.0"]
    Root --> A["Detected landmark could be L1"]
    Root --> B["Detected landmark could be L2"]
    Root --> C["Detected landmark could be L3"]
    A --> A1["After motion: w=0.40"]
    A --> A2["After wrong sensor match: w=0.05"]
    B --> B1["After good match: w=0.50"]
    B --> B2["Pruned: w < threshold"]
    C --> C1["Plausible alternative: w=0.04"]
    C --> C2["Pruned: w < threshold"]
    A2 --> A2End["Discarded"]
    B2 --> B2End["Discarded"]
    C2 --> C2End["Discarded"]
    A1 --> Survive["Surviving hypotheses continue to next step"]
    B1 --> Survive
    C1 --> Survive
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]**
*Define a multiple-hypothesis belief in mobile robotics. In what situation is it preferred over a single-hypothesis belief? (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A multiple-hypothesis belief (MHB) is a representation of a robot's state uncertainty as a finite weighted sum of $M$ candidate state hypotheses:

$$bel(x_t) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} \, \delta(x_t - x_t^{[i]}), \quad \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} = 1$$

where each $x_t^{[i]}$ is a candidate state and $w_t^{[i]}$ is its non-negative weight summing to 1. It is preferred over a single-hypothesis belief in situations involving **multi-modal uncertainty**, such as the **kidnapped-robot problem** (global localization without prior pose), **perceptual aliasing** in symmetric environments, and **data association ambiguity** in landmark matching. A single Gaussian cannot represent multiple distinct modes of a posterior distribution, whereas MHB naturally captures all plausible candidate states.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]**
*What is the effective sample size $N_{eff}$ in a particle-filter-based multiple-hypothesis belief? When is resampling triggered? (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The effective sample size (Kish's formula) measures the number of *statistically independent* hypotheses in a weighted particle set:

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} (w_t^{[i]})^2}$$

For uniform weights, $N_{eff} = M$; for a single dominant hypothesis, $N_{eff} \to 1$. **Resampling is triggered** when $N_{eff}$ falls below a threshold — typically $N_{eff} < 0.5 \, M$ (or another fraction chosen by the designer). At that point, low-weight hypotheses are pruned and high-weight hypotheses are duplicated to maintain a healthy and diverse hypothesis population.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Preferred Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, OERUT721]**
**(a)** Derive the recursive update rule for the weights of a multiple-hypothesis belief represented by a particle filter. State clearly the role of the motion model and the sensor model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot maintains 6 hypotheses with unnormalized weights $p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$ as shown below (assume prior weights uniform $= 1/6$):

| $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$ | 0.10 | 0.30 | 0.45 | 0.05 | 0.08 | 0.02 |

Compute: (i) the normalized weights, (ii) the MAP hypothesis, (iii) the effective sample size $N_{eff}$, and (iv) decide whether resampling is justified if the threshold is $0.5 M$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A (a):**

**Step 1 — Begin from the Bayes filter posterior over the trajectory.** By Bayes' rule and the Markov property:

$$p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, p(x_{1:t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}, m)$$

**Step 2 — Marginalize to the current state.** Integrate over $x_{1:t-1}$:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

This is the predict-correct form of the Bayes filter.

**Step 3 — Approximate the prior belief as a sum of Dirac deltas.** This is the multiple-hypothesis belief at $t-1$:

$$bel(x_{t-1}) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, \delta(x_{t-1} - x_{t-1}^{[i]})$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and use the sifting property of the Dirac delta.** The integral becomes a sum:

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$$

The **motion model** $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$ is responsible for propagating hypothesis $i$ to its predicted new state.

**Step 5 — Apply the sensor correction (multiplication by likelihood).** The **sensor model** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ re-weights each hypothesis by how well its predicted measurement matches the actual measurement:

$$w_t^{[i]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid \tilde{x}_t^{[i]}, m)$$

**Step 6 — Normalize:**

$$w_t^{[i]} = \eta \, w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid \tilde{x}_t^{[i]}, m), \quad \eta = \left[\sum_{j=1}^{M} w_{t-1}^{[j]} \, p(z_t \mid \tilde{x}_t^{[j]}, m)\right]^{-1}$$

**Role summary:** The *motion model* propagates hypotheses forward in state space; the *sensor model* updates the weight of each propagated hypothesis based on measurement consistency. The combination yields the new MHB $bel(x_t) = \sum_i w_t^{[i]} \delta(x_t - \tilde{x}_t^{[i]})$.

---

**Model Solution for Question A (b):**

**(i) Normalized weights:**

Sum of likelihoods: $0.10 + 0.30 + 0.45 + 0.05 + 0.08 + 0.02 = 1.00$

Since priors are uniform, normalized weights equal the likelihoods divided by 1.00:

| $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $w_t^{[i]}$ | 0.10 | 0.30 | 0.45 | 0.05 | 0.08 | 0.02 |

Sum: $1.00$ ✓

**(ii) MAP hypothesis:**

$w_t^{[3]} = 0.45$ is maximum, so $x_{MAP} = x_t^{[3]}$.

**(iii) Effective sample size:**

$$N_{eff} = \frac{1}{0.10^2 + 0.30^2 + 0.45^2 + 0.05^2 + 0.08^2 + 0.02^2}$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.0100 + 0.0900 + 0.2025 + 0.0025 + 0.0064 + 0.0004}$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.3118} \approx 3.21$$

**(iv) Resampling decision:**

Threshold = $0.5 \times 6 = 3.0$. Since $N_{eff} \approx 3.21 > 3.0$, resampling is **not yet triggered** (though we are close to the threshold). The hypothesis set is diverse enough to defer resampling for one more step.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, OERUT721]**
**(a)** Explain with a suitable example why a single Gaussian belief representation is insufficient for global localization. Describe how the multiple-hypothesis belief overcomes this limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot operates in a symmetric corridor with two identical sections. After observing a wall, the belief is split into 3 hypotheses: $w_1 = 0.40, w_2 = 0.35, w_3 = 0.25$ at positions $x_1 = 1.0\,\text{m}, x_2 = 5.0\,\text{m}, x_3 = 9.0\,\text{m}$. The robot moves and observes a unique pattern at the *end* of the corridor. The new likelihoods are $p(z_t \mid x_1) = 0.02, p(z_t \mid x_2) = 0.94, p(z_t \mid x_3) = 0.04$. Compute the updated weights, identify the surviving hypothesis, and explain what happens to the others. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B (a):**

A single Gaussian belief $bel(x_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \sigma_t^2)$ is *unimodal* — it has exactly one peak and the density falls off monotonically on both sides. In **global localization**, the robot does not know its pose, and the posterior can be *flat* (no information) or *multi-modal* (multiple equally likely places).

**Example:** A robot is switched on in a long, symmetric office corridor. It sees a wall and a fire extinguisher. The fire extinguisher exists at three different corridor locations. The true posterior over the robot's position is a sum of three narrow peaks (one at each fire-extinguisher location), not a single Gaussian.

A single Gaussian would either (i) place the mean at the corridor center (which may be *physically impossible* for the robot to be at, e.g., inside a wall) or (ii) become extremely wide to cover all three modes, in which case it conveys no useful localization information.

The **multiple-hypothesis belief** explicitly represents the posterior as a sum of $M$ weighted Dirac deltas, one per mode. Each hypothesis has its own position and weight, so the three corridor locations are tracked independently. As the robot gathers more measurements, the wrong hypotheses are pruned (low weight) and the correct one is selected — this is the **hypothesis-discrimination** property of MHB.

**How MHB overcomes the limitation:**
- Hypotheses are placed at *all* plausible locations (e.g., drawn from the prior).
- Each hypothesis is re-weighted by sensor likelihoods.
- Low-weight hypotheses are pruned, collapsing the multi-modal distribution onto the correct mode.
- The system gracefully transitions from "no idea where I am" to "I am here" through principled Bayesian updates.

---

**Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the unnormalized posterior weights.**

Unnormalized: $w_t^{[i],*} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$.

| $i$ | $w_{t-1}^{[i]}$ | $p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ | $w_t^{[i],*}$ (unnormalized) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.40 | 0.02 | 0.0080 |
| 2 | 0.35 | 0.94 | 0.3290 |
| 3 | 0.25 | 0.04 | 0.0100 |

**Step 2 — Compute the normalizer.**

$$\eta = \frac{1}{0.0080 + 0.3290 + 0.0100} = \frac{1}{0.3470} \approx 2.882$$

**Step 3 — Normalize.**

| $i$ | $w_t^{[i]}$ (normalized) |
|---|---|
| 1 | $0.0080 \times 2.882 \approx 0.0231$ |
| 2 | $0.3290 \times 2.882 \approx 0.9481$ |
| 3 | $0.0100 \times 2.882 \approx 0.0288$ |

Sum check: $0.0231 + 0.9481 + 0.0288 = 1.0000$ ✓

**Step 4 — Identify the surviving hypothesis.**

Hypothesis 2 ($x_2 = 5.0$ m) survives with weight 0.9481 — over 94% of the belief mass.

**Step 5 — Fate of the other hypotheses.**

Hypothesis 1 (weight 0.0231) and hypothesis 3 (weight 0.0288) are below a typical pruning threshold of 0.05. They are **pruned** (discarded). In a particle-filter resampling step, the surviving particle at hypothesis 2 would be replicated to fill the population, and the belief becomes effectively unimodal at $x = 5.0$ m.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting normalization (–2 marks):** In part (b), writing the unnormalized weights as the final answer is a frequent error. Always compute and apply $\eta$.
> 2. **Wrong MAP identification (–1 mark):** The MAP hypothesis is the one with the *largest* weight, not the *first* hypothesis. Always check all weights.
> 3. **Confusing prior with posterior (–2 marks):** In part (a), the derivation must start from the Bayes filter and *derive* the weight update — not just state the formula. Examiners award full marks only for the full derivation.
> 4. **Missing the role of resampling (–1 mark):** When $N_{eff}$ falls below the threshold, mention that resampling duplicates high-weight hypotheses and discards low-weight ones. Just stating $N_{eff}$ without interpretation loses a mark.
> 5. **Unit confusion (–1 mark):** The weight $w_t^{[i]}$ is *dimensionless*. Writing "$w = 0.4$ meters" loses a mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Multiple-Hypothesis Belief**

- [x] **Definition:** MHB is $bel(x_t) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} \delta(x_t - x_t^{[i]})$ with $\sum_i w_t^{[i]} = 1$.
- [x] **Canonical example:** Particle filter (Monte Carlo Localization).
- [x] **Three-step particle filter update:** Predict (motion model) → Weight (sensor model) → Resample (prune + duplicate).
- [x] **Weight update formula:** $w_t^{[i]} = \eta \, w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, p(z_t \mid \tilde{x}_t^{[i]}, m)$.
- [x] **Normalization constant:** $\eta = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{M} w_{t-1}^{[i]} p(z_t \mid \tilde{x}_t^{[i]}, m)\right]^{-1}$.
- [x] **Kish's effective sample size:** $N_{eff} = 1 / \sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2$.
- [x] **Resampling trigger:** When $N_{eff} < \alpha M$ (typical $\alpha = 0.5$).
- [x] **When MHB is needed:** Kidnapped robot problem, symmetric environments, data association ambiguity, perceptual aliasing, multi-modal posteriors.
- [x] **When MHB is NOT needed:** Tracking with low, unimodal uncertainty → use EKF with a single Gaussian.
- [x] **Three hypothesis-management operations:** Generation (branching), Update (re-weighting), Pruning/Merging.
- [x] **Boundary cases for $N_{eff}$:** Uniform weights → $N_{eff} = M$; single dominant weight → $N_{eff} = 1$.
- [x] **Key properties:** MHB is a discrete, sample-based approximation to a continuous posterior; it converges to the true posterior as $M \to \infty$.
- [x] **Engineering applications:** Self-driving cars, warehouse robots, Mars rovers, cleaning robots, surveillance drones.
- [x] **Common pitfall to avoid:** Always normalize the weights; always state the MAP hypothesis explicitly; always interpret $N_{eff}$ in the answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Markov versus Kalman Localization (Basics Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT721 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Markov versus Kalman Localization (Basics Only) ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Mobile Robot Localization? **Localization** is the process of determining the pose (position and orientation) of a mobile robot relative to a known map of the environment, using sensor measurements and control actions. It is one of the fundamental problems in mobile robotics, often summarized as: *"Where am I?"* There are two foundational probabilistic paradigms taught at the KTU 2024 syllabus level for solving this problem: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)** > > 1. **Markov Localization** is a general, discrete-state probabilistic algorithm that estimates the robot's pose as a *probability distribution over the entire state space*. It makes no assumptions about the shape of the distribution and can simultaneously represent multiple hypotheses (multi-modal beliefs). > > 2. **Kalman Localization** is a specialized instance of the Bayes Filter that restricts the belief to a *single unimodal Gaussian distribution*, parameterized by a mean vector and a covariance matrix. It is the optimal estimator when noise is strictly Gaussian and systems are linear (or linearized). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 1 — "Finding a Lost Child in a Mall" (Markov Localization)** > Imagine a child is lost in a 3-floor mall. You maintain a *list* of probabilities for every possible location on every floor. At first, the probability is spread across many places (multi-modal). As you receive clues — "I hear a music box on Floor 2" — the probabilities on Floor 1 and 3 drop, but the distribution on Floor 2 might still have *multiple peaks* (e.g., near the toy store AND the candy shop). You track *all* possible locations simultaneously until one becomes overwhelmingly likely. This is **Markov Localization**. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 2 — "A Hiker with a GPS and Compass" (Kalman Localization)** > Now imagine a hiker who *already* knows roughly where they are (say, somewhere on Trail 7). The GPS gives noisy readings, and the compass has a small drift. The hiker tracks only *one* "best guess" location and a small "uncertainty circle" around it. As more measurements come in, the circle shrinks. There is *only one peak* in the hiker's belief. This is **Kalman Localization** — efficient, but if the initial guess is wrong, the hiker never recovers. --- ### 1.3 The Localization Problem — Formal Setup Let $x_t$ denote the robot's true pose at time $t$, $u_t$ the control input, and $z_t$ the measurement. The goal is to compute the posterior belief: $$ bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) $$ This is the **recursive Bayes filter** that both Markov and Kalman localization approximate. | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit | |---|---|---| | $x_t$ | Robot pose at time $t$ | meters, radians | | $u_t$ | Control / odometry input | m/s, rad/s | | $z_t$ | Sensor measurement | varies (m, pixels) | | $bel(x_t)$ | Belief — posterior probability | dimensionless $[0,1]$ | | $\mu_t$ | Mean of belief (Kalman) | meters, radians | | $\Sigma_t$ | Covariance matrix (Kalman) | $m^2$, $rad^2$ | > [!TIP] > **Key Takeaway for KTU:** Markov Localization is a *family* of algorithms (Grid-based, Topological, Monte Carlo). Kalman Localization is a *single, restrictive* algorithm. Every Kalman filter is a Markov filter, but not vice versa. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Bayes Filter Foundation Both algorithms implement the recursive **Bayes Filter**, which has two stages: 1. **Prediction Step** (using control $u_t$): $$ \overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} $$ 2. **Measurement Update Step** (using sensor $z_t$): $$ bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t) $$ where $\eta$ is a normalization constant. --- ### 2.2 Markov Localization — Operational Logic - Discretizes the state space into a finite set of hypotheses (e.g., grid cells, topological nodes). - Maintains a **discrete probability table** $p(x_t = x_i)$ for every cell $i$. - **No assumption** about distribution shape — supports **multi-modal** beliefs. - Computation grows with the number of cells. **Why it works:** The Markov assumption states that the future depends only on the present state, not the history. This makes the recursion tractable. --- ### 2.3 Kalman Localization — Operational Logic - Restricts $bel(x_t)$ to a **unimodal Gaussian**: $$ bel(x_t) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t) $$ - **Mean** $\mu_t$ = best estimate of pose. - **Covariance** $\Sigma_t$ = measure of uncertainty. - For non-linear motion/measurement models, the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** linearizes via Jacobians. - Computation is **constant time** per step — independent of environment size. **Why it works:** Under linear-Gaussian assumptions, the Bayes filter has a closed-form solution, which is the Kalman filter. --- ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Aspect | Markov Localization | Kalman Localization | |---|---|---| | Belief Representation | Discrete probability table / particles | Gaussian: $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ | | Distribution Shape | **Arbitrary** (multi-modal) | **Strictly unimodal Gaussian** | | Noise Assumption | None | **Gaussian, zero-mean** | | System Model | Any (non-linear, non-Gaussian) | Linear (or linearized via EKF) | | Prediction Equation | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \sum_i p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{(i)}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}^{(i)})$ | $\overline{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$
$\overline{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$ | | Update Equation | $bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | $K_t = \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$
$\mu_t = \overline{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - C_t \overline{\mu}_t)$
$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \overline{\Sigma}_t$ | | Computational Cost | $O(n)$ where $n$ = number of cells | $O(k^2)$ where $k$ = state dimension | | Global Localization | **Yes** (can start anywhere) | **No** (needs known initial pose) | | Kidnapped Robot Problem | **Can recover** | **Cannot recover** | | Sensor Map Size | Scales poorly for large maps | Constant cost | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU 2024:** Memorize the two-line Kalman update equations (Kalman Gain $K_t$ and covariance update). They are examiner favorites. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Markov Localization** is used in household robots (Roomba's descendants), warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva), and self-driving cars in complex urban grids where multi-modal hypotheses exist (e.g., "I could be in the left lane OR the right lane after a turn"). - **Kalman Localization** is the workhorse in GPS-INS fusion for aircraft, spacecraft attitude estimation, and SLAM front-ends due to its real-time, constant-time guarantees. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Bayes Filter Recursion **Step 1:** Apply Bayes' rule to factor the measurement likelihood: $$ p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) $$ **Step 2:** Apply the **Markov assumption** (state is complete): $$ p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t) $$ **Step 3:** Marginalize over the previous state $x_{t-1}$: $$ p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{1:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_{1:t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1} $$ **Step 4:** Apply Markov assumption again: $$ p(x_t \mid x_{1:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) $$ **Step 5:** Substitute to recover the canonical Bayes filter: $$ bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} $$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Kalman Filter (Linear-Gaussian Case) **Assumption 1 — Linear motion model:** $$ x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t) $$ **Assumption 2 — Linear measurement model:** $$ z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t, \quad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t) $$ **Step 1 — Prediction of state mean:** $$ \overline{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t $$ **Step 2 — Prediction of covariance (uncertainty grows):** $$ \overline{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t $$ **Step 3 — Innovation (measurement residual):** $$ \nu_t = z_t - C_t \overline{\mu}_t $$ **Step 4 — Innovation covariance:** $$ S_t = C_t \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t $$ **Step 5 — Kalman Gain (optimal blending weight):** $$ K_t = \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T S_t^{-1} $$ **Step 6 — Updated mean (corrected by measurement):** $$ \mu_t = \overline{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t $$ **Step 7 — Updated covariance (uncertainty shrinks):** $$ \Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \overline{\Sigma}_t $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** $K_t$ is a matrix that decides how much to *trust the new measurement* versus the *predicted estimate*. When sensor noise $Q_t$ is large, $K_t$ shrinks, and the filter trusts the prediction. When $Q_t$ is small, $K_t$ grows, and the filter trusts the measurement. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Detailed) | Comparison Axis | Markov Localization | Kalman Localization | |---|---|---| | **Mathematical Basis** | Bayes Filter (general) | Bayes Filter (linear-Gaussian special case) | | **State Space Type** | Continuous, discretized | Continuous, represented by Gaussian | | **Belief Storage** | $N$ probabilities (one per cell) | $k + k(k+1)/2$ scalars (mean + symmetric covariance) | | **Initialization** | Uniform over all cells (global) | Single Gaussian peak at initial guess | | **Convergence** | Asymptotic to true pose | Asymptotic to true pose *if* initialized near truth | | **Multi-hypothesis** | Native (multi-modal) | Impossible (forced to single peak) | | **Symmetric / Asymmetric Noise** | Both supported | Gaussian only | | **Loop Closure Handling** | Excellent | Poor (can lose track) | | **Algorithm Variants** | Grid, Topological, Monte Carlo (MCL) | KF, EKF, UKF, IEKF | | **Failure Recovery** | Robust to global relocalization | Requires external reset | | **Typical Application** | Indoor service robots, dynamic environments | Aerospace, GPS-INS, structured SLAM | | **Time per Step** | $O(\vert X \vert)$ | $O(k^3)$ from matrix inversion | | **Space Complexity** | $O(\vert X \vert)$ | $O(k^2)$ | Where $\vert X \vert$ is the cardinality of the discretized state space and $k$ is the state dimension. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Bayes Filter Family ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Bayes Filter\nGeneral Recursive Estimator]:::root --> B0{Predict Step} A0 --> C0{Update Step} B0 --> D0[Propagate belief using control u_t] C0 --> E0[Correct belief using measurement z_t] D0 --> F0[Posterior belief bel x_t] E0 --> F0 A0 --> G0[Markov Localization] A0 --> H0[Kalman Localization] G0 --> G1[Grid based] G0 --> G2[Topological] G0 --> G3[Monte Carlo MCL] H0 --> H1[Linear KF] H0 --> H2[Extended EKF] H0 --> H3[Unscented UKF] F0 --> I0[Output pose estimate] classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Markov Localization — Recursive Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Start: bel x_0 = uniform] --> S2[Read control u_t] S2 --> S3[Apply motion model\np x_t given u_t, x_t-1] S3 --> S4[Predicted belief\nbar bel x_t] S4 --> S5[Read measurement z_t] S5 --> S6[Apply sensor model\np z_t given x_t] S6 --> S7[Multiply and normalize\neta * p * bar bel] S7 --> S8[Updated belief\nbel x_t] S8 --> S9{Next time step} S9 --> S2 classDef step fill:#d9e8f5,stroke:#1f4e79 class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8,S9 step ``` ### 4.3 Kalman Localization — Recursive Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR K1[Start: mu_0, Sigma_0] --> K2[Predict mean\nmu_bar = A mu + B u] K2 --> K3[Predict covariance\nSigma_bar = A Sigma A_T + R] K3 --> K4[Compute innovation\nnu = z - C mu_bar] K4 --> K5[Compute Kalman Gain\nK = Sigma_bar C_T S_inv] K5 --> K6[Update mean\nmu = mu_bar + K nu] K6 --> K7[Update covariance\nSigma = I - K C Sigma_bar] K7 --> K8[Output pose: mu, Sigma] K8 --> K9{Next time step} K9 --> K2 classDef kstep fill:#fde9d9,stroke:#b45f06 class K1,K2,K3,K4,K5,K6,K7,K8,K9 kstep ``` ### 4.4 Side-by-Side Belief Evolution ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph M[Markov Localization Belief] M1[Time t=0: Uniform distribution] --> M2[Time t=1: Two peaks appear] M2 --> M3[Time t=2: One peak dominates] M3 --> M4[Time t=3: Sharp unimodal peak] end subgraph K[Kalman Localization Belief] K1[Time t=0: Single wide Gaussian] --> K2[Time t=1: Gaussian shifts] K2 --> K3[Time t=2: Gaussian narrows] K3 --> K4[Time t=3: Tight Gaussian] end classDef mstep fill:#cce5cc,stroke:#38761d classDef kstep fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000 class M1,M2,M3,M4 mstep class K1,K2,K3,K4 kstep ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** In Markov, the belief can briefly hold *two competing peaks* before one wins. In Kalman, the belief is *always a single bell curve* — it can shift and narrow, but never split. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > > **Q1. Differentiate between Markov Localization and Kalman Localization based on belief representation.** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > 1. **Markov Localization** represents the robot's belief as a *discrete probability distribution* over the entire state space, with no restriction on the shape. It supports **multi-modal** beliefs and can represent multiple simultaneous pose hypotheses. *(1.5 marks)* > 2. **Kalman Localization** represents the belief as a *single Gaussian distribution* $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$, characterized only by a mean vector and a covariance matrix. It is strictly **uni-modal**. *(1.5 marks)* > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > > **Q2. Why is Kalman Localization unable to recover from the kidnapped robot problem, while Markov Localization can?** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > 1. In the kidnapped robot problem, the robot is teleported to an arbitrary new location without warning, so its true pose no longer matches its belief. *(1 mark)* > 2. Kalman Localization maintains only a *single Gaussian peak*. Once the true pose diverges from this peak, sensor measurements only serve to *re-tighten* the Gaussian around the wrong location, and the filter cannot spawn a new peak elsewhere — recovery is **impossible** without external re-initialization. *(1 mark)* > 3. Markov Localization keeps a *distribution over the entire state space*. If sensor data contradicts the current peak, the probability mass naturally migrates to the correct region, allowing **autonomous global recovery**. *(1 mark)* --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the **two-step recursive structure** (prediction and update) of the Bayes filter as used in Markov Localization. Derive the recursive Bayes update equation from first principles. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A mobile robot operates in a 1-D corridor. Its initial belief is uniform over three cells: $bel(x_0) = \{0.33, 0.33, 0.34\}$. The motion model says the robot moves one cell to the right with probability $0.8$ and stays put with probability $0.2$. The sensor reading is "wall-detected" and $p(z_t \mid x_t) = \{0.6, 0.9, 0.3\}$ for the three cells respectively. Compute the posterior belief after one prediction and one update step. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) — Recursive Bayes Filter in Markov Localization (7 marks):** *Block Diagram Description (2 marks):* The Markov Localization algorithm is structured as a closed loop with two stages per time step: - **Prediction Stage:** Uses the motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ and the previous belief $bel(x_{t-1})$ to compute a *predicted* belief $\overline{bel}(x_t)$. - **Update Stage:** Uses the sensor model $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ to weight the predicted belief and produce the *corrected* belief $bel(x_t)$ via Bayes' rule. *Derivation (5 marks):* Start from the definition of the belief: $$ bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) $$ By definition of conditional probability: $$ bel(x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})} $$ The denominator is independent of $x_t$ and acts as a normalizer $\eta$: $$ bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) $$ Apply the **Markov assumption** (current measurement depends only on current state): $$ p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t) $$ Marginalize the second term over $x_{t-1}$: $$ p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1} $$ Recognize $p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = bel(x_{t-1})$ and apply Markov again: $$ p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) $$ Final recursive Bayes filter: $$ \boxed{bel(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \cdot bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}} $$ **(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):** *Prediction Step (3 marks):* Motion model: $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ shifts mass one cell to the right with probability 0.8, or keeps it with probability 0.2. | Cell $i$ | $\overline{bel}(x_t = i)$ calculation | Result | |---|---|---| | 1 | $0.8 \times 0$ (no cell 0) $+ 0.2 \times 0.33$ | **0.066** | | 2 | $0.8 \times 0.33 + 0.2 \times 0.33$ | **0.330** | | 3 | $0.8 \times 0.33 + 0.2 \times 0.34$ | **0.332** | Sum check: $0.066 + 0.330 + 0.332 = 0.728$ *(normalizer needed)* *Measurement Update Step (3 marks):* Multiply predicted belief by sensor likelihood: | Cell $i$ | $p(z_t \mid x_t = i)$ | $\overline{bel} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t)$ | Unnormalized | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.6 | $0.066 \times 0.6$ | 0.0396 | | 2 | 0.9 | $0.330 \times 0.9$ | 0.2970 | | 3 | 0.3 | $0.332 \times 0.3$ | 0.0996 | Sum $= 0.0396 + 0.2970 + 0.0996 = 0.4362$. Therefore $\eta = 1/0.4362 = 2.2925$. *Final Normalized Belief (1 mark):* $$ bel(x_t) = \{0.0908,\ \ 0.6810,\ \ 0.2283\} $$ The robot is now most likely in **cell 2**, consistent with the strong sensor reading there. *(1 mark for interpretation)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** State the assumptions of the **Kalman Filter**. Derive the Kalman gain formula starting from the minimization of the mean-squared error of the posterior estimate. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A 1-D mobile robot has the following Kalman Localization parameters at time $t-1$: state $\mu_{t-1} = 5.0$ m, covariance $\Sigma_{t-1} = 1.0\ \text{m}^2$. The linear motion model is $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \epsilon_t$ with $u_t = 1.0$ m and motion noise variance $R_t = 0.5\ \text{m}^2$. A position sensor provides $z_t = 6.5$ m with measurement noise variance $Q_t = 0.4\ \text{m}^2$, and the measurement matrix $C_t = 1$. Compute the posterior mean $\mu_t$ and covariance $\Sigma_t$. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) — Assumptions and Kalman Gain Derivation (7 marks):** *Assumptions (2 marks):* 1. The motion model and measurement model are **linear** in the state. *(1 mark)* 2. All noise — process noise $\epsilon_t$ and measurement noise $\delta_t$ — is **zero-mean Gaussian** with known covariances $R_t$ and $Q_t$. *(1 mark)* *Derivation of Kalman Gain (5 marks):* The optimal estimate is the one that **minimizes the posterior mean-squared error**: $$ J(\mu_t) = \mathbb{E}[(x_t - \mu_t)^T (x_t - \mu_t)] $$ The corrected mean has the form $\mu_t = \overline{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \overline{\mu}_t)$, where $K_t$ is the Kalman gain to be determined. Substitute the measurement model $z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t$: $$ \mu_t = \overline{\mu}_t + K_t(C_t x_t + \delta_t - C_t \overline{\mu}_t) $$ The error $e_t = x_t - \mu_t$ becomes: $$ e_t = (I - K_t C_t)(x_t - \overline{\mu}_t) - K_t \delta_t $$ The posterior covariance is: $$ \Sigma_t = \mathbb{E}[e_t e_t^T] = (I - K_t C_t) \overline{\Sigma}_t (I - K_t C_t)^T + K_t Q_t K_t^T $$ Take the derivative of the trace of $\Sigma_t$ with respect to $K_t$ and set it to zero: $$ \frac{\partial \, \text{tr}(\Sigma_t)}{\partial K_t} = -2 \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + 2 K_t (C_t \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t) = 0 $$ Solving for $K_t$: $$ \boxed{K_t = \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}} $$ **(b) — Numerical Kalman Update (7 marks):** *Given:* $\mu_{t-1} = 5.0,\ \Sigma_{t-1} = 1.0,\ u_t = 1.0,\ R_t = 0.5,\ z_t = 6.5,\ Q_t = 0.4,\ A_t = 1,\ B_t = 1,\ C_t = 1$ *Prediction (2 marks):* $$ \overline{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t = 1 \times 5.0 + 1 \times 1.0 = 6.0\ \text{m} $$ $$ \overline{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t = 1 \times 1.0 \times 1 + 0.5 = 1.5\ \text{m}^2 $$ *Innovation and Innovation Covariance (2 marks):* $$ \nu_t = z_t - C_t \overline{\mu}_t = 6.5 - 1 \times 6.0 = 0.5\ \text{m} $$ $$ S_t = C_t \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t = 1 \times 1.5 \times 1 + 0.4 = 1.9\ \text{m}^2 $$ *Kalman Gain (1 mark):* $$ K_t = \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T S_t^{-1} = 1.5 \times 1 \times \frac{1}{1.9} = 0.7895 $$ *Posterior Mean and Covariance (2 marks):* $$ \mu_t = \overline{\mu}_t + K_t \nu_t = 6.0 + 0.7895 \times 0.5 = 6.3947\ \text{m} $$ $$ \Sigma_t = (1 - K_t C_t) \overline{\Sigma}_t = (1 - 0.7895 \times 1) \times 1.5 = 0.3158\ \text{m}^2 $$ **Interpretation:** The posterior estimate is $\mu_t = 6.39$ m with reduced uncertainty $\Sigma_t = 0.32\ \text{m}^2$, lying between the prediction (6.0) and the measurement (6.5) — exactly as expected from a Kalman filter. *(Bonus remark)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting normalization $\eta$** in the Bayes update — costs 1–2 marks. The probabilities must sum to 1. > 2. **Confusing $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ with $bel(x_t)$** — predicted belief and corrected belief are different. Use distinct notation. > 3. **Transposing matrices incorrectly** in the covariance update — $A_t \Sigma A_t^T$ is NOT $A_t \Sigma A_t$. > 4. **Assuming Kalman works for global localization** — the syllabus explicitly tests this misconception. If a question says "robot is kidnapped," do NOT recommend Kalman. > 5. **Skipping units** in numericals — KTU examiners deduct half-marks for unit-less final answers. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Markov Localization** = general Bayes filter; **Kalman Localization** = linear-Gaussian special case. - [x] Belief in Markov is a **discrete probability table** (can be multi-modal). Belief in Kalman is a **Gaussian** $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ (strictly uni-modal). - [x] Bayes filter has two steps: **Prediction** (motion model) and **Update** (measurement model with $\eta$ normalization). - [x] Kalman update requires **Kalman Gain** $K_t = \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$. - [x] Kalman cannot solve **global localization** or **kidnapped robot problem** — Markov can. - [x] Markov is computationally heavy ($O(\vert X \vert)$) but robust; Kalman is fast ($O(k^2)$) but fragile. - [x] **EKF** is the variant for non-linear systems; it uses Jacobian linearization. - [x] In numericals: always report belief normalized, and Kalman output with units (m, m²). - [x] **Common KTU keywords to memorize:** "Markov assumption," "recursive Bayes filter," "unimodal Gaussian," "Kalman gain," "multi-hypothesis tracking."

topics.title: Path Planning: Local vs Global Path Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT721 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Path Planning: Local vs Global Path Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — PATH PLANNING: LOCAL vs GLOBAL PATH PLANNING

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Path Planning** is the computational problem of finding a valid, collision-free trajectory from an initial configuration to a goal configuration through a robot's environment, optimizing a chosen criterion (shortest distance, minimum time, lowest energy, etc.).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, path planning is broadly classified into two paradigms based on **knowledge of the environment** and **time of computation**:

### 🔹 Global Path Planning (Off-line / Deliberative Planning)
Global path planning refers to the computation of a complete trajectory *before* the robot begins motion, assuming **complete a priori knowledge** of the environment (static map). It operates on a static representation of the workspace and produces a holistic, optimal (or near-optimal) path from start to goal.

**Standard global planners include:**
- Visibility Graphs
- Voronoi Diagrams
- Cell Decomposition (Exact & Approximate)
- A* and Dijkstra search
- Probabilistic Roadmaps (PRM)

### 🔹 Local Path Planning (On-line / Reactive Planning)
Local path planning computes motion commands *in real time* using **only locally sensed information** (e.g., LIDAR, sonar, camera). It reacts to dynamic, unforeseen obstacles and is inherently re-planning at every control cycle.

**Standard local planners include:**
- Bug Algorithms (Bug1, Bug2)
- Artificial Potential Field (APF)
- Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)
- Vector Field Histogram (VFH)
- Receding Horizon Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Configuration Space (C-space) $\mathcal{C}$:** The set of all possible poses $(x, y, \theta)$ a robot can attain. A configuration $q \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is *free* if the robot at $q$ does not collide with any obstacle. Path planning searches for a continuous map $\tau: [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ such that $\tau(0) = q_{\text{start}}$ and $\tau(1) = q_{\text{goal}}$.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Two Ways to Walk Home

> [!TIP]
> **🗺️ Think of it this way:** Planning a road trip from your hostel in Trivandrum to Cochin using Google Maps *the night before* — when you know the entire highway network, traffic rules, and toll booths — is **global path planning**. You compute the optimal route offline and just follow it.
>
> Now imagine you're *actually walking* through a crowded MG Road footpath during Onam festival. You see a street vendor blocking the way, a child running across, and a road-divider. You make **split-second local decisions** — step left, slow down, veer right. This is **local path planning**: you don't have the full map, you only see what's in front of you, and you react.

The same robot typically uses **both** in a hierarchical architecture: global planner gives a coarse route, local planner refines it in real time to handle dynamics.

# 📐 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Global Path Planning — Operational Breakdown

Global planners work in **three sequential stages**:

1. **World Modeling**: Build a 2D/3D occupancy grid, topological map, or C-space representation of the static environment.
2. **Graph Construction**: Reduce the continuous planning problem to a discrete graph search. Nodes = reachable regions; edges = feasible transitions.
3. **Search & Optimization**: Apply graph search (Dijkstra, A*, D*) to find minimum-cost path under metric $L: \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$.

### 2.1.1 Visibility Graph Method
A roadmap in which the graph nodes are:
- $q_{\text{start}}$
- $q_{\text{goal}}$
- All obstacle vertices

Edges connect every pair of nodes that are **mutually visible** (line segment does not intersect any obstacle interior). Shortest path lies on this graph. Complexity: $O(n^3)$ for $n$ vertices.

### 2.1.2 Cell Decomposition
- **Exact (Trapezoidal)**: Slice the free space into convex trapezoids; build adjacency graph; perform graph search.
- **Approximate (Grid)**: Discretize the workspace into uniform cells of resolution $\delta$. Apply BFS, A*, or D* on the resulting 4/8-connected grid. Complexity: $O(N \log N)$ where $N$ is cell count.

### 2.1.3 A* Search (Workhorse of Global Planning)
A* extends Dijkstra with a heuristic $h(q)$ that estimates the remaining cost. Evaluation function:
$$f(q) = g(q) + h(q)$$

where $g(q)$ is the cost-so-far from $q_{\text{start}}$ and $h(q)$ is the heuristic estimate to $q_{\text{goal}}$. If $h$ is **admissible** (never overestimates), A* is **optimal** and **complete**.

---

## 2.2 Local Path Planning — Operational Breakdown

Local planners operate within a **sensor horizon** $R_s$ (perception radius). At every control tick $\Delta t$:

1. **Perception**: Read range measurements $\{z_i\}_{i=1}^n$ from onboard sensors.
2. **Local Map Update**: Maintain a short-horizon local costmap (rolling buffer).
3. **Command Generation**: Compute control $u = (v, \omega)$ that drives the robot toward a local subgoal while avoiding nearby obstacles.
4. **Execute & Loop**.

### 2.2.1 Artificial Potential Field (APF) — Local Variant
Treat the robot as a positively charged particle in a field generated by:
- **Attractor** at goal (pulls the robot in),
- **Repulsors** at obstacles (push the robot away).

The total potential at configuration $q$ is the scalar sum:
$$U(q) = U_{\text{att}}(q) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} U_{\text{rep}_j}(q)$$

The control force is the **negative gradient**:
$$F(q) = -\nabla U(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}}(q) - \sum_{j=1}^{m} \nabla U_{\text{rep}_j}(q)$$

A standard attractive potential (conic well):
$$U_{\text{att}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\zeta\,\Vert q - q_{\text{goal}} \Vert^{2}$$

with $\zeta > 0$ a gain constant. This gives a linear attractive force:
$$F_{\text{att}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} = -\zeta\,(q - q_{\text{goal}})$$

A standard repulsive potential (Khatib, 1986) with influence distance $\rho_0$:
$$U_{\text{rep}_j}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q, o_j)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}, & \rho(q, o_j) \leq \rho_0 \\[6pt] 0, & \rho(q, o_j) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

where $\eta$ is a positive gain and $\rho(q, o_j)$ is the minimum distance from $q$ to obstacle $o_j$.

### 2.2.2 Bug Algorithms — Purely Reactive Baselines
- **Bug1**: Robot moves toward goal until hitting an obstacle, then *circumnavigates* the entire boundary, records closest approach to goal, and resumes.
- **Bug2**: Robot moves toward goal along the $m$-line; on obstacle contact, follows boundary until the $m$-line is re-crossed, then resumes.

> [!WARNING]
> APF is **susceptible to local minima** — configurations where $F_{\text{att}} + F_{\text{rep}} = 0$ but $q \neq q_{\text{goal}}$. Real systems add randomization, virtual obstacles, or harmonic functions to escape these traps.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|--------------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\tau : [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | Continuous collision-free path | Definition of feasible path |
| 2 | $f(q) = g(q) + h(q)$ | A* evaluation; $g$ = cost-so-far, $h$ = heuristic | Global grid/graph search |
| 3 | $h(q) \leq d(q, q_{\text{goal}})$ | Admissibility condition | Guarantees A* optimality |
| 4 | $U(q) = U_{\text{att}}(q) + \textstyle\sum_j U_{\text{rep}_j}(q)$ | Total potential energy field | Local reactive planning |
| 5 | $F(q) = -\nabla U(q)$ | Artificial force on robot | Local control command |
| 6 | $U_{\text{att}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\zeta\,\Vert q - q_{\text{goal}} \Vert^{2}$ | Quadratic attractive well | Pulls robot to goal |
| 7 | $U_{\text{rep}_j}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta \left(1/\rho(q,o_j) - 1/\rho_0\right)^{2}$ | FIRAS repulsive field | Pushes from obstacle $j$ within $\rho_0$ |
| 8 | $F_{\text{att}}(q) = -\zeta\,(q - q_{\text{goal}})$ | Linear attractive force | Goal-pulling term |
| 9 | $L = \int_{0}^{1} \Vert \dot{\tau}(s) \Vert\, ds$ | Arc-length path cost | Optimality criterion |
| 10 | $R_s$ | Sensor/perception radius | Defines local planner horizon |
| 11 | $v, \omega$ | Linear & angular velocity commands | Mobile robot differential drive |
| 12 | $n^3$ | Visibility graph construction | Complexity for $n$ obstacle vertices |
| 13 | $O(N \log N)$ | A* on grid | $N$ = number of cells |
| 14 | $D(q, o_j) \geq d_{\text{robot}} + d_{\text{safety}}$ | Clearance condition | Safe passage inequality |

> [!NOTE]
> All gains $\zeta, \eta$ are positive real scalars in SI-consistent units (N·m for potential, N for force). Distances are in **metres (m)**, angles in **radians (rad)**.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva)**: Hybrid global A* (warehouse map) + local DWA (avoid humans, forklifts).
- **Autonomous cars (Waymo)**: HD-map global route + local occupancy grid with APF/DWA for pedestrians.
- **Mars Rovers (NASA)**: Visibility graph on DTM terrain + APF for local rock avoidance.
- **Surgical robots**: Global RRT* in pre-op scan + local compliance control for tissue deformation.
- **Drones (UAV swarms)**: PRM global + VFH local for collision-free flight in cluttered airspace.

# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: The A* Optimality Condition

We want to show that if $h(q)$ is **admissible** (i.e., $h(q) \leq d(q, q_{\text{goal}})$ for all $q \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$), then the first goal node expanded by A* lies on an optimal path.

**Step 1 — Setup:**
Let $\mathcal{G} = (V, E, w)$ be the search graph with edge weights $w(e) \geq 0$. Let $\pi^*$ be an optimal path from $q_s$ to $q_g$ with true cost $g^* = L(\pi^*)$.

**Step 2 — Define the A* priority queue:**
At any iteration, A* selects node $q$ with minimum $f(q) = g(q) + h(q)$ from the OPEN list.

**Step 3 — Bound $f$ on the optimal path:**
For any node $q_k$ on $\pi^*$, let $g_k^*$ be the true cost from $q_s$ to $q_k$ along $\pi^*$. Then:
$$f(q_k) = g_k^* + h(q_k) \leq g_k^* + d(q_k, q_g) = g_k^* + (g^* - g_k^*) = g^*$$

The middle inequality is **admissibility** of $h$.

**Step 4 — Conclude:**
The goal node $q_g$ on $\pi^*$ satisfies $f(q_g) = g^* + h(q_g) = g^*$ (since $h(q_g) = 0$). So $f(q_g) = g^*$. Any node popped from OPEN has $f \leq g^*$ until $q_g$ is popped. Hence A* terminates with cost exactly $g^*$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Artificial Potential Field Force Equations

**Starting point:** Let the robot configuration be $q = (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$ and the goal be fixed at $q_g = (x_g, y_g)$. Define the distance vector:
$$d(q) = q - q_g = (x - x_g,\; y - y_g)$$

**Step 1 — Define attractive potential** (quadratic in distance):
$$U_{\text{att}}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\zeta\,(x - x_g)^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\zeta\,(y - y_g)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\zeta\,\Vert q - q_g \Vert^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute attractive force** as the negative gradient in 2D:
$$F_{\text{att}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}}(q) = -\left(\frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial x},\; \frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial y}\right)$$

Taking partials:
$$\frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial x} = \zeta\,(x - x_g), \qquad \frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial y} = \zeta\,(y - y_g)$$

Therefore:
$$F_{\text{att}}(q) = -\zeta\,(x - x_g,\; y - y_g) = -\zeta\,(q - q_g)$$

Magnitude grows linearly with distance from goal — a strong pull far away, gentle near the goal.

**Step 3 — Define repulsive potential** for a single obstacle $o_j$ at position $o_j$ with influence distance $\rho_0$:
$$U_{\text{rep}_j}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}, & 0 < \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\[6pt] 0, & \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

where $\rho(q) = \Vert q - o_j \Vert$ is the Euclidean distance from robot to obstacle center.

**Step 4 — Compute repulsive force** for $\rho(q) \leq \rho_0$:
$$F_{\text{rep}_j}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{rep}_j}(q) = -\eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\cdot\frac{1}{\rho(q)^{2}}\cdot\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial q}$$

Since $\partial \rho / \partial q = (q - o_j)/\rho(q)$:
$$F_{\text{rep}_j}(q) = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\cdot\frac{1}{\rho(q)^{2}} \cdot \frac{q - o_j}{\rho(q)}$$

Simplifying:
$$F_{\text{rep}_j}(q) = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\cdot\frac{1}{\rho(q)^{3}} \cdot (q - o_j)$$

**Step 5 — Total force on robot:**
$$F(q) = F_{\text{att}}(q) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} F_{\text{rep}_j}(q) = -\zeta\,(q - q_g) + \sum_{j : \rho(q,o_j) \leq \rho_0} \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho(q,o_j)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\cdot\frac{1}{\rho(q,o_j)^{3}} \cdot (q - o_j)$$

**Step 6 — Velocity command** (for a differential-drive mobile robot):
$$v(k) = k_v\,\langle F(q),\, \hat{d}(q, q_g)\rangle, \qquad \omega(k) = k_\omega\,\angle(F(q),\, \hat{d}(q, q_g))$$

where $\hat{d}$ is the unit vector toward $q_g$, and $k_v, k_\omega > 0$ are control gains.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Local vs Global Planning

| Parameter | Global Path Planning | Local Path Planning |
|-----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Environment knowledge** | Complete static map required | Only sensor horizon $R_s$ |
| **Computational timing** | Off-line (pre-move) | On-line (per control cycle) |
| **Optimality** | Globally optimal (under chosen metric) | Locally optimal / suboptimal |
| **Reactivity to dynamics** | Poor (needs re-planning) | Excellent (reacts instantly) |
| **Memory footprint** | High (full map storage) | Low (rolling local buffer) |
| **Computational cost per step** | High ($O(N \log N)$) | Low ($O(n)$ where $n$ = sensed obstacles) |
| **Completeness** | Resolution-complete (grids) | Trap-prone (local minima) |
| **Typical algorithms** | A*, D*, PRM, RRT*, Visibility Graph | Bug1, Bug2, APF, DWA, VFH |
| **Sensor requirement** | Pre-built map (or SLAM backend) | Onboard range sensors (LIDAR/sonar) |
| **Failure mode** | Time/space explosion on fine grids | Oscillation, local minima, deadlock |
| **Best deployment** | Structured known environments | Crowded, dynamic, unknown scenes |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: A* on a 4-Connected Grid

A $5 \times 5$ grid with $q_s = (0,0)$ and $q_g = (4,4)$. Obstacles at $(2,2), (2,3), (3,2)$. Heuristic $h(q) = \vert x - x_g \vert + \vert y - y_g \vert$ (Manhattan).

**f-values computed by A*:**

| Node $(x,y)$ | $g(q)$ | $h(q)$ | $f(q)$ | Status |
|---------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| $(0,0)$ | $0$ | $8$ | $8$ | start |
| $(1,0)$ | $1$ | $7$ | $8$ | OPEN |
| $(0,1)$ | $1$ | $7$ | $8$ | OPEN |
| $(1,1)$ | $2$ | $6$ | $8$ | OPEN |
| $(2,1)$ | $3$ | $5$ | $8$ | OPEN |
| $(1,2)$ | $3$ | $5$ | $8$ | OPEN |
| $(2,0)$ | $2$ | $6$ | $8$ | OPEN |
| $(0,2)$ | $2$ | $6$ | $8$ | OPEN |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $(4,4)$ | $8$ | $0$ | $8$ | GOAL |

A* explores in order of increasing $f$, terminating with optimal cost $g^* = 8$ (8 unit steps, 4 right + 4 up).

# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Hierarchical Hybrid Planning Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Static World Map] --> B[Global Planner Module]
    A1[Dynamic Sensor Stream] --> C[Local Costmap Builder]
    B --> D[Global Path P1 ... Pn]
    D --> E[Local Goal Selector]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Local Planner Module]
    F --> G[Velocity Command v omega]
    G --> H[Mobile Robot Actuators]
    H --> I[New Sensor Measurements]
    I --> C
    I --> B
```

**Architecture flow narrative:**
- The **Global Planner** consumes the static map and emits a sequence of waypoints $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ (the coarse path).
- The **Local Costmap Builder** continuously fuses the rolling window of sensor readings into a short-horizon local occupancy grid.
- The **Local Goal Selector** picks the next waypoint on the global path that lies within sensor range — this becomes the local subgoal.
- The **Local Planner** (APF, DWA, or VFH) computes the immediate $(v, \omega)$ command to reach the local subgoal while avoiding sensed obstacles.
- The cycle re-iterates at the robot's control frequency (typically 10–50 Hz).

---

## 4.2 Configuration Space & Roadmap Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workspace W in R2] --> B[Robot Geometry R]
    B --> C[Configuration Space C]
    C --> D[Free Space Cfree]
    C --> E[Obstacle Space Cobs]
    D --> F[Roadmap Graph G = V, E]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Graph Search A Star]
    G --> H[Optimal Path tau]
```

**Narrative:** The robot of shape $\mathcal{R}$ is reduced to a point in its **configuration space** $\mathcal{C}$. Obstacles in $\mathcal{C}$ are the Minkowski sum $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{q : \mathcal{R}(q) \cap \mathcal{W}_{\text{obs}} \neq \emptyset\}$. The remaining free space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is sampled/partitioned into a graph, over which search yields $\tau$.

---

## 4.3 APF Force Decomposition Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Robot Configuration q] --> B[Compute Distance to Goal]
    A --> C[Compute Distances to Obstacles]
    B --> D[Attractive Force Fatt = zeta times qg minus q]
    C --> E[Repulsive Forces Frep j for each j]
    D --> F[Total Force F = Fatt + sum Frep]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Convert to v omega Commands]
    G --> H[Apply to Mobile Robot]
    H --> A
```

---

## 4.4 Bug2 Algorithm State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MoveToGoal
    MoveToGoal --> FollowBoundary : obstacle hit
    FollowBoundary --> MoveToGoal : m-line re-crossed closer to goal
    FollowBoundary --> NoPath : full loop with no progress
    MoveToGoal --> GoalReached : q equals qg
    GoalReached --> [*]
    NoPath --> [*]
```

**State meanings:**
- `MoveToGoal`: robot travels straight toward $q_g$ along the $m$-line.
- `FollowBoundary`: robot circumnavigates the contacted obstacle.
- `GoalReached`: termination success.
- `NoPath`: termination failure (target unreachable from start).

---

## 4.5 Comparative Flow: Global vs Local Decision Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GLOBAL_PLANNER [Global Planner Off Line]
        G1[Load Map] --> G2[Build Roadmap]
        G2 --> G3[Graph Search A Star]
        G3 --> G4[Output Waypoint List]
    end
    subgraph LOCAL_PLANNER [Local Planner On Line]
        L1[Read Sensors] --> L2[Update Local Costmap]
        L2 --> L3[Compute Local Command]
        L3 --> L4[Send v omega]
        L4 --> L1
    end
    G4 --> L1
```

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is an admissible heuristic in the A* algorithm? Why is admissibility required for optimality?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A heuristic $h(q)$ is **admissible** if it never overestimates the true cost to the goal, i.e.,
$$h(q) \leq d(q, q_{\text{goal}}) \quad \forall q \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$$

Admissibility is required because A* expands nodes in order of $f(q) = g(q) + h(q)$. If $h$ is admissible, then no path with cost less than $g^*$ can be missed: the first time the goal is popped from OPEN, $f(q_g) = g^*$, guaranteeing that the returned path is optimal. If $h$ overestimates, A* may settle on a suboptimal path prematurely. *(1 mark definition + 2 marks justification.)*
