## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an A*-Based Mobile Robot Planner

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sensor Input Layer: LIDAR, Camera, IMU] --> B[Perception and SLAM Module]
    B --> C[Occupancy Grid Map Builder]
    C --> D[Graph Constructor: Nodes and Edges]
    D --> E[A Star Search Engine]
    F[Goal Pose Input] --> E
    G[Heuristic Function Module] --> E
    H[Cost Function Evaluator] --> E
    E --> I[Open List Priority Queue]
    E --> J[Closed List Hash Set]
    E --> K[Path Reconstructor]
    K --> L[Path Smoother and Post Processor]
    L --> M[Motion Controller]
    M --> N[Wheel Actuator Commands]
    E --> O[Replanning Trigger: D Star Lite]
    O --> E
```

### 4.2 Algorithm Flowchart — A* Search

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Robot Activated]) --> Init[Initialize OPEN with start node]
    Init --> SetG[Set g of start to 0]
    SetG --> LoopCheck{OPEN is empty?}
    LoopCheck -->|Yes| Fail[Return Failure: No Path]
    LoopCheck -->|No| PopNode[Pop node n with minimum f]
    PopNode --> GoalTest{n is goal?}
    GoalTest -->|Yes| Reconstruct[Reconstruct path via parent pointers]
    GoalTest -->|No| MoveClosed[Move n to CLOSED]
    MoveClosed --> Expand[For each successor n prime of n]
    Expand --> ComputeG[Compute g tentative equals g of n plus c of n and n prime]
    ComputeG --> RelaxCheck{n prime not in CLOSED AND g tentative less than g of n prime?}
    RelaxCheck -->|Yes| UpdateNode[Update g, parent, h, f of n prime]
    UpdateNode --> AddOpen[Insert or update n prime in OPEN]
    RelaxCheck -->|No| Skip[Skip this successor]
    AddOpen --> LoopCheck
    Skip --> LoopCheck
    Reconstruct --> Smooth[Apply path smoothing]
    Smooth --> SendCmd[Send trajectory to motion controller]
    SendCmd --> End([Path Execution])
    Fail --> End
```

### 4.3 Comparison Diagram — Search Strategy Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Uniform_Cost
        UC1[Expands in rings around start] --> UC2[Guarantees optimal path] --> UC3[Slow: explores all directions equally]
    end
    subgraph Greedy_Best_First
        GB1[Expands toward goal greedily] --> GB2[Fast but non optimal] --> GB3[May get trapped in local minima]
    end
    subgraph A_Star
        AS1[Balances g and h] --> AS2[Optimal with admissible h] --> AS3[Industry standard for robotics]
    end
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Map — Graph Search Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root[Graph Search Algorithms] --> Uninformed[Uninformed Search]
    Root --> Informed[Informed Search]
    Uninformed --> BFS[Breadth First Search]
    Uninformed --> DFS[Depth First Search]
    Uninformed --> UCS[Uniform Cost Search: Dijkstra]
    Informed --> GBFS[Greedy Best First Search]
    Informed --> AStar[A Star Search]
    Informed --> WAStar[Weighted A Star]
    Informed --> DStar[D Star and D Star Lite]
    AStar --> Variants[Variants: Anytime A Star, Theta Star, Field D Star]
    DStar --> Variants2[Dynamic Replanning Variants]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**Define graph search in the context of mobile robotics. List any two graph search algorithms used in robot path planning.**

**Model Answer:**

Graph search in mobile robotics is the systematic exploration of a graph $G = (V, E)$ representing the robot's configuration space to find a sequence of valid transitions (edges) from a start node $q_{start}$ to a goal node $q_{goal}$. The graph abstracts the continuous environment into discrete states, enabling algorithmic path planning.

Two graph search algorithms:
1. **Dijkstra's Algorithm (Uniform-Cost Search):** Guarantees minimum-cost path using only the actual cost $g(n)$.
2. **A* Search:** Uses the evaluation function $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ with an admissible heuristic to find the optimal path faster than Dijkstra.

*(Any two of: BFS, DFS, Greedy Best-First, A*, D* Lite, Weighted A* are acceptable.)* — **3 marks**

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**What is an admissible heuristic? Why is admissibility important for A* optimality?**

**Model Answer:**

An **admissible heuristic** $h(n)$ is one that **never overestimates** the true cost $h^*(n)$ from node $n$ to the goal:

$$0 \leq h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \forall n$$

**Importance for A\* optimality:** If $h(n)$ is admissible, then for any node $n$ on the optimal path, $f(n) \leq C^*$ (the optimal solution cost). This guarantees that A* will never prematurely terminate at a suboptimal goal — it must first expand all nodes with $f < C^*$, ensuring the returned path has cost exactly $C^*$. — **3 marks**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Back-pointer tracking errors:** Students frequently forget to record/update the `parent` field during node expansion. Without correct parent pointers, the final path cannot be reconstructed. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 2. **Heuristic admissibility violations:** Using Euclidean distance on a 4-connected grid is *not admissible* (it overestimates). Always match heuristic to movement model. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Confusing $g(n)$ and $f(n)$:** $g$ is actual cost; $f$ is total estimated cost. A* expands by *minimum $f$*, not minimum $g$. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 4. **Not showing iteration tables in grid problems:** Examiners award marks for explicit step-by-step trace. Skipping the table = skipping marks. **Loss: 3–4 marks.**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain the A* algorithm for mobile robot path planning with its evaluation function. State and justify the conditions for the heuristic function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply A* on a 5×5 grid with the following setup and show the iteration table: Start at $(0,0)$, Goal at $(4,4)$, obstacles at $(1,2), (2,2), (3,2)$. Use 4-connected movement (cost = 1) and Manhattan distance heuristic. Draw the final optimal path. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The A* algorithm (Hart, Nilsson, Raphael — 1968) is a best-first search that finds a minimum-cost path through a graph by combining:
- **Actual cost-so-far** $g(n)$: the known cost from start to node $n$.
- **Estimated cost-to-go** $h(n)$: a heuristic estimate from $n$ to goal.

The evaluation function is:
$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

**Procedure:**
1. Initialize $OPEN$ with the start node; set $g(s) = 0$, $f(s) = h(s)$.
2. While $OPEN$ is non-empty, pop the node $n$ with minimum $f(n)$.
3. If $n$ is the goal, reconstruct the path and terminate.
4. Otherwise, expand $n$: for each successor $n'$ of $n$, if the path through $n$ is cheaper than the current best, update $g(n')$, set parent, recompute $f(n')$, and add to $OPEN$.

**Conditions on the heuristic:**

1. **Admissibility** — $0 \leq h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$. Justification: prevents overestimation, ensuring A* never bypasses the optimal solution. If $h$ overestimates, A* may favor a path that *looks* cheap but is actually expensive.

2. **Consistency (Monotonicity)** — $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ for every edge $(n, n')$. Justification: ensures that $f(n)$ is non-decreasing along any path, so once a node is expanded it need not be re-expanded. This makes A* optimally efficient among algorithms using the same heuristic. — **7 marks**

**Model Solution to (b):**

Heuristic: $h(\text{row}, \text{col}) = \vert \text{row} - 4 \vert + \vert \text{col} - 4 \vert$

**Grid layout (X = obstacle):**

| | C0 | C1 | C2 | C3 | C4 |
|---|----|----|----|----|----|
| **R0** | S | | | | |
| **R1** | | | X | | |
| **R2** | | | X | | |
| **R3** | | | X | | |
| **R4** | | | | | G |

**Key h-values:** $h(0,0) = 8$, $h(0,4) = 4$, $h(4,0) = 4$, $h(4,4) = 0$, $h(1,0) = 7$, $h(1,1) = 6$, $h(1,3) = 4$, $h(1,4) = 3$, $h(2,1) = 5$, $h(2,3) = 3$, $h(2,4) = 2$, $h(3,1) = 4$, $h(3,3) = 2$, $h(3,4) = 1$, $h(4,3) = 1$.

**Iteration Table (abbreviated for space):**

| Iter | Expanded $n$ | $g(n)$ | $h(n)$ | $f(n)$ | New/Updated Nodes | $g$, $f$ for new nodes |
|------|--------------|--------|--------|--------|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $(0,0)$ | 0 | 8 | 8 | $(0,1)$, $(1,0)$ | $g=1, f=8$ each |
| 2 | $(0,1)$ | 1 | 7 | 8 | $(0,2)$ | $g=2, f=8$ |
| 3 | $(1,0)$ | 1 | 7 | 8 | $(2,0)$ | $g=2, f=8$ |
| 4 | $(0,2)$ | 2 | 6 | 8 | $(0,3)$ | $g=3, f=7$ |
| 5 | $(2,0)$ | 2 | 6 | 8 | $(3,0)$, $(2,1)$ | $g=3, f=7$ ; $g=3, f=8$ |
| 6 | $(0,3)$ | 3 | 5 | 8 | $(0,4)$, $(1,3)$ | $g=4, f=8$ ; $g=4, f=8$ |
| 7 | $(3,0)$ | 3 | 5 | 8 | $(4,0)$, $(3,1)$ | $g=4, f=8$ ; $g=4, f=8$ |
| 8 | $(0,4)$ | 4 | 4 | 8 | $(1,4)$ | $g=5, f=8$ |
| 9 | $(1,3)$ | 4 | 4 | 8 | $(2,3)$ | $g=5, f=8$ |
| 10 | $(4,0)$ | 4 | 4 | 8 | $(4,1)$ | $g=5, f=8$ |
| 11 | $(3,1)$ | 4 | 4 | 8 | $(4,1)$ (no improvement) | — |
| 12 | $(1,4)$ | 5 | 3 | 8 | $(2,4)$ | $g=6, f=8$ |
| 13 | $(2,3)$ | 5 | 3 | 8 | $(3,3)$ | $g=6, f=8$ |
| 14 | $(4,1)$ | 5 | 3 | 8 | $(4,2)$ | $g=6, f=7$ |
| 15 | $(2,4)$ | 6 | 2 | 8 | $(3,4)$ | $g=7, f=8$ |
| 16 | $(3,3)$ | 6 | 2 | 8 | $(4,3)$ | $g=7, f=8$ |
| 17 | $(4,2)$ | 6 | 2 | 8 | — | — |
| 18 | $(3,4)$ | 7 | 1 | 8 | $(4,4)$ | $g=8, f=8$ |
| 19 | $(4,3)$ | 7 | 1 | 8 | $(4,4)$ (no improvement) | — |
| 20 | $(4,4)$ | 8 | 0 | 8 | **GOAL!** | — |

**Path Reconstruction:**
$$(4,4) \leftarrow (3,4) \leftarrow (2,4) \leftarrow (1,4) \leftarrow (0,4) \leftarrow (0,3) \leftarrow (0,2) \leftarrow (0,1) \leftarrow (0,0)$$

**Optimal Path:** $(0,0) \to (0,1) \to (0,2) \to (0,3) \to (0,4) \to (1,4) \to (2,4) \to (3,4) \to (4,4)$ — Path cost = **8 steps**.

(Alternative equally-optimal path via the bottom route is also acceptable.) — **7 marks**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Compare Dijkstra's algorithm and A* algorithm in terms of evaluation function, optimality, and use of domain knowledge. State two situations where A* would be preferred over Dijkstra. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, explain how graph search integrates into the overall mobile robot navigation stack. Mention the role of SLAM, occupancy grid, and path smoother in this pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

| Aspect | Dijkstra (Uniform-Cost) | A* |
|--------|--------------------------|------|
| Evaluation function | $f(n) = g(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| Heuristic use | None (blind search) | Admissible heuristic $h(n)$ |
| Search direction | Expands uniformly in all directions (rings around start) | Biased toward goal via heuristic |
| Optimality | Optimal (for non-negative costs) | Optimal (if $h$ is admissible) |
| Efficiency | Slower — explores all reachable nodes up to $C^*$ | Faster — prunes nodes that cannot be on optimal path |
| Information required | Only edge costs | Edge costs + heuristic estimate |
| Memory | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ |
| Use case | Shortest path in graphs without goal information | Goal-directed search in known/semi-known environments |

**Two situations where A* is preferred over Dijkstra:**

1. **Large configuration spaces with a known goal:** When $\vert V \vert$ is large (e.g., a $1000 \times 1000$ occupancy grid = 1 million nodes), Dijkstra would expand nodes in all directions up to the goal cost. A*'s heuristic prunes a vast majority of these, often reducing expanded nodes by 10×–100×.

2. **Real-time replanning needs:** In dynamic environments, A* (or D* Lite) can find a new path quickly when the goal or obstacles change, because the heuristic guides search directly toward the new goal.

(Other acceptable answers: known environment with Euclidean grids, robot path planning in warehouses, video game AI pathfinding.) — **7 marks**

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Sensors[LIDAR, Camera, IMU, Wheel Encoders] --> SLAM[SLAM Module: Localization and Mapping]
    SLAM --> Grid[Occupancy Grid Map: 2D probabilistic grid]
    Grid --> Graph[Graph Constructor: nodes at free cells, edges between neighbors]
    Goal[Goal Pose Input] --> Search[Graph Search Engine: A Star or D Star Lite]
    Graph --> Search
    Heur[Heuristic Module: Manhattan or Euclidean] --> Search
    Search --> RawPath[Raw Sequence of Waypoints]
    RawPath --> Smoother[Path Smoother: spline, line of sight pruning]
    Smoother --> SmoothPath[Executable Trajectory]
    SmoothPath --> Controller[Motion Controller: PID or MPC]
    Controller --> Motors[Wheel Motor Commands]
    Motors --> Robot[Mobile Robot Platform]
    Robot --> Sensors
```

**Role of each component:**

1. **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping):** Combines sensor data to build a map of the unknown environment while simultaneously estimating the robot's pose within it. Provides the foundational map upon which the occupancy grid is built.

2. **Occupancy Grid:** A 2D discretized map where each cell stores a probability of being occupied. The graph constructor extracts **free cells as nodes** and **adjacent free cells as edges**, forming the searchable graph $G = (V, E)$.

3. **Graph Search Engine:** Executes A* (or D* Lite) on the graph to find an optimal collision-free path from the robot's current pose to the goal pose, using the heuristic to guide the search efficiently.

4. **Path Smoother:** The raw grid path contains many sharp 90° turns. The smoother applies line-of-sight pruning (removing intermediate waypoints visible from the next one) and spline fitting to produce an **executable, smooth trajectory** that respects the robot's dynamic constraints (max velocity, acceleration, turning radius).

5. **Motion Controller:** Translates the smoothed trajectory into low-level motor commands using control algorithms such as PID, pure pursuit, or model predictive control (MPC). The loop closes as the robot moves and new sensor data updates the SLAM map, enabling continuous re-planning. — **7 marks**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 4 — Graph Search):**

- [x] **Graph $G = (V, E)$:** Nodes = states, Edges = transitions.
- [x] **A\* evaluation function:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — the *most tested formula* in this module.
- [x] **Admissible heuristic:** Never overestimates true cost: $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$.
- [x] **Consistent heuristic:** $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$; implies admissibility.
- [x] **BFS:** Level-by-level expansion; optimal only for uniform costs.
- [x] **DFS:** Goes deep first; memory-efficient but not optimal.
- [x] **Dijkstra / Uniform-Cost:** Uses $f(n) = g(n)$; optimal but slow.
- [x] **Greedy Best-First:** Uses $f(n) = h(n)$; fast but not optimal.
- [x] **A\*:** Combines $g$ and $h$; optimal *and* efficiently focused.
- [x] **Weighted A\*:** $f(n) = g(n) + \varepsilon h(n)$ with $\varepsilon > 1$; bounded-suboptimal, faster.
- [x] **D\* Lite:** Backward dynamic search; re-plans efficiently when environment changes.
- [x] **Manhattan heuristic** for 4-connected grids; **Euclidean** for 8-connected grids.
- [x] **Open list = priority queue (min-heap)** sorted by $f$; **Closed list = hash set** of expanded nodes.
- [x] **Path reconstruction** is done via back-pointers (`parent` field) — never forget this.
- [x] **Termination:** Stop when goal is popped from $OPEN$, not when it is *added* to $OPEN$.
- [x] **A\* is optimally efficient:** No other algorithm using the same heuristic expands fewer nodes.
- [x] **Real-world applications:** Self-driving cars, warehouse AMRs, drones, vacuum robots, game AI.
- [x] **Key complexity:** A* worst-case is exponential in solution depth, but average is near-polynomial with good heuristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Memorize $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$, the admissibility inequality, the Manhattan/Euclidean formulas, and the A\* iteration procedure. These four items carry 70%+ of marks in any graph-search question."*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT721 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Dijkstra's Algorithm # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!NOTE] > **Dijkstra's Algorithm** is a **graph search algorithm** that solves the **single-source shortest path problem** for a graph with **non-negative edge weights**, producing a shortest-path tree. In the context of mobile robotics, it is used as a **global path planner** to compute the **optimal (minimum-cost) trajectory** from a robot's start configuration to a goal configuration on a discretized configuration or workspace map. For a weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with a cost function $w: E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$, Dijkstra's algorithm finds the minimum accumulated cost $d(v)$ from a source node $s$ to every other node $v \in V$: $$d(v) = \min_{P \in \mathcal{P}_{s \rightarrow v}} \sum_{e \in P} w(e)$$ where $\mathcal{P}_{s \rightarrow v}$ is the set of all paths from $s$ to $v$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Water Flowing Through Pipes" Imagine a robot is in a building trying to reach a charging station. The floor between rooms is made of different materials — **smooth tile, carpet, gravel**. The robot moves slower on carpet and gravel. Imagine **water** is poured onto the robot's current position. The water naturally flows through the **easiest (cheapest) path** to the goal. - **Nodes** = Rooms / waypoints - **Edges** = Corridors / doorways - **Edge weights** = Energy or time required to traverse that corridor (carpet $\rightarrow$ higher weight; tile $\rightarrow$ lower weight) - **Algorithm** = Simulating how water would find the path of least resistance The robot "wants" to minimize battery use or travel time, just like water wants to minimize friction. Dijkstra's algorithm does exactly this **mathematically**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In mobile robotics, Dijkstra's algorithm is classified as a **graph-based global planner**. It guarantees **optimality** for environments represented as weighted graphs with non-negative costs (such as grid maps, topological maps, or roadmaps). ## 1.3 Why Dijkstra's Algorithm Matters in Mobile Robotics | Robotic Function | Role of Dijkstra's Algorithm | |---|---| | **Global Path Planning** | Computes shortest path in known static environment | | **Warehouse Automation** | Routes AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) optimally between stations | | **Autonomous Cars** | Pre-computes routes on digitized road networks | | **Service Robots** | Plans navigation in structured indoor environments | | **Multi-Robot Coordination** | Computes conflict-free paths in centralized planners | The **standard metric** used in robotics for cost evaluation is typically **path length (meters)**, **traversal time (seconds)**, or **energy consumption (Joules)**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Algorithmic Philosophy — The "Greedy Expansion" Principle Dijkstra's algorithm operates on a **greedy best-first** philosophy. It maintains a set of nodes whose minimum distance from the source is **already finalized** (the *closed set* or *visited set*) and gradually expands this set by always selecting the **unvisited node with the smallest tentative distance** (the *open set* or *frontier*). ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic The algorithm maintains four key data structures during execution: 1. **$d[v]$** — Current best-known distance from source $s$ to node $v$ 2. **$\pi[v]$** — Predecessor of $v$ on the shortest path (used for path reconstruction) 3. **$Q$** — Priority queue (min-heap) of unvisited nodes, keyed by $d[v]$ 4. **$S$** — Set of visited nodes with finalized shortest distances **Operational Steps:** - **Step 1 (Initialization):** Set $d[s] = 0$ and $d[v] = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$. Set $\pi[v] = \text{NIL}$. Insert all nodes into $Q$. - **Step 2 (Selection):** Extract the node $u$ from $Q$ with the **minimum** value of $d[u]$. Add $u$ to $S$. - **Step 3 (Relaxation):** For each neighbor $v$ of $u$, compute the candidate distance: $$d_{candidate}(v) = d[u] + w(u, v)$$ If $d_{candidate}(v) < d[v]$, then update: $$d[v] = d_{candidate}(v), \quad \pi[v] = u$$ - **Step 4 (Termination):** Repeat Steps 2–3 until $Q$ is empty (all nodes finalized) or until the goal node is extracted from $Q$. - **Step 5 (Path Reconstruction):** Starting from goal node $g$, follow predecessors $\pi[g], \pi[\pi[g]], \dots$ back to source $s$. Reverse the sequence to obtain the shortest path. > [!TIP] > **Why does greedy work?** Because all edge weights are non-negative, once a node $u$ is extracted from the priority queue (i.e., has the smallest $d[u]$ among all unvisited nodes), no future path through unvisited nodes can ever provide a smaller distance to $u$. This is the **cutting property** of shortest paths. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | **Distance Update (Relaxation)** | $d[v] = \min\bigl(d[v],\; d[u] + w(u,v)\bigr)$ | where $u$ is current node, $v$ is neighbor | | **Candidate Distance** | $d_{cand}(v) = d[u] + w(u, v)$ | computed for every neighbor | | **Predecessor Update** | $\pi[v] = u$ if $d_{cand}(v) < d[v]$ | only on successful relaxation | | **Total Path Cost** | $C(P) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} w(v_i, v_{i+1})$ | sum of edge weights along path $P$ | | **Heuristic (for comparison with A*)** | $h(v) = 0$ | Dijkstra uses zero heuristic (uninformed) | | **Time Complexity (Naive)** | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | when using array-based min-search | | **Time Complexity (Heap)** | $O\bigl((V + E) \log V\bigr)$ | with binary/Fibonacci heap | | **Space Complexity** | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | stores graph + distance array | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse $w(u,v)$ (edge weight) with $d[v]$ (shortest distance to $v$). The edge weight is a **static property** of the graph; the shortest distance is a **dynamic quantity** computed by the algorithm. ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility In **autonomous mobile robotics**, Dijkstra's algorithm is foundational for: - **Warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva, Fetch):** Compute optimal pick-and-place routes in structured grid environments. - **Self-driving cars:** Pre-compute fastest routes over road network graphs (OpenStreetMap, OSRM). - **Search and rescue:** Plan low-energy paths over uneven terrain maps. - **Service robots (Pepper, Roomba):** Plan room-to-room navigation in home/office environments. The algorithm's **guarantee of optimality** is its most prized property in safety-critical robotic applications, even though it is computationally heavier than greedy or heuristic methods. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — Robot Path Planning in a Warehouse Consider a **mobile robot** in a warehouse with **5 waypoints (nodes)** plus a **Start (S)** and **Goal (G)** node. The numbers on edges represent **travel cost in energy units** (lower = easier to traverse). ### 3.1.1 Graph Definition | Edge | Weight $w$ | Robot Interpretation | |---|---|---| | S $\rightarrow$ A | 4 | Smooth tile corridor | | S $\rightarrow$ B | 2 | Direct shortcut, slight slope | | A $\rightarrow$ B | 1 | Short doorway between rooms | | A $\rightarrow$ C | 5 | Long carpeted aisle | | A $\rightarrow$ D | 6 | Carpeted ramp | | B $\rightarrow$ C | 3 | Tiled shortcut | | B $\rightarrow$ D | 7 | Long detour around obstacle | | C $\rightarrow$ D | 2 | Short cross-corridor | | C $\rightarrow$ G | 4 | Final aisle to charging dock | | D $\rightarrow$ G | 3 | Direct loading-dock path | **Source:** S     **Goal:** G ### 3.1.2 Initialization $$d[S] = 0, \quad d[A] = d[B] = d[C] = d[D] = d[G] = \infty$$ $$\pi[v] = \text{NIL} \;\; \forall v, \quad S = \emptyset, \quad Q = \{S, A, B, C, D, G\}$$ ### 3.1.3 Iteration-by-Iteration Trace **Iteration 1:** Select $u = S$ (min $d[u] = 0$). Add $S$ to $S$ (visited set). Relax neighbors of $S$: - Relax S $\rightarrow$ A: $d_{cand}(A) = 0 + 4 = 4 < \infty$, so $d[A] = 4$, $\pi[A] = S$ - Relax S $\rightarrow$ B: $d_{cand}(B) = 0 + 2 = 2 < \infty$, so $d[B] = 2$, $\pi[B] = S$ State after Iteration 1: | Node | $d$ | $\pi$ | |---|---|---| | S | 0 | NIL | | A | 4 | S | | B | 2 | S | | C | $\infty$ | NIL | | D | $\infty$ | NIL | | G | $\infty$ | NIL | **Iteration 2:** Select $u = B$ (min $d[u] = 2$ among unvisited: A=4, B=2, C=$\infty$, D=$\infty$, G=$\infty$). Add $B$ to $S$. Relax neighbors of $B$: - Relax B $\rightarrow$ C: $d_{cand}(C) = 2 + 3 = 5 < \infty$, so $d[C] = 5$, $\pi[C] = B$ - Relax B $\rightarrow$ D: $d_{cand}(D) = 2 + 7 = 9 < \infty$, so $d[D] = 9$, $\pi[D] = B$ State after Iteration 2: | Node | $d$ | $\pi$ | |---|---|---| | S | 0 | NIL | | A | 4 | S | | B | 2 | S | | C | 5 | B | | D | 9 | B | | G | $\infty$ | NIL | **Iteration 3:** Select $u = A$ (min $d[u] = 4$). Add $A$ to $S$. Relax neighbors of $A$: - Relax A $\rightarrow$ B: $d_{cand}(B) = 4 + 1 = 5$. But $d[B] = 2 < 5$, **no update**. - Relax A $\rightarrow$ C: $d_{cand}(C) = 4 + 5 = 9$. But $d[C] = 5 < 9$, **no update**. - Relax A $\rightarrow$ D: $d_{cand}(D) = 4 + 6 = 10$. But $d[D] = 9 < 10$, **no update**. State after Iteration 3: | Node | $d$ | $\pi$ | |---|---|---| | S | 0 | NIL | | A | 4 | S | | B | 2 | S | | C | 5 | B | | D | 9 | B | | G | $\infty$ | NIL | **Iteration 4:** Select $u = C$ (min $d[u] = 5$). Add $C$ to $S$. Relax neighbors of $C$: - Relax C $\rightarrow$ D: $d_{cand}(D) = 5 + 2 = 7 < 9$, so $d[D] = 7$, $\pi[D] = C$ - Relax C $\rightarrow$ G: $d_{cand}(G) = 5 + 4 = 9 < \infty$, so $d[G] = 9$, $\pi[G] = C$ State after Iteration 4: | Node | $d$ | $\pi$ | |---|---|---| | S | 0 | NIL | | A | 4 | S | | B | 2 | S | | C | 5 | B | | D | 7 | C | | G | 9 | C | **Iteration 5:** Select $u = D$ (min $d[u] = 7$). Add $D$ to $S$. Relax neighbors of $D$: - Relax D $\rightarrow$ G: $d_{cand}(G) = 7 + 3 = 10$. But $d[G] = 9 < 10$, **no update**. State after Iteration 5: (no changes) **Iteration 6:** Select $u = G$ (min $d[u] = 9$). Goal reached. ### 3.1.4 Path Reconstruction Trace back from G using $\pi$: $$\pi[G] = C, \quad \pi[C] = B, \quad \pi[B] = S$$ Reversing: $S \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow G$. **Optimal Path:** $S \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow G$ **Optimal Cost Verification:** $$C(P^*) = w(S,B) + w(B,C) + w(C,G) = 2 + 3 + 4 = 9 \text{ energy units}$$ ### 3.1.5 Alternative Path Check (Sanity Verification) - Path $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow C \rightarrow G$ has cost $4 + 5 + 4 = 13$ ❌ (suboptimal) - Path $S \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow G$ has cost $2 + 7 + 3 = 12$ ❌ (suboptimal) - Path $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow G$ has cost $4 + 1 + 3 + 4 = 12$ ❌ (suboptimal) **Confirmed:** $S \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow G$ with cost $\mathbf{9}$ is the **unique optimal path**. ### 3.1.6 Proof of Optimality (Sketch for KTU 14-Mark Questions) By **induction** on the number of visited nodes: - **Base case:** $S$ is visited first with $d[S] = 0$, which is trivially optimal. - **Inductive step:** Assume all nodes in $S$ have correct shortest distances. Let $u$ be the next node extracted from $Q$. For any path $P$ from $S$ to $u$ that goes through an unvisited node $x$, the cost satisfies: $$C(P) \geq d[x] + w(x, u) \geq d[x] \geq d[u]$$ The first inequality is the path sum decomposition; the second uses non-negative weights ($w \geq 0$); the third uses the **min-heap property** ($u$ was minimum). Hence $d[u]$ is final and optimal. $$\blacksquare$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart (Dijkstra's Algorithm) ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start]) --> init["Initialize:
d s = 0, d v = Infinity for all v
pi v = NIL
Visited S = empty
Queue Q = all nodes"] init --> check{Q is empty?} check -- No --> extract["Extract node u with
minimum d u from Q"] extract --> addS["Add u to Visited set S"] addS --> relaxLoop["For each neighbor v of u"] relaxLoop --> compute["Compute d_cand v = d u + w u,v"] compute --> compare{d_cand v < d v ?} compare -- Yes --> update["Update d v = d_cand v
Set pi v = u"] compare -- No --> next update --> next["Get next neighbor"] next --> moreN{More neighbors?} moreN -- Yes --> relaxLoop moreN -- No --> goalCheck{u is Goal node?} goalCheck -- No --> check goalCheck -- Yes --> reconstruct["Reconstruct path:
Follow pi from Goal to Source
Reverse sequence"] reconstruct --> done([Output shortest path and cost]) check -- Yes --> done ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Robot Path Planning using Dijkstra ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph inputStage[Input Stage] envMap["Environment Map
Grid or Topological"] startPose["Robot Start Pose
x_s, y_s, theta_s"] goalPose["Goal Pose
x_g, y_g, theta_g"] end subgraph graphStage[Graph Construction Stage] discretize["Workspace
Discretization"] nodes["Node Set V
Waypoints / Cells"] edges["Edge Set E
with non-negative weights"] end subgraph algoStage[Algorithm Execution Stage] initModule["Distance Initialization
d s = 0, d others = Infinity"] priQueue["Priority Queue Q
Min-Heap Data Structure"] relaxEngine["Relaxation Engine
d v = min d v, d u + w u,v"] end subgraph outputStage[Output Stage] shortPath["Shortest Path
S to G sequence"] pathCost["Minimum Cost
energy / time / distance"] cmdVel["Velocity Commands
v, omega to Actuators"] end envMap --> discretize startPose --> discretize goalPose --> discretize discretize --> nodes nodes --> edges edges --> initModule initModule --> priQueue priQueue --> relaxEngine relaxEngine --> priQueue relaxEngine --> shortPath shortPath --> pathCost pathCost --> cmdVel ``` ## 4.3 Sample Graph for the Worked Example ```mermaid graph LR S((S)) -- "4" --> A((A)) S -- "2" --> B((B)) A -- "1" --> B A -- "5" --> C((C)) A -- "6" --> D((D)) B -- "3" --> C B -- "7" --> D C -- "2" --> D C -- "4" --> G((G)) D -- "3" --> G ``` **Optimal Path Highlighted:** $S \xrightarrow{2} B \xrightarrow{3} C \xrightarrow{4} G$ with total cost $\mathbf{9}$. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **State any three conditions under which Dijkstra's algorithm fails or cannot be applied directly in mobile robot path planning.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Dijkstra's algorithm fails or cannot be applied directly when: 1. **Negative edge weights are present:** The algorithm assumes non-negative traversal costs, since its greedy selection relies on the cutting property. Negative cycles cause infinite cost reduction. **(1 mark)** 2. **Continuous (unbounded) configuration space:** The algorithm requires a discretized graph representation. If the environment is continuous without discretization, a roadmap or grid must first be constructed. **(1 mark)** 3. **Dynamic / unknown environment:** Dijkstra's is a **static global planner**. If obstacles move or appear after planning, replanning or local planners (e.g., D*, Dynamic Window) are needed. **(1 mark)** --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Differentiate between Dijkstra's algorithm and A* algorithm in the context of mobile robot path planning.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Dijkstra's Algorithm | A* Algorithm | |---|---|---| | Heuristic function | $h(v) = 0$ (none) | $h(v) = $ estimated cost to goal (e.g., Euclidean) | | Search nature | **Uninformed**, expands uniformly | **Informed**, expands goal-directed | | Optimality | Optimal for non-negative weights | Optimal if $h$ is **admissible** | | Speed | Slower (explores all directions) | Faster (focuses toward goal) | | Evaluation function | $f(v) = g(v)$ | $f(v) = g(v) + h(v)$ | **(1 mark per correct point, up to 3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure of Dijkstra's algorithm. State its time and space complexity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A mobile robot operates in a warehouse represented by the following weighted graph. Compute the shortest path from node **S** to node **G** using Dijkstra's algorithm. Show all iterations in a table. **(7 marks)** | Edge | Weight | Edge | Weight | |---|---|---|---| | S-A | 6 | B-D | 4 | | S-B | 3 | C-D | 2 | | A-B | 1 | C-G | 5 | | A-C | 4 | D-G | 6 | | B-C | 2 | A-D | 7 | --- ### Model Solution for Question A: **(a) Procedure & Complexity — 7 marks:** **Step 1:** Initialize $d[s] = 0$, $d[v] = \infty$ for $v \neq s$. **(0.5 mark)** **Step 2:** Insert all nodes into priority queue $Q$. **(0.5 mark)** **Step 3:** While $Q$ is not empty: - Extract $u = \arg\min_{v \in Q} d[v]$ **(1 mark)** - Add $u$ to visited set $S$ **(0.5 mark)** - For each neighbor $v$ of $u$, perform **relaxation**: $d[v] = \min(d[v], d[u] + w(u,v))$ and update predecessor if relaxed. **(2 marks)** **Step 4:** Terminate when $Q$ is empty or goal reached. **(0.5 mark)** **Step 5:** Reconstruct path using $\pi[\cdot]$ chain. **(1 mark)** **Complexity:** - Time: $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ for naive implementation; $O\bigl((V + E) \log V\bigr)$ with binary heap **(0.5 mark)** - Space: $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ **(0.5 mark)** **(b) Worked Example — 7 marks:** **Iteration Table:** | Iter | Visited | $d[S]$ | $d[A]$ | $d[B]$ | $d[C]$ | $d[D]$ | $d[G]$ | Action | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Init | — | 0 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Initialize | | 1 | S | 0 | 6 | 3 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Pick S; relax S-A, S-B | | 2 | S,B | 0 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 7 | $\infty$ | Pick B; relax B-A, B-C, B-D | | 3 | S,B,A | 0 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 7 | $\infty$ | Pick A; relax A-C, A-D (no improvement) | | 4 | S,B,A,C | 0 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 7 | 10 | Pick C; relax C-D (no), C-G | | 5 | S,B,A,C,D | 0 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 7 | 10 | Pick D; relax D-G (no improvement since $7+6=13>10$) | | 6 | All | 0 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 7 | 10 | Pick G; done | **Detailed Relaxation Steps:** - **Iter 1 — Pick S ($d=0$):** $d[A] = 0+6 = 6$; $d[B] = 0+3 = 3$. **(1 mark)** - **Iter 2 — Pick B ($d=3$):** $d[A] = \min(6, 3+1) = 4$; $d[C] = 3+2 = 5$; $d[D] = 3+4 = 7$. **(1.5 marks)** - **Iter 3 — Pick A ($d=4$):** $d[C] = \min(5, 4+4) = 5$ (no change); $d[D] = \min(7, 4+7) = 7$ (no change). **(1 mark)** - **Iter 4 — Pick C ($d=5$):** $d[D] = \min(7, 5+2) = 7$ (no change); $d[G] = 5+5 = 10$. **(1 mark)** - **Iter 5 — Pick D ($d=7$):** $d[G] = \min(10, 7+6) = 10$ (no change). **(0.5 mark)** - **Iter 6 — Pick G ($d=10$):** Goal reached. **(0.5 mark)** **Path Reconstruction:** $$\pi[G] = C, \quad \pi[C] = B, \quad \pi[B] = S$$ **Optimal Path:** $S \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow G$ **Optimal Cost:** $3 + 2 + 5 = \mathbf{10}$ **(0.5 mark)** --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** (14 Marks) **(a)** Discuss the role of Dijkstra's algorithm as a **global path planner** in mobile robotics. Explain with a suitable block diagram how it integrates with local planners. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a hospital service robot application, the floor map is discretized as shown below. The robot starts at S and must reach G. The numbers on the grid cells represent **traversal difficulty** (1 = smooth tile, 5 = carpet with obstacles). Use Dijkstra's algorithm to find the **minimum difficulty path** from S to G. **(7 marks)** > *Grid representation (4x4):* > > | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 | Col 4 | > |---|---|---|---| > | **S**(1) | 3 | 4 | 2 | > | 2 | 5 | 3 | 1 | > | 4 | 6 | 2 | 3 | > | 1 | 4 | 5 | **G**(1) | > > *(4-connected grid; movement allowed up/down/left/right with cost equal to the destination cell value.)* --- ### Model Solution for Question B: **(a) Role of Dijkstra's in Mobile Robotics — 7 marks:** **1. Global vs. Local Planning Distinction (2 marks):** - **Global planner:** Computes an overall path from start to goal in a **known static map** using complete world information. Dijkstra's algorithm serves this role. - **Local planner:** Generates real-time motion commands while avoiding immediate obstacles detected by sensors. Examples: Dynamic Window Approach (DWA), Vector Field Histogram (VFH). **2. Role of Dijkstra's (2 marks):** - Discretizes the workspace into a graph (grid, visibility graph, or PRM roadmap). - Computes the **minimum-cost global path** with guaranteed optimality for non-negative weights. - Pre-computes a coarse path that the local planner follows and refines. **3. Block Diagram Description (2 marks):** - **Inputs:** Map, start pose, goal pose $\rightarrow$ **Graph Builder** $\rightarrow$ Dijkstra's Solver $\rightarrow$ **Global Path** $\rightarrow$ **Local Planner** (uses sensor data) $\rightarrow$ **Velocity Commands** $\rightarrow$ **Robot Actuators**. - Feedback from sensors updates the local planner, while the global path remains a high-level reference. **4. Advantages & Limitations (1 mark):** - ✅ Optimal, complete, deterministic. - ❌ No heuristic guidance (slower than A*), requires full re-planning if environment changes. **(b) Grid-based Dijkstra's — 7 marks:** **Setup:** Each cell is a node; movement cost = value of destination cell. Start $S$ at (1,1), Goal $G$ at (4,4). **Key Candidate Paths and Costs:** | Path | Cells Traversed | Cost Calculation | Total | |---|---|---|---| | P1: Right-Right-Right-Down-Down-Down | 3+4+2+3+1+1 | $3+4+2+3+1+1$ | 14 | | P2: Down-Down-Right-Right-Right-Down | 6+4+4+3+2+1 | $6+4+4+3+2+1$ | 20 | | P3: Right-Down-Right-Down-Right-Down | 3+5+3+2+2+1 | $3+5+3+2+2+1$ | 16 | | P4: Down-Right-Down-Right-Right-Right | 4+5+2+3+2+1 | $4+5+2+3+2+1$ | 17 | | P5: Right-Right-Down-Right-Down-Down | 3+4+3+2+2+1 | $3+4+3+2+2+1$ | 15 | **Best Path (P1):** $S \rightarrow (1,2) \rightarrow (1,3) \rightarrow (1,4) \rightarrow (2,4) \rightarrow (3,4) \rightarrow G$ **Minimum Cost = 14 energy/difficulty units** **(2 marks for table, 2 marks for calculation, 1 mark for identification, 1 mark for path tracing, 1 mark for final answer)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing $d[v]$ (running cost) with edge weight $w(u,v)$:** Many students add the source cell value instead of the destination cell value. Always re-read the problem statement carefully. > 2. **Forgetting predecessor update $\pi[v]$:** Path reconstruction is incomplete without maintaining and tracing the predecessor chain. > 3. **Not showing all iterations:** KTU examiners award marks for **iteration tables**. Skipping iterations leads to mark deductions. > 4. **Marking a node as "visited" too early:** A node is only finalized when extracted from the priority queue, not when first relaxed. > 5. **Ignoring the "internal choice" rule:** In Part B, KTU 2024 scheme mandates that students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (full 14 marks), not a mix. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Dijkstra's Algorithm (KTU 2024)** - 🔹 **Definition:** Greedy graph search algorithm that finds the shortest path from a source node to all other nodes in a graph with **non-negative** edge weights. - 🔹 **Core Data Structures:** Distance array $d[v]$, predecessor array $\pi[v]$, priority queue $Q$ (min-heap), visited set $S$. - 🔹 **Key Operation:** **Relaxation** — $d[v] = \min\bigl(d[v],\; d[u] + w(u,v)\bigr)$. - 🔹 **Optimality Guarantee:** Yes, but **only** for graphs with **non-negative** edge weights. - 🔹 **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ naive; $O\bigl((V+E)\log V\bigr)$ with binary heap; $O(E + V\log V)$ with Fibonacci heap. - 🔹 **Space Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. - 🔹 **Robotic Application:** Global path planner on grid/roadmap representations; used in warehouse AGVs, autonomous vehicles, service robots. - 🔹 **Key Limitation in Robotics:** No heuristic guidance $\Rightarrow$ explores in all directions; inefficient for large maps. (Use **A*** for faster goal-directed search.) - 🔹 **Differs from BFS:** BFS gives shortest path in **unweighted** graphs; Dijkstra handles **weighted** graphs (positive weights). - 🔹 **Reconstruction:** Always trace back via $\pi[\cdot]$ from goal to source and reverse the chain. - 🔹 **Common Mistake:** Mixing up edge weight $w(u,v)$ with shortest distance $d[v]$. Remember — $w$ is fixed; $d$ evolves. - 🔹 **KTU-Specific Tip:** For 14-mark problems, always provide a full **iteration table** with $d$, $\pi$, and visited set. Marks are awarded for **process**, not just the final path.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Topic:** D* Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4: D* ALGORITHMS — DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **D\* (Dynamic A\*) Algorithm** is an incremental, heuristic-based path planning algorithm developed by **Anthony Stentz in 1994**. It is an extension of the classical **A\* algorithm** specifically designed to operate efficiently in **dynamic, partially known environments** where the robot discovers unknown obstacles or terrain changes during traversal.

The D\* algorithm performs a **backwards search from the goal to the start**, maintaining a state-space cost map that is **incrementally updated** when new environmental information is acquired. This allows the mobile robot to **re-plan trajectories in real-time** without recomputing the entire path from scratch, making it computationally efficient for autonomous navigation in unknown terrains.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, D\* belongs to the family of **reactive / online path planning algorithms** for mobile robots operating in **non-deterministic, partially observable** environments.

### Key Terminology Decoded

| Term | KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **State Space** | The discretized 2D grid of the robot's operational environment, denoted as $\mathcal{X}$ |
| **Cost Function** $c(X,Y)$ | Edge cost of traversing from state $X$ to neighboring state $Y$ |
| **Heuristic** $h(X)$ | Estimated cost from state $X$ to the start (or goal, depending on convention) |
| **OPEN List** | Priority queue of states waiting to be processed |
| **CLOSED List** | Set of states already expanded and removed from OPEN |
| **Tag (NEW/OPEN/CLOSED/RAISED/LOWERED)** | Status identifier for each state in the search process |
| **Re-planning** | Local update of path costs when new obstacles are detected |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of D\* as a "smart hiker" lost in foggy mountains.** 🏔️

Imagine you are hiking in a **foggy mountain range** trying to reach a base camp (the goal). The fog is so thick that you can only see a few meters ahead. As you walk:

- **You constantly discover the terrain beneath your feet** — sometimes it's smooth (low cost), sometimes it's a cliff (infinite cost).
- Each time you find a new obstacle, you don't throw away your entire map and start over (which would be wasteful).
- Instead, you **only re-route around the discovered obstacle**, reusing the rest of your known map.

**This is exactly how D\* works for a mobile robot:**

- 🤖 The robot doesn't know the full map in advance.
- 🚧 As it moves, its sensors (LIDAR, sonar, cameras) detect new obstacles.
- 🔄 D\* **incrementally updates the cost function** for affected cells.
- 🗺️ A new optimal path is computed **locally**, not globally.

### Real-World Analogy Table

| D\* Concept | Hiker Analogy |
|---|---|
| Goal | Base camp |
| Start | Current location |
| $g(X)$ | Actual distance walked so far |
| $h(X)$ | Estimated remaining distance (as the crow flies) |
| $c(X,Y)$ | Terrain difficulty between two points |
| New obstacle discovered | Sudden rockfall blocking the path |
| Re-planning | Finding a detour around the rockfall |

---

## 1.3 Why D\* is Critical for Mobile Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **D\* is the workhorse of real-world autonomous navigation** in unknown environments. Its primary use cases include:
> - **Mars Exploration Rovers** (NASA's Spirit, Opportunity, Curiosity) used D\* derivatives for path planning.
> - **Autonomous Ground Vehicles (AGVs)** in unmapped warehouses.
> - **Search and Rescue Robots** in collapsed buildings.
> - **Mine-clearing robots** where the terrain is progressively revealed.

### Key Distinction: A\* vs. D\*

| Feature | A\* Algorithm | D\* Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| Search Direction | **Forward** (Start → Goal) | **Backward** (Goal → Start) |
| Environment | **Static, fully known** | **Dynamic, partially known** |
| Re-planning | Full recomputation required | **Incremental re-planning** |
| Computational Cost | High for repeated searches | Low — reuses prior information |
| State Tagging | OPEN / CLOSED only | NEW / OPEN / CLOSED / RAISED / LOWERED |
| Use Case | Maps known beforehand | Robot explores & learns map |

---

## 1.4 The KTU Exam-Relevant Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Must-memorize for KTU Board Exam:**
>
> 1. **D\* = Dynamic A\*** — an incremental, sensor-based path planning algorithm.
> 2. **Backwards search** — propagation of cost values moves from goal to start.
> 3. **Three key cost variables**: $g(X)$, $rhs(X)$, and $h(X)$.
> 4. **State consistency condition** — a state is **locally consistent** when $g(X) = rhs(X)$.
> 5. **Re-planning is local**, triggered by `MODIFY-COST(X, Y, c_{new})` after edge costs change.

# 🧠 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Underlying Philosophy of D\*

The D\* algorithm is built upon three foundational principles that differentiate it from classical graph search:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3 Pillars of D\*:**
> 1. **Heuristic Guidance** — borrowed from A\*, ensures optimality.
> 2. **Backwards Search Direction** — propagation from goal to start, so re-planning is localized.
> 3. **Incremental Updates** — only the affected regions of the cost map are recomputed when changes occur.

---

## 2.2 Cost Variables — The Mathematical Heart of D\*

For every state $X$ in the state space $\mathcal{X}$, D\* maintains **three critical cost values**:

### 🔹 1. $g(X)$ — Current Best Path Cost
$$g(X) = \begin{cases} \text{currently known minimum cost from } X \text{ to goal} & \text{if path exists} \\ \infty & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> Represents the **best estimate** of the cost from $X$ to the goal based on current knowledge.

### 🔹 2. $rhs(X)$ — Right-Hand Side / One-Step Lookahead
$$rhs(X) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } X = G \text{ (goal)} \\ \min_{X' \in \text{Succ}(X)} \left[ c(X, X') + g(X') \right] & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> The **predicted cost** from $X$ to the goal, computed by examining the cost to each successor plus that successor's $g$-value.

### 🔹 3. $h(X, S)$ — Heuristic Estimate
$$h(X, S) = \text{estimated cost from } X \text{ to the start } S$$

> Admissible heuristic — must **never overestimate** the true cost. Common choice: **Euclidean distance**.
> $$h(X, S) = \sqrt{(x_X - x_S)^2 + (y_X - y_S)^2}$$

### 🔹 State Consistency (Optimality Condition)
A state $X$ is **locally consistent** if and only if:
$$g(X) = rhs(X)$$

> When **all** states are consistent, the path is optimal.

---

## 2.3 The Priority Key Function

Each state in the OPEN list is associated with a **pair of priority keys** $[k_1, k_2]$:

$$k_1(X) = \min(g(X), rhs(X)) + h(X, S)$$
$$k_2(X) = \min(g(X), rhs(X))$$

> [!NOTE]
> These keys determine the order in which states are expanded. The state with **minimum $k_1$** (and ties broken by $k_2$) is processed first.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEEP THIS TABLE HANDY FOR EXAM REVISION**

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| $X, Y \in \mathcal{X}$ | States in the grid | Cell coordinates in 2D map |
| $c(X, Y)$ | Edge cost between adjacent states | Terrain traversal cost |
| $g(X)$ | Current path cost from $X$ to goal | Updated during search |
| $rhs(X)$ | One-step lookahead cost | Used to detect inconsistency |
| $h(X, S)$ | Heuristic from $X$ to Start | Euclidean / Manhattan distance |
| $k_1(X) = \min(g, rhs) + h$ | Primary priority key | Sorting OPEN list |
| $k_2(X) = \min(g, rhs)$ | Secondary priority key | Tie-breaking |
| $g(X) = rhs(X)$ | Consistency condition | Local optimality check |
| $X = \arg\min k_1, k_2$ | Next state to expand | Top of priority queue |
| $c(X, Y) = \infty$ | Blocked edge (obstacle) | Untraversable terrain |
| $\text{Succ}(X)$ | Successor states of $X$ | 4 or 8-connected neighbors |

---

## 2.5 The State Tag System

D\* uses **five tags** to track each state's lifecycle:

> [!NOTE]
> **Tag Lifecycle:**
>
> - **NEW** 🆕 — State has never been on the OPEN list. Initial value: $g(X) = \infty$, $rhs(X) = \infty$.
> - **OPEN** 📂 — State is currently on the OPEN list, awaiting expansion.
> - **CLOSED** 🔒 — State was on OPEN but has been processed and removed.
> - **RAISED** ⬆️ — State was CLOSED but $g$-value increased (cost rose due to new obstacle).
> - **LOWERED** ⬇️ — State was CLOSED but $g$-value decreased (cost dropped).

### State Transitions Diagram
```
NEW → OPEN (when inserted)
OPEN → CLOSED (when processed)
CLOSED → RAISED (when g increases)
CLOSED → LOWERED (when g decreases)
RAISED / LOWERED → OPEN (when re-inserted)
```

---

## 2.6 The Three Core D\* Functions

### 🔹 Function 1: `PROCESS-STATE()`
The main workhorse. Pop the **minimum-key state** $X$ from OPEN and:
- **If $X$ is RAISED** ($g(X) > rhs(X)$): $X$ is locally over-consistent → set $g(X) = rhs(X)$, mark CLOSED, and update predecessors.
- **If $X$ is LOWERED** ($g(X) < rhs(X)$): $X$ is locally under-consistent → set $g(X) = \infty$ (temporarily), expand to compute correct cost, then restore.
- For each predecessor $Y$ of $X$, update $rhs(Y)$ and re-insert into OPEN if changed.

### 🔹 Function 2: `MODIFY-COST(X, Y, c_{new})`
Triggered when the robot's sensors detect a change in edge cost.
- Update $c(X, Y) = c_{new}$
- If $rhs(X) \neq \min(c(X, Y) + g(Y))$, update $rhs(X)$ and insert $X$ into OPEN.

### 🔹 Function 3: `INSERT(X, h_{new})`
Inserts state $X$ into OPEN with updated key $[k_1, k_2]$ based on $h_{new}$.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of D\*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why engineers prefer D\* in deployment:**
>
> 1. **Sensor-driven** — naturally integrates with LIDAR, sonar, and camera data.
> 2. **Real-time re-planning** — supports dynamic obstacle avoidance (moving pedestrians, other robots).
> 3. **Bounded re-computation** — only affected region is re-searched, not the entire map.
> 4. **Provably optimal** — like A\*, it guarantees shortest path if heuristic is admissible.
> 5. **Mars rovers, AGVs, drones** — battle-tested in the field.

---

## 2.8 Variants of D\*

| Variant | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **D\*** | Original (Stentz, 1994) | Core syllabus topic |
| **D\* Lite** | Simplified version using A\* machinery (Koenig & Likhachev, 2002) | Frequently compared with D\* |
| **Focused D\*** | Optimized for real-time robot navigation | Practical implementation |
| **Anytime D\*** | Provides bounded suboptimal plans quickly | Advanced topic |

# 🔬 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: The $rhs$ Function

The $rhs$ value is **not a free variable** — it is **derived** from the $g$-values of successors. Let us derive it rigorously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Goal:** Express $rhs(X)$ purely in terms of $c(X, Y)$ and $g(Y)$.

### Step 1 — Define Successor Set
For state $X$, the **set of successor states** $\text{Succ}(X)$ consists of all grid cells reachable by a single edge traversal. In a 2D occupancy grid:
$$\text{Succ}(X) = \{Y \in \mathcal{X} : \text{dist}(X, Y) = 1 \text{ (4-connected) or } \sqrt{2} \text{ (8-connected)}\}$$

### Step 2 — Express the Cost-to-Goal as a Recursion
The **true optimal cost** $G^*(X)$ from $X$ to the goal satisfies the **Bellman optimality equation** for shortest paths:
$$G^*(X) = \min_{Y \in \text{Succ}(X)} \left[ c(X, Y) + G^*(Y) \right]$$

### Step 3 — Replace $G^*$ with $g$ (current estimate)
If the $g$-values of all successors are already optimal (i.e., the search has converged), then $g(Y) = G^*(Y)$. The **one-step lookahead** approximation is:
$$rhs(X) = \min_{Y \in \text{Succ}(X)} \left[ c(X, Y) + g(Y) \right]$$

### Step 4 — Special Case: The Goal
By definition, the cost from the goal to itself is **zero**:
$$rhs(G) = 0$$
$$g(G) = 0$$
$$h(G) = 0 \text{ (trivially)}$$

### Step 5 — Special Case: Unreachable Successors
If a successor $Y$ is blocked (an obstacle is detected), then $c(X, Y) = \infty$. The $rhs$ minimization skips such successors naturally:
$$rhs(X) = \min_{Y \in \text{Succ}(X), c(X,Y) \neq \infty} \left[ c(X, Y) + g(Y) \right]$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Priority Key Computation

Given a state $X$ with known $g(X)$, $rhs(X)$, and $h(X, S)$:

### Step 1 — Compute the Minimum of $g$ and $rhs$
This represents the **best known lower bound** on the cost from $X$ to the goal:
$$m(X) = \min(g(X), rhs(X))$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Primary Key $k_1$
$k_1$ combines the best-known cost-to-goal with the heuristic estimate to the start:
$$k_1(X) = m(X) + h(X, S)$$

> **Interpretation:** $k_1$ is the **expected total cost** (cost-to-goal + heuristic-to-start). States with smaller $k_1$ are processed first.

### Step 3 — Compute the Secondary Key $k_2$
$k_2$ is used as a **tie-breaker** to ensure FIFO-like behavior:
$$k_2(X) = m(X)$$

### Step 4 — Ordering Rule
Two states $X$ and $Y$ are compared as:
$$X \prec Y \iff \left[ k_1(X), k_2(X) \right] < \left[ k_1(Y), k_2(Y) \right] \text{ lexicographically}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: D\* on a 4×4 Grid

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A mobile robot must navigate from Start $S = (0,0)$ to Goal $G = (3,3)$ in a 4×4 grid. Initially, all cells are free. Mid-traversal, an obstacle is detected at cell $(2,2)$.

### Step 1 — Initialization
- Set $g(X) = \infty$ for all $X \neq G$.
- Set $g(G) = 0$, $rhs(G) = 0$.
- Set $h(X, S) = \text{Euclidean distance from } X \text{ to } S$.
- Insert $G$ into OPEN with key:
$$k_1(G) = \min(0, 0) + h(G, S) = 0 + \sqrt{18} \approx 4.24$$
$$k_2(G) = \min(0, 0) = 0$$

### Step 2 — First Expansion
Process state $G$. Compute $rhs$ for each predecessor of $G$. For example, predecessor $P = (2,3)$:
$$rhs(P) = c(P, G) + g(G) = 1 + 0 = 1$$
$$k_1(P) = 1 + h(P, S) = 1 + \sqrt{13} \approx 4.61$$
$$k_2(P) = 1$$

Insert $P$ into OPEN. Continue propagating $rhs$ values for all neighbors of $G$.

### Step 3 — Search Converges (Initial Path)
After iterations, the OPEN list contains all cells with $g$-values representing the **backward distance to the goal**. A typical map of $g$-values for the 4×4 grid (Euclidean heuristic, 8-connected):

| | | | |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $1$ |
| $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $2$ | $0$ ← Goal |

The robot follows the **gradient of decreasing $g$-values** to reach the goal.

### Step 4 — Obstacle Detected at $(2,2)$
Mid-traversal, sensors detect an obstacle at cell $(2,2)$:
$$c((2,2), \text{neighbors}) = \infty$$

### Step 5 — Call `MODIFY-COST`
For each cell $X$ adjacent to $(2,2)$ where the edge cost just changed:
- If $rhs(X)$ depends on the blocked edge (i.e., $rhs(X) = c(X, (2,2)) + g((2,2))$), then $rhs(X)$ becomes invalid.
- Recompute $rhs(X) = \min$ over all remaining successors.
- If $rhs(X)$ changes, re-insert $X$ into OPEN.

### Step 6 — Re-Expansion
The algorithm re-processes the affected states. Since the obstacle is local, only states within a **bounded "ripple zone"** around $(2,2)$ are re-expanded. States far from the obstacle retain their old $g$-values, saving computation.

### Step 7 — New Path Computed
The robot extracts the new optimal path by following the **steepest descent in the updated $g$-field**, detouring around cell $(2,2)$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: D\* vs. Repeated A\*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a KTU exam favorite.** ⭐

| Criterion | Repeated A\* | D\* |
|---|---|---|
| Initial search cost | High (full A\*) | High (full A\*) |
| Cost per re-plan | High (full re-search) | **Low (incremental)** |
| Search direction | Forward (Start → Goal) | **Backward (Goal → Start)** |
| Heuristic usage | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $k_1 = \min(g,rhs) + h$ |
| State info stored | $g, h, f$ | $g, rhs, h, k_1, k_2, \text{tag}$ |
| Memory complexity | $O(\vert\mathcal{X}\vert)$ | $O(\vert\mathcal{X}\vert)$ |
| Time complexity per re-plan | $O(\vert\mathcal{X}\vert \log \vert\mathcal{X}\vert)$ | $O(\vert C \vert \log \vert\mathcal{X}\vert)$ where $\vert C \vert$ is the size of the affected region |
| Optimality | Optimal (admissible $h$) | Optimal (admissible $h$) |
| Suits dynamic env? | No (must restart) | **Yes (re-plans locally)** |

---

## 3.5 KTU-Pattern Question Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **"Given a 3×3 grid with Start S at (0,0), Goal G at (2,2), and an obstacle at (1,1), compute the OPEN list after the first 3 iterations of D\*. Use 4-connectivity and unit edge costs."**

### Solution:

**Initial State:**
- $g(X) = \infty$ for all $X \neq G$
- $g(G) = 0$, $rhs(G) = 0$
- OPEN $= \{G\}$ with key $[\sqrt{8}, 0]$

**Iteration 1: Process $G$**
- Predecessors of $G$ (4-connected): $(2,1)$ and $(1,2)$
- For $(2,1)$: $rhs(2,1) = c((2,1), G) + g(G) = 1 + 0 = 1$
  - Key: $[1 + h((2,1), S), 1] = [1 + 2, 1] = [3, 1]$
- For $(1,2)$: $rhs(1,2) = 1 + 0 = 1$
  - Key: $[1 + h((1,2), S), 1] = [1 + 2, 1] = [3, 1]$
- Mark $G$ as CLOSED. OPEN $= \{(2,1), (1,2)\}$

**Iteration 2: Process $(2,1)$ (or $(1,2)$ — tied)**
- Predecessors: $(2,0)$, $(1,1)$, $(2,2)=G$
- $(2,0)$: $rhs = 1 + 1 = 2$, key $[2 + 2, 2] = [4, 2]$
- $(1,1)$: $rhs = 1 + 1 = 2$, key $[2 + \sqrt{2}, 2] \approx [3.41, 2]$
- Mark $(2,1)$ as CLOSED.

**Iteration 3: Process $(1,2)$**
- Predecessors: $(1,1)$, $(0,2)$, $(2,2)=G$
- $(1,1)$: $rhs$ might change. Recompute as $\min$ over $(1,1)$'s successors: $(1,2) \to 1+1=2$, $(2,1) \to 1+1=2$, $(0,1) \to$ not yet known. Current minimum: $2$.
- $(0,2)$: $rhs = 1 + 1 = 2$, key $[2 + 1, 2] = [3, 2]$

**OPEN list after 3 iterations:** Contains $(1,1), (2,0), (0,2)$ and others.
The algorithm continues until the Start's $g$-value is finalized.

# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 D\* Algorithm — High-Level Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Initialize State Space] --> B[Set g(X) = infinity, rhs(X) = infinity for all X]
    B --> C[Set g(Goal) = 0, rhs(Goal) = 0]
    C --> D[Insert Goal into OPEN List with Key k1, k2]
    D --> E{OPEN List Empty OR Start Consistent?}
    E -- No --> F[Pop State X with Min Key from OPEN]
    F --> G{X is RAISED gX > rhsX?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Set gX = rhsX Mark CLOSED]
    G -- No --> I{X is LOWERED gX less than rhsX?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Set gX = infinity Expand Pred]
    I -- No --> K[Mark X as CLOSED]
    H --> L[For each predecessor Y of X]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{Update rhsY and re-insert if changed}
    M --> E
    E -- Yes --> N[Extract Path by Gradient Descent on g values]
    N --> O{New Obstacle Detected by Sensors?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Call MODIFY-COST for affected edges]
    P --> Q[Re-insert affected states into OPEN]
    Q --> E
    O -- No --> R[Robot Executes Planned Path]
    R --> O
```

---

## 4.2 D\* State Space Representation (Backwards Search)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S[Start S 0,0] -- c=1 --> A[Cell A]
    S -- c=1 --> B[Cell B 1,0]
    A -- c=1 --> C[Cell C 1,1]
    B -- c=1 --> C
    B -- c=1 --> D[Cell D 2,0]
    C -- c=1 --> E[Cell E 2,1]
    C -- c=1 --> F[Cell F 1,2]
    D -- c=1 --> E
    E -- c=1 --> G[Cell G 2,2]
    F -- c=1 --> G
    E -- c=INFINITY --> H[Obstacle 3,1]
    F -- c=1 --> I[Cell I 0,2]
    G -- c=0 --> T[GOAL 3,3]
    I -- c=1 --> T

    style T fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style H fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style S fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Interpretation:** The graph shows the 2D grid with Start $S$, Goal $T$, free cells, and a detected obstacle $H$ (red). The D\* algorithm propagates cost values **backwards from $T$ to $S$**, computing the optimal path that avoids $H$.

---

## 4.3 D\* State Lifecycle Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW
    NEW --> OPEN : INSERT into priority queue
    OPEN --> CLOSED : PROCESS-STATE completes
    CLOSED --> RAISED : g value increases due to MODIFY-COST
    CLOSED --> LOWERED : g value decreases due to MODIFY-COST
    RAISED --> OPEN : Re-inserted for re-expansion
    LOWERED --> OPEN : Re-inserted for re-expansion
    OPEN --> CLOSED : PROCESS-STATE completes
    CLOSED --> [*]
```

---

## 4.4 D\* vs. A\* — Functional Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Static_Planning[A Star - Static Planning]
        A1[Known Full Map] --> A2[A Forward Search]
        A2 --> A3[Compute Optimal Path]
        A3 --> A4[Execute Path]
    end

    subgraph Dynamic_Planning[D Star - Dynamic Planning]
        D1[Partial Map] --> D2[Backward Search from Goal]
        D2 --> D3[Compute Initial Path]
        D3 --> D4[Execute and Sense Environment]
        D4 --> D5{New Obstacle?}
        D5 -- Yes --> D6[MODIFY-COST: Update affected edges]
        D6 --> D7[Incremental Re-plan]
        D7 --> D4
        D5 -- No --> D4
    end

    style D5 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style D6 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    style D7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
```

---

## 4.5 D\* Re-planning Concept — Localized Ripple Effect

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph "Before Obstacle Discovery"
        P1[Path 1: S → A → B → C → G] --> P2[Direct route, short distance]
    end

    subgraph "Obstacle Discovered at B"
        P3[Cell B becomes blocked] --> P4[Cost c B and neighbors = infinity]
    end

    subgraph "After D Star Re-planning"
        P5[Path 2: S → A → D → E → F → G] --> P6[Detour around B]
        P5 --> P7[Only cells near B re-searched]
        P7 --> P8[Cells far from B keep old g values]
    end

    P1 --> P3
    P3 --> P5
    style P3 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    style P7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style P8 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
```

> **Insight:** The ripple effect is **local** — D\* only re-evaluates the cost field in a small region around the discovered obstacle, achieving dramatic computational savings vs. full re-planning.
